I did enjoy 50 shades tho ... it didn't strain my brain in the least! so it does have some merit!

### janagram — 2013-02-19T20:33:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/janagrams-journey.2399/post-76517>

well, I hit about 75% at reading the books. We choose up a years worth around the first of the year, and decide who will host, etc. I like our system, its just so hard not to "party" with these gals, and almost all love to cook! But, I'm getting better.
Yesterday, DH and I drove (an hour) over the mountains to the coast and walked at a state park beach with dunes! Oh, it was cold! I'm afraid we didn't walk all that long....but the it was a good day. I really need to look at new stuff now and then....I stay home a lot.....I have a studio for painting in my house, and while that is nice, I could scream sometimes...
(friends think I'm always gallivanting cuz I try to get out and I have groups....but there's a reason for that!!)

### janagram — 2013-03-25T21:58:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/janagrams-journey.2399/post-81765>

Ok, once......upon a time......once, a couple years ago, I went slightly mad...stressed out over mom's cancer diagnosis (terminal), and began to behave strangely. I went around with a cloth wiping every corner of my house (I may have reported this before, sorry) and cleaning all day..as well as eating very rigidly...VLC and not much of it. One poached egg in the a.m., 2 grassfed beef hotdogs for lunch, maybe some lettuce and small pc of meat for dinner. It was strange. I lost weight. I never thought about food except how to avoid it. I wouldn't go to book club or out with friends. Oh, yeah, I lost a lot of weight. I loved it.
But sooner or later, sanity returned, I went out with friends, I cooked meals, I returned to my previous weight. (25 lbs. higher)
So, I guess I know how it is done, don't I? (yiiikes)

### caroline — 2013-03-25T23:48:57+0200

### Inger — 2013-03-26T09:28:21+0200

# Thread: Buffalo heart
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buffalo-heart.2401/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jmathisen — 2012-07-13T11:30:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buffalo-heart.2401/post-37447>

A cousin (inherited in the divorce, ) is giving me a bunch of organ meat that they don't eat. Elk liver, deer liver, moose liver, some various bear parts, (I drew the line at kidneys...) and some other stuff, but tonight I stopped by and picked up a buffalo heart.

Any ideas on how to prepare it? Quite frankly, it looks like a medium-sized ham. Sucker is big...

I'm thinking bacon has to be involved in the process, but outside of slice and fry, no real good ideas are coming to mind. I did enjoy the chicken hearts, but now that "fowl is foul" from BG4, I'm all depressed, as I liked the hearts and the liver, well, with time, I can see it happening.

### vkiernan — 2012-07-13T14:39:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buffalo-heart.2401/post-37457>

> **jmathisen;37614 wrote:** A cousin (inherited in the divorce, ) is giving me a bunch of organ meat that they don't eat. Elk liver, deer liver, moose liver, some various bear parts, (I drew the line at kidneys...) and some other stuff, but tonight I stopped by and picked up a buffalo heart.
>
>
>
> Any ideas on how to prepare it? Quite frankly, it looks like a medium-sized ham. Sucker is big...
>
>
>
> I'm thinking bacon has to be involved in the process, but outside of slice and fry, no real good ideas are coming to mind. I did enjoy the chicken hearts, but now that "fowl is foul" from BG4, I'm all depressed, as I liked the hearts and the liver, well, with time, I can see it happening.

Lucky you. I am not sure the organs are foul from fowl. Good question for Jack. The meat is but I would further research before you give up on the organs.

### tweety — 2012-07-13T15:51:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buffalo-heart.2401/post-37469>

I still eat the chicken livers and hearts. I figured if they are properly raised they still have important nutrients. I haven't worked up the courage to down the beef hearts and livers. Plus every time we buy a quarter side of beef they discard the organs, despite my requests to leave them in!

As to the original poster, I would grind it up and add it to my ground meat. But I like Souldanzer's plan to make jerky! I wish we had a good source of jerky here- that would be awesome!

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-13T16:26:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buffalo-heart.2401/post-37475>

I've go heart defrosting in my fridge right now as we speak - I'm planning on making jerky - but there's a part of me that wants DH to throw it on the grill for me tonight

### Tizawee — 2012-07-13T16:47:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buffalo-heart.2401/post-37476>

Heart is a working muscle and tends to be tough. You could braise it (check any recipe site for braising instructions; it's basically cooking in liquid). Or you could cube it & make stew.

### johnnyb — 2012-07-13T16:52:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buffalo-heart.2401/post-37478>

The beef heart I have is scheduled to go into the grinder and become one of the meats in a meatloaf.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-13T19:34:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buffalo-heart.2401/post-37525>

I had my cow heart ground up with the liver and I add the mix to ground beef to make burgers. They have been my breakfast staple since January. I love the mix and so easy to get your organ meat into the diet.

I would try what some of the others have suggested and if you cannot stand the taste you can always try this idea as I don't hardly taste the liver in my burgers at all.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-14T03:42:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buffalo-heart.2401/post-37662>

We regularly get pastured ox and lamb hearts. I do what Jonnyb does and grind them up and add them to ground beef (our burgers are usually 1/3 heart, 1/3 liver/kidney and 1/3 ground beef.)

### SeaHorse — 2012-07-14T04:06:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buffalo-heart.2401/post-37667>

You can also stuff a heart with herbs and veggies and braise in a dutch oven with broth and wine (if that works for you).

# Thread: C677T  A1298C MTHFR mutations
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/c677t-a1298c-mthfr-mutations.2402/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 18
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### primat — 2012-07-13T12:51:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/c677t-a1298c-mthfr-mutations.2402/post-37449>

I'm wondering what's considered the normal and what's the mutation for the MTHFR gene, but I guess the way of referencing is used for other mutations, too.

Does C 677 T mean that the C is normal and one or more T's are hetero/homozygous ?

For the curious;

me C677T:AG & A1298C:GG

SO C677T:GG & A1298C:GT

Would this mean that I've got the homozygous mutation for C677T and homozygous for A1298C while and my wife is homozygous for A1298C only?

Edit: from 23andme

### JanSz — 2012-07-13T18:53:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/c677t-a1298c-mthfr-mutations.2402/post-37508>

> **primat;37616 wrote: I'm wondering what's considered the normal and what's the mutation for the MTHFR gene, but I guess the way of referencing is used for other mutations, too.
>
>
>
> Does C 677 T mean that the C is normal and one or more T's are hetero/homozygous ?
>
>
>
> For the curious;
>
> me C677T:AG & A1298C:GG
>
> SO C677T:GG & A1298C:GT
>
>
>
> Would this mean that I've got the homozygous mutation for C677T and homozygous for A1298C while and my wife is homozygous for A1298C only?
>
>
>
> Edit: from 23andme
>
>
> 23andme may be more detailed, but this is what I got:
>
>
>
> [http://labtestsonline.org/understanding/analytes/mthfr/tab/faq](http://http://labtestsonline.org/understanding/analytes/mthfr/tab/faq)
>
>
>
>
> > MTHFR
> >
> >
> >
> > Also known as: MTHFR DNA testing
> >
> > Formal name: Methylenetetrahydrofolate Reductase Mutations, C677T and A1298C
> >
> > Related tests: Homocysteine, Factor V Leiden Mutation and PT 20210 Mutation, Vitamin B12 & Folate, Cardiac Risk Assessment, Methotrexate
>
>
>
> The Test Sample
>
>
>
> What is being tested?
>
> The methylenetetrahydrofolate reductase (MTHFR) gene oversees the production of the MTHFR enzyme. This enzyme changes one form of folate into another and is part of the process that converts homocysteine into methionine, an important building block for many proteins.
>
>
>
> MTHFR testing detects two relatively common DNA sequence variants (single nucleotide polymorphisms, SNPs) in the MTHFR gene, C677T and A1298C. These SNPs result in changes in the DNA (or mutations) and are associated with increased homocysteine levels in the blood, which may increase the risk of premature cardiovascular disease (CVD), thrombosis, and stroke. Approximately 10-12% of the North American population is homozygous for C677T. There is some ethnic variability in the frequency, with the highest being in those of Mediterranean ancestry and the lowest in those of African ancestry.
>
>
>
> The C677T variant results in a **less active form of the MTHFR enzyme. When a person has two copies of the MTHFR C677T gene mutation (homozygous) or one copy of MTHFR C677T and one copy of A1298C (compound heterozygous), decreased MTHFR enzyme activity slows down the homocysteine-to-methionine conversion process and can lead to a buildup of homocysteine in the blood**.
>
>
>
> The increase in homocysteine is often mild to moderate but will vary from person to person depending upon the amount MTHFR enzyme activity. Homocysteine buildup is worsened when the affected person is folate-deficient.
>
>
>
> Results of some studies suggest that high levels of homocysteine in the blood may contribute to risk of CVD by damaging blood vessel walls and promoting formation of plaque (atherosclerosis) and inappropriate blood clots (thrombosis). However, a direct link between homocysteine levels and CVD or thrombotic risk has not been proven. For more on this, see the article on Homocysteine.**

**

Read top four lines in the blue chart for supplementation.

Use this test to guide doses.

Test check 34 items, ( 33 vitamins, minerals, amino acids and antioxidants are evaluated) repeat this test every 2x/year until all 34 are satisfied, then test 1x/year

Spectracell Comprehensive Nutritional Panel

[http://www.healthremedies.com/intracellular\_vitamin\_analysis\_spectracell.html](http://http://www.healthremedies.com/intracellular_vitamin_analysis_spectracell.html)

**

Thanks!

"When a person has two copies of the MTHFR C677T gene mutation (homozygous) or one copy of MTHFR C677T and _one copy of A1298C (compound heterozygous)_"

Does that mean ONLY one copy of A1298C? I got two A1298CC, and I'm not sure if that make it apply to me then?

HI, just saw your post. this is what I see

For the curious;

me C677T:AG & A1298C:GG you are +/- for C677t & +/+ for A1298C (corrected this. I looked at the chart wrong) Thanks WeAre Devo for responding.

SO C677T:GG & A1298C:GT wife -/- for C677T & -/+ for A1298C

Did you use Raw data rs1801133 for MTHFR C677T

rs1801131 for A1298C?

### [email redacted] — 2012-07-22T18:01:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/c677t-a1298c-mthfr-mutations.2402/post-40224>

Primat - yes the way it is written, C677T is where the T replaces the normal C at location 677 on the MTHFR gene. For A1298C a C is replaces the normal A at location 1298 on the MTHFR gene. This gene encodes the enzyme methylenetetrahydrofolate reductase. Reduces 5-10 methylene THF to,5 methyl-THF for methionine biosynthesis. If yours is not working well you can attempt to by-pass this limitation by supplementing with 5 methyl-THF.

[http://www.genecards.org/cgi-bin/carddisp.pl?gene=MTHFR&search=MTHFR#function](http://http://www.genecards.org/cgi-bin/carddisp.pl?gene=MTHFR&search=MTHFR#function)

Just an aside, depending on the orientation of 23andMe you will have to remember when comparing to yasko documentation that C=G and A=T (if orientation is minus versus plus).

If I am reading your post correctly, you are hetero (+/-) for C677T and homo for A1298C (+/+). Your SO is hetero for 1298 only. Now that you know you might have an issue you may want to consider doing a methlyation panel to see how your current boichemistry is working. I would recommend doing this prior to supplementing. There is only one lab that does this (Vitamin Diagnostics). Maybe you can talk your doc into ordering this for you, but expect to pay out of pocket on this one.

[http://www.seekinghealth.com/methylation-pathway-panel-vitamin-diagnostics.html](http://http://www.seekinghealth.com/methylation-pathway-panel-vitamin-diagnostics.html)

Recycled info (about a year old) from a forum on mothering.com....

MTHF. FOR C667T, make sure you take 200mg folate, 200mg folinic acid a day (Yasko doesn't recommend high doses here). For A1298C, she believes it has more to do with recycling BH4 than with folate. People with this mutation tend to end up low in BH4 (which is hard to supplement). Consider the metagenics supp intrinsi B12/folate (it has intrinsic factor that helps B12 get absorbed, and enough mthf folate).

Anyhow, the C677T one means you will not use folic acid very well, and need to take folinic & mthf folate (Yasko says 200mg of each a day, others have used more and seen benefit). A1298C is less about folate, more about recycling BH4. Low BH4 can cause low serotonin & dopamine, and build-up of ammonia. Liver support is good (liver glandulars, milk thistle, dandelion root). And something to clear ammonia if you need to. You can tell if you have an ammonia problem by doing a urine amino acids test.

And of course this is a good source as well. [http://mthfr.net/additional-resources/](http://http://mthfr.net/additional-resources/)

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-22T18:59:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/c677t-a1298c-mthfr-mutations.2402/post-40231>

This directly affect phase 1 and phase two detox of estrogens........as Jansz pictured and buried in that illustration......it also is what alters DNA methylation and this alters your epigenetic switches......this changes how your DNA is expressed......and what it is more sucseptible too........like Cancer etc........Key things are sunlight and oxygen tensions in the environment.

### Joann — 2012-08-03T01:26:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/c677t-a1298c-mthfr-mutations.2402/post-42580>

After reading and reading .....the more I know the less I know. The is data out there that states hetero for c677t is no big deal, but if also a1298c then it is called "compound" and should be treated as homo. Then I have read it could raise homocysteine, cause blot clots, risk for CRC, heavy metal toxicity. BUT it may also be protective for CRC, leukemia.

Also, very interesting, is the video by DR Ben, I think thats his name......says Italians in Sicily that have the snps show no risk ......food is better. He says not to take folic acid, but ok from food. only the already converted folacin supplement wit b12 and I think b6.

### MamaGrok — 2013-02-27T21:02:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/c677t-a1298c-mthfr-mutations.2402/post-78078>

Bumping old post ... I just got my 23andme results, and cannot figure out what I'm looking at.

When I search for MTHFR, it shows me neural tube defects analysis for marker rs1801133, and says:

\- for C677T, I am AG (typical odds of having a child with a neural tube defect). Clearly this is heterozygous, but I cannot yet understand whether simply having C677T, or having one A, is a problem

It also offers a whole chart showing all the SNPs they chart for MTHFR. For instance, the first few entries look like this:

Gene Position SNP Versions MamaGrok's Genotype
LOC100506310, MTHFR 11846198 rs15854 A or G AA
LOC100506310, MTHFR 11846252 rs4846048 A or G AG
LOC100506310, MTHFR 11846270 rs1057624 C or T TT
...
MTHFR 11854476 rs1801131 G or T GT

I don't really yet understand what MTHFR means, what methylation defects imply, how they form, how there are so many SNPs for MTHFR, or what C677T means, or where the A1298C y'all mention above is, but all I'm trying to figure out right now is...

Does what I found show far show a methylation defect issue? How do I interpret the data to know this?

### ATL_Paleo — 2013-02-27T21:39:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/c677t-a1298c-mthfr-mutations.2402/post-78091>

Have you tried GeneticGenie.org ?

It provides an easy to understand document of SNPs from your 23andme raw data. A result of +/-, +/+ or -/- is the easiest way to know if a SNP is heterozygous, homozygous or not an issue.

### lioness7 — 2013-02-28T16:42:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/c677t-a1298c-mthfr-mutations.2402/post-78305>

>
> Have you tried GeneticGenie.org ?
>
> It provides an easy to understand document of SNPs from your 23andme raw data. A result of +/-, +/+ or -/- is the easiest way to know if a SNP is heterozygous, homozygous or not an issue.

Livewello has come out with an app which reads even more snps than GG and is constantly being updated. It is 19.95. The link is found at [www.mthfrsupport.com](http://www.mthfrsupport.com). The site recently had a blogtalk radio explaining how it works. The app is free, you only pay to have your 23andme uploaded and deciphered.

[http://blogtalk.vo.llnwd.net/o23/show/4/465/show\_4465349.mp3](http://blogtalk.vo.llnwd.net/o23/show/4/465/show_4465349.mp3)

Hope this helps.

Detox Profile

I have copied both results as a picture files.

Addressing MTHFR A1298C
Addressing ACAT and SHMT SNPs
Addressing the CBS Mutation
Addressing the BHMT mutations

Above is for Methylation Analysis

There is no suggestions in Detox Profile section.

some of the advice, off hand, seems repetitive or contradictory.
But I have not spend enough time on this to have better understanding.

Comments are welcomed.

### TheKid — 2013-02-28T21:45:46+0200

### freesia — 2013-02-28T22:29:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/c677t-a1298c-mthfr-mutations.2402/post-78387>

What I got from the podcast was that regardless of SNPs, if you're running hard in the non fat burning (quick ATP recycling) pathways you deplete methyl groups, particularly through the ATP PC system. This is a double whammy because if you're in that pathway it's at the expense of the PPP where you make reduced glutathione. I **think**(?) this is where you're going to get optimal gene expression too.

Some SNPs may be significant (I guess thats where people like Tim Jackson come in) but you could still have plenty of the biomarkers of a methylation block like high oxidative stress, low reduced glutathione, low nadph....and not have anything "wrong" with your genetics.

### freesia — 2013-02-28T22:36:43+0200

Jan,

if you go to genova diagnostics (gdx.org) they have a sample detoxigenomic profile (?? think I have the name of that right) and you can compare their detox snps with the ones geneticgenie has pulled out for you.

Detox snps can indicate possible problems with things like charred meat, cigarette smoke, solvents that kind of thing...

>
> Hope this helps.

What is Livewello ?
Livewello is a Social Health Platform that provides tools to make it easier for people to be cared for, in collaboration with their Health Practitioners or Health Coaches.

[http://www.mthfrsupport.com/livewello-mthfr.html](http://www.mthfrsupport.com/livewello-mthfr.html)

Where can I go to start using Livewello?
Livewello is available as a free iPhone, iPad and iPod touch App.
Livewello is also free to use online; Go to livewello-app.appspot.com to Sign Up for a Livewello account.
You will need to Sign Up for a Livewello account if you need your 23andMe Raw Data interpreted.

[http://livewello-app.appspot.com/](http://livewello-app.appspot.com/)

.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-14T03:50:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mackerel-head-smoothie.2403/post-37665>

> **BJK77;37800 wrote:** Is the raw head that much more nutrient dense than just plain old cooked fish? Convince me it's worth it and I'm totally in

OK, here is my guess (not to say that Inger's word isn't good enough). The fish's brain contains the highest levels of the stuff in fish that we're after. Like with meat, when we avoid the 'gross parts' (offal, etc) we often miss out on the highest nutrient-dense bits.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-14T05:00:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mackerel-head-smoothie.2403/post-37687>

> **BJK77;37800 wrote:** Is the raw head that much more nutrient dense than just plain old cooked fish? Convince me it's worth it and I'm totally in

I'm guessing yes. You eat a lot of parts that you don't normally eat with the head. There's the brain which probably concentrates DHA and choline, then there's the eyes, which are super rich in zinc, and you'll also eat the thyroid gland if I recall correctly from my WAPF readings. I would love to hear from Dr K though if there's something else we're going for and why raw (might protect the DHA from oxidizing).

Sunday is live crab eating (we'll boil it first ) planned with one of my friends and maybe tomorrow he'll take me out to the best seafood place in Boulder (Jax ) so I may finally learn how to slurp raw oysters. I'm sorta excited about my new food adventures.... currently trying to learn how to make pickled salmon without whey. Any ideas?

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-15T08:45:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mackerel-head-smoothie.2403/post-37999>

OK, so I think I've found an optimal way to eat the fish head smoothies. Here's my method (combining the advice of Inger and Souldanzer and then a bit of my own). Frozen fish heads are the way to go... keeps smell to a minimum. Put fish heads in blender. Add fresh juice of 2 lemons and some salt. Blend to smooth (I don't add any extra water, just the lemon juice). Before you drink it, pour about 1 inch of red wine in a glass. Then drink smoothie as fast as possible. Immediately after, drink the red wine (actually, I used half of it to swish in my mouth and sort of gargle and then I drank the rest... even if you're doing a protocol where you can't drink any wine, just the swishing really did the trick... totally killed the lingering fish taste.) All in all, pretty palatable.

### Linz — 2012-07-15T11:36:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mackerel-head-smoothie.2403/post-38005>

Successful second attempt. I used herring as they were much fresher than the mackerel.

I added some lemon and parsley, held my breath and drank with a little tabasco - quite fishy and think it will take a bit of practice before the smoothie tastes as good as the herring and roe baked in the oven afterwards.

Still need to perfect the recipe before I can give my friends fish head smoothie for dinner (can I even admit to trying it myself?) but they are starting to ask the right questions. It's just so hard to overcome their fear of fats so imprinted on our brain by cw.

Inger, how hard was that first bite of high meat?? I've still got a long way to go.

> **BJK77;38495 wrote:** Are you all freezing the mackerels first or buying frozen ones? Does it matter?

I'm freezing first... I think Soul and others were thinking this could help with potentially bad bacteria. But I freeze to reduce smell and taste!

And, yes, Nonchalant, I got a bit of a high off it (in a good way). I hated the taste (and the texture, no straw) but I'm totally ready to do it again. I'm even going to go talk to the fishmonger who regularly puts fresh heads out for sale if they can save the livers for me. I'm thinking 2-3x week as a starting goal. (As someone on a budget, I also love that this super-meal is super cheap!).

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-17T08:07:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mackerel-head-smoothie.2403/post-38664>

Optimal fish head smoothie accomplished. I cut whole fish at the middle (I know that includes the liver... just don't tell me what else I'm eating!), put 2 in the blender with ghee and 2 Tablespoons of wild caught, raw fish roe. Added juice of 3 lemons and salt and blended. I do not have a super-duper blender so it was rather, um, thick (and bigger than last time because of the added fish bits and the roe) but it got drunk! Three big gulps of Australian red wine were consumed immediately afterwards. Optimal dinner complete!

### Inger — 2012-07-17T08:49:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mackerel-head-smoothie.2403/post-38668>

> **KiwiLauren;38841 wrote:** Optimal fish head smoothie accomplished. I cut whole fish at the middle (I know that includes the liver... just don't tell me what else I'm eating!), put 2 in the blender with ghee and 2 Tablespoons of wild caught, raw fish roe. Added juice of 3 lemons and salt and blended. I do not have a super-duper blender so it was rather, um, thick (and bigger than last time because of the added fish bits and the roe) but it got drunk! Three big gulps of Australian red wine were consumed immediately afterwards. Optimal dinner complete!

LOL...

perfect!

I did this a week ago, with two fishes that a friend had caught himself- somewhere . I just cut the whole fish with innards and shells and head all in pieces and mixed with water into a smoothie. It was sooooo nasty!! UH! It was totally slimy and strange.. smelled so not good.. it was not mackerel though, but a local freshwater-fish from waters that are half salty half sweet. And not the purest by any means.

After that nasty experience, drinking the mackerelhead-smoothies are just so easy, nice texture, like a creamy chocolate for sure!

I was nauseated the whole day after. I bet the fish was really polluted. I will never do that again.

But, I am still here, alive and well.. LOL. So guys and girls who are reading this, you can all do the mackerelhead-smoothie! No question.

It really is not that bad at all.

Beautiful beautiful mackerels. Because they are, take a closer look next time!

I bet any fish from deep cold clean waters will be just as good.

### Glamazon — 2012-07-17T15:54:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mackerel-head-smoothie.2403/post-38700>

My favorite part of sushi is the raw roe: Masago and tobiko. I like tobiko best which is harvested in Iceland or Canada, sent to Japan for processing and then sold in the US. Tobiko is from a tiny cold water fish. [http://www.sustainablesushi.net/the-fish/masago/](http://http://www.sustainablesushi.net/the-fish/masago/)

>
> Mom, dad, two brothers!!! And they don't even hold their breath, not one of them!!! They said, it was not bad at all, and now my dad wants one smoothie EVERY day!!!

They didn't hold their noses? They didn't gag between gulps? No drowning it in lemon juice? They didn't have to talk themselves into it? They didn't do it outside in case it came right back up?

Wow, I am a serious amateur at this. Hats off to you Norwegians!

### Rosekelly — 2012-07-18T21:16:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mackerel-head-smoothie.2403/post-39119>

I'm joining the club. This morning I pulverized three fresh whole sardines in my blender, added lemon juice, ghee, and water, and drank that sucker down. It wasn't bad. Just too much. It made over a cup. I wanted it to serve as an entire BAB, so I weighed out 7 oz of fish. I think I'll do less next time and just saute some shrimp to go along with it. The fish smoothie was just so darn fishy.

Now I know this wasn't a mackerel but I just discovered my local Asian grocery store yesterday and went wild over the seafood section. They have everything, and they have it frozen and fresh, and they have it marked wild or farmed and the country of origin. Having never cooked a whole fish, I wanted to start slow, so I bought a pound of whole sardines with the heads attached. Then I was reading this thread and thought, hmm, why not -- a sardine smoothie! So I did it.

The tip about having a sip of red wine after? Awesome. I swished it around and swallowed it, leaving my mouth refreshed and not fishy.

Then I drove over to the ocean and swam for a half hour. So far, I'm sure feeling great. I think if I keep the smoothie to half that size, I can do this every day. That was an awful lot of fish to glug down.

### nonchalant — 2012-07-18T21:44:58+0300

Rosekelly you did it! And Linz too!

There's a lot of us willing to go the distance!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-19T07:17:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mackerel-head-smoothie.2403/post-39298>

> **Cavemam;39405 wrote:** Has anybody tried with a food processor? I've got a superduper one of those, so not inclined to go get a blender to handle scales (there goes the gag reflex again!)

Well, I don't have a Vitamix (they start at $600 here) and with today's fish head smoothie my trusted blender broke. In the middle of attempting to blend the frozen fish and not getting too far. So I can now answer your question, Cavemam. YES, a food processor works fine. I put whole (minus the tail) fish plus extra roe with lemon juice and salt into it (only fish frozen) and it worked like a charm. Yum. (Scales are the least of your worries... I've learned gills and spines and fins are much trickier to puree.) Keep that red wine on hand... it really helps!

### Glamazon — 2012-07-22T03:27:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mackerel-head-smoothie.2403/post-40158>

### JoeBranca — 2012-07-27T02:13:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mackerel-head-smoothie.2403/post-41395>

I may attempt this with a Magic Bullet... which is just asking for trouble. stay tuned

likely scale down to whole sardines which are on sale at 99 . . .

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-27T02:41:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mackerel-head-smoothie.2403/post-41405>

> **Jennifer;40329 wrote:** What other fish heads do you all think would be optimal for smoothie. I mean if I'm gonna drink fish head I want it to be worth it nutrition wise. I haven't come across a mackerel yet but I've seen wild croaker, whiting and porgies.

I asked Dr K if there was any part of a raw fish that wasn't good for us, and he said the whole thing was good. So I add the entire thing, cut into chunks, except the tail. I figure that's got the liver and skin (and of course brains and eyes), and then some! If you've got roe, add that. And extra fish liver too. Soul had the idea of adding chlorella. I've tried that, along with ground seaweed too.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-27T03:10:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mackerel-head-smoothie.2403/post-41415>

I did herring today. Worked well. Go for smaller ones that blend up better. I added a spoon of roe today and that made it almost undrinkable  can you say tasted like the ocean! I rather swallow the roe whole me thinks. Or learn to like it. Chlorella works well for me for covering up the taste b/c I used to drink chlorella in green smoothies and green juices for years with lots of lemon. So now my body just thinks it's another one of those disgusting concoctions .... nothing to do with fish heads.

But the roe.... no more! I've also done sardines, mackerel, and smelt. All good  My mackerels have too big of a spine to blend up entirely.

### [email redacted] — 2012-07-27T05:01:02+0300

### Martin — 2012-07-28T23:02:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mackerel-head-smoothie.2403/post-41707>

It's got lots of muscle meat on it as well. Might make a fish dish tonight.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-29T01:26:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mackerel-head-smoothie.2403/post-41728>

> **Martin;41934 wrote:** Thanks for the "heads" up, Souldancer. I need to dissect this six-pound _head_ into manageable hunks. It may well be too big to do smoothies easily, but it's the only head our market had yesterday. Cheap fun!
>
>
>
> It's got lots of muscle meat on it as well. Might make a fish dish tonight.

Hi Martin. +1 on not putting too much in your blender. I don't have a Vitamix, but I broke my trusty old blender with my whole-fish smoothies... and the other day I put too big/too frozen fish into my food processor and they busted out (kinda like Soul's dream). They literally spun the thing off it's feet, ripped the top open and ended up all over the counter. So I'm for pre-chopping and sticking with smaller fish. Those big spines (and those big gills) just don't chop up and I'm guessing they'd even give your Vitamix a hard time. Welcome to the girls' club! Optimal has a steep learning curve.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-02T05:01:48+0300

### Inger — 2012-08-02T06:58:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mackerel-head-smoothie.2403/post-42458>

Patty Cakes, great! You did it!!! Your boyfriend is brave! My dad told me it was not bad at all too! I do hold my nose every time still though, I do not care to put anything in it to cut the taste as I do not taste anything anyways then..

I figure men has it easier with the smoothie? Maybe they have different taste-buds..hmm.

I have been drinking smoothie from anisakiasis-infected mackerels the last few days. Raw, without freezing first. I need to figure out if it is safe and I know I will not die from it anyways as the worms survive only a few weeks in human.

So far everything fine, no complications at all. Tummy happy, feeling great. I am curious to see if they will not affect me at all. That would be great, then I need to not worry when my mackerels are a bit wormy!

Maybe the head is worm-free.. I do try to clean the worms off as good as possible from the liver and roe I put into my smoothie too.

I have not noticed my blender smell, but I clean it real well with soap after every smoothie, maybe that is why? And I have a glass-blender.

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-30T16:37:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mackerel-head-smoothie.2403/post-48351>

To me, oysters are yummy raw just as they come. Also, when starting down the oyster path - the fancy restaurants often put a little topping on them to add a bit of flavor. I had one recently that had some kim-chi and other 'pickles', I thought it was great! Might want to top with finely chopped pickled veggies.

I still can't conceptualize downing a MHS. I think I'm ready to try a small frozen chunk that I can just swallow like medicine. Perhaps if I knew there was something in the "head" that was super good for us? I don't recall ever seeing anything that indicated a blended head would be better than, say, ceviche?

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-30T16:56:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mackerel-head-smoothie.2403/post-48356>

> **Cavemam;48822 wrote:** To me, oysters are yummy raw just as they come. Also, when starting down the oyster path - the fancy restaurants often put a little topping on them to add a bit of flavor. I had one recently that had some kim-chi and other 'pickles', I thought it was great! Might want to top with finely chopped pickled veggies.
>
>
>
> I still can't conceptualize downing a MHS. I think I'm ready to try a small frozen chunk that I can just swallow like medicine. Perhaps if I knew there was something in the "head" that was super good for us? I don't recall ever seeing anything that indicated a blended head would be better than, say, ceviche?

It's where the super-high nutrition is. Brain has DHA, eyes are concentrated sources of zinc, you get the thyroid gland which is filled with iodine and other good stuff, you get the skin (vit D) and the bones (calcium).... cut the fish a little further back and you get the liver. If indigenous people give all the fish heads to the kids they do it for a reason.... grow a brain. Well. I'd like to grow mine a bit. So I eat fish heads

Btw, I survived the squid heads  More today.

### Stavity — 2012-08-30T16:59:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mackerel-head-smoothie.2403/post-48358>

@Cavemam, I recently made my own fermented ketchup or is it katsup? lol. And now making my own cocktail sauce for the oysters. Chopped pickled veggies sounds yummy too. I do realize the problem with deadly nightshades for many of us, but found that while I have a huge problem with white potatoes, I have no reaction to peppers or tomatoes.

I also went from not liking much fish to eating ceviche and salmon tartare so I feel comfortable with that for now. The more I eat fish the more I like it. My doctor also told me not to eat fish heads and guts because of the health problems I face. No one could have told me I would be eating seafood for both my meals. It sure uncomplicates my food decisions. What a F'n relief!!!!

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-02T02:24:27+0300

Apart from protein, fat, calcium, phosphorus, iron and vitamin B1, it is believed that the fish head also contains abundant unsaturated fatty acid which is vital for brain development. Apart from that, the part beneath the fish gills is a transparent jelly which contains collagen, known for its anti-aging effect and ability to repair cell tissue. It is widely believed, therefore, that fish head as a soup ingredient is nutritious;

I have not gotten to my Katsup chapter in the ferment book, but it's on my "I WANT TO DO THIS" list!! How did it go?! The reality is after "meaning to" all summer, the closest I've gotten to actually fermenting is reading about 1/3 of a book and buying some pretty crocks

### Stavity — 2012-09-02T16:46:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mackerel-head-smoothie.2403/post-48933>

> **Cavemam;49323 wrote:** I have not gotten to my Katsup chapter in the ferment book, but it's on my "I WANT TO DO THIS" list!! How did it go?! The reality is after "meaning to" all summer, the closest I've gotten to actually fermenting is reading about 1/3 of a book and buying some pretty crocks
>
>
>
> I just saw on another thread, there is a test you can do to find out what you are actually sensitive to. . . I may have to actually do that one- seems practical. Everyone keeps trying to tell me I should be AI, but I'm stubborn.

I made this one

[http://www.ournourishingroots.com/real-food-101-how-to-make-lacto-fermented-ketchup/](http://http://www.ournourishingroots.com/real-food-101-how-to-make-lacto-fermented-ketchup/) Pretty easy except I had to make the whey first and that is fussy. I make it out of grass fed pastured yogurt I get at my local farmer's market. This takes a couple of days to ferment and is much shorter time then when I made fermented daikon. I only used salt and it's own juices for that and it took 3 weeks and needed to be watched. Was scared it would explode or get moldy.

If you find out about the test please let us know. I know immediately when my left ankle and right knee swell that potatoes are harming me, but it does not happen when I eat peppers, or tomatoes. Potatoes in any form were always my favorite food and the hardest food for me to give up, but the swelling had to go or I would be walking like a cripple.

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-09T20:45:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mackerel-head-smoothie.2403/post-50540>

MHS has been consumed. I did not get as much as I was planning b/c leeches showed up and noshed 1/2 of it.

[](http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/51095=447-ImageUploadedByTapatalk1347212407.762084.jpg)

 [](http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/51095=448-ImageUploadedByTapatalk1347212425.763360.jpg). Havoc preparing to share my MHS

 [](http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/51095=449-ImageUploadedByTapatalk1347212455.893680.jpg). Chaos joining Havoc (she didn't like it)

 [](http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/51095=450-ImageUploadedByTapatalk1347212499.544199.jpg)havoc asked for more when we finished

It wasn't that bad. Kinda like drinking the green pastures fermented CLO

I used 2 heads chopped and frozen over night, 2 lemons zested & juiced, 1/2 c water(didn't have a lot of juice), sea salt, & cilantro (hence why it's green)

So 15 mins after consuming I feel like everything is crisper. This might just become a weekly thing.

Mitch asks: @Jack so I re=read the H101 post. I read it to say high stress causes HIGH CORTISOL which, due to pregnenolone steal, causes decreased T3, sex hormones, sarcopenia and osteopenia.

But you said my cortisol was LOW..and you wanted it higher

So low cortisol could be a symptom also of pregnenelone steal – right? And increasing T3 and vit D is the key to making more pregnenelone and other hormones as well – right?

Jack Says:

September 9th, 2012 at 4:37 pm

@Mitch when the effect is CHRONIC the decision in the cell is always on…..meaning all the pregnenolone shunts to cortisol to help you survive. When we measure it in the plasma it is low, because it is being used to keep you in survival mode (cortisol path)…..that is a sign the PVN nucelus is working over time and this is sign your oxidizing your cells. The result is all the hormones going the other way in the hormone synthesis chain are very low……..that is the reduction path. Re Read BG 11. Low cortisol= low melatonin = epithelial cancers= LR. Oxidation =LR. Low cortisol is not a good thing. When the process first begins……ACUTELY you will have hypercortisolism for a time until you fatigue your PVN nucleus in the hypothalamus. Do it long enough and you oxidize (age) your body, your sleep declines and your body fails with your body composition.

The critical equation of life = LDL + T3 = pregnenolone. If free T3 is low you cant make pregnenolone. If you live a life in the modern world you are in survival mode shunting the low amount of pregnenolone your cells can make to cortisol at the sake of progesterone. Progesterone is the base hormone for the testosterone, estrogen, DHEA, etc……..The more we shunt to survival mode the more oxidized the cell becomes…….the more oxidized the cell becomes the more Vitamin D has to be used to offset the oxidation by the immune system so it also falls. I hope this clarifies it. This is why modern humans are infertile (1 in 7 couples) because to have a child you must be more chemically reduced than oxidized because it favors a higher level of the pro- GESTATION by the hormone called progesterone. Most modern humans are starved for progesterone. Hence why we see upside down PG/E2 ratios in all modern neolithic diseases. When this happens the lab panels generally show increases in HS CRP, increased E2, and LR, with LOW free T3 levels.

### JackStapleton — 2012-10-31T00:15:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mackerel-head-smoothie.2403/post-61005>

I googled "fish head smoothie" and this thread is the only link that came up. Cutting edge stuff here I guess.

I tried using the head of a 2 pound "red fish" (that's what the fishmonger called it - a type of snapper I think). It was pretty bony and I don't have a super-strength blender. Ran it for about 5 minutes in homemade chicken broth and then strained it through a sieve. There was still a lot of bones/scales/whatever in the sieve. It looked like a cup of very rough beach sand. I threw that stuff out. I don't eat stuff that looks and feels like broken-shell sand.

But the broth was fantastic! Super creamy and like a delicious bisque. I won't be throwing away any more fish heads.

### LinD — 2012-11-09T19:32:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mackerel-head-smoothie.2403/post-63163>

Someone (Leanne Ely) must be reading here, too!:

[https://savingdinner.com/articles/holy-mackerel/](http://https://savingdinner.com/articles/holy-mackerel/)

We know that we should be adding fish to our diets for optimal health, but which fish should you be reaching for?

It’s hard to go wrong as long as it’s wild fish caught in a sustainable, responsible manner, but one of the healthiest fishes you can buy is mackerel, a fatty deep water fish with a very firm flesh and a strong flavor.

Mackerel is easy to cook, it tastes great and it’s very good for you.

Let’s look at some of the health benefits of this delicious fish:

Omega-3. Mackerel is famous for being a very oily fish. It’s very high in those omega-3 fatty acids that we all should be eating more of.

Heart health. Mackerel can actually improve the condition of the heart. It lowers blood pressure and can prevent stroke.

Low in calories. A 3-ounce serving of this firm fish contains roughly 230 calories (providing it’s been cooked without added butter or sauces), which makes a fillet lower in calories than other meats.

High in protein. There are 21 grams of protein in that 3-ounce serving of mackerel and like other fishes, it contains no carbs, making it a wonderful item to add to your dinner plate.

Minerals. Mackerel contains calcium, potassium, phosphorous, magnesium and selenium. It’s a good source of folate, choline, Niacin, Vitamins A, D, K, B12 and C.

Mackerel lowers cancer-causing agents in the body, improves our immune systems and is great for brain health. Mackerel can help ease symptoms of arthritis, arthrosis and even migraine pain. Holy mackerel!

Now, one thing to be aware of when purchasing mackerel is that it is a deep water fish and depending on where it came from it may contain mercury. Pregnant women should not eat mackerel or any food that may contain mercury because it may harm the developing baby’s nervous system.

To cook mackerel, simply pan fry it at medium heat until it flakes with a fork!

Have you ever tried mackerel?

[http://www.facebook.com/savingdinner](http://http://www.facebook.com/savingdinner)

# Thread: Hormone test timing
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hormone-test-timing.2405/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### MartiD — 2012-07-13T17:16:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hormone-test-timing.2405/post-37481>

I have seen alot of people mention when they do their hormone labs in relationship to their cycle. I am hopefully getting a full thyroid and hormone panel done in next month but I had a partial hysterectomy 3 years ago so I assume it doesn't matter what part of the month I do the testing? Does having an hysterectomy effect hormone levels differently from others? I do still have ovaries and am 42 with no pre-meni signs.

Also, should I stop all supps a week before the test so it doesn't mess up results? My main concerns on supps vit D, Iron, and 7 Keto. Thanks for any help!

### JanSz — 2012-07-13T19:16:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hormone-test-timing.2405/post-37512>

> **MartiD;37648 wrote: I have seen alot of people mention when they do their hormone labs in relationship to their cycle.** I am hopefully getting a full thyroid and hormone panel done in next month but I had a partial hysterectomy 3 years ago so I assume it doesn't matter what part of the month I do the testing? Does having an hysterectomy effect hormone levels differently from others? I do still have ovaries and am 42 with no pre-meni signs.
>
>
>
> Also, should I stop all supps a week before the test so it doesn't mess up results? My main concerns on supps vit D, Iron, and 7 Keto. Thanks for any help!

This timing is mostly about testing hormones that change daily during menstrual cycle.

LH

FSH

Progesterone

Estradiol

Since it is not practical to draw blood daily for the whole cycle, days when min and max are expected are chosen for testing.

I prefer venous blood testing at LabCorp(I am not a doctor).

Genova Diagnostics offers saliva test, it takes 11 samples over cycle.

..........

When attempting Steroid Hormone Optimization, I suggest to start project optimizing day 2-4 of menstrual cycle.

.

### MartiD — 2012-07-13T23:35:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hormone-test-timing.2405/post-37609>

So since I don't have a cycle it won't matter what day I draw?

### JanSz — 2012-07-13T23:56:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hormone-test-timing.2405/post-37611>

> **MartiD;37776 wrote:** So since I don't have a cycle it won't matter what day I draw?

yes

....

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-14T03:07:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hormone-test-timing.2405/post-37649>

if you still have your ovaries you do have a cycle.... just no period I would say.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-14T03:14:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hormone-test-timing.2405/post-37652>

### MartiD — 2012-07-14T15:39:52+0300

**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 18
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-07-13T20:49:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-eat-the-whole-shrimp.2410/post-37556>

So I really want to figure out ways to prepare and eat the whole shrimp - shell and all. Are you trying anything like that? Recipes or techniques?

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-13T21:18:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-eat-the-whole-shrimp.2410/post-37563>

you can simmer the shells to make broth and then puree and eat. good for chondroitin and glucosamine Dr K style.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-13T21:42:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-eat-the-whole-shrimp.2410/post-37573>

I just bought some shrimp and had them cleaned. I can't believe I forgot to ask them to also pkg up the shells for a broth w/ ground up exoskeleton! Was really looking forward to trying that. Seriously......

### BJK77 — 2012-07-14T02:05:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-eat-the-whole-shrimp.2410/post-37631>

Has anyone made broth from the shells like Dr K suggested? I cooked shells from a pound of shrimp in my crockpot for 24 hours and then blended everything up in my Vitamix, but the shells were still quite evident! The broth was tasty, but gritty. Blech!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-14T03:45:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-eat-the-whole-shrimp.2410/post-37663>

Just cook and eat them whole. It's pretty easy. We throw the entire shrimp into a pan with CO and garlic/onions and cook with spices (often of the curry sort) and then squirt some lemon juice in and, voila, eat. I just have to shut my eyes when I eat the heads because they still have those beady little eyes looking at me (The only bit I have trouble with is the tails - the shells are pretty easy to eat and usually well flavoured... and that's with big shrimp/prawns that are 4-5 inches long).

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-14T04:30:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-eat-the-whole-shrimp.2410/post-37677>

Holy shrimp, Kiwi. Maybe that will be my next challenge on the road to optimal.

### [email redacted] — 2012-07-14T04:37:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-eat-the-whole-shrimp.2410/post-37680>

Yea, that was my experience as well - with no industrial grinder getting them powdery seemed impossible.

### kathylu — 2012-07-14T05:40:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-eat-the-whole-shrimp.2410/post-37692>

Some chinese restaurants (authentic, not americanized) make a dish called "salty shrimp". Whole, in the shell, just pick them up and eat! Delicious...maybe a good way to try shrimp in the shell for the first time.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-14T05:44:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-eat-the-whole-shrimp.2410/post-37695>

I want to try the salty shrimp, the AI way of course. I guess the trick is in frying the shrimp with the shell on with lots of salt and get the whole thing crunchy. It looks yummy for sure.

### kathylu — 2012-07-14T05:48:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-eat-the-whole-shrimp.2410/post-37696>

> **kathylu;37859 wrote:** Some chinese restaurants (authentic, not americanized) make a dish called "salty shrimp". Whole, in the shell, just pick them up and eat! Delicious...maybe a good way to try shrimp in the shell for the first time.

[http://www.myrecipes.com/recipe/crunchy-salt-shrimp-with-ginger-sauce-10000000600528/](http://http://www.myrecipes.com/recipe/crunchy-salt-shrimp-with-ginger-sauce-10000000600528/)

### JanSz — 2012-07-17T22:05:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-eat-the-whole-shrimp.2410/post-38822>

In Brazil I eat whole large shrimp fried on a stick.

At first I did not recognized the animal, usually I see just shrimp tails.

Well, that was in 1980, I have not heard of eating whole shirimps then.

Latter, back in USA, I was going for lunches with Philipinos,

some of them eat little shrimps, cooked, just tails complete with shells.

On my last trip to Korean store I got tiny whole shrimps and anchovies, they are wild caught a complete, dried.

First batch, I soaked them up with 3 parts of water per part of shrimps & anchovies, hoping that they would re-hydrate.

Then added half a stick of butter, garlic, hot pepper, lemon and salt.

Heated couple minutes, just to kill smell of garlic.

It was ok to eat it, but not really, they were not really re-hydrated, overly crunchy.

Now I weight 1.5oz shrimp + 1.5oz anchovies + (1.5 + 1.5)\*3=9oz water put in Vitamix blender (Total 12oz)

sea salt, hot pepper, 5-6 garlic cloves

mix, liquefy

empty into narrow tall pot, covered, heat until boil (exhaust fan overhead, lots of pungent smell)

add 4 tbsp Palm Oil, mix

eat

Tastes real good (or possibly I am just used to it).

To make it more eye appealing, garnish with some green, onion or parsley.

\--------------

# Thread: Can you take a full spectrum enzyme supp hours after eating?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-you-take-a-full-spectrum-enzyme-supp-hours-after-eating.2414/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### janagram — 2012-07-13T22:15:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-you-take-a-full-spectrum-enzyme-supp-hours-after-eating.2414/post-37584>

my question is just that: can u take a full spectrum enzyme hrs after eating? sometimes at night I feel like I could use some digestive help....(mine has oxbile, etc.)

### vkiernan — 2012-07-14T00:49:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-you-take-a-full-spectrum-enzyme-supp-hours-after-eating.2414/post-37619>

> **janagram;37751 wrote:** my question is just that: can u take a full spectrum enzyme hrs after eating? sometimes at night I feel like I could use some digestive help....(mine has oxbile, etc.)

My last doc told me to take them before BAB, mid-morn, lunch, mid-afternoon, supper, before bed. Hows that! She told me you need them in between, not just when eating.

### SeaHorse — 2012-07-14T04:13:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-you-take-a-full-spectrum-enzyme-supp-hours-after-eating.2414/post-37671>

My understanding is that they have a strong anti-inflammatory effect when they are not breaking down food, so in-between meals is great just like vkiernan said.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-14T04:17:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-you-take-a-full-spectrum-enzyme-supp-hours-after-eating.2414/post-37672>

I've heard the same thing on the inbetween doses. My BF sometimes takes them before bed to help with things other than digestion. Not sure about the ox bile though. If not an amount like 100 mg I would think it would be OK and maybe still OK...just not sure.

### janagram — 2012-07-15T04:15:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-you-take-a-full-spectrum-enzyme-supp-hours-after-eating.2414/post-37936>

thanks...good to know!

# Thread: Cancer
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cancer.2417/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 63
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-14T02:40:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cancer.2417/post-37639>

Hello all. Rosekelly and I are looking to establish a thread for those dealing with cancer/cancer history via Leptin Rx, CT, circadian cycles, epi-paleo diet, BHRT, etc. It can be for you, someone in your family and/or if you have expertise/interest. Just wanted to create a separate thread to track things.

Chime in if you've got experience and/or are interested.

I am about to embark on BHRT, 2 years post a breast cancer diagnosis, as well as following Dr K's protocols and specific recommendations to me. We'd love to share experiences, how to deal with docs, testing, etc. As Rosekelly said, we're pioneers!

Looking forward to connecting with anyone who is interested.

Lauren

### Rosekelly — 2012-07-14T07:18:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cancer.2417/post-37705>

Hello everyone,

I too am about to embark on BHRT. I'm 6 weeks post-hysterectomy for endometrial (uterine) cancer. I also had breast cancer 8 years ago. That time, I did chemo and radiation. This time, I declined the offer. I'm off the grid, conventional-medicine-wise. I'm taking charge of my health.

Rose

### kathylu — 2012-07-14T16:24:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cancer.2417/post-37741>

Can I chime in with the thought that I am simply afraid of getting cancer? My mother had uterine cancer, an aunt with breast cancer (though she passed from other causes), my sister had melanoma, so I feel like I'm just history waiting to repeat itself. My best girlfriend is fighting breast cancer now, and several people in my husband's family have had cancer too. Two brothers with melanoma and his sister just had a complete hysterectomy due to uterine cancer. It's funny, I asked his sister if her doctors had suggested HRT (bioidentical or otherwise) and she said no one mentioned it and she didn't know enough to ask. I directed to her to Suzanne Sommers' books and asked her to at least consider the topic. I don't think she will though. She is deeply invested in the "hormones cause cancer" paradigm.

Not me though. I started BHRT three months ago and boy do I ever feel better!

### Rosekelly — 2012-07-14T16:44:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cancer.2417/post-37748>

Thank you, Kathylu! I too have a lot of cancer in my family. I'm really glad to hear you've started BHRT and feel so good. That's what I like to hear!

### [email redacted] — 2012-07-14T23:36:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cancer.2417/post-37844>

Hello everyone, People under estimate the difficulty of NOT doing what the professionals recommend in these circumstances. Thanks for starting this thread - please keep posting! I commend you for forging ahead with an "alternate" route. Hopefully we can all learn from this!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-15T01:10:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cancer.2417/post-37875>

Suzanne Sommers was the first women to publicaly put this out there for women. Her reputation was trashed and they called her a Kook. Last time I checked she looks unreal at close to 70 and all her critics have died off. That is paradigm changing.

### caroline — 2012-07-15T11:46:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cancer.2417/post-38007>

> **Cavemam;38012 wrote:** Hello everyone, People under estimate the difficulty of NOT doing what the professionals recommend in these circumstances. Thanks for starting this thread - please keep posting! I commend you for forging ahead with an "alternate" route. Hopefully we can all learn from this!

I so agree - it is very difficult to go against what the professionals are recommending. I was in that situation with my husband and it was impossibly hard. I too am looking forward to learning from this

### chocolate — 2012-07-16T00:21:19+0300

New MIT study offers comprehensive look at chemical and genetic changes that occur as inflammation progresses to cancer.**

### colleencoble — 2012-07-16T04:15:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cancer.2417/post-38258>

Great thread! I'm still reeling from the news that my dearest friend in the world is facing her 3rd bout with ovarian cancer. Anything I can do to help her, I would do in a heartbeat.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-16T04:38:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cancer.2417/post-38263>

> **colleencoble;38428 wrote:** Great thread! I'm still reeling from the news that my dearest friend in the world is facing her 3rd bout with ovarian cancer. Anything I can do to help her, I would do in a heartbeat.

Colleen, Suzanne Somer's book Knockout may be really helpful to her. It is mostly interviews with the cutting edge doctors re: treating and preventing cancer (with a bit of her story/words). There is amazing info in there. It's all here with Dr K, too, but the book may be an easier read for someone not ready to jump into the ice bath and eat mackerel head smoothies. It gives a wealth of info about supplements, why chemo doesn't work (and how to find out if it is even possible to work) etc. You are such a treasure with all your knowledge, I'm guessing you already know most of what's in the book, but in case she'd like to read something. (Warning, if you've undergone conventional treatment, it can make you pretty angry!)

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-16T04:41:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cancer.2417/post-38264>

> **Cavemam;38012 wrote:** Hello everyone, People under estimate the difficulty of NOT doing what the professionals recommend in these circumstances. Thanks for starting this thread - please keep posting! I commend you for forging ahead with an "alternate" route. Hopefully we can all learn from this!

Thanks for saying this Cavemam. Not doing what the professionals say is just dandy when the stakes are low. But when you're alone in your doc's office and they are talking about what you 'must' do to deal with cancer, it's bloody difficult even when you know it's wrong advice. That is why this forum is a lifeline. Because I can learn and learn and re-read and read some more and start to really understand these issues. Combine that with educational consults and I feel I have the information I need to get brave and not follow the rubbish advice out there. But it's still scary. I feel like Mork (remember Mork and Mindy) trying to summon up Dr K while dealing with doctors... Mork calling Orson, come in Orson. Maybe it's a good thing I have yet to hear his voice

### [email redacted] — 2012-07-16T04:58:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cancer.2417/post-38266>

> **KiwiLauren;38434 wrote:** Thanks for saying this Cavemam. Not doing what the professionals say is just dandy when the stakes are low. But when you're alone in your doc's office and they are talking about what you 'must' do to deal with cancer, it's bloody difficult even when you know it's wrong advice. That is why this forum is a lifeline. Because I can learn and learn and re-read and read some more and start to really understand these issues. Combine that with educational consults and I feel I have the information I need to get brave and not follow the rubbish advice out there. But it's still scary. I feel like Mork (remember Mork and Mindy) trying to summon up Dr K while dealing with doctors... Mork calling Orson, come in Orson. Maybe it's a good thing I have yet to hear his voice

Know that simply the physiological reaction prevents you from even thinking clearly. In this circumstance, assuming it's a stressor for you (and who wouldn't it be one for?!) your heart rate goes up, you have a hormonal cascade that ruins logical thinking.

NOBODY can think while sitting there under the gun. hehe MORK CALLING ORSON. Amen on that.

I freeze up like a mute and just sound stupid. Inside my head I'm cursing and screaming, "but don't you know how much smarter I am than you, why can't you cooperate."

### colleencoble — 2012-07-16T05:09:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cancer.2417/post-38268>

That's a great idea, Lauren, I'll get it for her. Thanks!

### Destiny — 2012-10-05T01:25:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cancer.2417/post-56354>

I just got a breast cancer diagnosis today. It is in the early stages, no lump and would not be found without a mammogram.

Now, I have to interview breast surgeons. Any suggestions would be very much appreciated. I am a bit lost.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-05T01:52:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cancer.2417/post-56359>

Im sorry to hear this.........rely on your instincts.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-05T01:53:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cancer.2417/post-56360>

[http://www.naturalnews.com/037421\_breast\_cancer\_pink\_ribbons\_sponsors.html](http://http://www.naturalnews.com/037421_breast_cancer_pink_ribbons_sponsors.html)

### KiwiLauren — 2012-10-05T02:15:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cancer.2417/post-56365>

> **Destiny;57612 wrote:** I just got a breast cancer diagnosis today. It is in the early stages, no lump and would not be found without a mammogram.
>
>
>
> Now, I have to interview breast surgeons. Any suggestions would be very much appreciated. I am a bit lost.

Destiny, I'm not sure my experience is anything to model on, but I'd be interested in surgeons who really understand your diagnosis. In other words, not ones who are just reactive. If it's early stages, I'm guessing that almost every surgeon is going to recommend lumpectomy with sentinel node biopsy. This means they will look to remove the cancer, have clear margins around it (the entire area will be fully biopsied post-surgery) and have clear lymph nodes. I will PM you the specifics of my procedures.

It is really important to not panic and to have good support around you. And to let yourself fall apart as you need to (I promise you, you'll be able to reassemble yourself.) There is a lot of grief work to be done with this diagnosis and fear is something you will confront at every turn. The biggest thing I had to deal with re: fear was the fear that came from so many around me. That is another thing to look for in a doctor... one who operates from a place of understanding and confidence, not fear.

I am so sorry you're going through this. Everyone's experience is different, but I know how painful this experience was for me. There were a million 'gifts' that came in the aftermath of this for me, too. But that was down the track. Right now for you, it will be very painful and scary. Hang in there.

### SeaHorse — 2012-10-05T02:19:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cancer.2417/post-56366>

Destiny, you certainly did the right thing to have the second mammogram,...sending support your way.

### [email redacted] — 2012-10-05T02:26:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cancer.2417/post-56368>

> **Destiny;57612 wrote:** I just got a breast cancer diagnosis today. It is in the early stages, no lump and would not be found without a mammogram.
>
>
>
> Now, I have to interview breast surgeons. Any suggestions would be very much appreciated. I am a bit lost.

I wish I had something to say, which I don't. I'm so sorry. I offer my ear, please always feel free to bounce ideas and questions or just vent. I hope having all the information here and support of a few like minded ladies help.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-05T02:52:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cancer.2417/post-56376>

Destiny, I'm sorry to hear this. I know you will do what you have to do and it will be the right thing for you. I don't have any personal experience with this so nothing really to add to help. Just that my thoughts are with you and I wish you the best in whatever you decide to do.

### PaleoCarol — 2012-10-05T02:54:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cancer.2417/post-56377>

Destiny, I'm so sorry to hear this news, but if you put all the information together I am sure you will make the right decision. I am very pleased with my gyn-oncology surgeon (for endometrial cancer) at Columbia Presbyterian and I would be happy to call and ask him for a recommendation. Everyone there was very helpful to me and they couldn't be nicer. You could start by asking them.

### kathylu — 2012-10-05T03:10:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cancer.2417/post-56383>

Destiny, if there is anything we can do please tell us. Please ask for support. It's very hard to tell when someone needs help when you are sitting with them, so it is much harder when communicating over a forum. Just be out there with your thoughts, ask questions and use the best ideas from the wealth of experience here. Remember that diet is critical, you can change your epigenetics that way. Please get Suzanne Somers' book "Knockout". It will give you lots of ideas, resources and a lead on which questions are important to ask first.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-05T03:10:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cancer.2417/post-56384>

> **KiwiLauren;57623 wrote:** Destiny, I'm not sure my experience is anything to model on, but I'd be interested in surgeons who really understand your diagnosis. In other words, not ones who are just reactive. If it's early stages, I'm guessing that almost every surgeon is going to recommend lumpectomy with sentinel node biopsy. This means they will look to remove the cancer, have clear margins around it (the entire area will be fully biopsied post-surgery) and have clear lymph nodes. I will PM you the specifics of my procedures.
>
>
>
> It is really important to not panic and to have good support around you. And to let yourself fall apart as you need to (I promise you, you'll be able to reassemble yourself.) There is a lot of grief work to be done with this diagnosis and fear is something you will confront at every turn. The biggest thing I had to deal with re: fear was the fear that came from so many around me. That is another thing to look for in a doctor... one who operates from a place of understanding and confidence, not fear.
>
>
>
> I am so sorry you're going through this. Everyone's experience is different, but I know how painful this experience was for me. There were a million 'gifts' that came in the aftermath of this for me, too. But that was down the track. Right now for you, it will be very painful and scary. Hang in there.

sentinel node biopsies are now not in vogue...........in 2012

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-05T03:11:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cancer.2417/post-56386>

> **kathylu;57641 wrote:** Destiny, if there is anything we can do please tell us. Please ask for support. It's very hard to tell when someone needs help when you are sitting with them, so it is much harder when communicating over a forum. Just be out there with your thoughts, ask questions and use the best ideas from the wealth of experience here. Remember that diet is critical, you can change your epigenetics that way. Please get Suzanne Somers' book "Knockout". It will give you lots of ideas, resources and a lead on which questions are important to ask first.

excellent advice here!!!!!!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-10-05T03:40:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cancer.2417/post-56392>

> **kathylu;57641 wrote:** Destiny, if there is anything we can do please tell us. Please ask for support. It's very hard to tell when someone needs help when you are sitting with them, so it is much harder when communicating over a forum. Just be out there with your thoughts, ask questions and use the best ideas from the wealth of experience here. Remember that diet is critical, you can change your epigenetics that way. Please get Suzanne Somers' book "Knockout". It will give you lots of ideas, resources and a lead on which questions are important to ask first.

Knockout is fabulous. The only one I didn't read from the library but bought a copy of. Just realise that it can also overwhelm you in another way... by giving loads of info that your doctors won't typically be aware of. It can be really tricky to learn so much (like we have on this forum) and then be at the mercy of surgeons who don't know and don't want to know. I'm not at all trying to telling you not to read the book, quite the opposite. I just know that in the immediate aftermath of a cancer diagnosis you need support and it can feel quite adversarial when the doctors won't come on board. (I found even asking the questions was enough to have doctors refuse to care for me. That's tolerable but can be really scary when you believe you need surgery.) Don't give up, though. Just know that balancing all this and all your feelings can be quite a challenge.

### kathylu — 2012-10-05T03:53:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cancer.2417/post-56397>

I've been reading a lot recently that indicates that surgery needs to be considered very carefully...it seems tissue injury can cause more of a problem than it solves at times. Each case is so individual though. One thing that certainly can't hurt is ketosis and lots of seafood, green veggies, iodine and digestive enzymes (google Nicolas Gonzales, MD).

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-05T03:54:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cancer.2417/post-56398>

They are evolving big time........sentinel nodes dissection is no longer standard of care.

Enough of the science.......Destiny does not want to hear science now because her emotions are controlling her thoughts in her brain.

Even before there were homo sapiens, feelings like compassion and love and sympathy had earned their way into the gene pool. I am thinking of you........and hoping to god you re read BG 11 a lot and start putting together a lot of what is in this series......because I am trying to keep people out of this predicament.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-05T04:12:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cancer.2417/post-56401>

Living in your Epi-genome is the history of our species. Mother Nature has provided us a spectacular biologic toolbox. The toolbox exists in our DNA but is expressed in a much more sophisticated patttern. The genome is the first draft of what we might be, but our life experience is the final draft and is made by the choices we made in our the time we were given. An architect far better and smarter than us has given us that genetic toolbox, and we now have the ability to use it by altering our behaviors to change our lives. That is the story being written on modern epigenetics. None of them are aware of what they dont know........and that to me is sadder than \*\*\*\*.

I would eat high heart too. High meat is a old cancer remedy in certain cultures like Siberian. Just get some lambsheart, cut the fat away and cut it in mouth-pieces and put in a glass jar, air it for a minute every day. Soon you will have great medicine.

I would be out in the sun as much as possible. Naked if possible. Tan your breast. They need it! Stop using ANY chemicals like lotion.. deo.. shampoo.. I use only Ghassul-"mud" it works well. Or use any other natural stuff, eggyolk works well too. That is how my hair looks after washing it with Ghassul for years. I have very thin hair. ### AdrianaDr. Radler-Pohl — 2012-10-05T09:42:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cancer.2417/post-56428>

@ destiny - having had a bc diagnosis more than 5 years ago I think that you have the best odds on your side. You have access to so many important Infos here. when I was in your situation it was very stressful to find "other wisdom". I had to read so much - thx God I had a scientific background .... So I could evaluate. At that time in Germany everything was (and is) cw. Since I used to work at the German Cancer Research Center also a lot of my colleagues are on the "other" side. Really though.... . At that time I was not strong enough to refuse the conventional approach - I had surgery twice, chemotherapy and radiation. But I started to change my diet ... Lots of supp ... VitD was very low. ... Started to read about the connection of low VitD and cancer. .. Realized that I was metabolicly in really bad shape .... High FBG ... Fatty liver ... Etc .... At that time I started with B17 supplementation which I'm sill doing but at a much lower dose. several month ago I stumbled upon this site .... So now it's hormon tweaking now and Jack showed me some pretty amazing things in my consult with him. My thyroid is not operating well and my hormones are off .... So that means for all of us reading SS books....So you get the Infos and the mental support here and start to change yor Heath ....So big Huggs from Germany!!!!!

Adriana

### kathylu — 2012-10-05T15:07:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cancer.2417/post-56454>

Destiny, B17 is laetrile (or amygdalin), a naturally occuring cyanogenic glycoside. It is currently unavailable in the US. Some alternative medical practitioners feel it has anti-cancer activity. I wouldn't take it without the guidance of someone knowledgable. You can get some in a natural package by eating apricot seeds (also called kernels). For instance, a health food store near me sells apricot kernels by the pound. They need to be refrigerated and fresh. Oxidized (rancid) ones are poisonous. This is just for info...I'm not recommending this.

### CarolMorris — 2012-10-05T19:42:20+0300

Destiny,

I just read this thread. I'm so sorry you have to deal with this. I say thank God you are here. When my husband got prostate cancer 5 1/2 years ago at age 57, I would have been in heaven to have all of you on this site to talk with and Dr. Jack to consult with. His no bs, no politically correct attitude, brilliance and fearless march for answers would have been sooo welcome.

You be really good to yourself, listen and learn, cry and especially laugh alot, even when you don't feel like it. Most importantly, go with your gut(intuition). Don't be afraid to ask for help...by asking someone else to help, you give them a very meaningful gift.

If I can do anything to make your journey a tadbit easier it would be my pleasure.

Big hugs and positive energy...Carol

### Destiny — 2012-10-05T23:28:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cancer.2417/post-56527>

Thank you, guys! I wish I could put it into words how much I appreciate you. If you hear any good tidbit about breast cancer, please let me know.

I was pretty healthy for 50 yrs and 18 months later this! A part of me is angry about this, I always ate much better than many people around me. Never smoked, never was a drinker or a drug user and was no couch potato. I guess I just want to reason why I have cancer at 51..

Anyway, my daughter and 5 grandsons are coming to visit us from Canada this weekend. They don't know anything which is good. The boys (1-8 yrs old) will make me laugh and happy no matter what. There will be no time to dwell on anything.

Happy Thanksgiving to all fellow Canadians!

### [email redacted] — 2012-10-06T00:11:20+0300
