>
>
>
> Happy Thanksgiving to all fellow Canadians!

I was at a company retreat with a motivational speaker today, and I thought of you.

We were supposed to share something bad that happened to us that we got over, then looked back on and it. . . and on and on. The lady sitting next to me turned to me and said "I had breast cancer, but really it wasn't that big of a deal. I powered through it and haven't looked back. That was 10 yrs ago".

Anywhooo - thinking of you today. Hope we can help you power through and make it such a thing that 10 yrs from now you can turn to a relative stranger and say with a perky voice "I have breast cancer once, and just powered through it".

### Rosekelly — 2012-10-07T03:26:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cancer.2417/post-56659>

Hi Destiny,

Sorry to hear this! I had breast cancer at age 49 and endometrial cancer at 57. It is shocking to hear, yes, but cancers are not all the same, and yours could be quite manageable.

The further question, and one that my functional medicine doc asked at my first visit (and one Dr. Kruse encourages, I would say) is, what are my individual risk factors that made me a match for cancer? Also, how likely is to happen again, based on my unique genome and how I take care of myself?

I discovered through testing that I'm missing a gene for glutathione, a major antioxidant, and also have defects effecting estrogen metabolism. We're all different, and some of us are more prone to cancer than others. But a cancer can be surgically removed and never come back, and that can be the end of the story.

Wishing you awesome good fortune in dealing with this.

Rosekelly

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-14T03:06:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mushrooms-candida.2418/post-37648>

I love mushrooms but since they are a fungus figured they shouldn't be eaten when trying to get rid of candida. Sure would like to add them into my food supply. Does anyone know if eating mushrooms exacerbates a candida problem?

### chocolate — 2012-07-14T04:07:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mushrooms-candida.2418/post-37668>

[http://www.thecandidadiet.com/foodstoavoid.htm](http://http://www.thecandidadiet.com/foodstoavoid.htm)

> MUSHROOMS / MOLDS
>
>
>
>
>
> Mushrooms
>
>
>
> Truffles
>
>
>
>
>
> Eating some fungi can cause an inflammatory reaction if you are already suffering from Candida.
>
> Mushrooms also often have mold on their exterior.
>
> **On the other hand, some medicinal mushrooms are actually OK on the diet and have strong immune-boosting properties. Good examples are Reishi and Maitake**

I like mushrooms, too.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-14T04:10:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mushrooms-candida.2418/post-37669>

I read all the other stuff you posted on the Candida thread. It seems mushrooms are either good or bad and I don't seem to get a clear picture of them. Now if there is mold on them that is a whole other piece that I don't want to ingest!

Unfortunately I don't think medicinal mushrooms are as tasty (if tasty at all) when making something like cream of mushroom soup!

# Thread: Diet and Thyroid
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diet-and-thyroid.2421/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 18
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-07-14T11:16:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diet-and-thyroid.2421/post-37711>

How many carbs you eat and what effect that has on your thyroid seems to have been an issue that's been simmering along in Paleo circles for a while. For example, there's a Chris Kresser episode on Ask the Low Carb Experts:

[http://livinlavidalowcarb.com/blog/ask-the-low-carb-experts-episode-10-all-things-thyroid-thyroid-101-chris-kresser/13707](http://http://livinlavidalowcarb.com/blog/ask-the-low-carb-experts-episode-10-all-things-thyroid-thyroid-101-chris-kresser/13707)

I may be misunderstanding him, but he seems to be saying that eating low carb is a bad idea because of its effect on your thyroid.

(I seem, actually, to be hearing that in a lot of places. Sometimes it seems to be because the speaker is "against" low carb. Actually, Chris seems quite dismissive of low carb diets. In that interview he says something like: you eat carbs; you release insulin; there's no problem. Maybe he was asked on a low-carb show as a devil's advocate?)

But recently Petro Dobromylskyj of Hyperlipid has published something on the matter

Peter, being a vet, perhaps has a different perspective on these things. It'll often be animals he's looking at:

> For these (usually cats) we check the free T4 level.
>
>
> I assume no-one is arguing that cats need to be fed high-carb diets!
>
>
>
> Peter seems to be saying it can revolve around whether the brain "thinks there's enough T3":
>
>
>
> Summary: Despite the limited fall in T3 on higher carb diets, the brain is not happy with thyroid status. TSH goes up. Gimme gimme gimme, more more more.

[http://high-fat-nutrition.blogspot.co.uk/2012/07/measuring-thyroid-hormone-level-is-very.html](http://http://high-fat-nutrition.blogspot.co.uk/2012/07/measuring-thyroid-hormone-level-is-very.html)

Not being a medical doctor or veterinarian it comes across to me as a frightfully complex matter. I get the uneasy feeling that while I haven't much understanding of these matters, some people who are pronouncing quite confidently on them may be mistaken. (After all, there are different views around, and not everyone can be correct.) I don't know how much research there's been into all this, and whether people on different diets have been measured and monitored over protracted periods. Too late now to do it with the Inuit on their traditional diet, I guess. That would have been interesting.

Any thoughts?

### Michael — 2012-07-14T16:45:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diet-and-thyroid.2421/post-37750>

Not up to date, am I?

I should have read Brain Gut 4.

> The ‘bad mojo’ that low carb diets get in paleo is disturbing, and is perpetrated by those trying to control the “meme” of what paleo is to them and not to science.
>
>
> What's going on with the thyroid, according to Jack, also depends on:
>
>
>
>
> > Selenium, Iodine, Iron, Magnesium, Zinc, and DHA.
>
>
>
> This I had already cottoned on to:
>
>
>
> ... we also know eating carbs all day is a great way to destroy a brain with Alzheimer’s and neurologic diseases we see today commonly.

Oh, well. I suppose it's tactless to say it, but Chris Kresser is an acupuncturist ... not that I've got anything against acupuncture, but maybe someone needs a little more in the way of training before pronouncing on the thyroid.

### [email redacted] — 2012-07-14T17:16:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diet-and-thyroid.2421/post-37762>

If I recall correctly Dr Broda Barnes believed that the body needed 30 grams of carb in the diet to facilitate conversion of T4 to T3, that falls well within a very low carb diet plan for the most part.

### Destiny — 2012-07-14T21:25:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diet-and-thyroid.2421/post-37811>

It took me to see 3 doctors to get a full panel .. the 1st did the regular one with free T4, second one did the old fashion panel but added the antibodies. Then, I convinced a 3rd doctor that I need to complete the info about my free T3 and reverse T3. Honestly, I was at the point that I wanted to buy them on my own. Then, I thought I pay all this insurance money - for what? Good luck!

### [email redacted] — 2012-07-14T21:33:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diet-and-thyroid.2421/post-37812>

One idea I keep coming back to is this: It seems to be a gentler and smoother process (less work, more longevity) for our bodies if there is just enough dietary glucose comin in so the body doesn't have to switch to converting protein. What's that magic number that is ideal? Probably much much closer to the amount you get in a vlc diet. Certainly not a sweet potato a day, but perhaps some berries in a salad in the summer, some winter squashes in season, some foraged type tuber in the winter?

To keep along that thought, were there any foodstuffs along that rift coast in the dead of winter that would have provided enough glucose for a human that slept half the day? Sea-vegetables perhaps?

### Michael — 2012-08-06T13:59:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diet-and-thyroid.2421/post-43177>

> **Destiny;43460 wrote:** I would not do soybeans, peanuts, and millet for sure!

Certainly the first and third would seem to be contra-indicated for anyone who had problems with their thyroid. (And peanuts are not a good food anyway.) But the post also mentions several cruciferous vegetables - again not an obvious choice for people with problems in this area, although it may not be necessary to actually avoid them (particularly if cooked).

That post just smells deeply suspicious to me. I don't think the list of foods has anthing to do with the theme of the thread, and is just a random list of foodstuffs. That leads me to believe that the post is spam and its sole purpose is get people to click the hyperlink. I did - just to see - and found that the linked page has no relation to thyroid health at all, which kind of confirms my suspicion.

### vkiernan — 2012-07-14T16:01:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-doesnt-keep-me-full-you.2424/post-37730>

I like fish and seafood but it doesn't seem to keep my full. Why? No matter what I cook it in, I will be hungry 4 hours later for sure. I can eat it for supper cuz I will be going to bed in a few hours but if I eat it for lunch I will be hungry way too early for dinner. Do I just need to eat more? Anyone else experience this?

### Destiny — 2012-07-14T16:16:38+0300

### vkiernan — 2012-07-17T20:43:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-doesnt-keep-me-full-you.2424/post-38799>

> **Shijin13;38972 wrote:** bubbies sauerkraut & pickels, kimchee. I've been going though so much sauerkraut I bought a fermentation jar - I've got my 1st pot started!!!! hoping for a success and lots of kraut!

I have to attempt to make my own too. Just haven't gotten there yet. I have bought 2 heads of cabbage though for it but just never started it. Once I make the plunge, then I'm good. It is just starting that frustrates me.

### nonchalant — 2012-07-17T21:58:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-doesnt-keep-me-full-you.2424/post-38818>

Actually, I used to feel this way about seafood. Hungry an hour after I eat.

Wish I could tell you what has changed. It's quite filling and satisfying now.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-17T23:08:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-doesnt-keep-me-full-you.2424/post-38836>

I have the same, Nonchalant. I almost couldn't finish my BAB this AM (crab with greens in nori rolls) which really wasn't that much imo. It's 2pm and I'm still full. Sucking some ice cubes b/c it's hot.

### Melinda — 2012-07-17T23:55:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-doesnt-keep-me-full-you.2424/post-38846>

> **vkiernan;38977 wrote:** I have to attempt to make my own too. Just haven't gotten there yet. I have bought 2 heads of cabbage though for it but just never started it. Once I make the plunge, then I'm good. It is just starting that frustrates me.

Homemade is so good. I have 4 jars going: sauerkraut sort of (made with napa cabbage), salsa with mango, garlic pickles and beets with cabbage. As a jar empties of veggies, I take a clean jar and put cut up whatever in it, usually matching what I just finished and I strain the old juice onto the new veggies but for the last inch in the jar as it is cloudy. I usually have to add a cup of salt water (1 TBP in one cup) to get the level up to almost cover the veggies, place a clean jar on the top of the food to keep it submerged, cover with a clean tea towel and wait. My first efforts took 4-6 days, taste every day then refrig when it tastes good to you. Now they are ready in about 24 hours. We all have seen great improvements in our various digestion issues. We pretty much don't eat veggies any other way. So much less waste as this preserves so well. I hope you leap in and try it soon.

### vkiernan — 2012-07-18T00:17:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-doesnt-keep-me-full-you.2424/post-38852>

> **Melinda;39025 wrote:** Homemade is so good. I have 4 jars going: sauerkraut sort of (made with napa cabbage), salsa with mango, garlic pickles and beets with cabbage. As a jar empties of veggies, I take a clean jar and put cut up whatever in it, usually matching what I just finished and I strain the old juice onto the new veggies but for the last inch in the jar as it is cloudy. I usually have to add a cup of salt water (1 TBP in one cup) to get the level up to almost cover the veggies, place a clean jar on the top of the food to keep it submerged, cover with a clean tea towel and wait. My first efforts took 4-6 days, taste every day then refrig when it tastes good to you. Now they are ready in about 24 hours. We all have seen great improvements in our various digestion issues. We pretty much don't eat veggies any other way. So much less waste as this preserves so well. I hope you leap in and try it soon.

Thanks for sharing. I will try now for sure.

### [email redacted] — 2012-07-18T01:34:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-doesnt-keep-me-full-you.2424/post-38867>

ok, I want to try this fermented veggie making too. Can you guys share pics? I need pics!

### vkiernan — 2012-07-18T04:11:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-doesnt-keep-me-full-you.2424/post-38902>

> **Cavemam;39046 wrote:** ok, I want to try this fermented veggie making too. Can you guys share pics? I need pics!

### Melinda — 2012-07-18T05:22:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-doesnt-keep-me-full-you.2424/post-38930>

[https://tryingtraditional.wordpress.com/2008/08/03/lacto-fermented-salsa/](http://https://tryingtraditional.wordpress.com/2008/08/03/lacto-fermented-salsa/) this site has a nice pictorial on making salsa. I used these pics to make my first batch. I put all of mine into one bigger jar that looks like this:

[http://ecx.images-amazon.com/images/I/110lSh7BLML.\_SL500\_AA300\_.jpg](http://http://ecx.images-amazon.com/images/I/110lSh7BLML._SL500_AA300_.jpg)

It gets a little fizzy with the mango added in, very yummy as I don't let myself have fruit much.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-18T05:27:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-doesnt-keep-me-full-you.2424/post-38931>

Think of food as hormone information, not as a metabolic fuel. Think of the Epi-paleo template as human jet fuel for the our nervous system.

### Joann — 2012-07-18T06:27:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-doesnt-keep-me-full-you.2424/post-38946>

> **vkiernan;37897 wrote:** I like fish and seafood but it doesn't seem to keep my full. Why? No matter what I cook it in, I will be hungry 4 hours later for sure. I can eat it for supper cuz I will be going to bed in a few hours but if I eat it for lunch I will be hungry way too early for dinner. Do I just need to eat more? Anyone else experience this?

For me it depends on the fish. halibut, scallops, shark are more filling then say sole, salmon etc. I put shrimps cooked in coconut oil in coconut flakes delish

# Thread: Testosterone and Insomnia - for the Men
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/testosterone-and-insomnia-for-the-men.2425/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### tweety — 2012-07-14T16:19:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/testosterone-and-insomnia-for-the-men.2425/post-37737>

Posting a question for my husband.

So he just started the injections for testosterone, received two this week. After the first he experienced a super surge in energy and well being, and then promptly suffered insomnia for the next two nights. The third night he slept nine hours!! (unheard of, by the way, for him)

Then he got his second injection yesterday morning. Nothing noteworthy during the day, but he suffered insomnia again last night.

Has this been your experience? Alternately, what have you experienced from the injections? He was very low in T so as his body adjusts to higher levels will his sleep even out? He is doing morning injections, so are there other things he should consider?

Thanks!

### [email redacted] — 2012-07-14T23:53:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/testosterone-and-insomnia-for-the-men.2425/post-37851>

> **tweety;37904 wrote:** Posting a question for my husband.
>
>
>
> So he just started the injections for testosterone, received two this week. After the first he experienced a super surge in energy and well being, and then promptly suffered insomnia for the next two nights. The third night he slept nine hours!! (unheard of, by the way, for him)
>
>
>
> Then he got his second injection yesterday morning. Nothing noteworthy during the day, but he suffered insomnia again last night.
>
>
>
> Has this been your experience? Alternately, what have you experienced from the injections? He was very low in T so as his body adjusts to higher levels will his sleep even out? He is doing morning injections, so are there other things he should consider?
>
>
>
> Thanks!

Can I ask how he ended up getting the prescription for it? My hubby needs, but we are on 3rd doc that won't go down that road as he's still in "range", like the very bottom of range. What kind of doc?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-15T00:12:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/testosterone-and-insomnia-for-the-men.2425/post-37859>

tweety when your gas tank is low for so long and you get some gas it excites the system but then the system falls into allostasis as much of the testosterone is shuttled down to Vitamin D.

### tweety — 2012-07-15T14:00:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/testosterone-and-insomnia-for-the-men.2425/post-38019>

thanks Dr. Kruse. thats what we are hoping, as I am thinking that this surge isn't a bad sign... it just shows how much his body needed it.

Cavemam, we went to our PCP and she was very hesitant to put him on the dose that he wanted, but he explained why he believed he needed it, and following other recommendations (thank you Dr. K) he had lost 7lbs and was showing progress. She has talked to him previously about losing weight and improving his labs, so she was willing to do t injections for 6-8 weeks as long as he promised to come in weekly so she could monitor his blood pressure and follow him with more labs in 6 weeks.

After that she wants him to switch to gel, so we are gathering the info now to show her why the injections are better. I am seeing how lucky we are to have her- she may not be very forward thinking about some of this stuff as we would want her to be, but she is always willing to let us try something if we have the info to back it up. We've been seeing her for a year now, so she has gotten to know both of us and I think sees where we are coming from.

By the way, based on his labs, she had previously told him she thought his T was low, but she wasn't worried about it.  Thats the same level it sounds as your husband. Just at the bottom of it.

### Lyndra — 2012-07-16T02:37:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/testosterone-and-insomnia-for-the-men.2425/post-38210>

> **Cavemam;38019 wrote:** Can I ask how he ended up getting the prescription for it? My hubby needs, but we are on 3rd doc that won't go down that road as he's still in "range", like the very bottom of range. What kind of doc?

His total cholesterol was way above "normal" and instead of prescribing a statin, he congratulated us on our great lifestyle that made HDL high and trigs low. You could call his office (Paul Berger) and ask about testosterone prescribing. I suspect he does as testosterone level was one of the blood tests he ran.

### chocolate — 2012-07-14T20:48:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/meat-and-micronutrients-great-article.2430/post-37807>

> **janagram;37951 wrote:** couldn't open...

[http://www.google.com/url?sa=t&rct=j&q=meat%20and%20micronutrients&source=web&cd=1&ved=0CFUQFjAA&url=http%3A%2F%2Fjournals.cambridge.org%2Fproduction%2Faction%2FcjoGetFulltext%3Ffulltextid%3D747604&ei=Z68BUObMFtOK2QXL9ZmfCw&usg=AFQjCNETYNmxCKQUdJJE6zKmI5T0GqL\_Qw&cad=rja](http://http://www.google.com/url?sa=t&rct=j&q=meat%20and%20micronutrients&source=web&cd=1&ved=0CFUQFjAA&url=http%3A%2F%2Fjournals.cambridge.org%2Fproduction%2Faction%2FcjoGetFulltext%3Ffulltextid%3D747604&ei=Z68BUObMFtOK2QXL9ZmfCw&usg=AFQjCNETYNmxCKQUdJJE6zKmI5T0GqL_Qw&cad=rja)

Animal (2007), 1: Pages 309–316 Q The Animal Consortium 2007

doi: 10.1017/S1751731107657796

‘Mealthy’ food: meat as a healthy and valuable source

of micronutrients

D. Nohr† and H. K. Biesalski

Department of Biological Chemistry and Nutrition, University of Hohenheim, Garbenstrasse 30, 70593 Stuttgart, Germany

It has a section on bioavailable folate. Apparently, some of us plum aren't supposed eat veggies. (my personal conclusion) It has Dr. Kruse's pet collection of disorders mentioned in several subtopics. Iceberg is looking to be the food of champions.(again, my personal conclusion) I guess it's fitting. Iceberg is a ct food.

# Thread: Small hunger issue
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-hunger-issue.2433/>
**Subforum:** HCG Protocol
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### DarleenMB — 2012-07-14T19:25:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-hunger-issue.2433/post-37799>

Today is my 3rd injection day, first VLCD day and I haven't eaten yet this morning (it's now 10:30). I'm having a tiny bit of hunger, nothing I can't ignore but I'm wondering if I'm using enough hcg. I've been taking 125units and thinking maybe I should bump that up just a bit.

Colleen? Anyone?

### [email redacted] — 2012-07-15T00:20:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-hunger-issue.2433/post-37860>

What brand/kind of hcg? It took me a week to get it all adjusted - and I had stomach cramp hunger the first few days. Don't know if that just settles down as a matter of course, or if it was dosing adjustments.

I started at 175, way too high. Seems there is a bit of a curve towards more fat needing less dosage. I was 240 lbds after load so a little bigger than you.

From Colleen I've seen the Corian needing smaller doses than say the HUCOG.

Also what is in your calorie free dressing? Not worried it will interfere?

### [email redacted] — 2012-07-15T00:21:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-hunger-issue.2433/post-37862>

Also Darleen - for me I just couldn't make it doing breakfast. . .I was much less hungry in the a.m., and just had to go with that to get into the groove. I also saved fruit for afternoon snack. I didn't have any problems not eating after dinner - perhaps because I was already trained into that behavior.

### Lynn1 — 2012-07-15T00:24:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-hunger-issue.2433/post-37864>

Hi Darleen, I am on VLCD day 1 today, (r3), and I can tell you that sometimes if your load was not 'enough' fat, that it may take another few days for the hcg to hold down that hunger. How was your load? Also, don't forget the water.

### DarleenMB — 2012-07-15T00:31:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-hunger-issue.2433/post-37866>

I'm using Bharat hcg. Same kind I used before from the same place.

I ate my usual 75% fat both loading days. My biggest problem has been the 90+ heat for the last several weeks. It has KILLED my appetite. I eat that BAB and then don't WANT to eat any more the rest of the day. I might get a little hungry but I don't WANNA eat! So I don't THINK it was not enough fat. Maybe ... I don't know.

The hunger has been just there. A little noticeable but not too much. I still think I"m going to bump to 130 units tomorrow and see how it goes. Everything else seems fine. Checked my blood glucose just before I ate and it was 86. ??? I held off eating until noonish because stuff just got in the way. I was fine with it. But I'm noticing a little hunger again. wondering if I'm just thirsty so am drinking/chugging a big glass of iced green tea.

### DarleenMB — 2012-07-15T18:15:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-hunger-issue.2433/post-38062>

Bumped my dose this morning to 30cc or 150 units. Dropped 2.6 pounds yesterday.

### [email redacted] — 2012-07-16T00:03:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-hunger-issue.2433/post-38139>

Woot. How's hunger today? I'm back in my hunger groove after being a little too hungry last couple of days.

What I found was the kind of hunger that makes your tummy growl and wiggle is too much, and the kind of hunger where you think too much of eating too many yummy foods is a sign that the dose is too low.

### DarleenMB — 2012-07-16T01:09:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-hunger-issue.2433/post-38161>

Cavemam mine was the 2nd kind ... We'll see how I do on the slightly higher dose but I'm noticing this afternoon the hunger thing appears to have left.

### colleencoble — 2012-07-16T04:08:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-hunger-issue.2433/post-38250>

Sorry I"m traveling and just saw this. Sometimes the first day can have a TAD of hunger but if you're extremely hungry the first day the dose is usually too high. And with each subsequent round, you usually need to lower the dose so keep an eye on things. good luck!

### DarleenMB — 2012-07-16T19:09:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-hunger-issue.2433/post-38392>

Thanks Colleen. I wasn't extremely hungry just aware of hunger. I upped the dose but now I'm dealing with fractured sleep from heat, cats and having to get up 10 times a night to pee. Plus I have been headachy since last night. Not sure what's going on but am trying not to just give up. I feel THAT lousy. I'm sure it will pass. I just remind myself this is JUST a 21 day round. I can DO this. The whole thing is an attempt to jumpstart my weight loss.

### jordenarmed — 2012-07-30T13:07:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-hunger-issue.2433/post-41919>

100 years is a long time may be there will be a new means of producing food so put your practicalyty on crating something for the future.

# Thread: homocysteine and proper supplements
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/homocysteine-and-proper-supplements.2437/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-07-15T00:04:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/homocysteine-and-proper-supplements.2437/post-37855>

[http://www.socialanxietysupport.com/forum/f11/homocysteine-levels-sa-relation-52566/](http://http://www.socialanxietysupport.com/forum/f11/homocysteine-levels-sa-relation-52566/)

> Homocysteine levels SA relation
>
> a few links
>
>
>
> 1) Decreased choline and creatine concentrations in centrum semiovale in patients with generalized anxiety disorder:
>
>
>
> linkinghub.elsevier.com/retrieve/pii/S0925492706000035
>
>
>
> 2) Choline, creatine, and N-acetylaspartate were inversely correlated to total social phobia and fear symptoms, as measured by the Brief Social Phobia Scale, in the thalamic and noncortical gray areas.
>
>
>
> [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/8276746](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/8276746)
>
>
>
> 3) Low Dopamine D2 Receptor Binding Potential in Social Phobia
>
>
>
> ajp.psychiatryonline.org/cgi/content/full/157/3/457
>
>
>
> Here comes their relation homocysteine
>
>
>
> 1) **When homocysteine levels are too high, this interferes with the functioning of receptors located all over the brain -- called NMDA ( N-methyl-D-aspartate) receptors -- that are critical to learning, memory, brain development, and general neural processing.
>
>
>
> [http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases...0604155820.htm](http://http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases...0604155820.htm)
>
>
>
> 2) homocysteine selectively decreased the ability of D2 receptor stimulation
>
>
>
> homocysteine acts as an allosteric D2 receptor antagonist
>
>
>
> [http://cat.inist.fr/?aModele=afficheN&cpsidt=18274309](http://http://cat.inist.fr/?aModele=afficheN&cpsidt=18274309)
>
>
>
> 3) in humans who are fed a low- choline diet is an exaggerated increase in plasma total homocysteine
>
>
>
> [www.ajcn.org/cgi/content/full/82/4/719](http://http://www.ajcn.org/cgi/content/full/82/4/719)
>
> 4) Creatine supplementation decreases homocysteine
>
>
>
> [http://www.ingentaconnect.com/conten...00004/art00021](http://http://www.ingentaconnect.com/conten...00004/art00021)
>
>
>
> \--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
>
>
>
> So we lack creatine and choline and N-acetylaspartate according to the first two articles. Creatine and choline decreases homocysteine. Homocysteine decreases n-methyl-d-aspartate and has negative effects on d2 receptors. According to the third link we have problems with d2 receptors.
>
>
>
> There are other supplements like folic acid, b-12, **NAC, b-6, TMG, **MSM** which reduces homocysteine.
>
>
>
> The thing with the articles I sent is, that it seems to me there is a chain reaction between choline creatine and homocysteine in our case specifically.
>
>
>
> I mean I used NAC, TMG, b-6 did not make much difference. Never tried choline+creatine together. But when I used them seperately they help a little bit. But I don't think I took these supplements very long time to effect my homocysteine levels very significantly. Now that I don't have creatine I have to wait for stipend to get one. Then I will share my experience.
>
>
>
> I did not have a test of homocysteine but as a prediabetic it is very likely that I have high homocyteine levels. What else increases it, I think diet is a factor but genetic is anoth**er big factor in the metabolism of this toxic substance.
>
> **
>
> About N-acetylaspartate and N-methylaspartate I dont know what their difference is but I think they are very much linked.

This is someone's journey and they are sharing....I guess I will be adding back the NAC and MSM... but not using folic and using the bioactive folate.

### chocolate — 2012-07-15T00:12:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/homocysteine-and-proper-supplements.2437/post-37858>

[http://www.lef.org/protocols/heart\_circulatory/homocysteine\_reduction\_01.htm](http://http://www.lef.org/protocols/heart_circulatory/homocysteine_reduction_01.htm)

> Homocysteine Basics
>
>
>
> All homocysteine in the body is biosynthesized from methionine, an essential amino acid found abundantly in meats, seafood, dairy products, and eggs. Vegetables, with few exceptions (eg, sesame seeds and Brazil nuts), are low in methionine; even such protein-rich legumes as beans, peas, and lentils contain relatively small amounts of methionine compared to animal-derived foods.
>
>
>
> Homocysteine exists in several forms (Jacobsen 1998); the sum of all homocysteine forms is termed ‘total homocysteine.’ Protein-rich diets contain ample amounts of methionine and consequently produce significant levels of homocysteine in the body (Verhoef 2005).
>
>
>
> Homocysteine is metabolized through two pathways: remethylation and transsulfuration (See Figure 2 Homocysteine Metabolic Pathways). Remethylation requires folate and B12 coenzymes; transsulfuration requires pyridoxal-5’-phosphate, the B6 coenzyme (Selhub 1999a).


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Thread: homocysteine and candida
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/homocysteine-and-candida.2439/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-07-15T00:27:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/homocysteine-and-candida.2439/post-37865>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21689631](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21689631)

> Structure of Candida albicans methionine synthase determined by employing surface residue mutagenesis.
>
> Ubhi D, Kavanagh KL, Monzingo AF, Robertus JD.
>
> Source
>
>
>
> Institute for Cellular and Molecular Biology, Department of Chemistry and Biochemistry, University of Texas at Austin, United States.
>
> Abstract
>
>
>
> Fungal methionine synthase, Met6p, transfers a methyl group from **5-methyl**\-tetrahydrofolate to homocysteine to generate methionine. The enzyme is e**ssential to fungal growth** and is a potential anti-fungal drug design target. We have characterized the enzyme from the pathogen Candida albicans but were unable to crystallize it in native form. We converted Lys103, Lys104, and Glu107 all to Tyr (Met6pY), Thr (Met6pT) and Ala (Met6pA). All variants showed wild-type kinetic activity and formed useful crystals, each with unique crystal packing. In each case the mutated residues participated in beneficial crystal contacts. We have solved the three structures at 2.0-2.8Ã… resolution and analyzed crystal packing, active-site residues, and similarity to other known methionine synthase structures. C. albicans Met6p has a two domain structure with each of the domains having a (βα)(8)-barrel fold. The barrels are arranged face-to-face and the active site is located in a cleft between the two domains. Met6p utilizes a zinc ion for catalysis that is bound in the C-terminal domain and ligated by four conserved residues: His657, Cys659, Glu679 and Cys739.
>
>
>
> Copyright Â© 2011 Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

### chocolate — 2012-07-15T00:34:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/homocysteine-and-candida.2439/post-37867>

interesting burb when googling biotin and candida... some guy said it on another forum type:

> [http://www.waiworld.com/waitalk/phpBB3/viewtopic.php?f=17&t=2912](http://http://www.waiworld.com/waitalk/phpBB3/viewtopic.php?f=17&t=2912)
>
>
>
> Have you got candida djkvan ?
>
>
>
> Intresting. Do you know the theory behind this.
>
>
>
>
>
> **I know biotin competes width alpha lipoic acid for the same receptors.** But I don't know if that will affect you at this dosage.
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
> Biotin for candida
>
>
>
> Postby djkvan Â» Wed 07 Sep 2011 15:54
>
> Food intolerance + history of multiple oral antibiotic treatments = tendency towards candidiasis. Food intolerance can cause intestinal inflammation lead to malabsorption and potentially results in enteropathy. Vitamin/mineral supplements are generally a bad idea for candida as they can feed it, but it is theorized that biotin in therapeutic doses (5-7mg/day) can help balance intestinal flora and stop candida from transforming to its pathogenic form. If you are intolerant to eggs you likely won't be absorbing enough biotin anyways. Brittle nails, hair loss (body, head or both) and seborrheic dermatitis are good indicators of biotin deficiency. I know that Wai is against supplementation, but biotin is a water-soluble vitamin so what you don't use you pee out. There are no reports of toxicity due to excessive consumption of biotin.
>
>
>
> Yamaguchi reported extensive mycelial formation of c. albicans when grown in a medium with a suboptimal concentration of biotin (0-1 ng/ml) as compared to primarily yeast cell growth in optimal levels of biotin.

### chocolate — 2012-07-15T00:41:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/homocysteine-and-candida.2439/post-37870>

Another some guy on another forum said

[http://www.longecity.org/forum/topic/3707-mixing-r-ala-acetyl-l-carnitine/](http://http://www.longecity.org/forum/topic/3707-mixing-r-ala-acetyl-l-carnitine/)

> Posted 18 September 2004 - 05:51 PM
>
> Scott,
>
>
>
> Dave from Bulk Nutrition says:
>
>
>
> [http://www.1fast400....and\_Health.html](http://http://www.1fast400....and_Health.html)
>
>
>
> Quote
>
> As a supplement, biotin even in large doses is ~100 percent bioavailable \[12\]. Taking a multivitamin that provides biotin should provide one with enough to avoid suboptimal status. The general recommendation for biotin supplementation to prevent deficiency due to ALA is 1 mg or more per 100 mg of ALA. If one is taking more than a gram of pantothenic acid daily, a biotin supplement providing at least 1 mg is warranted, and 3-5 mg may be best to stay on the safe side (it should also be noted that biotin and pantothenic acid exhibit a synergistic relationship under some circumstances). Because large amounts adminstered at a time might increase renal excretion, biotin is more effective when taken as two or three smaller doses throughout the day \[12\].

### chocolate — 2012-09-26T23:05:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/homocysteine-and-candida.2439/post-54565>

[http://mdschoice.com/articles/vitamin-mineral\_pantothenic\_acid.php](http://http://mdschoice.com/articles/vitamin-mineral_pantothenic_acid.php)

Pantothenic Acid

> V. Requirements
>
>
>
> Effect of environment
>
> Excretion of pantothenic acid is greater under hot moist conditions
>
> Effect of other nutrients
>
> High protein spares requirement
>
> Vitamin B12 spares pantothenic acid if diet is marginal in choline or methionine
>
> **Deficiency of folic acid and biotin may interfere with utilization of pantothenic acid
>
> C. Recommended allowances
>
> Human........5-10 mg/day probably adequate. American diet contains 6-20 mg/day
>
> Swine..........4-6 mg/lb feed
>
> Poultry.........4 mg/lb feed
>
>
>
> VI. Deficiency
>
>
>
> Human
>
> Burning feet syndrome - abnormal skin sensations in feet and lower legs
>
> Exacerbated by warmth
>
> Diminished with cold
>
> Vomiting, fatigue, weakness
>
> Deficiency associated with:
>
> Alcoholism (decreased intake)
>
> Diabetes mellitus (increased excretion)
>
> Inflammatory bowel diseases (impaired absorption)**

# Thread: Buying a chest freezer
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buying-a-chest-freezer.2440/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Glamazon — 2012-07-15T00:40:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buying-a-chest-freezer.2440/post-37869>

I need to start buying in bulk for savings and a place to put ice.

A chest freezer, not the huge long one, seems to be appropriate.

Any one have recommendations?

thanks

### vkiernan — 2012-07-15T03:36:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buying-a-chest-freezer.2440/post-37903>

I have gone down the chest freezer route and the best thing they are good for is saving money on the electric bill. I have bought a couple of freezers after that and I go for the uprights now. So much easier to get at things and organize. If you have to dig underneath to find something, chances are you won't or will quit after the first few times. The last freezer I just bought works great but I went a little too cheap. I should have paid a little more and got a better one. My grass fed burger packages tend to slide off the shelf if they aren't positioned right. Will hurt the toe if one ever lands on it. The door shelves have cheap holders that flex with the frozen meat. Just some quality issues. But, it does keep everything separated, organized and easy to reach.

### Glamazon — 2012-07-15T18:09:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buying-a-chest-freezer.2440/post-38059>

I have to go chest freezer due to space considerations. And I am a single person so something bigger is overkill I would think. But thank you!

### Huck — 2012-07-15T18:29:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buying-a-chest-freezer.2440/post-38066>

I bought [this](http://http://www.homedepot.com/Appliances-Kitchen-Appliances-Freezers-Chest-Freezers/h_d1/N-5yc1vZbv40/R-100598963/h_d2/ProductDisplay?catalogId=10053&langId=-1&storeId=10051) one last year and so far, it has worked great. I can easily put a quarter side of beef in it and have plenty of room for seafood. Home Depot has a big appliance sale every year and I believe I only paid about $150 for this one (7 cu).

### Glamazon — 2012-07-16T03:07:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buying-a-chest-freezer.2440/post-38217>

> **Huck;38235 wrote:** I bought [this](http://http://www.homedepot.com/Appliances-Kitchen-Appliances-Freezers-Chest-Freezers/h_d1/N-5yc1vZbv40/R-100598963/h_d2/ProductDisplay?catalogId=10053&langId=-1&storeId=10051) one last year and so far, it has worked great. I can easily put a quarter side of beef in it and have plenty of room for seafood. Home Depot has a big appliance sale every year and I believe I only paid about $150 for this one (7 cu).

sweet! thanks

### Marie — 2012-07-16T07:10:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buying-a-chest-freezer.2440/post-38297>

I bought a small top loading ($25 on CL) chest freezer for my (Raw Fed/ Paleo) dog 7 years ago and have not looked back since. GO FOR IT!!!

I now have an extra freezer for the CT ice that I paid a little more for..but I'm just as happy.

### nonchalant — 2012-07-16T19:53:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buying-a-chest-freezer.2440/post-38410>

I like my two chest freezers because all the cold air doesn't exit every time I open the doors. I just have to remember to turn off the ceiling fan first! But yes, I do have to dig to find the jars of lard I rendered, and other such goodies.

I bought a small freezer first, then purchased a slightly larger one later. I'd rather have 2 than one larger one, for organizational reasons.

### [email redacted] — 2012-07-16T20:07:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buying-a-chest-freezer.2440/post-38418>

I splurged on a big standing one with lots of shelves and door space. Best purchase eva! It's the garage freezer - and we got the one without a defrost feature, that is the most important feature!!!

In order to defrost it brings temp up. It doesn't necessarly thaw the food - but it makes sense to me that a constant low frozen temp keeps things better longer.

### kathylu — 2012-07-16T20:38:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buying-a-chest-freezer.2440/post-38431>

Got one on Craigslist for $25 a few years ago. It works fine.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anybody-got-the-suzanne-sommers-book-on-kindle-is-it-loanable.2441/post-38281>

_Ageless is about BHRT and her journey with breast cancer. _Knockout is about cancer in general, with a lot of very informative interviews with cutting edge alternative docs. (I'm almost done with Knockout_ and highly recommend. Ageless_ is next (if that's what you're downloading, I'd love to hear what you think of it when you read it). Have them in paper format, though, not kindles.)

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-16T06:11:13+0300

### caroline — 2012-07-15T11:24:52+0300

What a beautiful spot you have - not to mention your little cutie pie!! I am loving my glasses. I try not to turn on any lights at all and am ready for bed by 10.00pm. and play blind getting to bed. ### ealachan — 2012-07-15T13:57:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-circadian-living.2442/post-38018>

Love your outside space, Mam! I've always been drawn to stone. I'd happily live in a house with all stone floors if I could. Remnants of cave living, maybe?

As for my attempts at optimizing my circadian stuff - I wake up with the sun in my eyes every morning, as my bedroom window faces East. At work, I don't have a florescent light over my desk - I got my boss to help me take the bulbs out of it long ago because it was giving me migraines and making my vision all wonky. I do stare at a computer screen all day, unfortunately...but I have f.lux installed on it and have dimmed the monitor down considerably. At home, I make sure that the lights are all off and my blue-blockers are on when the sun drops below the horizon. I usually take the blue-blockers off to go outside and walk the dog one last time around dusk...I figure the lowering natural light exposure to my unprotected eyes is good? Then I pop the blue-blockers back on as I come inside. Pretty strict no-laptop-after-9pm rule for me, so I usually take my Mg and NAC supplements, then watch a little TV or read a little before bed. I have to get up in the middle of the night three days a week at least, because my husband works evening shifts and I have to go pick him up, but I make sure to wear my blue-blockers the whole time I'm up in the middle of the night. I can honestly say that I have a MUCH easier time falling back to sleep since I got the blue-blockers.

### tweety — 2012-07-15T18:23:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-circadian-living.2442/post-38065>

I am working on our circadian rhythms here too. We have been much better about eating early and getting things done before dusk. We put the kids to bed right around the time the sun goes down, and then DH and I usually go to bed to read then too. Our lights need to be changed to the orange bulbs, but honestly we're usually asleep within twenty or so minutes of laying down to read! DH found some sunglasses he had that block blue light, so he is using those. He falls asleep much earlier than he used to, but he doesn't wake up with the sun just yet. I wake with the sun... regardless of how I slept I usually am up around 6. A few weeks ago I was up between 5:30 and 5:45, so I am glad its shifting back to 6!

I am curious what to make of kids and waking with the sun. Usually mine both do. But I have one that tends to stay awake later, even in the dark; and the other falls asleep immediately but will sleep late in the morning. She is the youngest, and always the last one awake in the morning.

I am trying to figure out what we will do this winter. When it gets dark around 5:30pm, we will need to make a few changes. And kids have dance a couple of nights a week that keeps us out later than I prefer. I thought about candles but i am not confident we're the family for that! I think we will be investing in orange bulbs and blue blocker glasses for all of us. Or reading by the fire sounds nice!

We've got some time to practice, but I do need to start now because I want my kids on this schedule for sure.

### BJK77 — 2012-07-15T18:47:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-circadian-living.2442/post-38071>

Love the outdoor space Cavemam! What could be better than earthing with the natural light?!?! I may need to do something like that too.

I have really been making a concerted effort to work on my circadian rhythm. I've been doing well with keeping the lights off as soon as darkness hits and wearing my glasses. I usually put my glasses on about 30 minutes before it goes dark and am in bed shortly after. My problem is waking in the morning - I just never feel rested and ready to get up. I've been trying to look into the sun and get as much natural light as possible when I first wake up, but so far I haven't seen an improvement.

### [email redacted] — 2012-07-15T19:20:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-circadian-living.2442/post-38081>

Yea, we struggle with wanting to do the best thing for the kiddo. I've started saying verbally things like "when the sun goes down, lights go out, no electronics" He of course noted that I watched TV at night. I did say that was why I used the glasses - I may have to give that up (hehe untill he's deep asleep?)

He's been on his own little rhythm that gets him up at 6:00 everyday, no matter WHEN he goes to bed! I was hoping this summer would cure that, in I would make his bedtime with the sun but nope - I just end up with a very tired boy.

So nice thing is my Husband is way on board now with his own blue blockers - he did a consult and Jack got across the importance of this in a way I was never able to - probably because it was specific to him, and Jack has clearly evolved so his voice mesmerizes people

I bought the orange bulbs, but they don't go in all our fancy lighting so I'm afraid we are going to have to do dinner at 4p! I'll figure something out . . . but keep posting what your doing.

### BJK77 — 2012-07-15T22:12:08+0300

Somebody tell me more about the orange bulbs - are these for all the time or just after sundown? Where did you get them?

### tweety — 2012-07-15T23:00:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-circadian-living.2442/post-38117>

the orange bulbs are on the jack recommends link- where you get the blue blocker glasses. I think they are primarily for after dark. Same deal as the glasses, they allow you to do more things after dark with out the harmful effects.

Cavemam: See I thought the fact that our kids woke up at 6 was in response to the summer sun coming up then. At least thats when it does for us. In the winter both my kids sleep in a little later, so I took that as more confirmation it was circadian rhythm. And then going to bed later in the summer and still being up early made sense too because it doesn't get dark until later. Summer is for being out and about, winter is for hibernating!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-15T23:54:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-circadian-living.2442/post-38134>

> **Cavemam;38250 wrote:** Y
>
> I bought the orange bulbs, but they don't go in all our fancy lighting so I'm afraid we are going to have to do dinner at 4p! I'll figure something out . . . but keep posting what your doing.
>
>
>
> Love having a few partners in crime.

We pretty much do have dinner at 4:00pm! My kids are in bed by 8 and I want them to have no food for 4 hours before sleep, too. Plus this ensures that we eat during daylight (sun currently setting here at 5:30). It's an easy adjustment... kids are always hungry at the end of the school day so it works just fine. And as I'm down to 2-meals per day, it works for my second meal, too.

I can promise anyone trying to make the transition that it is mostly a psychological barrier to overcome. You have to deal with the 'I have to give up my (fill in the blank: TV, dinner parties, etc)" but once you get over that hurdle, we've found it pretty easy. We never bought the glasses and just do no screens/electronics/lights at all post sundown. I wanted to also reduce the EMF exposure. For my children, it's meant that their computer time has come before homework time because they can do homework by candlelight, but that has not been a problem at all. If there is something on TV we really want to see, we can record it and watch it during the day on a weekend. (My husband has made us promise that if the All Blacks go the Rugby World Cup finals again next time, we'll keep the TV on and watch live!)

We all wind down naturally, my kids never complain about going to bed anymore, they sleep like rocks and they wake naturally before sunrise. Even the dog now follows this exact pattern (I figure he was exposed to a lot of unnatural light before, too, and he can't wear the glasses!)

We save a bundle on electricity (and heat, of course)... the additional cost for us is candles. We use a LOT!

### janagram — 2012-07-16T00:50:50+0300

### [email redacted] — 2012-07-16T01:00:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-circadian-living.2442/post-38154>

> **janagram;38319 wrote:** cavemam, any thoughts about the best earthing material for a patio? Love yours!!

I tried not to go all OCD on this, plus - I was on a very specific budget. SO I went with natural materials, didn't use anything that had plastics or stuff in it. The nice thing is the cheaper stuff actually has less additives and fancy things to make it last longer.

So, mine are all stamped concrete bricks and blocks on top of a sand/pebble base which just lays on TOP of the ground directly. There were fancier options (read expensive) where they do underlays and barriers - and would probably last a lot longer than mine will. BUT, if concrete is indeed a good earthing substance like I've heard - then I'm set!

My materials are actually the cheap home depot bricks. Most of the contractors poo-pooed it - but I had earthing and optimal on my side. Of course, after a few seasons in the crazy colorado freezing/thawing/arid climate I might regret going cheap.

Mine have tons of color variation (according to contractors it would look bad when done) and inconsistancies and won't look right from batch to batch if they are not colored properly. I went au natural and will let nature take its course and the colors will be bland (read less desirable according to landscaping CW). I shall plant pretty plants to bring color - I want the materials to be as earthy as possible!

If I can finish this week I'll show off the final product!

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-16T16:02:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-circadian-living.2442/post-38337>

so I bought some battery operated candles that putt off orange light. DH and I have been using them at night and we're both now wearing our orange glasses. We've also got two head lamps that have red lights for reading at night. biggest issue is doing clean up, and prep work for the next day - still need some light in the kitchen to make that work...

Going to shoot for eating earlier; yesterday we'd ate dinner btwn 6-630 - it was great. the kids had time to play and we had a great family sit down and reading books. I nursed ds at this same time, the kids were in bed by 810, and the boy was out snoring by 820. the girl was down for the count by 840 - she usually fights going to bed, and is still awake at 9pm (even if she's in bed) I have to say working from 8-5 suxs some times... right now I'm waiting to find out who my new client will be in August - and where I'll be working - Fingers crossed its still at home...but I might be working on site - so I'll have to play that by ear - then I need to figure out what my schedule will be - If I could work a 630-330 - that would rock - except I'd have to drive in instead of metro - but I'd be home by 4-430, could get dinner prepped, get a workout in, then pick up the kids, and DH could be home by 6pm and we'd be eating w/o issue. CT would have to be AM sessions 1st thing, or right before bed, dinner prep will be the night before so dinner won't take so long. I've been using the phillips wake up light - and while I'm not ready to get up yet...when it goes off, it is helping... 6am is suddenly easier to get up; guess its time to move the wake up light back to 530! I've been in bed before 1030 and DH is even going to bed earlier since he's accepted the Gospel of Kruse!

next up is to kick the cable TV - DH wants to wait until the Olympics are over - He's plotting to DVR the Wrestling and swimming (since Wrestling will be shown at obscure times, and swimming will be primetime). since we don't watch TV live - I figure we'll just do hulu (not the paid version), and get 2 digital TV receivers so we can watch Football in the fall. I also think we're going to be investing in BIG CANDLES for the winter.... I'm looking fwd to CTing in the winter w/a roaring fire in the fire pit!

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-30T21:23:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-circadian-living.2442/post-48425>

It has been known since 2001 that it is the blue component in ordinary white light that is most effective in suppressing melatonin. Studies at the University of Toronto (2) verified that wearing glasses that block blue light at wavelength shorter than 530nm allowed subjects working a night shift under bright lights to continue making melatonin just as they had on a previous night spent in darkness. In other words, wearing the glasses produces “virtual darkness”.

[http://www.seattlepi.com/business/press-releases/article/New-Evidence-Shows-Avoiding-the-Blue-Light-from-3609337.php#ixzz253TWWHZI](http://http://www.seattlepi.com/business/press-releases/article/New-Evidence-Shows-Avoiding-the-Blue-Light-from-3609337.php#ixzz253TWWHZI)

### Destiny — 2012-08-31T01:55:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-circadian-living.2442/post-48473>

I have been using this product during a couple of last winters. I just checked on it and it says among other things ...blue light. Not good, right?

**Zadro Sun 365 Portable Mood Adjusting Light

Light regulates our lives. We wake up with the light and go to sleep when it is dark. During winter months when there is not a lot of natural sunlight and it’s raining or snowing, you might be effected by not being as active and feeling the blues. A bright Light in a sunlight frequency can help as your brain perceives it as a natural sunlight.

• You can travel with it

• It's safe as there is no UV

• There are no chemicals

• It's portable – you can travel with this unit

Your Sunlight 365 Includes:

• 35 Blue LED’s** / Light Frequency 470 nm

• Adjustment of Light Intensity

• Countdown Timer

• Desktop Clock

• LED Backlight

• Free pouch

• Power Adapter

> **Destiny;48956 wrote:** Then, I understood Dr. K incorrectly, I thought he was saying that blue light is bad any time.

I understood as you did, Destiny. Blue light, anytime, is not good. It is artificial and not biologically appropriate. However, if we are going to be exposed to blue light we should optimise the timing... during sunlight hours. ### Shijin13 — 2012-08-31T02:58:26+0300

Artificial light before sunrise and after sunrise is not optimal. That doesn't mean you can't make adjustments to get your self to as close as optimal astound can.

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-31T04:41:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-circadian-living.2442/post-48509>

> **Shijin13;48972 wrote:** Destiny & Lauren are right Jack has said several times that blue light is bad at any time. He's even said working in high blue light during the day can mess w/circadian cycles and recommends considering blue blockers of you don't have access to natural light during the work day
>
>
>
> Artificial light before sunrise and after sunrise is not optimal. That doesn't mean you can't make adjustments to get your self to as close as optimal astound can.

This is what's frustrating me about Jack. He makes statements like this, but if I'm to listen to him and question everything as he says, I want to see the evidence. Where's he coming up with this? The problem is that Jack's whole way of thinking pulls so many aspects into one place, it's hard to back it all up. That's where a book would help, I'd think. I don't doubt him on this, but his vagueness about some things especially when he doesn't provide any other cites/info is frustrating when we're looking for clarfiication.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-31T06:07:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-circadian-living.2442/post-48533>

> **Nate R;48986 wrote:** This is what's frustrating me about Jack. He makes statements like this, but if I'm to listen to him and question everything as he says, I want to see the evidence. Where's he coming up with this? The problem is that Jack's whole way of thinking pulls so many aspects into one place, it's hard to back it all up. That's where a book would help, I'd think. I don't doubt him on this, but his vagueness about some things especially when he doesn't provide any other cites/info is frustrating when we're looking for clarfiication.

Once you have the answer all you really gotta do is plug it into google or pubmed etc. Look for the cite yourself? I don't think Dr K could answer any questions anymore if he had to provide a cite for everything. It's accumulated knowledge... over years. And I was the one who asked about the blue light exposure during the day and the answer was it's always bad. Instead of looking for a cite I went and tried it out. What happened so far is much needed sleep if I refrain from blue light/artificial light exposure during the day. That's enough of a cite for me.

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-31T15:52:05+0300

>

> Nate - keep questioning... you've got to do that if you want to figure out what you need to do to get to optimal... here's the thing... its all about context... for you some blue light in the day time might not be a bad thing.... for others it can be a bad thing...
>
>
>
> I've already noticed after 2wks working in a high blue light environment... I'm tired in the am and tired in the evening - so my cycle is off.... b/c of where I work I can't get away w/my at home orange blue blockers, and sunglasses won't work either - so last week I special ordered some fashionable glasses that block 100%blue light - I take multiple breaks outside so I can re-adjust... my body -but until I get my fashionable blue blockers that will look acceptable w/a suit in a business environment -I'm hanging in there... they'll be here next week.
>
>
>
> Each of us is our own N=1 look at Soul's post below.....
>
> Souldanzer;49010 wrote:** Once you have the answer all you really gotta do is plug it into google or pubmed etc. Look for the cite yourself? I don't think Dr K could answer any questions anymore if he had to provide a cite for everything. It's accumulated knowledge... over years. And I was the one who asked about the blue light exposure during the day and the answer was it's always bad. Instead of looking for a cite I went and tried it out. What happened so far is much needed sleep if I refrain from blue light/artificial light exposure during the day. That's enough of a cite for me.

N=1 YMMV. what works for me, may not work for you, and it may not work for Soul... continue to question and read. search out those journal articles... there are tons out there on artificial light... there's also plenty of blogs people have posted regarding their own experiments w/limiting artificial light - some of which go back to the early -mid 2000's.

you have to figure out what you need to do to make things work... right now I'm not CTing as much as I should - but finding ice on a regular basis has been hard... this weekend we'll fill up the stock tank and do a family CT... I'm looking fwd to CTing in the Winter - b/c it won't require ICE... I'm looking fwd to having lunch out in the courtyard at work sans jacket in the middle of winter - just to keep my body in the zone...

getting to optimal doesn't mean you move at my speed or someone elses... you have to get their via your own journey - Jack is just laying out how you get there... how fast you get there is determined by you and no one else....

I'm looking fwd to having lunch out in the courtyard at work sans jacket in the middle of winter - just to keep my body in the zone...
>

> getting to optimal doesn't mean you move at my speed or someone elses... you have to get their via your own journey - Jack is just laying out how you get there... how fast you get there is determined by you and no one else....

Dr k during the Q & A last night went into how we tend to compare ourselves on the forums but each of us is unique and what works for one might not work for the other. I believe that is true and I also believe that suggestions along my path what has worked for others I can go and try for myself.... you never know. And obviously, if things are still not working, then maybe more labs or a chat with Dr K is in order.

I have the same problem with CT right now and I think most of us do to a degree. My ice melts before I get it home.... I've settled for tap water temp hanging out in the tub, I have quite a lot of frozen plastic bottles that I throw in, I do my head dunking in real icy water. That's as much as I can come up with right now. I can't afford to spend ten or twenty bucks on ice every day.

I guess as I am sitting with last night's answers to a couple of my questions.... yes, everyone has a unique journey but I'm not sure we're in control of the speed of it, honestly. I know I'm not. I know I have to do some things nobody else really needs to.... that's okay. But do I determine the speed of getting to the promised land? Unfortunately, I really don't. It's probably the part that drives me crazy the most. Enjoy the journey, let go of the goal......

that's why N=1 is so important. we don't fit the box. no one does for that matter. once we realize that, and our doctors realize that...we can move fwd for treatment...

### SeaHorse — 2012-08-31T20:18:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-circadian-living.2442/post-48599>

Another really important point that was brought up was how we all finally "see the light" metaphorically speaking. Some people need to read things over and over and then will jump in, some people don't need to know the science they just want to "do," others can only feel comfortable if the information is channelled through "official" voices, i.e. Doctors etc. Last night I asked a question that I knew the answer to because my daughter needed to hear if from Dr. Kruse....(about wearing blue blockers at school). I have talked to her about this a lot, but she sees it as being personal (mom is ruining her life)...hearing it from the outside puts a whole new spin on the information and I believe it has made a difference.

And then, of course, there was the other bit of wisdom that came out of the Q and A about not trying to fix your family and friends if they aren't willing and able....I still feel I have the ability to guide my family in the right direction and expose them to some great information...I know they are the only ones who can make the changes internally.

### ealachan — 2012-09-04T18:03:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-circadian-living.2442/post-49357>

> **Cavemam;49878 wrote:**
>
> I think there is something to this "earthing" thing. I look at it like I have to wear shoes, and generally ones that are somewhat socially acceptable (child stuff, work stuff, not be denied entry stuff) so it's kinda a win win.

Sure, understandable. I should also add my own context: I've pretty much always worked at jobs where the dress-code has been...lax. I've almost always been able to get away with scuffing around in bare feet whenever I wanted to. In fact, at my current job (been here 5 years), people actually pick on me when I HAVE shoes on, because they're so used to seeing me without them!  My mom always had to struggle with me to get me to wear shoes and socks as a kid (even though she hates wearing them, too), and I just never grew out of that. I keep a pair of flip-flops in the car for instances when I need to run errands or if I happen to get pulled over (driving barefoot is illegal in VT), but other than that, I'm barefoot pretty much from the time the mud dries up in May until the snow sticks to the ground in December. Whether it does me any good or not, I don't rightly know...but it feels right, so that's how I roll.

### melrito — 2012-09-06T00:59:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-circadian-living.2442/post-49617>

> **Patty Cakes;50141 wrote:** I only know a little bit about this stuff but yes the earthing/grounding shoes are supposed to do what walking on the earth barefoot does. I used to power walk on the beach at 5am 3x/wk to get grounded. Then I moved away from the beach.  Personally I am mostly worried about the computer since I am on it all day most days. Check out that website ([www.earthing.com](http://http://www.earthing.com)) as I think it explains more than I could.

I'm fairly certain that grounding shoes only work if the shoes can ground - in other words, they work if you are walking on dirt, grass, the beach with them, but there is no way they can ground you if you're walking indoors or on concrete (since the copper insert thing has no way to ground, unless you are using a grounding mat at a desk, for example).

### Stavity — 2012-09-06T18:03:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-circadian-living.2442/post-49759>

> **melrito;50152 wrote:** I'm fairly certain that grounding shoes only work if the shoes can ground - in other words, they work if you are walking on dirt, grass, the beach with them, but there is no way they can ground you if you're walking indoors or on concrete (since the copper insert thing has no way to ground, unless you are using a grounding mat at a desk, for example).

I spoke with someone at Juils, and he said they have tested the sandals and that they are conductive to concrete as long as the concrete is poured over natural surfaces. This would not be the case for carpet, asphalt, or concrete at in a building like Costco.

I did order a pair and can't wait to get them.

### Glen PDQ — 2012-09-06T18:58:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-circadian-living.2442/post-49782>

I'm not sure where this should really be posted. Been eating paleo for the first 3.5 months and epi-paleo for about 1.5 months including seafood and have lost 35-40 pounds without counting calories. I've been putting on the over-the-glasses blu blockers every evening at sundown and have been getting into bed using the sleep mask more or less about 8-9pm the last few evenings. Yesterday morning I woke up after sleeping for about 7 hours when it seemed a flashbulb went off twice with my eyes shut, 1 second apart. This morning I woke up after sleeping for 8.5 hours when the flash bulb went off once with my eyes closed. This is not external; it is an internal event. Could my brain be activating a new way to use my circadian alarm clock? Jack says he purposefully does not tell us everything we will notice on this path. Is this one of those? Anybody else experience something similar?

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-09-07T03:36:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-circadian-living.2442/post-49905>

Depending how sensitive you are, then you should keep it a low brightness even during the day. You should put f.lux on your computer to all day setting even during the day. The only light that's good is the natural sun. Artificial lighting even during the day can be too harsh on the eyes and too stimulating. Also if you're indoors for your job, then taking breaks often and looking at the natural light for 30-60 seconds can help re-orient your eyes.

I got these 'Night Optics' from Nuvatek, so hoping they are on the blue blocking spectrum. The Uvex ones are probably better, but I was at the store and they had these ones so it was convenient. They are yellow tinted.

### Jennie747 — 2012-09-07T12:38:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-circadian-living.2442/post-49945>

Is artificial light in the morning before sunrise as bad for you as it is at night? I tend to be on the computer early in the morning but I do have f-lux on it.

### ealachan — 2012-09-07T15:22:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-circadian-living.2442/post-49964>

> **Jennie747;50490 wrote:** Is artificial light in the morning before sunrise as bad for you as it is at night? I tend to be on the computer early in the morning but I do have f-lux on it.

I asked a similar question a while ago in Ask Jack and was told that, yes, it's still pretty bad for you. I can't get around having to get up before sunrise some days, so I have taken to putting my blueblockers on as soon as I get up those days, and trying to keep the inside lights to a minimum until dawn, at which point I ditch the blueblockers and go outside to get my light exposure. I don't think that's likely optimal, but it's my current work-around.

### prAna303 — 2012-09-07T15:45:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-circadian-living.2442/post-49971>

> **Jennie747;50490 wrote:** Is artificial light in the morning before sunrise as bad for you as it is at night? I tend to be on the computer early in the morning but I do have f-lux on it.

I would say that all artificial lightning is bad in some kind of way. Back in the days i worked with electronic components, the industry used to be worried about the impacts it would have on humans. Today we switch all lightning products to LED or LCD and that should worry us BIG TIME.

### Glen PDQ — 2012-09-07T17:43:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-circadian-living.2442/post-50015>

If I remember correctly, many aquatic food species come to the shallows or shore near the time of the full moon. Maybe there is some built in circadian allowance for the extra light from a full moon at this time. Hunter gatherers probably took advantage of this extra food source. So I have decided its okay to stare at a laptop when there is a full moon if you are a werewolf, but only while standing ankle deep in the surf.

Makes me wonder if this going crazy during the full moon stuff came about because of the hurried activities of the hunter gatherers tripping over each other to get to the shallows to grab easy pickin's. Full moon = Aquatic Ape Wal Mart?

### Destiny — 2012-09-08T14:20:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-circadian-living.2442/post-50227>

Interesting article at: [http://www.webrn-maculardegeneration.com/blue-blockers-6.html](http://http://www.webrn-maculardegeneration.com/blue-blockers-6.html)

From the article:

How and where are these glasses used or worn? Are there different lenses for different uses?

In addition to the blue light we are exposed to outdoors (the sky is blue) we have a growing indoor blue light hazard. Compact Fluorescent Light bulbs (CFL) are replacing incandescent bulbs to save energy and our television, computer screens and digital devices are backlit by fluorescent and LED bulbs that have a high blue spectral emission. So the answer is when you are indoors, wear the indoor lenses and when outdoors wear the outdoor sun lens. Doing so will provide critical protection against blue light as well as significantly enhancing contrast providing a visually soothing effect.

Who would benefit from wearing melanin/OLP glasses?

Everyone!

If someone is interested in getting these new lenses how would they go about getting them for those with and without prescription eye wear?

The easiest way is to go to [www.BluTechLenses.com](http://http://www.BluTechLenses.com) and send an email to [[email redacted]](mailto:[email redacted]) Indicate your name, contact information and current eye doctor if applicable. Eye Solutions will make it easy to obtain both the prescription and non-prescription Blu-Tech lenses for indoor and outdoor use.

### Jennie747 — 2012-09-11T00:43:21+0300

### Primal Dream — 2012-09-19T15:28:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-circadian-living.2442/post-52669>

> **Shijin13;50716 wrote:** I just went in and asked if they could help me. I told them at the end of the day I was getting headaches for the fluorescent lights and they says sure. Blue blocker lenses w/antiglare. The quoted me a price and I jumped on it. Eff them if they look at you weird they don't know Jack (pun intended)

I have decided that I need prescription blue blockers. I have many night meetings and sometimes I have to drive at night. Last spring I bought a pair of over-glasses from Low Blue Lights. they are awkward and don't fit, and they look terrible. So I've gotten a regular pair of blue blockers so I take off my glasses, put them on, and can't see.

So yesterday I went to Lenscrafters. I also saw a post where Dr. K said that he got a pair of Oakleys. So I decided that was the way to go, but the lenses I was offered, I don't think will work (but I ordered them-- can change them in the next couple of days).

If anyone knows Oakley, I ordered the VR28, a reddish one. Second choice is gold iridium, but neither of them did what my blue blockers did. Going on their website, they do have one called 'persimmon' which looks orange. I could probably beg Lenscrafters to see if these could be ordered.

Any other suggestions? I may have ordered an expensive pair of sunglasses that won't work for what I need. My night environments usually aren't fluorescent, but lots of regular light. Plus many times I need my computer during these meetings. I do use f.lux, but as we know that ain't optimal.

I appreciate any help on this!

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-19T15:35:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-circadian-living.2442/post-52671>

I wore my Fashion blue light blocking glasses last night at the monthly La Leche League meeting ... I was styling no one batted an eye that I was a) wearing glasses and b) that they had an orange/red tint to them...

### Primal Dream — 2012-09-19T22:53:11+0300

> **Shijin13;53297 wrote:** I wore my Fashion blue light blocking glasses last night at the monthly La Leche League meeting ... I was styling no one batted an eye that I was a) wearing glasses and b) that they had an orange/red tint to them...
