### nonchalant — 2012-07-20T21:44:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-seafood.2559/post-39754>

Has anyone dehydrated fresh fish? I was thinking about this, with the smaller fish. Am I asking for an odorous nightmare stinking up my house?

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-20T21:53:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-seafood.2559/post-39763>

> **Nonchalant;39941 wrote:** Has anyone dehydrated fresh fish? I was thinking about this, with the smaller fish. Am I asking for an odorous nightmare stinking up my house?

I've done this I think I mentioned it somewhere. Yes, it stinks. I've done it with salmon and smelts. The salmon I eat, the smelts are really dog treats, which I ate some of

The thing with dehydrating fish is that you expose it to warm temps for a long time. So some people would say that that might not be a good idea for PUFAs. The fish still gets cooked, unless you use an Excalibur or other dehydrator that has a precise temperature control option (raw foodie speaking here...). Anything over 118 is dead/cooked, most raw foodies won't go over 115, some won't go over 105. So, the lower the temp, the greater the risk for spoilage of meat/fish which isn't that much of a problem for raw vegans b/c it's all veg after all.

Drying stuff has been part of human's conversation methods for a very long time. Drying in the sun is the best option b/c it helps with preservation, as opposed to using a dehydrator. But then you have bugs that will attack your fish/meat and no control over temps.

# Thread: epi-paleo diet and supplements
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-diet-and-supplements.2560/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Souldanzer — 2012-07-19T21:12:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-diet-and-supplements.2560/post-39434>

I'm tired of taking supplements and even more tired of spending money on them!

So I'm thinking of phasing most of them out and buying more stuff like fish roe etc instead. I've already dropped my D for the summer and am sunbathing/eating whole fish instead. I'm also taking a mix of different choline for my brain, and am not planning on replacing any of those (PS, choline bitartrate, alpha GPC). I'm kinda fond of the alpha GPC but not sure I need it if I suck down raw mackerel heads most days.

I will keep up with my NT support program and co-factors for now (5-htp/tyrosine/cysteine/b-complex/vitamin c). I'll also keep some mg for now since my levels were borderline low on Exatest. Probably 2g of mg threonate a day with loads of topical mg oil from sea water. K2 has to stay for my bones (I think? or is that in fish, too?). DHEA will stay, too. Everything else will go.

Anybody else turning fully epi-paleo? What supplements are really needed if you eat Inger's Cocktail and shellfish and graved lachs and fish roe every day?

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-19T21:54:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-diet-and-supplements.2560/post-39453>

[http://wholehealthsource.blogspot.com/2009/01/seafood-and-vitamin-k2.html](http://http://wholehealthsource.blogspot.com/2009/01/seafood-and-vitamin-k2.html)

> How about K2? Is seafood a good source? At first glance, it doesn't appear to be. **Mackerel is the best source I came across on NutritionData, with one serving delivering 5.6 micrograms of vitamin K. It wasn't specified, but only a portion of that will be vitamin K2 MK-4, with the majority probably coming from K1. Most other types of fish have very low levels of vitamin K.
>
>
>
> But we have to probe deeper. Nutrition information for fish refers to muscle tissue. Muscle is a poor source of K2 in mammals, could that be the case in fish as well? It turns out, the **organs are the best source of K2 MK-4 in fish**, just as they are in mammals. It's most concentrated in the liver, kidneys, heart and gonads. This loosely resembles the situation in mammals, which also retain MK-4 in their kidneys and gonads (along with pancreas, salivary glands, and brain).
>
>
>
> .....
>
>
>
> One thing that is universally relished by traditional groups is fish eggs, which of course develop from the gonads. A number of cultures dried fish eggs, sometimes trading them far into the interior. Although they haven't been analyzed for MK-4 content in modern times, Price found fish eggs to be a rich source of K2**. Speaking of vitamin K2, he said: "its presence is demonstrated readily in the butterfat of milk of mammals, the eggs of fishes and the organs and fats of animals". Unfortunately, Price's assay was not quantitative so we don't have numbers.

### BJK77 — 2012-07-19T22:09:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-diet-and-supplements.2560/post-39458>

I've cut down on my supplements too. I'd love to be able to eliminate them all. The only extras I take at this point are K2, a thyroid glandular (that I plan to kick soon as I increase my seafood), probiotics and my beloved DHEA. It used to be a HUGE list!

I was actually just going to research the content of Vit K in seafood. Thanks for doing the work for me  This is good to know - I think I'll be kicking it next.

ETA: I forgot I started on some COQ10 and resveratrol after my consult with Dr K too. He also suggested I start iodine, but now I'm wondering if it's really necessary if I eat enough seafood ??

Could you change the settings of the blog posts so that we can open a link in a new window? ### caroline — 2014-07-20T10:52:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-anyone-here-taking-provigil-to-improve-their-hpa-axis.2565/post-134977>

I have been thinking about this ...and will take a crack at it.......mostly because I think that it is not magic!

#1 .. Passion ...Dr. K. has a huge passion for life, his day job, his blog, his wife and family and this journey to change medicine and inspire all of us to learn and ask better questions and to become involved in our own healthcare

#2 .... Curiosity ......Dr. K. is infinitely curious about everything - and nothing is sacred

#3 .... Compassion ..... and he listens..... never underestimate the value of listening

#4 ..... Oysters, oysters, oysters and lots of seafood

#5 ....... Lots and lot of C/T to mitigate the damages of his day job and being here and helping us

#6 ..... lots and lots of grounding and sunshine - probably mostly naked!

#7 ....... lots of great water

#8 ....... lots and lots of oxy - in all forms ...... sex, dancing, laughter - you name it - I am positive that he does it
lots! and often!!! This should have been tied to #1 I suspect!

#9 ...... A great work ethic - can't wait to get out of bed in the morning

#10 .... A great play ethic ..... When it is time to play .......Dr. K. lives large ... he squeezes the lemons of life often
and hard

#11 ...... lots of great wine and lots of great food and lots of great music

#12 ..... a circle of friends he trusts and respects and admires and who inspire him to be a better person - and to
expand his boundaries beyond even what he thinks is possible!

#13 .... us ....... I am pretty positive we drive him crazy on a regular basis - but then the lights will go on
for someone and then he must think to himself .... Some of them are actually getting this! and my critics
thought this is an impossible task!

#14 ..... Dr. K. is a regular guy ... but he has been inspired to do better ......

and Dr. K. in turn, is inspiring us to be better and do better....

simple ... but not easy .... but not exactly magic either

What is stopping any one of us from doing all of the above???

we all have a lot of background noise in our life - daily frustrations and disappointments etc ... but we only have one life to live.

So lets all dance at the edge of our life and push our boundaries ...we have nothing to lose and everything to gain!

What have I missed?

As i write this i give you a feeling of my life and how i think but also an idea that to become the new modern human it can mean to ride a horse, fish from a lake and sleep in a tipi outside of the city. Now you can use technology for meaningful things instead of joy and pleasure.

I think it has its applications for occasional use much like melatonin. Use it for jet lag, long drives, etc. Taking it every day would just be chemical dependence, and as Dr.K says, just a shortcut. # Thread: lithium is proportionate to chlorine in the ocean...

**Subforum:** Factor X
**Posts:** 4

### chocolate — 2012-07-20T00:42:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lithium-is-proportionate-to-chlorine-in-the-ocean.2569/post-39492>

[http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/0011747164900026](http://http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/0011747164900026)

> Abstract
>
>
>
> Lithium has been separated from the other ions present in sea water by ion exchange, and determined by flame spectrophotometryâ€”the method shows a variance of 1Â·1%. Thirty samples of sea water from all the oceans and major seas have been analysed, and it was found that their lithium contents were proportional to the chlorinity. The average lithium content of water of chlorinity 19Â·374% was 183 μg/l.

I wonder if being on the swim team and water polo for three years could cause a chlorine overload/lithium deficiency along with a bromide overload/iodine deficiency that completely exacerbated with a chronic sodium dump?

### chocolate — 2012-07-20T00:46:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lithium-is-proportionate-to-chlorine-in-the-ocean.2569/post-39494>

[http://forums.usms.org/showthread.php?t=20299](http://http://forums.usms.org/showthread.php?t=20299) US Masters Swimmers forum

Nope, according to this kid....

> Re: Chlorine Detox Zinger!
>
> How is this supposed to work. Lots of antioxidants?
>
>
>
> Your body maintains chloride levels to very precise levels. If not, you'd be dead, or at least very sick.
>
> Normal chloride serum levels are 98-108 mmol/L. Sorry, I just had a test on electrolytes.
>
>
>
> It's a very beautiful color.

### chocolate — 2012-08-06T20:11:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lithium-is-proportionate-to-chlorine-in-the-ocean.2569/post-43248>

I'm sure that it does do lots of really great things. I think it may just be a mismatch though. The lithocholic acid is only active during vitamin d activity. We have the opposite symptoms of nice vitamin d. Low vitamin d deactivates it, the way I remember. Its kind of like eating fructose.

# Thread: CW medicine isn't all that bad
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cw-medicine-isnt-all-that-bad.2571/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 36
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### primat — 2012-07-20T01:10:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cw-medicine-isnt-all-that-bad.2571/post-39500>

After a miscarriage we got pregnant last october - an easy pregnancy with no major issues.

At 23:15 on the 18th my wife started getting contractions, and after a while we went to the local hospital for a planned water birth. After 21 hours of contractions the cervix still had some hard edges (at 9.5cm) but my wife was totally exhausted. Last resort before C-section was oxytocin IV drops which worked, and one hour later I could cut the cord on a beautiful daughter, after helping (a bit) with the delivery: 21:15. It was a wicked experience to see nature at work; that hairy head coming closer with every push and my wife being totally lost to the most perfectly honed process a mammal could utilize. (And she only had two 500mg paracetamols as pain relief )

However after a too long period of less effective contractions the womb was exhausted and failed to close itself, causing the bleed from detaching and birthing the placenta to not stop. This is not all that uncommon, and at our local hospital everyone routinely gets an oxytocin shot to help close the uterus which then closes the bleed. All newborns are placed on their mothers belly to find their way to the breast which also helps contracting the uterus. Funny the see how she started salivating after smelling the breast

Trouble was that the uterus didn't contract at all, and the bleeding wouldn't stop. There was no outside tear and only minor (class 1) internal ones so it was pretty obvious what had happened. As it turns out my wife had a really low platelet count (trombocytes) so the blood was to thin to clot properly. Luckily she was too fogged to realize the consequences.

She got more and more pale as the sheets and mattresses were soaking in blood and an ER team tried to halt the bleeds. Even a 100 years ago this would likely mean certain death even close to a hospital, but thanks to a transfusion of two litres rhesus negative A I don't have to be a single parent.

And as if this wasn't tough enough our daughter had an oxygen saturation of only 80 at birth, which triggered screening for heart failure, lung failure or infections. The saturation of newborns can be lowered but below 92 is usually a cause for serious concern. As I know from the experience with CT I'm guessing that even lower might not really be a problem in itself, though.

Luckily the saturation increased by a little on itself by the early morning, but still not enough - so she got a comprehensive blood panel and even an x-ray of the lungs although I was quite hesitant to do that. I guess I figured that if my wife had an undiscovered blood content issue that might also have affected the baby. Thankfully there was nothing out of the ordinary to be seen, and by noon today the saturation was up to 96%.

My girls are still at the hospital and I'm home after 48 hours after the whole thing started; more exhausted and completely drained than I though was possible - but I made it. We all made it though the first hours. I'm very thankful for what I have and that I now have the opportunity to create a family with my soulmate and our baby girl. I will remember this for the rest of life when I see them smile.

Writing this is my way of sorting it- now I'm finally going to have a small breakdown, and get ready to focus on what I have, and not what's missing...

Primat - Human Milk 4 Human Babies isn't a milk bank - its a milk share btwn moms. here's their direct link on Facebook for where you're at. [https://www.facebook.com/pages/Human-Milk-for-Human-Babies-Norway/157090461015026](http://https://www.facebook.com/pages/Human-Milk-for-Human-Babies-Norway/157090461015026)

post on there, and let them know you may need help - you'd be surprised how many people will donate - I know ppl in my local area who received milk from Canada, and Mexico - you connect with the donors and then arrange pickup/shipping etc - usually moms will ship at no charge - and may only ask for replacement milk storage bags...

Way better than a milk bank!

additionally - you wifes milk can help her as well, she should drink it!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-22T16:39:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cw-medicine-isnt-all-that-bad.2571/post-40216>

Sorry to hear that........First-line treatment of AML consists primarily of chemotherapy, and is divided into two phases: induction and postremission, also called consolidation therapy. The goal of induction therapy is to achieve a complete remission by reducing the number of leukemic cells to an undetectable level; the goal of consolidation therapy is to eliminate any residual undetectable disease and achieve a cure. Hematopoietic stem cell transplantation is usually considered if induction chemotherapy fails or after a patient relapses, although transplantation is also sometimes used as front-line therapy for patients with high-risk disease. Here is the real irony........that little baby maybe your wife's survival card. Make sure you tell all your wife's relatives to go get tested immediately for their compatibility........she is likely going to need a bone marrow transplant.

second round of chemo with full remission already. hoping for the best, but there are some weird complications with her heart. I've brought a jar of coconut oil to supplement the hospital food since the mct should be beneficial. I managed to set up gluten free diet for her, but it's still very suboptimal. They think we're crazy for taking double dose of cod liver oil on top of 5kIU vit D, too, but refuse to measure vit d levels...  Can't wait to get her home to clean food again...

Sent from my U8860 using Tapatalk 2

### SeaHorse — 2012-09-02T16:33:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cw-medicine-isnt-all-that-bad.2571/post-48929>

Keep up the great care and best of luck with the recovery!

# Thread: Is my testosterone too high (female)?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-my-testosterone-too-high-female.2572/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jennie747 — 2012-07-20T01:22:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-my-testosterone-too-high-female.2572/post-39505>

The lab says 79 with a reference range of 14-76. My Doc wrote "OK" next to it. He didn't test estrogen that time, I don't know why, but he did on the previous labs. 6 months previously testosterone was 55, total estrogen was 166.4 but I can't tell which reference range to use as I had a Mirena IUD in at the time and wasn't menstruating, so I don't know where I was in my cycle. They range from 30-650 depending on the cycle phase. Maybe the testosterone was higher after taking out the Mirena?

From reading I wonder if that's causing my adult acne (I'm 46, had it since teen years) and chin hairs? I don't have excess body hair in other places or a deep voice or apple-shape any of the other symptoms I've read about. The only time my acne has ever improved much is when I was pregnant though, that must mean something?

How does one go about lowering testosterone if this is indeed a problem?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-20T01:23:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-my-testosterone-too-high-female.2572/post-39507>

you need a new doc.........

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-my-testosterone-too-high-female.2572/post-39598>

**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-improved-1-lb-or-1-2-kilo-of-seafood-a-day-challenge-for-30-days.2573/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 91

### Glamazon — 2012-07-20T03:51:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-improved-1-lb-or-1-2-kilo-of-seafood-a-day-challenge-for-30-days.2573/post-39549>

In honor of BG 5:

For 30 days eat either one pound of seafood a day or 1/2 kilo of seafood a day. It doesn't have to be all in one meal.

For me tonight!

SHRIMP drenched in butter!

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-20T03:58:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-improved-1-lb-or-1-2-kilo-of-seafood-a-day-challenge-for-30-days.2573/post-39554>

I'll step the challenge up for those interested - 1lb a day and 50 percent of it raw  Any takers??

### Glamazon — 2012-07-20T04:11:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-improved-1-lb-or-1-2-kilo-of-seafood-a-day-challenge-for-30-days.2573/post-39560>

Any pointers on how to choose a good piece of fish to eat raw?

I had salmon for lunch - cooked half way. I looked up a recipe for scallop ceviche for tomorrow - like juice, plums and tarragon. It's summer so I'm going for it and skipping any honey. ### chocolate — 2012-07-20T05:00:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-improved-1-lb-or-1-2-kilo-of-seafood-a-day-challenge-for-30-days.2573/post-39586>

My daughter was telling me about these little whole dried fish that were setting around the Thai family's house she babysat for as a teen. The little boy used to get up on her lap and try to kiss her. She would just tell him he needed to save his sweet kisses for his mommy (fishies or no). And how lucky he was to have such nice treats, of course. She said the kids always ate them head first. I can't imagine they're any different from anchovies. IDK. I see them in the stores, but I thought they were used in a pestle and mortar with lime, cilantro, and spices for a dressing. That might be good. At least its raw and easy to get your hands on and sounds concentrated. I can't find anchovy sauce or past without bad stuff in it. I know if you feed a rooster fish meal, you have to keep a rake to swat him always. He gets too mean. And he doesn't learn his lesson. He just keeps coming back for more.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-20T05:32:03+0300

that's what I was telling myself feeding my dog raw fish that had been frozen and she still got buggers all the time....

### caroline — 2012-07-20T10:49:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-improved-1-lb-or-1-2-kilo-of-seafood-a-day-challenge-for-30-days.2573/post-39624>

Does ceviche count as raw fish?? theoretically - the lime juice is cooking it I think.

### Destiny — 2012-07-20T14:42:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-improved-1-lb-or-1-2-kilo-of-seafood-a-day-challenge-for-30-days.2573/post-39643>

I did smoked salmon for lunch and cooked shrimps for dinner. I have to admit that there was some gag reflex at the end of eating shrimps. It will take some time to get to anything raw. A memo to myself: Love all seafood.

### kathylu — 2012-07-20T14:46:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-improved-1-lb-or-1-2-kilo-of-seafood-a-day-challenge-for-30-days.2573/post-39644>

> **caroline;39811 wrote:** Does ceviche count as raw fish?? theoretically - the lime juice is cooking it I think.

The lime juice doesn't cook the fish, just gives it a texture similar to lightly cooked fish. That's because the acid in the lime juice precipitates the proteins, changing their structure a bit.

Parasites (as well as viruses and bacteria) are definitely a concern in raw seafood. To eliminate parasites the seafood must be frozen for 7 days at -20 degrees celsius (centigrade). Only 2 hours if you have access to a -70 degree C freezer. To eliminate bacteria and viruses there is not much you can do except cook thoroughly. However, I have been eating raw seafood most of my life (not as often as I would like unfortunately) and I have never gotten sick. Raw oysters are probably the riskiest, they are filled with viruses and vibrio species (bacteria) and I will still eat them raw. Live dangerously!

Maybe we'll evolve by incorporating some new viral sequences into our DNA...

### bigknitwit — 2012-07-20T15:12:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-improved-1-lb-or-1-2-kilo-of-seafood-a-day-challenge-for-30-days.2573/post-39649>

I just ate my first 1/3 pounds of oysters this morning - undercooked. Can't say I loved them like I love the scallops. In fact, I was pretty happy when they were gone. How do you make oysters so they taste good?? I may just add a few to my morning (allready seafood) BAB, and hopefully I'll acquire a taste for them...

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-20T15:15:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-improved-1-lb-or-1-2-kilo-of-seafood-a-day-challenge-for-30-days.2573/post-39650>

### BJK77 — 2012-07-20T15:49:11+0300

### Glamazon — 2012-07-20T15:57:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-improved-1-lb-or-1-2-kilo-of-seafood-a-day-challenge-for-30-days.2573/post-39657>

But for now I think I will still buy my seafood at WF. The seafood was looking a little picked over last night at Hmart. I plan on shrimp for tonight.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-20T19:24:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-improved-1-lb-or-1-2-kilo-of-seafood-a-day-challenge-for-30-days.2573/post-39696>

But for now I think I will still buy my seafood at WF. The seafood was looking a little picked over last night at Hmart. I plan on shrimp for tonight.

I'm on my way there, quite a drive for me. And now you're saying it's picked over! Wonder if maybe they got fresh stuff for the weekend?

### Glamazon — 2012-07-20T22:24:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-improved-1-lb-or-1-2-kilo-of-seafood-a-day-challenge-for-30-days.2573/post-39777>

> **Souldanzer;39883 wrote:** I'm on my way there, quite a drive for me. And now you're saying it's picked over! Wonder if maybe they got fresh stuff for the weekend?

I don't know if that was because I went shopping at 8pm and those remaining fish had been probably laying on ice since the morning but it is worth the look. Just be aware that everybody and their dog goes shopping there on the weekend and the crowds are tremendous. Very international in type of shoppers and the whole family goes. Good thing because that means a good turnover of product. The seafood department starts on the back right of the store past the produce section. The store is HUGE! Huge selection of many types of seafood, live, fresh, frozen, and dried in bulk and enormous packages. Lotsa offal too. Mussels are like 4.99 a pound. Yes, they have fresh mackerel. LOL

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-20T22:28:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-improved-1-lb-or-1-2-kilo-of-seafood-a-day-challenge-for-30-days.2573/post-39780>

> **Glamazon;39964 wrote:** I don't know if that was because I went shopping at 8pm and those remaining fish had been probably laying on ice since the morning but it is worth the look. Just be aware that everybody and their dog goes shopping there on the weekend and the crowds are tremendous. Very international in type of shoppers and the whole family goes. Good thing because that means a good turnover of product. The seafood department starts on the back right of the store past the produce section. The store is HUGE! Huge selection of many types of seafood, live, fresh, frozen, and dried in bulk and enormous packages. Lotsa offal too. Mussels are like 4.99 a pound. Yes, they have fresh mackerel. LOL

I ended up exiting at Broomfield b/c I wasn't feeling very well. POM does have a lot of choice but I've heard Denver is huge. Another day. My freezer is also kinda full and I need to solve the freezer space issue before I really can stock up... like I should if I have a couple hours round trip!

### Glamazon — 2012-07-20T22:35:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-improved-1-lb-or-1-2-kilo-of-seafood-a-day-challenge-for-30-days.2573/post-39784>

> **Souldanzer;39967 wrote:** I ended up exiting at Broomfield b/c I wasn't feeling very well. POM does have a lot of choice but I've heard Denver is huge. Another day. My freezer is also kinda full and I need to solve the freezer space issue before I really can stock up... like I should if I have a couple hours round trip!

I don't know if you take I 25 or I 225 down but you want to exit at I 225 and Parker. It's about three stop lights to the north. You want to take a left at the light at Mickey D's. Mickey D's is on the left north corner of the intersection. The parking lot is huge and it is also on the left. It's an all purpose store so you are going to find clothes, chinese medicines, appliances and other furniture there too. It's basically an asian Walmart.

I also want to add you might want to scope out Federal and also Alameda avenues in central west denver south of 6 th avenue. That's where you will find a lot of mom and pop asian markets as well as some bigger markets that have these butcher cases where you buy things like pig uteruses, live fish, and tea smoked duck.

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-23T14:58:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-improved-1-lb-or-1-2-kilo-of-seafood-a-day-challenge-for-30-days.2573/post-40375>

yesterday we scored blue crabs for a $1.99/LB... unfortunately they'd been picked over - that there were only 6 alive when we got to the store... but we had fish at all 3 meals yesterday. Graved Lachs and eggs for BAB - the graved lachs were amazing!!!! definitely going to make that again! lunch was sardine salad, and dinner was crabs and left over pulled pork!!!

BAB this am was a salmon burger - DS wouldn't eat his eggs until he'd eaten his fill of my salmon burger... then he ate his egg, some of my eggs and some of dh's eggs!

### SeaHorse — 2012-07-23T20:17:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-improved-1-lb-or-1-2-kilo-of-seafood-a-day-challenge-for-30-days.2573/post-40428>

While on vacation found that if I eat freshly steamed mussels and clams I don't have any histamine reaction...so I had some for breakfast this morning back in Vermont...found a local market that brings them in twice a week. Tried some salmon and anchovies in a salad last night when we ate out and had a horrible time...anchovies are off the list for good....along with all the canned stuff.

I have to remember to add some fat as my breakfasts aren't holding as long as the offal/meat ones were.

I envy you guys with your roe...I loved shad roe as a child, but I can't find it here.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-improved-1-lb-or-1-2-kilo-of-seafood-a-day-challenge-for-30-days.2573/post-40814>

If you don't eat it for lunch, it goes in the fridge and it's for dinner" and on and on. . .He went to bed without supper only once, and hunger won out. ### Souldanzer — 2012-07-24T22:52:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-improved-1-lb-or-1-2-kilo-of-seafood-a-day-challenge-for-30-days.2573/post-40827>

_\*puts a toe back into this thread\*_

3/4lb head-on shell-on shrimp fried in CO, few T of coconut milk in tea, and a couple of spoonfuls of ghee for desert, a sip of kombucha for digestion purposes

### freesia — 2012-07-25T00:03:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-improved-1-lb-or-1-2-kilo-of-seafood-a-day-challenge-for-30-days.2573/post-40867>

I'm upgrading! (I've already been eating fish for my BAB 4 days out of 7 and for my evening meal 2 x per week.

I'm driven to do this for a few reasons. One is the recent hints that it may affect gut flora positively. That in itself would have so many flow on effects I can't even imagine right now... Another reason is the well studied DHA effect on Glutathione Pdx and superoxide dismutase...again WOW...and already knowing both those enzymes are compromised in my case means I can't really _not_ take part here.

Third reason, well I'm a dummie... For years, back when I was in better health than I am now, I had several occasions per year when I would need to work to critical deadlines. At these times of extra stress, and only these times, I would get a sudden intense desire for shellfish. I didn't grow up by the sea and shellfish was not a regular part of my diet...I didn't ever particularly like shellfish consciously...but my body knew something back then.

My seafood order arrives tomorrow and then I'm in, 100% for 30 days. (Woot!)

I've got frozen seafood coming to compliment the cod and turbot we already get fresh at the market. ..Whole sardines, Whelks (sea snails), Kina Roe, and some southern bluefin tuna which is frozen extra cold and meant to be eaten raw. I can get oysters locally but haven't been doing it often, so will make that more regular....$$$ depending...

Thanks to those who've already sheared pics (YUM) and recipe ideas

### David — 2012-07-25T00:22:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-improved-1-lb-or-1-2-kilo-of-seafood-a-day-challenge-for-30-days.2573/post-40875>

My 6AM BAB for the last eight days has been a crab omelet with a side of kippers, plus four strips of bacon and Coffee w/butter, stevia, chocolate, CO and cinnamon. Totals about 60 grams of protein and I am never hungry until dinner at 4PM. Dinner is now scallops, salmon, baked fish or a shrimp dish, plus greens, as well as a small West Indies salad (cold crab + sweet onion marinated in oil and lemon juice).

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-25T19:11:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-improved-1-lb-or-1-2-kilo-of-seafood-a-day-challenge-for-30-days.2573/post-41029>

kay' I'm getting my foot back in the door here: BAB this morning within 30min Inger's cocktail with 1oz mackerel, 3oz whole smelts, and a chunk of cod liver with oil, 3/4lb sole fillets cooked in CO, a bit of coco milk and a bit of ghee. Can you say full.

### nonchalant — 2012-07-25T22:17:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-improved-1-lb-or-1-2-kilo-of-seafood-a-day-challenge-for-30-days.2573/post-41069>

Oysters on the half shell. A first for me. I had been concerned earlier about eating oysters in a month without an "R", but I threw caution to the wind. After trying an Inger's Cocktail, it twern't no big deal. Actually, they are good!

### BJK77 — 2012-07-26T03:25:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-improved-1-lb-or-1-2-kilo-of-seafood-a-day-challenge-for-30-days.2573/post-41139>

I am so impressed by all of you raw seafoodistas! I totally planned on joining in with the MHS, but just couldn't do it . . . YET! For now I'll continue on with my cooked seafood and the occasional raw oyster or two. I can feel a difference, so as long as I think it's doing something I'll take things slowly.

Today was salmon for BAB and shrimp with avocado, mushrooms and sauerkraut for dinner followed by a raw oyster for dessert -- YUMMO!

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-26T17:40:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-improved-1-lb-or-1-2-kilo-of-seafood-a-day-challenge-for-30-days.2573/post-41201>

BAB: Inger's Cocktail with 5oz herring head and guts only, chunk of cod liver w/ oil, chlorella and lime juice. 8oz oysters cooked in CO, T of Sweet Fish roe, 2 raw (ceviche) sea scallops, coco milk. Yuck. I can honestly say I didn't like any of it

Dinner last night: 7oz graved lachs with garden greens, spring onions, soaked arame in nori and 2T salmon roe. Some Sea Crunchies. With an Inger's Cocktail as appetizer, lol. That was a 4oz sardine head guts and all, with a few smelts and a cod liver with oil, chlorella and lemon. Coconut milk and a spoon of ghee for dessert. A few almonds, oops.

### Glamazon — 2012-07-22T22:09:19+0300

### nonchalant — 2012-07-23T19:05:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/if-i-had-a-lab.2575/post-40406>

"We know experimentally that CMRO2 (cerebral metabolism) is tied directly to microtubule function and mitochondrial ATP production in the brain. The only way we have as neurosurgeons to uncouple neuronal oxygen consumption from cerebral blood flow and not kill neurons is inducing anesthesia. Today we don’t know precisely how anesthesia works, but Stuart Hameroff, MD (an Anesthesiologist at Univ of AZ) believes he does.... We know that volatile anesthetic gases act by Van deer Waals (London Convention) forces in hydrophobic pockets of select brain proteins to ablate consciousness. It’s called the induction of gamma coherence of neuronal microtubules."

"In AD, the neurons are diseased from protein folding defects but the blood vessels can deliver a normal cerebral perfusion pressure. This means oxygen flow is intact in the AD patients’ brain. The neurons however on the dynamic PET/SPECT scans show major hypo-metabolism and hypo-perfusions in the frontal and temporal lobes. Autoregulation is clearly uncoupled in AD." [http://jackkruse.com/do-food-electrons-impart-a-quantum-effect/](http://http://jackkruse.com/do-food-electrons-impart-a-quantum-effect/)

Perhaps Alzheimers Disease might also be called Anesthesia Disease (AD).

### Glamazon — 2012-07-23T23:42:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/if-i-had-a-lab.2575/post-40478>

> **Nonchalant;40600 wrote:** The two times DH has been under general anesthesia were very disturbing to me. Even after he was released, and he seemed normal, he ended up with zero memory of events for a while. I'd say, from my experience, that is was somewhat similar to Alzheimers.

Could have been the anesthetic agent that was used had induced temporary amnesia. Works in the hippocampus. It is involved with short term memory retention. Nurses rely on ativan IV in the ICU a lot to help keep patients calm but patients end up missing chunks of memory after discharge. Versed is another IV drug that induces temporary amnesia. It is a common agent used for procedures in the hospital.

### nonchalant — 2012-08-14T00:26:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/if-i-had-a-lab.2575/post-44778>

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-14T02:35:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/if-i-had-a-lab.2575/post-44796>

> **Souldanzer;40964 wrote:** children don't develop a sense that they have their own mind until the age of around 7 (iirc)... before that they feel as part of their mother or primary caretaker

not sure I buy this at all.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-14T03:17:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/if-i-had-a-lab.2575/post-44814>

> **Jack Kruse;45108 wrote:** not sure I buy this at all.

somebody taught me that in grad school..... child psychology. Can't remember the name but he was pretty popular. It was related to the concept of understanding that there is "I" vs "you" or "someone else" and that the thinking/mind of the "I" is entirely separate from the mind of the other person. Basically, the guy's point was that children do not develop an understanding of what "mind" means/entails until they are about 7 years old. Before they experience their own mind as part of their mother's mind, they don't get that every person has their own and it is private.

Not married to this theory. Only wrote a term paper about it at some point in my life.

Thinking about how we think in different terms, the one's that were layed out in BG5..... at what point in life is there "enough" DHA to make the thinking process work enough to have a sense of one's own mind as separate from someone elses? I know when my brain went really bad right after TBI/TIA I was having a lot of dissociation symptoms.... things dissolving into.... nothing in particular... more like particles.... had to think hard before I could name and understand a tree (or car, or person, or anything) from the brown and green particles that my eyes perceived. Things didn't appear so separate anymore. Ok, now you're going to think my brain is lost for good  most days when I look at things now they appear complete with form and function in my mind again. It was pretty cool in retrospect, if scary. I still get it a little bit from time to time.

# Thread: Why Gray Hair Can Be a Good Thing
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-gray-hair-can-be-a-good-thing.2581/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-07-20T07:31:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-gray-hair-can-be-a-good-thing.2581/post-39611>

> Would you rather have fiery red locks or hair of gray? For some animals, a grizzled look may be a sign of health, new research finds.
>
>
>
> Wild boars with reddish coats have more cell damage than more mundanely colored wild pigs, Spanish researchers report in the July/August issue of the journal Physiological and Biochemical Zoology. The reason seems to be that the production of red pigment uses up an antioxidant that could otherwise be chasing down the free radicals that damage cells.
>
>
>
> The findings could offer a tantalizing hint as to why red seems to be a costly color. In humans, studies have found that red hair and red pigments, or melanins, in skin are linked to higher rates of cancer.
> [http://news.yahoo.com/why-gray-hair-good-thing-222553435.html](http://http://news.yahoo.com/why-gray-hair-good-thing-222553435.html)
>
> This suggests that certain colorations may have important consequences for wild boars," GalvÃ¡n said. "Pheomelanin responsible for chestnut colorations may make animals more susceptible to oxidative damage."
>
>
>
> Meanwhile, gray hair, which results from an absence of melanin, seemed to be a mark of good health in wild boars.
>
>
>
> "As with human hair, wild boars show hair graying all across their body fur," GalvÃ¡n said. "But we found that boars showing hair graying were actually those in prime condition and with the lowest levels of oxidative damage. Far from being a sign of age-related decline, hair graying seems to indicate good condition in wild boars."
>
>
>
> In contrast, other experiments have suggested that gray hair in other animals is caused by cellular stress. Damage to pigment-producing cells called melanocytes may explain why our roots turn gray with age.
>
>
>
> If pheomelanin has such a negative effect, the researchers aren't sure why it evolved in the first place. One hypothesis, GalvÃ¡n said, has to do with a chemical called cysteine. Cysteine is part of GSH and can be toxic in large quantities. Pheomelanin may have evolved to get rid of cysteine, with the loss of GSH as an unfortunate side effect.

# Thread: Kelp noodles?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kelp-noodles.2584/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-07-20T15:34:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kelp-noodles.2584/post-39652>

So I found these bags of kelp noodles at Whole foods. I'm having a hard time figuring out if they are a processed food and have no nutrients in them anymore or if they are a great way to get some seafood. I want to branch out in this area and I really think my kids would eat these.

Thoughts?

### kathylu — 2012-07-20T16:09:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kelp-noodles.2584/post-39659>

If they are high carb or mixed with grains I would stay away from them. I feed my son toasted seaweed sheets. You can get them in any asian grocery. Salty and tasty...we eat eat this instead of chips. It will take a while to get kids used to it though. Kids tend to be pretty intolerant of new foods (at least mine is). You can also use the sheets as a wrap for veggies or meat.

### janagram — 2012-07-21T00:56:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kelp-noodles.2584/post-39833>

I bought some and just ate a very small serv. with almond butter and water stirred up. They were fine. But, crunchy. Not expected. Once I realized that they were crunchy I was ok with it.

### [email redacted] — 2012-07-21T04:42:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kelp-noodles.2584/post-39900>

It SEEMS like a highly proccessed food - Ive been wondering if there was any nutritional goodness left in that - its super duper with Alfredo!

### [email redacted] — 2012-07-21T05:02:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kelp-noodles.2584/post-39908>

In searching for processing info on kelp noodles - came across this! Sounds promising.

[http://www.webnat.com/proddetail.asp?prod=seas](http://http://www.webnat.com/proddetail.asp?prod=seas)

### [email redacted] — 2012-07-21T16:30:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kelp-noodles.2584/post-39988>

Good find Cavemam! I will definitely be trying that even though it is twice the cost of kelp noodles. But then again, more nutricious food usually is more expensive.

### [email redacted] — 2012-07-21T16:49:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kelp-noodles.2584/post-39990>

Yea, I thought it would be fun for doing "italian" with the family. . .like a spaghetti and meatballs, or seafood alfredo.

### [email redacted] — 2012-07-21T16:53:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kelp-noodles.2584/post-39991>

> **kjgraffin;40175 wrote:** Good find Cavemam! I will definitely be trying that even though it is twice the cost of kelp noodles. But then again, more nutricious food usually is more expensive.

Did you order from them? We have a lot of crunchy granola stores around here - so I'm going to check them out before I order and see if anybody carries it. It is quite expensive, so I can't see it becoming a staple. . .but right now we have a local place that makes almond meal pasta that is super clean (almonds and eggs) and that's become a big favorite for "fast food" as it only takes 45 seconds to cook. I limit it . . maybe 2X a month. It's about the same price. But at least the kelp stuff seems like it might actually be GOOD for you.

I'm picturing something like a cold asian soba noodles salad with it.

### AmyJ — 2012-07-21T17:28:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kelp-noodles.2584/post-40000>

Thanks for the Sea Spaghetti link. I think it might make a good salad. I found this too, sometimes I'd like a thickener (seaafood gumbo) so I'm going to try it.

[http://www.amazon.com/Fresh-Whole-Irish-Raw-Wildcrafted/dp/B002EUZEVY/ref=pd\_sbs\_hpc\_5](http://http://www.amazon.com/Fresh-Whole-Irish-Raw-Wildcrafted/dp/B002EUZEVY/ref=pd_sbs_hpc_5)

I went to a Japanese restaurant the other day and had sashimi and a side of seaweed salad. Won't do the salad again even though it was fabulous. They used roasted sesame seed oil, not very much, it was very light. Very tasty. Would really like to be able to buy that type of seaweed, to make a salad with. Then I can control what else is in it. They have seaweed salad in the grocery store deli, (full of crap oil) where do they get it??

I love seaweed. Working to make it my veggie, period.

### janagram — 2012-07-21T23:11:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kelp-noodles.2584/post-40083>

Oh, my, that sea spaghetti looks really good! Thanks, and hope I can find it.

### Lyndra — 2012-07-22T01:01:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kelp-noodles.2584/post-40099>

> **Cavemam;40178 wrote:** Did you order from them? We have a lot of crunchy granola stores around here - so I'm going to check them out before I order and see if anybody carries it. It is quite expensive, so I can't see it becoming a staple. . .but right now we have a local place that makes almond meal pasta that is super clean (almonds and eggs) and that's become a big favorite for "fast food" as it only takes 45 seconds to cook. I limit it . . maybe 2X a month. It's about the same price. But at least the kelp stuff seems like it might actually be GOOD for you.
>
>
>
> I'm picturing something like a cold asian soba noodles salad with it.

Let me know if you find a local source - that looks tasty! Where do you go for almond meal pasta?

### [email redacted] — 2012-07-22T01:56:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kelp-noodles.2584/post-40120>

> **Lyndra;40286 wrote:** Let me know if you find a local source - that looks tasty! Where do you go for almond meal pasta?

# Thread: Hot and Had to Lower AC - Is It a Sign?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hot-and-had-to-lower-ac-is-it-a-sign.2586/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Huck — 2012-07-20T17:40:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hot-and-had-to-lower-ac-is-it-a-sign.2586/post-39677>

Last evening after dinner (salmon), I couldn't seem to cool down. AC was set where it normally is, but I still felt hot. In order to feel comfortable in bed, I had to lower AC 2 degrees cooler than normal. Then I had very vivid dreams most of the night.

Is this possibly a sign of becoming leptin sensitive? After 9 months, it would be nice!

### vkiernan — 2012-07-20T20:23:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hot-and-had-to-lower-ac-is-it-a-sign.2586/post-39710>

Hey Huck, I sure hope so for your sake. For me it's over a year now and nope on the LS but not stopping now....

### Huck — 2012-07-20T20:33:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hot-and-had-to-lower-ac-is-it-a-sign.2586/post-39714>

> **vkiernan;39897 wrote:** Hey Huck, I sure hope so for your sake. For me it's over a year now and nope on the LS but not stopping now....

I went from rarely eating seafood to eating it almost every day. If I start getting some energy, I will know things are finally starting to work.

### vkiernan — 2012-07-20T20:37:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hot-and-had-to-lower-ac-is-it-a-sign.2586/post-39717>

> **Huck;39901 wrote:** I went from rarely eating seafood to eating it almost every day. If I start getting some energy, I will know things are finally starting to work.

Sounds like a good plan. I am eating a lot more too. I need to buy more things like scallops though to get my shellfish in. I believe thosed are shellfish, yes? The dh likes it too so that helps.

### Huck — 2012-07-20T21:52:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hot-and-had-to-lower-ac-is-it-a-sign.2586/post-39762>

> **vkiernan;39904 wrote:** Sounds like a good plan. I am eating a lot more too. I need to buy more things like scallops though to get my shellfish in. I believe thosed are shellfish, yes? The dh likes it too so that helps.

I just had 2 lbs of cod, 2 lbs of flounder, and 1 lb of scallops delivered to my door. Actually, he pulled up in the driveway and I had to go out to his truck. It worked out to about $10/lb. Going to have to eat more beef to get the cost down!

Actually, it is the scallops that drove up the price - $17.95/lb. Cod and flounder was only $7.95.

Yes, scallops are shellfish.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Any one notice an Increase in Smell sensitivity?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-one-notice-an-increase-in-smell-sensitivity.2587/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 25
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Shijin13 — 2012-07-20T20:16:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-one-notice-an-increase-in-smell-sensitivity.2587/post-39705>

Ok... I've got a super sniffer! Such that I can't be around people who wear cologne/perfume etc! I can smell something rotting from a mile away, and poopie diapers - I smell them before the Havoc is done!

Well, since the 30DSC - my smeller has been going off. I was at the pool swimming for lunch - just puddling, and suddenly there was this overwhelming dank earthy smell - like rain was coming - it was over cast. then suddenly a down pour occurred.

I'm also smelling the neighbors cigarette smoke come through the cracks in the house (we have a post WWII house w/OEM windows/doors etc - so its not an environmentally bubble wrapped house like they are these days)

any one else suddenly having these extra sensory things occur?

### vkiernan — 2012-07-20T20:19:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-one-notice-an-increase-in-smell-sensitivity.2587/post-39707>

I remember that being a sign of LS. I had it for a week and then it went away. Was good and bad when it happened. Some smells you just don't need to be aware of.

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-20T20:23:05+0300

### TerrierMom — 2012-07-21T01:41:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-one-notice-an-increase-in-smell-sensitivity.2587/post-39848>

Ditto to nonchalant. DH and I have always had super sniffers, but I feel as though I'm getting even more so lately. I've read in the 'literature' long time ago that women's sense of smell is at its peak during ovulation, so chart it and see if there's anything to it for you, I know it is true for me.

But ditto do the smelling things from waaaaay far away. When I go the movies or church, I use my nose to find a place to sit next to people who are already present. No smoking, b.o., or excess perfume, I will sit down. Otherwise, if I sit in an empty area and an offender sits down near me, we literally have to get up and move.

### TerrierMom — 2012-07-21T07:34:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-one-notice-an-increase-in-smell-sensitivity.2587/post-39949>

She gets grain free kibble and all the good protein and seafood we do. Lots of pro - and pre-biotics in the kibble too (canine caviar holistic brand). Haven't gone raw yet but considering! She is 10 and struggled with "allergies" that make her bite or scratch herself raw for at least the last 5 years.

Finally got tired of the steroids and antibiotics she was on multiple times yearly after her liver enzymes went off the charts..almost lost her. Going au natural and cleaning up her diet, if I can do it she can do it!! The "comfy cone" plus a tshirt on her helps keep her from biting and scratching but would like her to heal already.

Was going to ask you what you fed your pups, SD.... Can dogs have leaky gut too!?!? Or just humans?

### TerrierMom — 2012-07-21T07:37:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-one-notice-an-increase-in-smell-sensitivity.2587/post-39950>

Btw.... When her liver enzymes went wacky guess what the vet told me to feed her?

OMEGA 3s and deep ocean whitefish!!!

Which is the kibble we feed her plus salmon and herring wet food and the fish we eat too......!!! (this was a year before I found JK so somehow I'm not surprised to hear seafood helps with liver and LR and inflammation....!)

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-21T08:23:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-one-notice-an-increase-in-smell-sensitivity.2587/post-39954>

I'm not sure what it's called for dogs but there is a very common thing going on..... gas and skin issues. If you got the two or even just one of them it points to food issues. The kibble looks good, high protein which is a bit hard to find, and no canola oil. Dogs can be allergic to fish. They can be allergic to anything. Like pea protein, which is not a good protein but suddenly all kibble manufacturers switched over to it b/c what they used before went up in price.

I'd recommend an elimination diet. If you're open to raw that would maybe be best for her. I'm happy to coach you if that's what you want to do. She's a bit older so this might be a process.

I'd also recommend you start supplementing her with zinc. Or you feed her oysters on a regular basis. You watch her paw pads. Are they rough and maybe cracked? They should be soft and supple.

What kind of treats does she get? You watch everything that goes into her tummy. That one treat every few days can start it all over.

Definitely switch her to a different kibble without either the pea protein or the fish. Preferable neither for now. She's not well on this so you need to try something different. O3 is great but if she's allergic to fish no good. Does she get diarrhea a lot?

Do you use a furminator on her? Terrible for the skin. What kind of shampoo do you use?

Skin issues take months to go away. It took my foster pup 6 months to clear it all.

### nonchalant — 2012-07-21T08:39:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-one-notice-an-increase-in-smell-sensitivity.2587/post-39956>

Ever since we found her, I've been feeding my kitty canned fish (breakfast) and raw liver (dinner). I have been impressed with her personality and how flexible her back and neck are, even for a cat. And she's never coughed up a hairball! I feed her quite a bit of liver, since it's so cheap. She will eat her fill, hang around for about 30 minutes, and then eat some more. Then with her tummy bulging, she'll go back outside. Sometimes she refuses or eats very little of the fish, so that just goes to the chickens. She knows shes getting liver later. I like how she's not getting tubby like our previous pets.

Perhaps I should look at the asian grocery store for some cheap fresh seafood.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-21T14:33:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-one-notice-an-increase-in-smell-sensitivity.2587/post-39970>

Just FYI, cats eat the same ratios as dogs for raw food, that means 80 percent muscle meat, 10 percent bones, 10 percent organs (5 percent liver and 5 percent other secreting organs like kidney, spleen). I'm not saying you should do this, it's just the accepted way of feeding cats and dogs raw by calculating percentages of a whole animal prey. Feeding that much liver might result in too much vitamin A /iron and a mineral deficiency.

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-21T15:18:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-one-notice-an-increase-in-smell-sensitivity.2587/post-39980>

Soul. You remind me oft his guy who works w/my local Lab-rescue. He's the go to guy that fosters all the sick & problem dogs. Depending on the dog he either feeds dry grain free like taste of the wild or Evo. Or a straight raw food diet. He usually has a rescue for an average of 14mths before they're ready to be adopted and he works with the adopters so they know how to feed the dog.

Our lab is on Evo. And we give raw meat scraps like heart trimmings, ckn backs & bones. Today at his evening feed he's going to get some fish broth & ghee left over from last nights dinner/this am's BAB.

He got chicken back this am and boy was he 1 happy dog!!!

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-21T15:31:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-one-notice-an-increase-in-smell-sensitivity.2587/post-39981>

I haven't met a dog who doesn't love coconut oil and milk. And it's so good for them. Especially with skin issues.... I'd give my foster frozen kongs full of coco milk - doggie popsicle!

Some dogs can handle raw some can't. Once you got a senior dog and they've eaten kibble all their life it can be difficult. But some take well to it and have another "youth" from it.

I like helping out problem doggies  You change their experience, you change their reality, you change their behavior. But there's limits of what I will take on b/c I don't want to stress over it. But I do love training dogs.

Maybe we need a dog thread instead of hijacking this one

# Thread: 5 week breakthru
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/5-week-breakthru.2588/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### msclman — 2012-07-20T20:42:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/5-week-breakthru.2588/post-39718>

So i started on armour 5 weeks ago. 1 grain a day adding one additional grain per week. I am on 5 grains a day now. i was having a very hard time losing weight. i also did ct 4 times a week the first week, then 3 times, then 2 , now 1 time a week (due to a technical problem w my tub). my body temp at the beginning was all over the map but never getting over 98.2 really and id wake up at 96.5

so just a couple days ago i started losing weight again, my body temp when i get up is 97.6 - .8 and climbs to 98.7 during the day. I feel hot all the time now (even when my BT is 98 I am almost sweating).

My question is do I add another grain next week???

### vkiernan — 2012-07-20T20:45:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/5-week-breakthru.2588/post-39722>

> **msclman;39905 wrote:** So i started on armour 5 weeks ago. 1 grain a day adding one additional grain per week. I am on 5 grains a day now. i was having a very hard time losing weight. i also did ct 4 times a week the first week, then 3 times, then 2 , now 1 time a week (due to a technical problem w my tub). my body temp at the beginning was all over the map but never getting over 98.2 really and id wake up at 96.5

>
>

>
>
>
> My question is do I add another grain next week???

Holy grains. I wouldn't add anymore until you stabilize. This doesn't happen overnight. You also should be tested before you add more but need to let your system settle a bit. Does your doc have you on this much?

### vkiernan — 2012-07-20T21:03:55+0300

### msclman — 2012-07-20T21:11:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/5-week-breakthru.2588/post-39744>

I will stop once i feel heavy palpitations, anxiety, racing pulse.

### bigknitwit — 2012-07-20T22:06:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/5-week-breakthru.2588/post-39770>

I'm at 5 grains too - just upped to 5 three days ago. As if that's not enough, I'm also taking 50mcg synthroid. My weight has been steadily dropping since slowly increasing the Armour from 1.5 grains (and implementing the leptin rx strictly). Now I'm within 2 pounds of my ideal weight (I'm 142 pounds). Unfortunately my symptoms are anything but ideal. Chronic low blood pressure, and higher heartrate, and feeling dizzy. It's difficult because these are the adrenal symptoms I get when I don't have enough thyroid hormone, but I'm starting to get nervous that it's because I have too much and somewhere I crossed the ideal point without a change in symptoms. It gets worse as I take more time between dose increases. Now doctor wants me to drop to 4.5 grains (she never approved the last increase) + 50mcg synthroid, and sit where it levels. I don't know how I'm going to survive a few more weeks feeling the way I do right now... Sorry for the post hijack.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-20T22:17:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/5-week-breakthru.2588/post-39775>

> **vkiernan;39912 wrote:** Really, that much is ok and more??? How does he know when to back off of it then?

when he sees no effect at all (Increasing RT3) or he gets side effects from excess thyroid hormone......(excesssive T3)

### bigknitwit — 2012-07-21T01:21:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/5-week-breakthru.2588/post-39841>

> **msclman;39987 wrote:** why the synthroid too? armour has natural t4 in it.

Because my labs at 4.5 grains alone showed barely in range for total T4 (5.4, range 5.3-12.3) (free T4 was 1.25 range 0.9 - 1.7), and my Free T3 was mid range (3.6 range 2.3-4.2), and I'm still symptomatic. I think pork thyroid has more T3 proportional to T4 compared to human thyroid (not 100% sure on this though), so increasing the T4 can balance that out. For someone like me whose gland is destroyed by long term auto-immune disease I'm looking to completely replace what my gland would have done. I'm wondering if I'm not absorbing the T4 very well from the Armour. I seem to be getting the T3 pretty well though, so I don't know why it'd be different. I'm nervous that the extra T4 is going to increase my reverse T3 ratio (which has improved from 6 to 15 over the past fwe months). But this would also show me that things are still not right in my body causing my brain to increase reverse T3 levels. At that point, I give in and schedule a consult! For now I'm going to hold everything a few weeks and re-test... How I wish I had my gland back operational!!

### bigknitwit — 2012-07-21T03:26:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/5-week-breakthru.2588/post-39875>

My T3s are okay, but I think that's all I'm absorbing from the Armour. T4 is a storage hormone, but without anything in storage to convert to T3, you go hypoT pretty quickly. I think that's what's happening to me, I'm living off the T3 I take. I (was) mainly symptomatic of hypoT in the morning (low blood pressure, high bpm - adrenals trying to compensate and failing). And as the day would go on (and I'd take the Armour), things would improve. Now, I'm symptomatic all the time, just different symptoms in the morning then the afternoon and evenings (blood pressure comes up a little (100/60), and pulse drops to normal ranges, but I feel dizzy like there's not enough blood going to the brain. You only convert T4 to rev T3 if your body is trying to shut your metabolism down, due to HPA issues, or what not (hazy on the "what-nots" here).

I did a salivary cortisol back in February. It wasn't great, but wasn't horrendous compared to others I've done a few years ago. My numbers were 17 first thing, in range the next 2 (forget exactly where), and high in the evening. I wonder if I messed up the evening one though, because I did it at 10pm before going to bed (and lots of bright lights! Didn't know of JK back then). I take DHEA and pregnenelone per labs drawn, but I don't feel them improving anything for me.

I could be wrong but getting off Armour and onto T3 only would help with symptoms, but not address the underlying issue causing your body to want to shunt everything to reverse T3. That is the real goal in my mind. With high RT3, figuring out what's driving it.

> **msclman;40053 wrote:** dr k would have some great insight for you on this i would definitely give him a call.
>
>
>
> your free t4 looks pretty good. your t3 is ok. did u do a salivary cortisol? I could be way off on this but I thought t4 was really just a storage moecule and that t3 was where the activity was. taking more t4 will convert to rt3 like you said. my rt3 is high as well (b4 armour so I am wondering where the hell it is now). I would love to get compounded t3 and get off the armour but my doc said no.

### kathylu — 2012-07-21T06:02:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water.2589/post-39920>

Get a reverse osmosis filter. They can cost as little as $250 and if you are handy at all, installation is pretty simple. The filter removes essentially all minerals, so re-mineralize with a pinch of Celtic sea salt.

# Thread: Parasite Thread

**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 41
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-07-20T20:56:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parasite-thread.2590/post-39732>

Let's talk parasites. I adore, neh, LOVE sushi. Now I know why my baby worked out so well after such a rough start.

The thing is, do I need to shell out on "sushi grade" foods. . .lets start talking about reality with risks on parasites. And, what KIND of parasites might one get.

If we are people who have leaky guts by design, to assimilate all the seafood mojo, what is the biological process of what happens to a parasite we eat? Does stomach acid get it? Is it simply a one or two day "sick tummy"?

Please put studies, stories, info here!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-20T21:14:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parasite-thread.2590/post-39747>

I'm very interested in this, too. I see in Ask Jack he says you need to 'assess you risk'... what risk are we assessing? Leaky gut equals less risk or more? Other risks? Immunity? I also saw a comment elsewhere that freezing raw food for 2 weeks eliminates the parasites. Is this risk reduction that then allows us the full benefit of raw?

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-20T21:26:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parasite-thread.2590/post-39751>

ughh... just lost my post..

I'm speaking as some one who's had parasites - and gotten rid of them

I'd eat raw seafood any day over, under cooked pork. I have also eaten steak tartar (dh made it from our grass-fed/finished beef)

that said I'd eat raw seafood from only 2 sushi restaurants in the area, and I'd also buy my fish from a fish monger I can trust... not every one behind the fish counter knows what the eff they're doing...

### [email redacted] — 2012-07-20T21:43:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parasite-thread.2590/post-39753>

> **KiwiLauren;39934 wrote:** I'm very interested in this, too. I see in Ask Jack he says you need to 'assess you risk'... what risk are we assessing? Leaky gut equals less risk or more? Other risks? Immunity? I also saw a comment elsewhere that freezing raw food for 2 weeks eliminates the parasites. Is this risk reduction that then allows us the full benefit of raw?

My understanding is it's X number of hours at X temp.. .and the temps have to be in the negative - so not something you can do at home. Commercial application. Restaurants serving raw sushi are required to do this freezing thing, so you CANT buy "fresh" sushi really.

At least that's what I think, sometimes I just make stuff up.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-20T21:44:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parasite-thread.2590/post-39755>

Parasites are going to be in any fish no matter where you buy it. It's part of being a fish as far as I know. It's just as much a problem in meat but most people cook it before they eat it.

There's parasites everywhere.... parasites are part of life and I'm pretty sure humanity didn't evolve without them. I think it's mostly a matter of who is stronger - the host or the parasite. Which will then depend on the strength of your immune system. Not all parasites want to damage you, you're their host after all.

I know dealing with this with dogs that raw meat and fish isn't the only place you can get parasites. I froze everything for my dog (raw prey model diet) at first and she was still getting parasites. Well, she was sticking her nose everywhere!! Including other dog's toilet spots. It's similar for humans, especially if you have kids.

I look at it from this perspective - transitional apes didn't cook their food to death b/c of fear of parasites. So why would I. I take precautions like freezing for a couple of weeks. But compared to what is going on with my brain, this risk is minimal to me. There's also natural ways (herbs etc) that can work as a preventive. I'd say this is a case of damned if you do, damned if you don't. If it's that much of a concern to you, I suggest you keep cooking as Dr K said and eat more of it.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-20T21:46:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parasite-thread.2590/post-39756>

> **KiwiLauren;39934 wrote:** I'm very interested in this, too. I see in Ask Jack he says you need to 'assess you risk'... what risk are we assessing? Leaky gut equals less risk or more? Other risks? Immunity? I also saw a comment elsewhere that freezing raw food for 2 weeks eliminates the parasites. Is this risk reduction that then allows us the full benefit of raw?

Nope, it reduces the number of parasites but doesn't necessarily eliminate it. And as Cavemam said, what you can do at home in your own freezer is limited. That's different if you buy frozen seafood instead.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-20T23:01:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parasite-thread.2590/post-39795>

> **Souldanzer;39943 wrote:** Nope, it reduces the number of parasites but doesn't necessarily eliminate it. And as Cavemam said, what you can do at home in your own freezer is limited. That's different if you buy frozen seafood instead.

OK, well I've done a mixture of both frozen and fresh fish heads/fish for my smoothies, as well as a lot of fresh raw roe, and so far, so good. I suppose my main question was how to assess one's risk. It seems Dr K isn't saying your damned either way, he's saying if you have low risk, raw is optimal. So is risk=immunity? If so, how does one calculate that?

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-20T23:16:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parasite-thread.2590/post-39803>

So instead we decided to re-build her immune system and de-stress her. I used some natural herbs (anti-parasitic), some chinese immunity mushrooms, and a few other things to rebuild her gut flora. That along with prey model took care of them.

### JanSz — 2012-07-21T02:31:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parasite-thread.2590/post-39859>

I used to have a fishing boat.

I would go from Point Pleasant NJ and fish tuna in Mud Hole.

Got wasabi, soy sauce and beer. When we got tuna, some of it we would eat right there, still hot from the fight.

Some tuna would have parasites in their bellies. Rather big, look almost like good size ants.

I have not seen anything within solid meat.

I like raw tuna. Cooked tastes bad (to me).

\-------

Fresh water sunnies, I left them filetted in refrigerator, couple hrs latter you could see red hairs (worms) wiggling their way thru.

I have not eaten them.

### kathylu — 2012-07-21T04:21:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parasite-thread.2590/post-39886>

Ok folks, I hate to interrupt the reverie, but most fish (unless you catch it yourself) is frozen ...and can be frozen for as long as two years before it is sold. That's unusual though. The US has laws in place saying that all fish to be eaten raw much be frozen to kill parasites first.

[http://www.nytimes.com/2004/04/08/nyregion/sushi-fresh-from-the-deep-the-deep-freeze.html?src=pm](http://http://www.nytimes.com/2004/04/08/nyregion/sushi-fresh-from-the-deep-the-deep-freeze.html?src=pm)

However, if fish is actually labeled "fresh", you should assume it is and either cook it thoroughly or freeze it at -20 degrees C (about -6 degrees F) for 7 days before eating it raw.

Parasites can be harmful or occasionally helpful. Read this article on helminth (worms) therapy for allergies for an example of a "good" infection.

[http://autoimmunetherapies.com/candidate\_diseases\_for\_helminthic\_therapy\_or\_worm\_therapy/allergies\_helminthic\_therapy.html](http://http://autoimmunetherapies.com/candidate_diseases_for_helminthic_therapy_or_worm_therapy/allergies_helminthic_therapy.html)

A good explanation from wikipedia:

Eating raw fish

Differential symptoms of parasite infection by raw fish: Clonorchis sinensis (a trematode/fluke), Anisakis (a nematode/roundworm) and Diphyllobothrium a (cestode/tapeworm),\[37\] all have gastrointestinal, but otherwise distinct, symptoms.\[38\]\[39\]\[40\]\[41\]

Though not a health concern in thoroughly cooked fish, parasites are a concern when human consumers eat raw or lightly preserved fish such as sashimi, sushi, ceviche, and gravlax. The popularity of such raw fish dishes makes it important for consumers to be aware of this risk. Raw fish should be frozen to an internal temperature of −20Â°C (−4Â°F) for at least 7 days to kill parasites. It is important to be aware that home freezers may not be cold enough to kill parasites.\[42\]\[43\]

Traditionally, fish that live all or part of their lives in fresh water were considered unsuitable for sashimi due to the possibility of parasites (see Sashimi article). Parasitic infections from freshwater fish are a serious problem in some parts of the world, particularly Southeast Asia. Fish that spend part of their life cycle in salt water, like salmon, can also be a problem. A study in Seattle, Washington showed that 100% of wild salmon had roundworm larvae capable of infecting people. In the same study farm raised salmon did not have any roundworm larvae.\[44\]

Parasite infection by raw fish is rare in the developed world (fewer than 40\[37\] cases per year in the U.S.), and involves mainly three kinds of parasites: Clonorchis sinensis (a trematode/fluke), Anisakis (a nematode/roundworm) and Diphyllobothrium (a cestode/tapeworm).\[37\] Infection risk of anisakis is particularly higher in fishes which may live in a river such as salmon (shake) in Salmonidae, mackerel (saba). Such parasite infections can generally be avoided by boiling, burning, preserving in salt or vinegar, or freezing overnight. Even Japanese people never eat raw salmon and ikura, and even if they seem raw, these foods are not raw but are frozen overnight to prevent infections from parasites, particularly anisakis.

The bottom line: Eating raw fish can be risky. Since most fish is frozen within minutes of being caught, you are probably fine eating raw fish from a commercial source. However, you can reduce or eliminate the risk by cooking thoroughly or freezing at -20 C for at least 7 days.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-21T04:24:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parasite-thread.2590/post-39888>

I asked WFM once and they said all their "fresh" fish has been frozen.... they defrost it to put it in the show case.

> Differential symptoms of parasite infection by raw fish: Clonorchis sinensis (a trematode/fluke), Anisakis (a nematode/roundworm) and Diphyllobothrium a (cestode/tapeworm),\[37\] all have gastrointestinal, but otherwise distinct, symptoms.\[38\]\[39\]\[40\]\[41\]
>
>
>
> Though not a health concern in thoroughly cooked fish, parasites are a concern when human consumers eat raw or lightly preserved fish such as sashimi, sushi, ceviche, and gravlax. The popularity of such raw fish dishes makes it important for consumers to be aware of this risk. Raw fish should be frozen to an internal temperature of −20Â°C (−4Â°F) for at least 7 days to kill parasites. It is important to be aware that home freezers may not be cold enough to kill parasites.\[42\]\[43\]
>
>
>
> Traditionally, fish that live all or part of their lives in fresh water were considered unsuitable for sashimi due to the possibility of parasites (see Sashimi article). Parasitic infections from freshwater fish are a serious problem in some parts of the world, particularly Southeast Asia. Fish that spend part of their life cycle in salt water, like salmon, can also be a problem. A study in Seattle, Washington showed that 100% of wild salmon had roundworm larvae capable of infecting people. In the same study farm raised salmon did not have any roundworm larvae.\[44\]
>
>
>
> Parasite infection by raw fish is rare in the developed world (fewer than 40\[37\] cases per year in the U.S.), and involves mainly three kinds of parasites: Clonorchis sinensis (a trematode/fluke), Anisakis (a nematode/roundworm) and Diphyllobothrium (a cestode/tapeworm).\[37\] Infection risk of anisakis is particularly higher in fishes which may live in a river such as salmon (shake) in Salmonidae, mackerel (saba). Such parasite infections can generally be avoided by boiling, burning, preserving in salt or vinegar, or freezing overnight. Even Japanese people never eat raw salmon and ikura, and even if they seem raw, these foods are not raw but are frozen overnight to prevent infections from parasites, particularly anisakis.

### MonteD — 2012-07-21T19:09:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parasite-thread.2590/post-40017>

[http://www.naturalnews.com/027333\_oregano\_parasites.html](http://http://www.naturalnews.com/027333_oregano_parasites.html)

Oil of Oregano is awesome. Get in capsule form. Take it periodically. I have people buying this all the time for trips to Mexico.....

> WHAT YOU CAN DO TO MINIMIZE PARASITES: Get in the habit of using garlic, oregano, and coconut, which are naturally anti-parasitic and anti-microbial. Eat onions, figs, and pumpkin seeds to create an uninhabitable environment for worms. Eat a diet rich in protein and vitamins. As absorption in the intestines is impaired by infestations, the body requires large amounts of nutrients found in fresh organic vegetables, almonds, and blackberries. Take a daily high quality probiotics supplement (friendly bacteria, such as those found in fermented foods).

Edit: I cook all my fish or eat it raw when it's been previously frozen....then again I also eat 1 pound per day...Jack said it was our choice. Less fish and raw or more fish and cooked...
