### Inger — 2012-07-27T10:11:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parasite-thread.2590/post-41470>

I opened some fresh mackerels yesterday that was full of worms. UH. Made me read about the risks once more.

This made me feel better though;

> For the worm, humans are a dead-end host. Anisakis and Pseudoterranova larvae cannot survive in humans, and will eventually die. In some cases, the infection will resolve with only symptomatic treatment

[http:/http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anisakis](http://http:/http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anisakis)

I am willing to take some abdominal discomfort, knowing they will eventually die. I must have gotten the worms already though, I am sure, and no symptoms so far. Worms are very common in mackerel.. now I saw it myself, huh. I put the head of the wormy fishes in the refridgerator though.. I guess I will freeze them for a while and then use to smoothies. But most likely my fridge is not cold enough anyway to kill parasites but just for my nerves. I ate some wermut from he garden too, it is good aganst parasites too. Maybe I will take some garlic too.. haha..

But this is what is hard about recommendig fisheadsmoothies to others.. sure you can get parasites. No question.

I am not too scared of them though.. as they have not hurt me as of yet. But I sure try to take care of myself and someoine who does not might suffer harder if infected? My brother and his wife just tried the smoothie too.. help. Hope they follow the other things too what I told, CT, epi-paleo-diet, natural circadian rythmus etc. They want it very much though. I am quite sure they want suffer then. That is, I guess you have to see it all in concept. For one eating a SAD diet and living aganst every rules in the natur, parasites might be a bad idea... for the other ones.. it might be maybe good? I wonder if?

I did ate high meat that had gotten some nasty bacteria in it once.. years ago. I had forgotten to air it for weeks, but still wanted to eat a few pieces. They do tasted a tiny bit odd to me but I just swallowed. I got really sick in my stomach, womited once and had diarrhea once, and got really high fever. All in the same evening. I got a little scared I must say, but went to bed.. Next morning I waked up.. healthy, totally healthy. I felt so great!!! Better that before even! This made me think, maybe it did some good after all?

Just my odd speculations...

### Inger — 2012-08-15T10:07:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parasite-thread.2590/post-44997>

Update on parasites...

I have had some issues last days. It started the night after I had consumed raw prefrozen scallops and sardines. The sardines were consumed whole, head, guts and all. The meat I ate for sashimi, very delicious. Guts and heads as a smoothie. But yes.. in the night after I got minor abdominal discomfort and then the runs.. I had to visit my bathroom many times that night. Loose stools.. that was all. Strange was, I got a strange herpes too at the same time on my lip, or three of them to be honest!!! One bigger and two small, all on the underlip. I used to get herpes all the time but now I have not had one for many months! And this was a strange kind of herpes.. hurtful and "deep" kind of! It is almost healed up now, very fast and well, but I tell it does not look so pretty.. how good I do not need to go to work until friday! It will be gone by then.

But yes.. I wonder what strange virus I catched.

One of the sardines looked strange on the surface, had strange "sores" or some kind of eksema on its skin. I though it would not matter and ate it up all, it tasted good to me. I wonder if it was some virus, and I got it too...

After the first night with diarrhea I felt fine but my stool were so strange... very pale in color and fatty. Maybe for two days. Then I got loose stools again, today too. But I feel good otherwise so I guess this will pass. Strange because I almost never get this kind of things lately, I am very regular too.

Have not seen any worms though.

I wonder if it could also be something they have used on the sardines, that does not get mentioned on the package? It says there are only sardines, whole, frozen.. but I wonder. Sardines are a very fatty fish and it would not keep long even frozen.. I wonder if they do something with it. I thought the meat had a strange consistense.. almost like cold-smoked. I have never had fresh sardines so I cannot compare but sure it felt different as any other fish I have ever had. It might be that sardines are like that though..

I wonder. I just have a total aversion aganst sardines now.. I just cannot eat the rest of them.. I feel nauseated just thinking about it.. uh.

I guess I need to be careful with prepackaged frozen fish.. if they add some stuff to it.

Or it was the sick fish with the eksema. Or it was the scallops.

Argh.. that is the problem with N1. experiments, how can you really know, if there were many factors involved? Sure I could try it again with the sardines but.. I just have such a strong aversion right now it will be hard! And I do not want these gut-problems to continue eather.. I am afraid I will poop out all my good bakterias fast.. HA. I have no high meat eather now but I ate some raw sauerkraut every day last days, took some colostrum-milk too, I can buy it here frozen.

I tend to believe (or I want to..) that it was the sick fish. Some virus thatI got. I would wish it was a virus that is similar to the one the Okinavians got that lives so long today.. LOL.

I will do the sardines again when my gut is healthy, I will. I need to know this.

And next time I will test the scallops and the sardines separately, and eat no sick fishes if there are any.

### kathylu — 2012-08-15T15:02:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parasite-thread.2590/post-45007>

Inger, I think it is a good idea to throw away any fish that has visible lesions or worms. It is really risky to eat them...that is visible proof of an active infection. If you don't want to throw them away, then cook the fish thoroughly. Also, it is just wrong to assume that the disease agent only affects fish and not humans. There are hundreds, if not thousands of bacteria, viruses and parasites that can infect marine life and us. Here are a few:

[http://www.fao.org/docrep/003/X9199E/X9199E03.htm](http://http://www.fao.org/docrep/003/X9199E/X9199E03.htm)

Just my opinion, but please be safe...some of these pathogens can actually kill you, especially the viral and bacterial agents.

### Inger — 2012-08-16T13:13:58+0300

Uh Kathylu do not scare me.. ! That sounds scary though.

Anyway, I did it, no way back. Now, suprise, I am totally healthy again! Tummy feels fine, stools are fine too! So experiment went well.

I better do not open that link. You know what, people are so scared of too many things today IMO. Many do not eat wild berries at all anymore without cooking, because media is writing about a scary worm that you can get infected with.. crazy. A worm that wanders into your brain and kills you slowly..

No way I will stop eating my beloved berries in the forest because of that, then I will rather die. What a life is that, to live like in prison..? Not able to eat what nature gives for free because too scared of bakterias.. viruses.. parasites.. No, I just can and will not live like that.

But it is my choise and I do not preach it to anyone never, I know it has risks and I take them willingly. I need to be free.

A friend of family never eat anything from outside that has been eaten on from a bird or snail or anything. He is very scared of viruses. He throw away all his self grown cucumbers because some animals had munched a bit on them.. just a tiny edge.

That is what media does here to people. Scary. Do not know what happends in the USA...

I must have got thousands of different virsues by now because I love the strawberries than have been munched on by some birds, they always taste the best to me! I am still alive and well.. makes me wonder...

But.. Kathylu I promise I will not eat sick looking fishes anymore though. That is quite esay to avoid.

### Birdy — 2012-07-21T00:57:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stuffed-clamshells.2593/post-39835>

I bought some clams, and they were pretty large. I saved the shells. My mom used to make stuffed shells, but all the recipes I could find include bread crumbs. Does anyone here have an OK recipe for any type of seafood to put in the shells?

### kathylu — 2012-07-21T06:04:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stuffed-clamshells.2593/post-39923>

Replace the bread crumbs with an egg and a little coconut flour for binding, then follow your favorite recipe.

### ealachan — 2012-07-21T12:10:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stuffed-clamshells.2593/post-39960>

lots of times when a recipe calls for bread crumbs for filler, i use very finely minced mushrooms instead. I've always had good luck with it, and it's tasty and good for you, too!

### [email redacted] — 2012-07-21T21:03:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stuffed-clamshells.2593/post-40050>

We do two things. . . just a little almond flour instead - or my favorite if motivated. . take a bunch of whole macadamia nuts and a little frozen kerrygold with garlic and herbs, and food process them together into a "savory" crust/filler.

### Birdy — 2012-07-23T02:37:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stuffed-clamshells.2593/post-40321>

thanks, I will try them. But I'll have to try this on the next batch of shells, because SOMEONE threw the shells out. I had to fish them out of the trash, but these babies are just going into the garden.

# Thread: Energy Saving Bulbs May Harm Your Skin Read more at http://medicaldaily.com/news/2012
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/energy-saving-bulbs-may-harm-your-skin-read-more-at-http-medicaldaily-com-news-2012.2595/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-07-21T03:16:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/energy-saving-bulbs-may-harm-your-skin-read-more-at-http-medicaldaily-com-news-2012.2595/post-39871>

[http://medicaldaily.com/news/20120720/10984/cfl-incandescent-bulbs-energy-saving-lamps.htm](http://http://medicaldaily.com/news/20120720/10984/cfl-incandescent-bulbs-energy-saving-lamps.htm)

> A new study says that energy efficient compact fluorescent light bulbs that benefit the environment may be harmful to your skin.
>
>
>
> For the study, the researchers collected CFL bulbs purchased from different locations across Suffolk and Nassau counties in New York State and measured the amount of UV emissions and the integrity of each bulbâ€™s phosphor coatings.
>
> energy saving bulbs
>
>
>
>
>
> They found that all CFL bulbs that they studied had cracks on their phosphorus coatings and they had significant levels of Ultraviolet C and Ultraviolet A.
>
>
>
> Then the researchers tested the effect of exposure of these bulbs on healthy human skin cells. Researchers repeated tests using incandescent light bulbs (of the same intensity).
>
>
>
> In another part of the study they introduced Titanium dioxide (TiO2) nanoparticles, used in personal care products, to see how its presence along with CFL or incandescent bulbs affects the human skin cells. Titanium dioxide is added to products because it absorbs UV radiations.
>
> "Our study revealed that the response of healthy skin cells to UV emitted from CFL bulbs is consistent with damage from ultraviolet radiation. Skin cell damage was further enhanced when low dosages of TiO2 nanoparticles were introduced to the skin cells prior to exposure," said Miriam Rafailovich, professor of materials science and engineering at Stony Brook University and an author of the study.
>
>
>
> Researchers found that, whether Titanium dioxide was present or not, incandescent light bulb of the same intensity had no effect on skin cells.
>
>
>
> Researchers say that an additional glass covers might reduce some of the harmful effects of CFL bulbs.
>
>
>
>
>
> "Despite their large energy savings, consumers should be careful when using compact fluorescent light bulbs. Our research shows that it is best to avoid using them at close distances and that they are safest when placed behind an additional glass cover," said Rafailovich.
>
> Read more at [http://medicaldaily.com/news/20120720/10984/cfl-incandescent-bulbs-energy-saving-lamps.htm#IGCrXXuqIGK51pcA.99](http://http://medicaldaily.com/news/20120720/10984/cfl-incandescent-bulbs-energy-saving-lamps.htm#IGCrXXuqIGK51pcA.99)

Another reason not to use artificial light.

### PrimalPam — 2012-07-22T16:29:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/teeth-thread-add-all-things-teeth.2600/post-40213>

Glad you opened up a thread about teeth, Dr. K. I had been wanting to put this in "Ask Jack", but I'll ask here:

I have palatal tori, and it's never been fully understoody why. I had 4 bi's extracted for ortho (I'm 50; it was the early 70's) and had extreme crowding of 23, 24, 25, 26. Did the tori develop due to "pushing" all the teeth together rather than expanding the palate? Or was the crowded palate due to maternal poor nutrition (and smoking, which she did through 3 pregnancies)? Or is it due to the asthma, poor breathing I experienced as a child, or was that a symptom of all the other AI issues I had due to the poor nutrition?

The tori has been relatively unchanged in size or texture since teen years; in fact, I can't remember not having it. But I have no idea when it developed.

### bigknitwit — 2012-07-31T17:54:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/teeth-thread-add-all-things-teeth.2600/post-42055>

I have great teeth - no fillings at age 36, although I did have braces. My husband was not so lucky and has had plenty of dental work. Interesting thing is that my older son has had lots of cavities, and his younger brother (2 years younger) has only had 1. His jaw formation is great (looks like no braces needed), whereas older son has already had orthodontal work, and will likely need a little more (he's 11). Did I mention younger son is celiac (both are gluten free), and has hashi's? And I tandem nursed when he was born... Also, younger son has a real innate sense of foods that he should not eat. Turns out a lot of the foods he refuses he is allergic to (we did intradermal allergy testing) - grapes, strawberries, apples, most fruits, some veggies, etc. And over the past few years he's really taken to eating _seafood_, almost daily, whereas older son did not, and does not feel his sensitivities the way younger one does.

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-24T21:13:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/teeth-thread-add-all-things-teeth.2600/post-60019>

I took my youngest (3.5) to our WAPF dentist this morning for sedation dentistry. She has cavities in all 4 of her back molars (which is devastating to me, but regardless, they need to be filled). She is very "willful," for lack of a better word. When she wants something (or when she doesn't), there is no level of redirection, that will change her perspective. She responds well by learning from her own past experiences (ie. learning the hard way).

I had already spent time explaining to her what was going to happen at the dentist's office. She took the tranquilizing med willingly, and it did sedate her to a certain extent, but not enough. She was happy to sit in the chair, and still chattering on about the blanket, and the TV, and the room, etc! With my older son (who is very much like her - moreso really!), the same med put him pretty much out, and he had no memory of the procedure. She did great for the novacaine shots, but then got very unsettled about the strange feeling of a numb mouth. Unsettled turned to panic after a minute or so of the block in her mouth to hold it open. The dentist had already started to drill one of the cavities, so it took 3 of us keep her still so she could finish the one cavity. Never mind the other ones...

It was not a good morning, to say the least. We will go back in 3 weeks, with a slightly higher dose of tranquilizer, and I'm crossing my fingers it will be enough. In the mean time, I would like to establish a one mile radius around my home - call it the "sugar-free" zone. Given my children eat a pretty solid diet, but that their father has bad teeth, what else can I do for them that might reduce the cavity load? It really is devastating to me (I have none), and disheartening to know their teeth are still rotting...

### MamaGrok — 2012-10-24T21:27:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/teeth-thread-add-all-things-teeth.2600/post-60021>

As I recall the conversation with the dentist going, some children will respond to certain sedatives by calming, some by excitation, and some by no notable change. And you can't predict which ones wiill be which. When we were still in the days of many cavities in every child, I just went with the papoose board, with me by their sides, calming them & crying along with them.

When we switched to a WAPF diet, though, all that went away. All the cavities stopped. But it's very possible that it stopped so quickly for my children b/c they may have just happened to have had guts that were ready, or some such. BTW, no one in either side of the family had bad teeth as children, but all of my children did. Heredity plays little role, IMO. Epigenetics is everything. I think that continued cavities in the face of plenty of butter, fCLO, liver, and other K2 sources might imply a malabsorption problem, especially with fat?

BTW, the worse my kids' teeth got, the more I had to eliminate grains, concentrated fruit sources, etc. Don't know if you've done that yet, but nutrient dense is super important. IMO, sugar's biggest contribution to cavities is simply taking the place of the things you need to be eating.

She was happy to sit in the chair, and still chattering on about the blanket, and the TV, and the room, etc! With my older son (who is very much like her - moreso really!), the same med put him pretty much out, and he had no memory of the procedure. The dentist had already started to drill one of the cavities, so it took 3 of us keep her still so she could finish the one cavity. Never mind the other ones...
>

In the mean time, I would like to establish a one mile radius around my home - call it the "sugar-free" zone. Given my children eat a pretty solid diet, but that their father has bad teeth, what else can I do for them that might reduce the cavity load? It really is devastating to me (I have none), and disheartening to know their teeth are still rotting...

Why do you need to get them filled?

I am in Finland so it might be different here.. but the dentist of my sister child found lots of cavities in her teeth (I think it was around 10 or so!) when she was around 5 yo or so. He said he will not fill them yet but let her try to suck on xylitol bonbons after every meal. They was her milch teeth that was affected so he did not see too much need to fill them if they caused no pain and the hole was not too huge. Heidi (the name of the girl) sucked on xylitol pills for about half a year and went to the dentist again. All holes were "hardened" as the dentist said and needed no threatment anymore.

I myself have some huge holes according to my dentist (about two years ago.. I was not by the dentist after). I am not filling them. I live right instead. I have had no troubles whatsoever. I believe as long as the tooth is alive there can happend a recovery, a "hardening" of the karies. I want to have as little poison in my mouth as possible. And the fillings are not really healthy, also not the stuff they put on to make the filling hold on the tooth.

If it were my child I would let her suck on xylitol often and give her proper foods and not worry anymore... she will get new, strong teeths coming out soon!

### MamaGrok — 2012-10-25T17:35:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/teeth-thread-add-all-things-teeth.2600/post-60142>

For me, even 1/2 svg wheat/day was enough to keep me in health disarray. Everything changed when I dropped that half serving.

For my kids, fCLO saved their teeth. I couldn't have done it without it.

My second child was one of those resist-to-the-end kind. That's why I didn't go with sedative for him. If he was going to thrash & scream the whole time, anyway, I didn't want the additional risk of the drug.

It's a whole load of terribly, agonizing decisions for a parent to make, and I don't envy anyone currently in that position.

BTW, a British study found no benefit whatsoever to filling deciduous teeth if the child was not in pain. My kids all have loads of half-decayed, but totally frozen and no longer decaying or hurting teeth that will stay in their mouths till they fall out. Again, a very personal & agonizing decision that I have no intention of judging any other parent on. It's HARD to go against a care provider, period, and you have no guarantee of being right either way.

### CoolSwede — 2014-06-09T13:56:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/teeth-thread-add-all-things-teeth.2600/post-130262>

So, here I am bumping an old thread for my own good:

Three years ago I managed to heal an old cavity in one of my upper teeth which had been lingering for a number of years. This was when I had started vit D supplementation. I was quite happy with the bewildered look my dentist gave me when he saw that I had managed to heal that cavity using low-carb and vit D!

Fost forward until two months ago, when my dental hygienist noticed that the cavity was back with a vengeance. Of course I was very disappointed believing that that very cavity was a thing of the past. I talked to my dentist and he agreed that we could wait and see. Unluckily now the tooth has started aching slightly so: enter panic!

Do you have any suggestions about how I should do? Of course I would like to heal the cavity naturally as I did three years ago, but at the same time, I do not want to risk needing a root canal.

Supplements:

1\. Calcium 450 mg daily (due to very low whole blood calcium in latest lab).
2\. Magnesium 300 mg daily (midrange whole blood mag in latest lab).
3\. Thorne K2-MK4 5 mg (5 drops) daily. Started 2 months ago.
4\. Vitamin D3. 7500 IU daily. (205 nmol/l in latest lab).

Remarks:

1\. My whole blood vit A is in the middle of the range. Should I supplement cod liver oil or retinol anyway?
2\. My HDL has dropped to a scaringly low 42. Has my gut becoming a sieve affected my oral health?

### ObstreperousPolarBear — 2018-01-14T01:02:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/teeth-thread-add-all-things-teeth.2600/post-232479>

Found this thread after bad news from my dentist - I have a few cavities and lots of gum recession. This thread has mostly focused on cavities, but I am sure that gum recession is part of the same process - low redox potential.

I've read this post: [https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/quantum-dentistry-jack-kruse](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/quantum-dentistry-jack-kruse)
So I'm sure that my #1 priority has to be the Redox Rx, and that this should clear up the recession AND cavities.

I was wondering if some fellow mitochondriacs would weigh-in on the following questions. I think I'm on the right track, but wanted to throw these ideas out there and see what other people think/have to add to them.
1\. Lots of dentists have told me that gum recession is due to tooth grinding. If weight-bearing exercise in the earth's magnetic field results in strengthening/increasing bone density, then why would tooth grinding cause generalized gum recession and then bone loss (my jaw bone loss is at 20% already, but I'm only 29)? I suspect tooth grinding is the effect of a quantum process, but is being labeled the cause by modern dentistry.
2\. If our teeth are designed to fit together, how does a disrupted bite alter the quantum mechanics of our teeth, resulting in dental disease of all kinds?
3\. Is gum recession the result of decreased DC current through the tooth, the gum, and the bone beneath it?
4\. If yes, then is the nerve of the tooth meant to energize the perineural water to maintain gum health?
5\. Every dentist I've spoken to has said that gum cannot regenerate - what if the problem is the tooth and its quantum health, rather than the gum, and the gum isn't regenerating because the tooth isn't strong enough? Could we cure gum disease by improving the DC electric current of the tooth?
6\. While we're busy straightening everyone's teeth, are we missing the actual, quantum disease process? Could our modern epidemic of tooth decay be worsened by our obsession with cosmetic dentistry?

Looking forward to your thoughts and advice, fellow mitochondriacs!

# Thread: My thought process sucks fasting ends weight up

**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet

### Raiken3712 — 2012-07-21T17:42:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-thought-process-sucks-fasting-ends-weight-up.2601/post-40002>

I think I'm ok now...I realize its temporary...its not a big deal, but I just broke my fast yesterday and I went up 3.6 pounds. At first I was annoyed and it bothered me....I guess my thoughts aren't that bad...after all it didn't really take me that long to realize this is just temporary..I knew before that when I started eating again my weight would go up a bit. Also some of it might be used for repaired muscle...it doesn't have to be fat gain. Though I am overweight and someone posted a charge that overweight people...or obese can't remember which tend to use more muscle fiber to fuel when fasting not all muscle but more than a fit person would while fasting.

I'm so hyped and anxious about getting to my low point again...I know its silly and yet I still have a momentary craziness over it. My low point before I started eating crap was 244...I thought it was 249 but looked at my Calorie Count logs and found the 244.6. I started at 285 went to 244 and regained to 277. I'm now back to 251.6 from 248.

I'm probably a bit OCD I know it would probably help to just ignore the scale...but I'm just so used to it. I like seeing the numbers go down. Oh well I"ll get to my goal eventually. On that note any thoughts on whats optimal weight for a 6'1" Male with a large frame? I want to be somewhat strong and athletic....probably not shooting for elite...probably just want to live a long life with lots of energy. Much depends on body fat %.

Thanks as always

### Raiken3712 — 2012-07-21T17:45:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-thought-process-sucks-fasting-ends-weight-up.2601/post-40003>

The other thing I hoped wouldn't occur is my blood sugar going up as much as it has.....my fbg is 101...high again while I was fasting it was 53-65. Ideal is 70-80...maybe I'm eating too much protein? Or maybe its just my eating last night....I got off work late and ate at around 9. I stayed up till around 12-1 to let my body deal with the food before I went to sleep. I guess I did eat a few steak fries...like maybe 7-8...never eat fries anymore.

I just hoped my FBG after fasting might lower a bit..I guess even with 101 its lower than 110 it was before and it might be the late eating...I'll test tomorrow and see I guess.

### PrimalPam — 2012-07-21T17:59:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-thought-process-sucks-fasting-ends-weight-up.2601/post-40004>

Raiken, I wouldn't even test BG after eating like that. Then again, we aren't supposed to eat like that to be optimal.

Remember: Eat your last meal before 7 pm. Get to bed around 9-10 pm.

Re-read the blog about Circadian Biology (I'm sorry, I can't remember which one). The 3 most important hours in human biology are sleep from 12 a.m. to 3 a.m., where all of your cells are being repaired. If you do not sleep during these hours, your body is waking up to bits of unrepaired cells trying to allow you to function.

Whenever I have a night where I can't sleep during those hours (so rare these days, only happens when I diss the protocol), I lay awake thinking....dammit...I'm missing my most important circadian hours!!!

Eating some steak fries won't kill you....but these days, before I eat something like that, I think, "Is it optimal?"

And when you do eat/sleep that way for a day, the important thing is, the very next time you eat, make it optimal. Wake up to BP coffee, whole eggs, salmon. Do your CT that day (not sure if you're doing it, but it's become part of my almost-daily routine). And get to sleep by 10.

I've found that it's become habit to eat this way, do the CT, buy fresh seafood, etc.

Also, get off the scale. You'll know when you've lost weight: your pants will be looser. These days, I only weigh when I KNOW I've lost, i.e. my clothes are so much looser, I know the scale will show a loss. Otherwise, we're just adding to our stress, no? Remember, scales are a modern invention.

### AKMan — 2012-07-21T20:17:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-thought-process-sucks-fasting-ends-weight-up.2601/post-40034>

Let me tell you about my experiences with weight fluctuations and fasting. I'm 46, m, 5'11, 165lbs. When I was in the 190's - 200's, my weight would fluctuate wildly. I could go a week at 190, then all of a sudden be up tp 210 and take weeks to get it off--all eating primally a-la Mark Sisson. Since i've lost nearly all excess body fat, my weight is very stable. My normal waking weight is 165 and my normal before-bed weight is 169. I can eat little or lots on a given day and my body regulates my weight right in that range. I have about 6 months in that range, so this isn't a one week observation.

I started weekly, one-day fasts a couple months ago. I stop eating usually after dinner on Wednesday and resume on lunch Friday. This is an approximate 42 hr fast.

Going in my weight will be 169. Next morning, 165. Next night (after 24 hr fasting--just water) my weight will be about 164. Next morning (after 36 hrs fasted) weight will be about 163. After 42 hours 162.

Then I eat normally for 2 meals, after which my weight will be 165, next morning still 165, next night back to my original 169.

In table form:

Hour 1 - 169 (after last meal)

Hour 12 - 165 (upon waking)

Hour 24 - 164

Hour 36 - 163

Hour 42 - 162

Hour 48 - 165 (after last meal)

Hour 60 - 165 (upon waking)

Hour 72 - 169 (after last meal)

### Raiken3712 — 2012-07-21T20:32:05+0300

I heard Tropical Traditions was fermented which is good for many things but wasn't as good for Coconut oil for some reason...I'm not absolutely sure about the truth of it though so maybe Tropical Traditions isn't that much worse....or maybe its better lol.

### PrimalPam — 2012-07-21T21:02:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-thought-process-sucks-fasting-ends-weight-up.2601/post-40048>

Re: BP coffee. I actually buy my own coffee at the grocery store and make my own version of BP coffee (CO & turmeric).

Also remember the guidelines: eat only 2-3 times a day, no snacking. For me, a huge BAB holds me till about 2, when I feel like a small meal (a couple of spoonfuls of Nutiva CO Manna & a few bites of smoked salmon, for example). Dinner around 5, which is a piece of seafood, veggies, Ghee. But being done eating by 7 pm, no matter what, is really important. If I know I'm not coming home till really late, I pack something to eat around 5, so that when I get home, I go straight to bed.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-07-21T21:23:01+0300

### bigknitwit — 2012-07-21T23:52:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-thought-process-sucks-fasting-ends-weight-up.2601/post-40089>

I experimented with fasting starting early last fall. Prior to that I had been low carb eating and doing P90X to maintain my weight, but it was no longer working (I was gaining slowly). I did a 5.5 day fast and lost 14 pounds I was ecstatic! Then the weight started coming back on, real quick. 3 pounds the first day, 2.5 the next, etc. A week or so later I decided it was time to start yet another fast, to drop some of the weight I had lost. I also started mixing in intermittent fast (eating only after 4:30pm). I cycled between intermittent fasting and 24-48 hour fasts all fall. It was the only way to even think about maintaining my new found low weight. Finallly, on Dec 24th, I decided to take a break from all this fasting/intermittent fasting, and I gained 8 pounds over 6 days. I was horrified. But after so many months of such restricted eating, I couldn't keep up the fasting routine (in case it's not obvious, I hadn't heard of JK at this point).

I gained still another 11 pounds in 5 weeks. I tried to eat "less", it didn't matter. The weight kept coming, with no slowing in sight. I finally decided it was time to get some testing done. I also did an elimination diet (down to turkey only for 24 hours) to figure out which foods were causing the explosive weight gains. It took some time but I found I could only eat fresh meats, fish, veggies, limited fruit, and coconut oil. I also found leptin RX around this time, and implemented it pretty strictly (what choice did I have? unlimited weight gain?). Welll, slowly, I lost all the weight - 20 pounds. It took about 4.5 months, and I still have a long way to go healthwise. But even today, I am still ecstatic that I can maintain, even lose weight without fasting!! Obviously I was leptin resistant, among other things.

All this written to say that I completely understand the frustration of regaining the weight lost in a fast.

# Thread: Menstrual cycles and things female :)
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/menstrual-cycles-and-things-female.2602/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### PrimalPam — 2012-07-21T18:07:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/menstrual-cycles-and-things-female.2602/post-40006>

Did my menstrual cycle return exactly 28 days after stopping the pill? Really?

Went to a new OB/GYN (from paleophysiciansnetwork.com), who took me off the pill (I'm 50) and wants me to be off for 3 months before doing hormone testing.

In the past, when I've gone off the pill, it's taken my body a few months to regulate, with spotting, then heavy periods and mind-bending cramps. Last OB/GYN wanted to hysterectomize (lol) me to get rid of the "parts I don't need anymore". I imagined one of those 1920's B&W horror movies where a mad doctor with a huge butcher knife chased me around the OR, and practically left in my gown.

These days, doing CT 5 x/week @ 50-55F, eating Paleo w/seafood almost daily, BP coffee (turmeric, CO) every day, CO in everything, sleeping great, mentally clearer. And 28 days to the DAY that I last took my pill, my period returned...with ZERO cramps. I woke up this morning to it, actually mad at myself that I hadn't even counted the days and worried about my sheets (sorry, graphic). I figured it'd be several weeks, with spotting, etc., but no, this is the actual thing.

I feel like I'm 15 again.

### NWgirl — 2012-07-21T20:05:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/menstrual-cycles-and-things-female.2602/post-40029>

Wow that is amazing! Gives me hope that my normal cycle will return as I get healthier. I'm only 43 and I'm already in "pre-menopause". But come to think of it, since I started LR I haven't had ONE hot flash at all. Hmmm...!

Kim

### PrimalPam — 2012-07-21T20:23:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/menstrual-cycles-and-things-female.2602/post-40035>

Kim, I was having hot flashes all through my mid to late 40's. One of the symptoms which led my ex-OB/GYN to recommend a hysterectomy. The funny thing is, I forgot all about the hot flashes till you just wrote about it. Haven't had them in so long now (since full-on Paleo & LR).

### NWgirl — 2012-07-21T20:28:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/menstrual-cycles-and-things-female.2602/post-40036>

I think we're defying aging! For me it's starting more on the inside though. So I'm getting healthier from the inside out.

# Thread: You're Already Eating Bugs!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/youre-already-eating-bugs.2606/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 23
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### kathylu — 2012-07-21T20:48:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/youre-already-eating-bugs.2606/post-40041>

How closely are shrimps, lobsters and crabs related to insects? Really closely. All are arthropods.

"Arthropods are animals with segmented bodies and six or more jointed legs. They are the largest animal group on Earth. In fact, more than three out of four of all animals are arthropods. They are found everywhere - on land, in trees, in freshwater and saltwater, and even underground. Arthropods are generally small. Most are less than 1 cm long. Some arthropods, however, are quite large. The giant king crab, for example, measures over 3.2 m from the tip of one outstretched leg to another. Some of the most familiar arthropods are butterflies, beetles, flies, ants, bees, spiders, scorpions, shrimp, lobster and crabs."

So, maybe we can find some tasty land based insects? What do you think?

### kathylu — 2012-07-21T20:51:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/youre-already-eating-bugs.2606/post-40042>

### Melinda — 2012-07-21T23:46:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/youre-already-eating-bugs.2606/post-40088>

I had a discussion with a Boy Scout Leader a few weeks ago who was in front of a display of live bugs. He proceeded to eat a few. I asked him what they tasted like and he said "dirt". He kept a jar of peanuts in the shell on the table to eat (shell and all) between bugs to get the taste out. He also fed the bugs cheezits and peanuts to improve how they taste. He described how to eat a scorpion and said they tasted good like crab. He had kind of a wild look in his eyes.

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-21T23:55:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/youre-already-eating-bugs.2606/post-40090>

Ants taste like lemon-aid. Best way to catch them is put a dollop of honey in a glass jar and put the jar over the top of that ant hill. When you get a good amt put the lid on and put the jar in the freezer. Once they are frozen shake the ants out on to a cookie sheet and broil on high. Add them to any dish. . Learned this in survival school.

# Thread: Epigenetics and psychopathic killers
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epigenetics-and-psychopathic-killers.2608/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 27
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Glamazon — 2012-07-21T21:03:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epigenetics-and-psychopathic-killers.2608/post-40049>

[http://youtu.be/u2V0vOFexY4\[/video\]\]\[video=youtube\_share;u2V0vOFexY4\]http://youtu.be/u2V0vOFexY4\[/video\]](http://[video=youtube_share;u2V0vOFexY4) A TED talk: _Psychopathic killers are the basis for some must-watch TV, but what really makes them tick? Neuroscientist Jim Fallon talks about brain scans and genetic analysis that may uncover the rotten wiring in the nature (and nurture) of murderers. In a too-strange-for-fiction twist, he shares a fascinating family history that makes his work chillingly personal._

I live about 5 miles from the theater where the killings took place last night. I can't help but think about epigenetic causes like industrial food and toxic environment on what seems to be a rise of psychopathic serial killers in contemporary times.

The killer here looks like he came from a good background. He was here in Denver studying for his PhD in neurosciences. No surface indicators for CW to grab on to give context for this craziness.

Would love to look deeper into his lifestyle.

### [email redacted] — 2012-07-21T21:06:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epigenetics-and-psychopathic-killers.2608/post-40052>

Can't wait to watch this - We are on the same wavelength. . .As I was listening to the news the first time that was the first thing that came to my mind - wondering if this type of mental break is cure able, or are there people born so broke. I want to talk to his family and see what was happening in the womb.

A few days ago, I listened to this podcast that includes Jim Fallon's own story. If you like fascinating personal stories The Moth is a terrific resource. Fallon's story shows that your DNA does not necessarily create your destiny.

### PrimalPam — 2012-07-23T00:10:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epigenetics-and-psychopathic-killers.2608/post-40280>

You know Glamazon, that was the very first thing I thought of when this story broke. This kid apparently was highly intelligent, grew up in a nice environment, and had everything in the world at his disposal. But I, too, wondered just how much more broken he became due to his (I'm certain) SAD diet. He was a typical kid of the 1990's, consuming (I bet) Cheerios, juice boxes, and microwave pizza roll-ups. I'm sure his mom thought she was doing the best she could, as she is a nurse, with all her CW knowledge, so she probably "knows" what are good things to feed him. And he's not overweight, and he was an athlete (track & soccer), so by all accounts, he looked like the picture of health.

I thought of Dr. Terry Wahls' TED talk and it really made me think....

His mother, a psychiatric nurse, immediately knew it was him when the police called her. Had she truly understood the epi-paleo template, could it have saved her son?

> Just gave this a listen - Awesome! I tried to buy the book - but not on Kindle. Pooh, and the HB is expensive.

Of course, now I'm trying to figure out on my 23andme if I have the psycho path thing going on! This guys description of himself sounds like me.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-23T04:47:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epigenetics-and-psychopathic-killers.2608/post-40339>

Diet is huge, but it is not the only epigenetic switch. Clearly, we know about light and temperature. But the epigenetic literature is FULL of data on the relational aspects that cause epigenetic activation. Huge parts of the brain are wired up before conscious memory is on board, thus the preverbal time period is huge for signaling. Just because the media dubs someone 'from a good home' means about as much as them saying 'they ate a healthy diet.'

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-23T04:49:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epigenetics-and-psychopathic-killers.2608/post-40340>

light is more important than humans even can imagine.

### Glamazon — 2012-07-23T23:50:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epigenetics-and-psychopathic-killers.2608/post-40483>

lately, I have been doing a lot of psychiatric patient reviews. No where, in any of the hundreds of pages of documentation that I reviewed in the last 6 months, has anyone once mentioned circadian rhthyms, diet, or run some of Dr. K's lab panels. It is scary.

Anecdotally, I can tell you that schizophrenia is showing up in younger and younger children. So are suicidal and homicidal thoughts and intents, violence, and explosive behavior. Kids are showing up with bipolar mania and depression. You also see incidences of genetic components being documented too for these psychotic behaviors. We even have 4 year olds that try to kill themselves. Makes me sad.

Kids are showing up with bipolar mania and depression. You also see incidences of genetic components being documented too for these psychotic behaviors. We even have 4 year olds that try to kill themselves. Makes me sad.

Wow. This is actually scary.

I watched a program about a couple with a schizophrenic child, maybe 5 or 6 years old. They had another child, and the schizophrenic one was so manic that the couple had to get two apartments in the same complex and switch nights, separating the children, for the sake and safety of their other child.

I was at my friend's house with her 3 year-old the other night. Aside from lack of discipline, this child was up, down, cranky, mad, happy, playful, and downright mean, all within a few hours. Now I realize she's 3, but this was consant mood changes by the minute. What did she eat while I was there...oh let me see...Sprite, SugarBabies, chips with tons of ketchup, rice, cake, whipped cream. Literally, that was what she ate. Now, her mother made beef & chicken fajitas, including veggies, guacamole, and we had berries for dessert. But the little girl only ate the junk, which apparently was fine with mom, 'cause hey, at least she's eating.

ugh, this one kills me. . ."but this is all he will eat". CW has made mothers think if there child goes a few hours without food they might just keel over.

### kathylu — 2012-07-24T03:09:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epigenetics-and-psychopathic-killers.2608/post-40544>

> **Cavemam;40720 wrote:** ugh, this one kills me. . ."but this is all he will eat". CW has made mothers think if there child goes a few hours without food they might just keel over.

Yeah, I get a lot of criticism for refusing to feed my kid junk food. Of course, he occasionally manages to slip some in. If he refuses to eat, I simply say "ok". If he tells me he is hungry later, then I warm up dinner for him. I've actually had moms say to me "Oh, I could never put my child to bed without dinner!". To which I reply "Why not? It's his choice...there's good food here if he wants it. It won't hurt him to skip a meal either, so I don't worry. He'll just have an extra egg in the morning..."

### BJK77 — 2012-07-24T03:49:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epigenetics-and-psychopathic-killers.2608/post-40558>

My son just went to bed hungry and I don't feel bad at all. Does that make me a bad mom? Quite the contrary! I've learned to take a hard stance on food issues. I'll admit to caving in a bit when we're on vacation and under the influence of others (we travel with my parents, what can I say?!?!), but at home there is no such thing as junk food and no snacking at all these days.

The kids get 3 solid, nutrient dense meals per day and they either eat them or don't - their choice. I've had people tell me everything from "you're starving them" to "they're only kids once. let them enjoy it." Yes, I know people think I'm nothing more than a healthnut, but I really don't care. I'm going to do whatever it takes to keep my kids healthy. I see kids constantly who are so strung out on sugar and grains it's unbelievably sad. Kids get put on dangerous drugs everyday because parents are too ignorant to even consider that diet could play a role in how a body functions. It's so sad.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-24T04:22:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epigenetics-and-psychopathic-killers.2608/post-40564>

Ok, count me in.....

> The orbitofrontal cortex and the serial killer
>
> According to Portales, "what lies behind the eyes" of the serial killer "is the key to his psychopathy". The orbitofrontal cortex is the neural region directly behind the eyes and is the last to develop. Portales quotes authoritative research that, due to childhood traumatic experiences, in some individuals the ortbitofrontal cortex - the tool judgment and decision making - may never completely develop: "individuals who have had damage to \[this\] area can observe social situations, but fail to respond to these situations in an appropriate manner."
>
>
> Dr K
>
> .....you likely have bundle damage in your orbital frontal lobes.

### Glamazon — 2012-07-21T21:16:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-oil.2609/post-40060>

Here's a cheap source on Amazon. [http://www.amazon.com/Magnesium-Oil-64-1-9-Liquid/dp/B002I5LO2S/ref=pd\_sbs\_hpc\_8](http://http://www.amazon.com/Magnesium-Oil-64-1-9-Liquid/dp/B002I5LO2S/ref=pd_sbs_hpc_8)

Does anyone have any insights to share about it? I am thinking about getting some.

### kathylu — 2012-07-22T13:33:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-oil.2609/post-40198>

Why not just buy magnesium chloride and make your own? It's much cheaper that way. To make a saturated solution, just stir MgCl2 into distilled water until it won't dissolve any longer...voila, magnesium oil!

### kathylu — 2012-07-22T13:34:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-oil.2609/post-40199>

[http://www.amazon.com/Magnesium-Chloride-Flakes-Swanson-Ultra/dp/B002E62HXQ/ref=sr\_1\_1?s=hpc&ie=UTF8&qid=1342952986&sr=1-1&keywords=magnesium+chloride](http://http://www.amazon.com/Magnesium-Chloride-Flakes-Swanson-Ultra/dp/B002E62HXQ/ref=sr_1_1?s=hpc&ie=UTF8&qid=1342952986&sr=1-1&keywords=magnesium+chloride)

### Destiny — 2012-07-22T14:03:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-oil.2609/post-40202>

> **kathylu;40385 wrote:** Why not just buy magnesium chloride and make your own? It's much cheaper that way. To make a saturated solution, just stir MgCl2 into distilled water until it won't dissolve any longer...voila, magnesium oil!

Kathylu, thank you for the suggestion. What ratio of the magnesium and distilled water do you use? Is it necessary to add any oil?

### kathylu — 2012-07-22T14:20:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-oil.2609/post-40203>

> **Destiny;40389 wrote:** Kathylu, thank you for the suggestion. What ratio of the magnesium and distilled water do you use? Is it necessary to add any oil?

There is no ratio that I know. A "saturated solution" means to dissolve salt crystals in water until it can't hold anymore, i.e. the crystals will stop dissolving. You may have to stir it for a few minutes to find that point. Calling it magnesium "oil" is a misnomer...there's nothing but MgCl2 and water in the mix, no oil. You don't need any preservative agents because the salt concentration is too high to allow bacteria to grow.

### Destiny — 2012-07-22T15:05:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-oil.2609/post-40204>

> **kathylu;40390 wrote:** There is no ratio that I know. A "saturated solution" means to dissolve salt crystals in water until it can't hold anymore, i.e. the crystals will stop dissolving. You may have to stir it for a few minutes to find that point. Calling it magnesium "oil" is a misnomer...there's nothing but MgCl2 and water in the mix, no oil. You don't need any preservative agents because the salt concentration is too high to allow bacteria to grow.

Thanks for explaining.

### janagram — 2012-07-22T19:18:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-oil.2609/post-40233>

I have a gallon jug of it (that I ordered over the net) and its great for adding to baths, or diluting and rubbing on legs, feet...it's not oil, as mentioned.

### [email redacted] — 2012-07-21T22:11:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-cookbook.2610/post-40079>

I have an excuse to go buy a real book! I want to find the quintessential seafood cookbook. . something that shows how to process whole, and all the gory details along with amazing food. Got any ideas?

### Lyndra — 2012-07-22T01:05:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-cookbook.2610/post-40102>

Not specific to fish, but Joy of Cooking has a section on fish cleaning with illustrations. It also details how to pluck, draw & dress poultry, should you ever need to do that. Also a section on various meat cuts. Now if only the print weren't so small...

### [email redacted] — 2012-07-22T01:53:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-cookbook.2610/post-40118>

### SeaHorse — 2012-07-22T01:58:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-cookbook.2610/post-40121>

I like Hugh Fernley Wittenstall....English guy who is trying to reform that country's diet through organic, local, homegrown, home raised everything. He is a fish fanatic and has fantastic recipes for things that most people don't eat. Loads of books, dvds etc. You can order from his website. "The River Cottage Fish Book" has lots of recipes that work for North America. He likes to smoke his own eels in the chimney...stuff like that.

### nonchalant — 2012-07-22T02:13:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-cookbook.2610/post-40125>

Jack Kruse has a cookbook. Great recipes, nice pics. Should be updated very soon.

Hint.

### kathylu — 2012-07-28T03:32:24+0300

# Thread: Nut butter cravings
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nut-butter-cravings.2612/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 31
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Eve — 2012-07-22T03:36:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nut-butter-cravings.2612/post-40160>

I have a leaky gut and I constantly battle cravings for nut butter, knowing that it's a no-no on LeakyGut Rx.

I especially get cravings for it when I'm reacting to a food/supplement (due to leaky gut) or going through a detox. Today in the afternoon my cravings for it were so bad I could have teared up if I pushed it...pretty sad, eh? lol. I think it's also b/c I'm really sick of eating an extremely plain diet -- few veggies, meat, and fish -- for so long.

To try and get rid of the craving, for dinner I had tons of coconut oil with meat, 2 salmon fillets, and broc with coconut oil...It kind of worked but not really.

What's interesting is a couple of weeks ago, I caved and finished about 1/2 small jar of organic almond butter and afterward, I felt a sense of relief and good energy. I wonder if it's b/c almond butter is rich in Mg and other vitamins....?

### Lyndra — 2012-07-23T00:42:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nut-butter-cravings.2612/post-40293>

I was hooked on almond butter and finally had to quit it cold turkey when I learned I had a huge inflammation issues (high hsCRP). That probably wasn't due entirely to PUFAs but I know they didn't help. I think I was craving it for the sweet taste - I've had a lifelong sugar addiction. I know how hard it is to give up, especially when you are feeling like you have given up so many other foods you're accustomed to.

### PrimalPam — 2012-07-23T02:34:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nut-butter-cravings.2612/post-40319>

> **Lyndra;40480 wrote:** I was hooked on almond butter and finally had to quit it cold turkey when I learned I had a huge inflammation issues (high hsCRP). That probably wasn't due entirely to PUFAs but I know they didn't help. I think I was craving it for the sweet taste - I've had a lifelong sugar addiction. I know how hard it is to give up, especially when you are feeling like you have given up so many other foods you're accustomed to.

+1.

I, too, have had a lifelong sugar addiction, and the nut butter fulfilled the creamy, sweet taste when I had given up so much. I just had to say no, and stop buying it. I do eat a couple of spoonfuls of Nutiva CO Manna now instead, but I always eat it as part of a meal, since I no longer snack. See, that was the other problem I had with the nut butter: I'd stick a spoon in the jar several times a day. So now, I have about 2 tbsp. of CO Manna as my "dessert", so I don't "get" to have it till it's dinnertime. Still fulfills that creamy texture sort-of-sweet thing, but way more controlled.

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-29T01:44:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nut-butter-cravings.2612/post-47967>

It's all about context on if it's a good idea or not for you. Many factors to consider:

Your omega ratios

Your adrenal/thyroid status

Do you tend to eating disorders

Are you over snacking on them

Is your gut leaking

Many people CAN eat them, but this forum is probably skewed to those that have something wrong, thus you get that statement more often.

I think those that get closer to optimal, end up not on here trying to "figure stuff out" as much.

Are you trying

### ElectricUniverse — 2019-03-09T17:25:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nut-butter-cravings.2612/post-264906>

I have few dietary vices (I eat to live rather living to eat like so many people).

But one thing I indulge in is delicious peanut butter (organic, of course).

I use it rarely as a special treat. Life is short so I don't feel guilty about a bit of extravagant indulgence.

I know it is problematic in many ways, including _mold toxins_.

One hack for those who enjoy a smidgen of peanut butter now and again is to take some activated charcoal before eating it. It should help absorb any mycotoxins you ingest. Like all nut butters, it is important to refrigerate peanut butter at all times to avoid rancidity (that may be masked by PB's strong flavor).

Being of northern European ancestry I steer towards hearty, fat-rich foods in winter and cooler seasons. Fruits and grocery veggies just simply don't satisfy or nourish at that time.

### Martha Ray — 2021-01-12T06:32:38+0200

>

> But one thing I indulge in is delicious peanut butter (organic, of course).
> I use it rarely as a special treat. Life is short so I don't feel guilty about a bit of extravagant indulgence.
> I know it is problematic in many ways, including mold toxins.
> One hack for those who enjoy a smidgen of peanut butter now and again is to take some activated charcoal before eating it. It should help absorb any mycotoxins you ingest. Like all nut butters, it is important to refrigerate peanut butter at all times to avoid rancidity (that may be masked by PB's strong flavor). Being of northern European ancestry I steer towards hearty, fat-rich foods in winter and cooler seasons. Fruits and grocery veggies just simply don't satisfy or nourish at that time.

I relate to what you shared. Appreciate the mention to refrigerate...that makes sense.

About recourse with indulgence...I wonder if a quality Vit E, like Unique E liquid or a capsule broken open taken with nut butter would be protective instead of charcoal? Nutritionist suggested that tip for me to use if I ever felt I'd eaten any questionable oils. I really like Activated Charcoal but doesn't activated charcoal interfere with good nutrients as well as toxins? I use activated charcoal if my stomach is upset (happens rarely) and it always seems to work for that. I've heard of people trying activated charcoal longer term for candida and I've also read that D-Mannose can also fight candida. Studied Candida overgrowth but never understood it well. I thought it might have been an issue for me but I never determined that definitively. I also have wondered if candida is a driving force behind carb and sugar cravings.

After sugar I tend to crave coffee, fat, salt and nuts. Most likely I pardon salt and nuts more.

# Thread: G'day mates!  New Member In Corpus Christi, Texas
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gday-mates-new-member-in-corpus-christi-texas.2613/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### LouTheNavi — 2012-07-22T07:19:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gday-mates-new-member-in-corpus-christi-texas.2613/post-40186>

G'day mates!

I am Lou living in Corpus Christi, Texas. I have been in the Telomere Modification Program since February 2008.

Before I started the program regimen I weighed over 147 kg. Now I weigh 95 kg. The last time I weighed 95 kg was in 1978. My biological age as of 20 July 2012 is 48 years even though my chronological age is 61.

Also prior to starting I had gout and asthma. I had to take 2 medications for gout and also 2 for asthma. Now both conditions are gone and I don't need to take medication for either one.

Looking forward to hearing how others are doing...

Cheers!

### caroline — 2012-07-22T10:12:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gday-mates-new-member-in-corpus-christi-texas.2613/post-40192>

Hi Lou. Welcome. Great job so far.

You must start a thread and tell us about your "telomere modification program" What was your telomere/biological age when you started back in 2008?

Welcome, Lou, and congratulations! I'm with Corvus, looking forward to hearing more about your telomere program.

Have you been ocean dipping? Do you do that regularly? I'm in CTX and have been thinking of driving down there. Forgot all about the Gulf until someone recently mentioned it here. I lived in Oregon for a while so I guess I'm a bit of an Sea Snob.

# Thread: ED gene
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ed-gene.2617/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-07-22T18:42:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ed-gene.2617/post-40225>

[http://sciencealerts.com/stories/1095161/The\_5HTTLPR\_polymorphism\_and\_eating\_disorders\_A\_metaanalysis.html#at\_pco=cfd-1.0](http://http://sciencealerts.com/stories/1095161/The_5HTTLPR_polymorphism_and_eating_disorders_A_metaanalysis.html#at_pco=cfd-1.0)

The 5-HTTLPR polymorphism and eating disorders: A meta-analysis

> Eating disorders are influenced by both environmental factors and genes. The 5-HTTLPR polymorphism of serotonin transporter gene has been suggested as a good candidate. This meta-analysis was undertaken: (1) to investigate the association between 5-HTTLPR and eating disorders considered as a whole, including anorexia (AN), bulimia (BN), and binge eating disorder (BED); to extend recently reported findings on the association between 5-HTTLPR and AN-BN.PubMed, ISI, and PsycINFO databases were searched for studies published until October 29. Fifteen studies have been included. Data were analyzed with the Cochrane Collaboration Review Manager Software. Quality of studies and publication bias were assessed.An association between S allele and eating disorders, in particular AN, has been found.To be carrier of the 5-HTTLPR S allele seem to represent a risk factor for eating disorders, especially for AN. However, considering the reported high between-study heterogeneity, future studies should focus on more homogeneous endophenotype.

lots of other articles on the link

### chocolate — 2012-07-22T18:49:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ed-gene.2617/post-40226>

[http://www.medical-hypotheses.com/article/S0306-9877%2812%2900297-6/abstract](http://http://www.medical-hypotheses.com/article/S0306-9877%2812%2900297-6/abstract)

> Abstract
>
>
>
> Polyunsaturated essential fatty acids (PUFAs) play a pivotal role in mediating cognitive, learning, and memory functions. We propose that PUFAs directly affect the neuronal membrane. PUFAs serve to stabilize and protect the structure and functions of the neuronal membrane. PUFAs exert many effects on the brain with respect to physiology, brain biochemistry, and disorders of the central nervous system. Many of these functions have effects at the cognitive level. This summary demonstrates that a deficiency in brain PUFAs will lead to cognitive deficits, while supplementation of PUFAs can rehabilitate cognitive deficits, as manifested in attention deficit hyperactivity disorder, stress/anxiety, and aging.

We already knew this. Just published the 17th.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ed-gene.2617/post-40228>

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ed-gene.2617/post-40324>

**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet

### chocolate — 2012-07-22T19:26:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fatty-acid-metabolism-variant-makes-me-feel-a-little-better-less-like-a-zombie.2619/post-40236>

[http://sciencealerts.com/stories/1926012/A\_novel\_FADS1\_isoform\_potentiates\_FADS2mediated\_production\_of\_eicosanoid\_precursor\_fatty\_acids.html#.UAwoBfX4K9o](http://http://sciencealerts.com/stories/1926012/A_novel_FADS1_isoform_potentiates_FADS2mediated_production_of_eicosanoid_precursor_fatty_acids.html#.UAwoBfX4K9o)

A novel FADS1 isoform potentiates FADS2-mediated production of eicosanoid precursor fatty acids

> The fatty acid desaturase (FADS) genes code for the rate-limiting enzymes required for the biosynthesis of long-chain polyunsaturated fatty acids (LCPUFA). Here we report discovery and function of a novel FADS1 splice variant. FADS1 alternative transcript 1 (FADS1AT1) enhances desaturation of FADS2, leading to increased production of eicosanoid precursors, the first case of an isoform modulating the enzymatic activity encoded by another gene. Multiple protein isoforms were detected in primate liver, thymus, and brain. In human neuronal cells, their expression patterns are modulated by differentiation and result in alteration of cellular fatty acids. FADS1, but not FADS1AT1, localizes to endoplasmic reticulum and mitochondria. Ribosomal footprinting demonstrates that all three FADS genes are translated at similar levels. The noncatalytic regulation of FADS2 desaturation by FADS1AT1 is a novel, plausible mechanism by which several phylogenetically conserved FADS isoforms may regulate LCPUFA biosynthesis in a manner specific to tissue, organelle, and developmental stage.

### chocolate — 2012-07-23T01:11:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fatty-acid-metabolism-variant-makes-me-feel-a-little-better-less-like-a-zombie.2619/post-40297>

[http://www.nature.com/news/europe-nears-first-approval-for-gene-therapy-treatment-1.11048](http://http://www.nature.com/news/europe-nears-first-approval-for-gene-therapy-treatment-1.11048)

> Europe nears first approval for gene therapy treatment
>
>
>
> Therapy for rare fat-processing disease gains medicines regulator’s approval.
>
>
>
> Daniel Cressey
>
>
>
> 20 July 2012
>
> Article tools
>
>
>
>
>
> Europe’s drugs regulator has for the first time recommended a gene therapy medicine for approval.
>
>
>
> Glybera, a treatment for patients who cannot produce enough of an enzyme crucial for breaking down fat, was backed by the European Medicines Agency's (EMA) Committee for Medicinal Products for Human Use (CHMP). This recommendation has to be endorsed by the European Commission before it becomes available, but it would be unusual for the Commission to reject the recommendation.
>
>
>
> Gene therapy involves transferring genes into patients to treat their diseases. **In this case Glybera uses a virus injected into a patient to deliver a working copy of a gene for producing lipoprotein lipase (LPL). LPL deficiency affect no more than one or two people in a million.**
>
>
>
> Back in 2004 China became the first country to approve a gene therapy product for commercial use, with a treatment for cancer. But Europe and the United States have yet to endorse any gene therapy treatments and the field has been plagued by issues such as carcinogenicity.
>
>
>
> JÃ¶rn Aldag, chief executive of uniQure, the Amsterdam-based company that owns Glybera, says today’s announcement from the EMA is an “overdue signal” to the gene therapy community that things are changing. “It unlocks the potential,” he told Nature. “You will see more investment coming.”

# Thread: man made jelly fish from scratch
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/man-made-jelly-fish-from-scratch.2621/>
**Subforum:** Factor X
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-07-23T00:56:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/man-made-jelly-fish-from-scratch.2621/post-40295>

[http://www.nature.com/news/artificial-jellyfish-built-from-rat-cells-1.11046](http://http://www.nature.com/news/artificial-jellyfish-built-from-rat-cells-1.11046)

Artificial jellyfish built from rat cells

Reverse-engineered life form could be used to test drugs.

Bioengineers have made an artificial jellyfish using silicone and muscle cells from a rat’s heart. The synthetic creature, dubbed a medusoid, looks like a flower with eight petals. When placed in an electric field, it pulses and swims exactly like its living counterpart.

“Morphologically, we’ve built a jellyfish. Functionally, we’ve built a jellyfish. Genetically, this thing is a rat,” says Kit Parker, a biophysicist at Harvard University in Cambridge, Massachusetts, who led the work. The project is described today in Nature Biotechnology1.

Parker’s lab works on creating artificial models of human heart tissues for regenerating organs and testing drugs, and the team built the medusoid as a way of understanding the “fundamental laws of muscular pumps”. It is an engineer’s approach to basic science: prove that you have identified the right principles by building something with them.

A jellyfish made of silicone and rat heart cells 'swims' in water when subjected to an electric field.

Harvard Univ./Caltech

In 2007, Parker was searching for new ways of studying muscular pumps when he visited the New England Aquarium in Boston, Massachusetts. “I saw the jellyfish display and it hit me like a thunderbolt,” he says. “I thought: I know I can build that.” To do so, he recruited John Dabiri, a bioengineer who studies biological propulsion at the California Institute of Technology (Caltech) in Pasadena. “I grabbed him and said, ‘John, I think I can build a jellyfish.’ He didn’t know who I was, but I was pretty excited and waving my arms, and I think he was afraid to say no.”

Janna Nawroth, a graduate student at Caltech who performed most of the experiments, began by mapping every cell in the bodies of juvenile moon jellies (Aurelia aurita) to understand how they swim. A moon jelly's bell consists of a single layer of muscle, with fibres that are tightly aligned around a central ring and along eight spokes.

To make the bell beat downwards, electrical signals spread through the muscle in a smooth wave, “like when you drop a pebble in water”, says Parker. “It’s exactly like what you see in the heart. My bet is that to get a muscular pump, the electrical activity has got to spread as a wavefront.”

Form and function

Nawroth created a structure with the same properties by growing a single layer of rat heart muscle on a patterned sheet of polydimethylsiloxane. When an electric field is applied across the structure, the muscle contracts rapidly, compressing the medusoid and mimicking a jellyfish’s power stroke. The elastic silicone then pulls the medusoid back to its original flat shape, ready for the next stroke.

Nature | News

Artificial jellyfish built from rat cells

Reverse-engineered life form could be used to test drugs.

Ed Yong

22 July 2012

Bioengineers have made an artificial jellyfish using silicone and muscle cells from a rat’s heart. The synthetic creature, dubbed a medusoid, looks like a flower with eight petals. When placed in an electric field, it pulses and swims exactly like its living counterpart./>

“Morphologically, we’ve built a jellyfish. Functionally, we’ve built a jellyfish. Genetically, this thing is a rat,” says Kit Parker, a biophysicist at Harvard University in Cambridge, Massachusetts, who led the work. The project is described today in Nature Biotechnology1.

Parker’s lab works on creating artificial models of human heart tissues for regenerating organs and testing drugs, and the team built the medusoid as a way of understanding the “fundamental laws of muscular pumps”. It is an engineer’s approach to basic science: prove that you have identified the right principles by building something with them.

A jellyfish made of silicone and rat heart cells 'swims' in water when subjected to an electric field.

Harvard Univ./Caltech

In 2007, Parker was searching for new ways of studying muscular pumps when he visited the New England Aquarium in Boston, Massachusetts. “I saw the jellyfish display and it hit me like a thunderbolt,” he says. “I thought: I know I can build that.” To do so, he recruited John Dabiri, a bioengineer who studies biological propulsion at the California Institute of Technology (Caltech) in Pasadena. “I grabbed him and said, ‘John, I think I can build a jellyfish.’ He didn’t know who I was, but I was pretty excited and waving my arms, and I think he was afraid to say no.”

Janna Nawroth, a graduate student at Caltech who performed most of the experiments, began by mapping every cell in the bodies of juvenile moon jellies (Aurelia aurita) to understand how they swim. A moon jelly's bell consists of a single layer of muscle, with fibres that are tightly aligned around a central ring and along eight spokes.

To make the bell beat downwards, electrical signals spread through the muscle in a smooth wave, “like when you drop a pebble in water”, says Parker. “It’s exactly like what you see in the heart. My bet is that to get a muscular pump, the electrical activity has got to spread as a wavefront.”

Form and function

Nawroth created a structure with the same properties by growing a single layer of rat heart muscle on a patterned sheet of polydimethylsiloxane. When an electric field is applied across the structure, the muscle contracts rapidly, compressing the medusoid and mimicking a jellyfish’s power stroke. The elastic silicone then pulls the medusoid back to its original flat shape, ready for the next stroke.

When placed between two electrodes in water, the medusoid swam like the real thing. It even produced water currents similar to those that wash food particles into jellyfish's mouths. “We thought if we’re really good at this, we’re going to recreate that vortex, and we did,” says Parker. “We took a rat apart and rebuilt it as a jellyfish.”

Related stories

“I think that this is terrific,” says Joseph Vacanti, a tissue engineer at Massachusetts General Hospital in Boston. “It is a powerful demonstration of engineering chimaeric systems of living and non-living components.”

Parker says his team is taking synthetic biology to a new level. “Usually when we talk about synthetic life forms, somebody will take a living cell and put new genes in. We built an animal. It’s not just about genes, but about morphology and function.”

The team now plans to build a medusoid using human heart cells. The researchers have filed a patent to use their design, or something similar, as a platform for testing drugs. “You’ve got a heart drug?” says Parker. “You let me put it on my jellyfish, and I’ll tell you if it can improve the pumping.”

They also hope to reverse-engineer other marine life forms, says Parker. “We’ve got a whole tank of stuff in there, and an octopus on order.”

### MJ* — 2012-07-23T19:50:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-and-viruses.2628/post-40419>

[From this abstract:](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/9501377)

"Viruses are the most significant cause of shellfish-associated disease"

Since viruses cause epigenetic change, and shellfish carry viruses, will eating more shellfish also cause epigenetic change in us? Will it make us sick or smarter or both? This is just a curious question I've been considering, is there an expense to eating so much shellfish?

### MJ* — 2012-07-24T04:37:47+0300

I do think that since viruses play a vital role in epigenetic changes this study at least corroborates the possible connection between viruses, eating seafood and accelerated epigenetic changes that led to our big brains...I'm just wondering if eating seafood today will cause further epigenetic changes or maintain status quo.

### kathylu — 2012-07-24T06:57:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-and-viruses.2628/post-40602>

Yes, viruses often insert sequences in our DNA upon infection. Often it is a random event and no harm comes of it, but sometimes viral sequences can activate other genes we possess. Think oncogene activation, which may lead to cancer, especially lymphomas, leukemias and sarcomas. Even though it can be damaging for the individual, for a mutation of this type to be passed on, the viral gene or gene fragment must be inserted into germ cells (eggs and sperm) then the host must reproduce for it to be stably maintained. Viral gene sequences can also "jump" to other locations taking host cell fragments with them and reinsert themselves. So gene mixing is not infrequent. There is quite a bit of evidence that this does occur. This is certainly one way that evolution occurs. Does it mean you shouldn't eat seafood? Not at all...viruses are everywhere, you can't avoid them. I'd be more worried about acute illness from a virus than genetic or epigenetic change.

[http://www.wired.com/wiredscience/2010/01/bornavirus-in-human-dna/](http://http://www.wired.com/wiredscience/2010/01/bornavirus-in-human-dna/)

### MJ* — 2012-07-24T16:39:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-and-viruses.2628/post-40674>

> **kathylu;40791 wrote:** Yes, viruses often insert sequences in our DNA upon infection. Often it is a random event and no harm comes of it, but sometimes viral sequences can activate other genes we possess. Think oncogene activation, which may lead to cancer, especially lymphomas, leukemias and sarcomas. Even though it can be damaging for the individual, for a mutation of this type to be passed on, the viral gene or gene fragment must be inserted into germ cells (eggs and sperm) then the host must reproduce for it to be stably maintained. Viral gene sequences can also "jump" to other locations taking host cell fragments with them and reinsert themselves. So gene mixing is not infrequent. There is quite a bit of evidence that this does occur. This is certainly one way that evolution occurs. Does it mean you shouldn't eat seafood? Not at all...viruses are everywhere, you can't avoid them. I'd be more worried about acute illness from a virus than genetic or epigenetic change.
>

>
> [http://www.wired.com/wiredscience/2010/01/bornavirus-in-human-dna/](http://http://www.wired.com/wiredscience/2010/01/bornavirus-in-human-dna/)

Interesting article thanks Kathy...germ cell insertion is a factor for anyone of reproductive age when considering all their habits. It is becoming abundantly clear from all that we are learning here that what we do, what our grandparents have done, in terms of what we consume, and are exposed to, and maybe even our physicality, directly effects the health of our offspring. For us as individuals, I agree acute illness is a concern with especially the consumption of raw or tainted seafood. Considering that there is no fossil evidence of the changes from ape to man, Dr. Kruse has contended that epigenetic changes to the individual matter - the speed of which allowed for encephalation of our brains. What we do and are exposed to matters and directly effects our own health and development. I agree it's hard to avoid viruses since they are everywhere, the point of this was that the abstract of this article shows that viruses are especially abundant in seafood. Therefore, by consuming seafood in abundance we are being exposed to more viruses as a result. We have no way of knowing what the exposure to viruses of today will bring tomorrow in terms of epigenetics. My takeaway LoL is this confirms what I'm already doing, cooking my seafood, for more than one reason.
