### kathylu — 2012-07-24T17:38:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-and-viruses.2628/post-40689>

Well, the two major types of immunity are humoral (antibodies) and cellular (NK, T cells, eosinophils, phagocytes, etc.). I don't think infection by a virus found in seafood would confer any humoral protection against the diseases you mentioned...unless you ate seafood containing Ebola (incredibly unlikely since it's a primate virus) and managed to survive long enough to form antibodies. HIV is a special case, since one of its unique features is its ability to escape a humoral response. Now you might be on to something if the infective agents in seafood were able to activate cellular immunity...which would also provide some nonspecific protection. That is...if you have lots of NK and T cells they may attack many different viral or bacterial agents. In fact, one of the diagnostic criteria for AIDS is a drop in T cell level coinciding with a rise in viral titer.

The bottom line for me...if you cook your seafood, I think any risks are truly negligible. If you eat raw seafood, the risk is higher, so source (clean water) and time of year (more infections occur in war months) becomes important and freezing certainly helps. Can you get sick from raw seafood? Yes. Does it happen frequently? Not really, though outbreaks are certainly reported every year. If you are immunocompromised, cook your seafood. If you are not, use common sense and don't complain if it does happen...you knew the risks. I still eat raw seafood...sushi/sashimi and raw clams/oysters occasionally.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-24T21:09:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-and-viruses.2628/post-40784>

The only time I ever got severe food poisoning was from a bag of store-bought beef jerky..... technically cooked. I eat raw veggies all the time pulled from the dirt when working on farms - we don't wash and yes there's manure in the dirt that \*we\* put there - we use good practices for the manure sourcing but after that.... a bit of dirt and bacteria have only been good for me. I would never use chlorox on my food. My mother never was a clean freak with us kids either... my immune system handles stuff for me now. I know I could get sick... but how much are you increasing your likelihood of getting sick by constantly disinfecting everything and getting no exposure at all?

### MJ* — 2012-07-24T21:30:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-and-viruses.2628/post-40798>

> **indigogirl;40956 wrote: Good to see you back, MJ! Thanks, I've been busy!
>
>
>
> Souldanzer;40975 wrote:** The only time I ever got severe food poisoning was from a bag of store-bought beef jerky..... technically cooked. I eat raw veggies all the time pulled from the dirt when working on farms - we don't wash and yes there's manure in the dirt that \*we\* put there - we use good practices for the manure sourcing but after that.... a bit of dirt and bacteria have only been good for me. I would never use chlorox on my food. My mother never was a clean freak with us kids either... my immune system handles stuff for me now. I know I could get sick... but how much are you increasing your likelihood of getting sick by constantly disinfecting everything and getting no exposure at all?

There is something to be said for not making your environment too sterile...and for allowing your immune system to do its good work. I stopped buying antibacterial soap for that reason a few years ago. There are good points being made on both sides of this.

### kathylu — 2012-07-24T22:19:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-and-viruses.2628/post-40821>

I agree with Soul, I wouldn't use chlorox on my food. I do wash things well though. I used a mild orange oil soap to wash fruits and veggies, then rinse well. I wash meat, chicken and fish too, with just water and then dry it with paper towels before cooking. It reduces the bacteria on surfaces.

### kathylu — 2012-07-26T18:01:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-and-viruses.2628/post-41206>

> **Nonchalant;41024 wrote:** So our DNA is passively receiving the sequences? Or does our immune system secret them away there?

Yes to all of that. There are thousands of viruses that are capable of integration into human DNA, many have cleverly made it part of their life cycle. Most, but not all are RNA viruses. Think herpes (DNA) virus that infects, an RNA intermediate integrates into neurons and may lay dormant for many years before being excised and starting viral replication (thus becoming infectious) again. There are thousands of HERV's (human endogenous retroviruses)...they make up roughly 8% of our genome. After integration many viral sequences are made "silent" by host cell processes (methylation, deletional recombination, histone deacetylation, etc), but as Dr. K has pointed out, many sequences have radically modified our epigenetics. HERV sequences can act as promoters and enhancers, turning our genes on and off. They are implicated in multiple forms of cancer and in autoimmune disease.

### Glen PDQ — 2012-08-04T08:46:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-and-viruses.2628/post-42817>

# Thread: The whole shrimp
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-whole-shrimp.2630/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Dali Dula — 2012-07-23T21:42:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-whole-shrimp.2630/post-40436>

I read some stuff on the forum about eating whole shrimp shell and all. I am not squeamish , but could use some suggestion on preparation. thanks

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-23T22:08:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-whole-shrimp.2630/post-40440>

steam, saute in ghee/butter/coconut oil, throw in soups... eat the whole dang thing... ds pops the whole thing in his little mouth and eats everything...If I don't manage my plate well, he'll just eat the tails...

### ealachan — 2012-07-23T22:35:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-whole-shrimp.2630/post-40450>

ooh, I just thought of something - when y'all are eating the "whole shrimp", are you de-veining it first, or is the vein along the back part of what we want / need? All the shrimp I buy frozen are already deveined and de-headed, though not usually peeled (because I can't afford the peeled ones...haha).

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-23T23:56:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-whole-shrimp.2630/post-40487>

> **ealachan;40638 wrote:** ooh, I just thought of something - when y'all are eating the "whole shrimp", are you de-veining it first, or is the vein along the back part of what we want / need? All the shrimp I buy frozen are already deveined and de-headed, though not usually peeled (because I can't afford the peeled ones...haha).

Yes, I eat the WHOLE thing (though I usually leave the tail). I buy whole frozen raw shrimp, heads still on (totally untouched, just frozen). I heat Coconut Oil (but you can use ghee, butter, etc) in a pan with garlic. When hot, I add the shrimp (I often do this when they're still frozen... they cook quickly) and saute for a few minutes. I then add spices of choice (usually curry flavours) and maybe some herbs and sometimes bone broth if I want a soup base, with a squeeze of lemon. If they're frozen, I'll put a lid over the pan for a minute or two to make sure they are cooked (though I'm assuming raw is fine). Then I put them in a bowl and eat. As I said to Soul, I eat the heads first (with my own eyes closed... the ones I buy are big... maybe 5 inches long?... and their eyes are large and they look right at you!),then I go back and eat the bodies. I often don't eat all the shells, but always the entire head, shell, eyes, brains and all. Inside of the head is a bit mushy but the outside is crunchy. They taste very good, it's more the psychological barrier.

### ealachan — 2012-07-24T00:01:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-whole-shrimp.2630/post-40489>

> **KiwiLauren;40675 wrote:** Yes, I eat the WHOLE thing (though I usually leave the tail). I buy whole frozen raw shrimp, heads still on (totally untouched, just frozen). I heat Coconut Oil (but you can use ghee, butter, etc) in a pan with garlic. When hot, I add the shrimp (I often do this when they're still frozen... they cook quickly) and saute for a few minutes. I then add spices of choice (usually curry flavours) and maybe some herbs and sometimes bone broth if I want a soup base, with a squeeze of lemon. If they're frozen, I'll put a lid over the pan for a minute or two to make sure they are cooked (though I'm assuming raw is fine). Then I put them in a bowl and eat. As I said to Soul, I eat the heads first (with my own eyes closed... the ones I buy are big... maybe 5 inches long?... and their eyes are large and they look right at you!),then I go back and eat the bodies. I often don't eat all the shells, but always the entire head, shell, eyes, brains and all. Inside of the head is a bit mushy but the outside is crunchy. They taste very good, it's more the psychological barrier.

I know that the "proper" way to eat a crawfish is to suck the head parts out...wonder if one could take the same approach with shrimp. Interesting.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-whole-shrimp.2630/post-40494>

### indigogirl — 2012-07-24T06:15:16+0300

# Thread: Weston Price on Fish and Sea Food
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weston-price-on-fish-and-sea-food.2632/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 18
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-07-23T23:51:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weston-price-on-fish-and-sea-food.2632/post-40484>

Jack mentioned Price in a recent blog post. Strangely, just before I'd got to that point I found comments of Price's coming into my head.

It's interesting the importance he sees in the maritime element in the Hebridean Islanders' diet. Informally, I believe Price was known for recommending seafood to inquirers looking for dietary advice as one of the most nutritious foods one could eat.

He also mentions fish and shellfish when discussing Polynesian Islanders -- still today very large and healthy people with a reputation at rugby that belies the size of their populations. (Genetic momentum?)

And there are some interesting comments with reference to African tribes living largely on fish:

> The Government of Kenya has for several years sponsored an athletic contest among the various tribes, the test being one of strength for which they use a tug-of-war. One particular tribe has carried off the trophy repeatedly. This tribe resides on the east coast of Lake Victoria and lives very largely on fish. The members are powerful athletes and wonderful swimmers. They are said not to have been conquered in warfare when they could take the warfare to the water. One of their methods is to swim under water to the enemy's fleet and scuttle their boats. They fight with spears under water with marvelous skill. **Their physiques are magnificent. In a group of 190 boys who had been gathered into a government school near the east coast of Lake Victoria **only one boy was found with dental caries, and two of his teeth had been affected.
>
>
> [http://www.journeytoforever.org/farm\_library/price/price9.html](http://http://www.journeytoforever.org/farm_library/price/price9.html)
>
>
>
> In the same chapter Price also has this interesting note on the Dinka:
>
>
>
> Dinkas, Jebelein, Sudan. This tribe lives on the Nile. Its members are not as tall as the Neurs. They are physically better proportioned and have greater strength. They use fish from the Nile and cereals for their diet. They decorate their bodies profusely with scars.****

****

The Nuer (I take it that's who he means by "Neurs") are cattle herders, living at that time on mostly milk, blood, and meat. It's interesting that these people should be a bit taller than the fish-eaters, but not so well-proportioned or strong.

Perhaps most striking is what's said about the Maori of New Zealand, however. In chapter 15, Characteristics of primitive and modernized dietaries, we have:

The native Maori in New Zealand, used large quantities of foods from the sea, wherever these were available.** Even in the inland food depots, mutton birds were still available in large quantities. These birds were captured just before they left the nests. They developed in the rockeries about the coast, chiefly on the extreme southern coast of the South Island. At this stage, the flesh is very tender and very fat from the gorging that has been provided by their parent. The value of this food for the treatment of tuberculosis was being heralded quite widely in both Australia and New Zealand. In the primitive state of the islands large quantities of land birds were available and because of the fertility of the soil and favorable climate, vegetables and fruits grew abundantly in the wild. Large quantities of fern root were used. Where groups of the Maori race were found isolated sufficiently from contact with modern civilization and its foods to be dependent largely on the native foods, they selected with precision certain shell-fish because of their unique nutritive value**.

A splendid illustration of the primitive Maori instinct or wisdom regarding the value of sea foods was shown in an experience we had while making examinations in a native school on the east coast of the North Islands. I was impressed with the fact that the children in the school gave very little evidence of having active dental caries. I asked the teacher what the children brought from their homes to eat at their midday lunch, since most of them had to come too great a distance to return at noon. I was told that they brought no lunch but that when school was dismissed at noon the children rushed for the beach where, while part of the group prepared bonfires, the others stripped and dived into the sea, and brought up a large species of lobster. The lobsters were promptly roasted on the coals and devoured with great relish. Other sea foods are pictured in Fig. 74.

Chapter 12 is devoted to the Maori -- "Isolated and modernized New Zealand Maori".

Price notes that an earlier researcher had found that:

> the incidence of caries in the Maori \[was\] 1.2 per cent in a total of 326 skulls. This is lower even than the Esquimaux, and **shows the Maori to have been the most immune race to caries, for which statistics are available.
>
>
> [http://journeytoforever.org/farm\_library/price/price12.html](http://http://journeytoforever.org/farm_library/price/price12.html)
>
>
>
> He describes the unmodernized Maori as being, physically, "on a pedestal of perfection".
>
>
>
> He concludes:
>
>
>
> The Maori race developed a knowledge of Nature's laws and adopted a system of living in harmony with those laws to so high a degree that they were able to build what was reported by early scientists to be the most physically perfect race living on the face of the earth.**

So eat up.

"What a man! What a brain! And all this is due to fish you say?"

1 minute 40 seconds

[http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NjbR73qSukY\[/video\]\]\[video=youtube\_share;NjbR73qSukY\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NjbR73qSukY\[/video\]](http://[video=youtube_share;NjbR73qSukY)

### nonchalant — 2012-07-24T00:54:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weston-price-on-fish-and-sea-food.2632/post-40501>

"Where groups of the Maori race were found isolated sufficiently from contact with modern civilization and its foods to be dependent largely on the native foods, they selected with precision certain shell-fish because of their unique nutritive value."

I'll bet those shellfish tasted best to them. The Maori knew that those shellfish were most satisfying.

### vlynnb — 2012-07-24T04:43:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weston-price-on-fish-and-sea-food.2632/post-40575>

So interesting. My daughter is married to 1/4 Maori. He is in very good physical shape, a little shorter than most but very muscular. He and his father, who was a professional Rugby player, love junk food and are still in good shape, apparently. But what do they crave, even in the inter-mountain west? Fish, fish, and more fish. His dad loves to splurge on shrimp for the barby. I think I will keep encouraging the fishing trips for steel-head and salmon that they live for! Maybe more and more fish will someday alter their taste for junk food. I am afraid that is unrealistic though.

### nonchalant — 2012-07-24T05:14:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weston-price-on-fish-and-sea-food.2632/post-40584>

You have to admit, we excel at making empty food taste fabulous. Pinnacle of human achievement.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-24T07:53:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weston-price-on-fish-and-sea-food.2632/post-40611>

Unfortunately, the SAD diet is alive and well here in NZ. Maori and Pacific Islanders in NZ are over-represented in every health risk category, including obesity and all the other diseases of modern life. You just can't substitute fried fish and chips for kai moana (the Maori term for seafood) and not flip epigenetic switches in the wrong direction. And, of course, our Western doctors deal with their obesity and health issues by pushing exercise, low fat, high carb diets on them (and all the rest of us). What remains, however, is a devotion to the land and the sea. Maori, like Native Americans, do not believe anyone owns the water but believe it is a human right to have access to it and that we are caretakers of the land. I hope that the epi-paleo information can take hold in the Maori community... we have a bicultural society here (not in name only) and the influence could be huge.

### Michael — 2012-07-24T08:57:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weston-price-on-fish-and-sea-food.2632/post-40617>

> **KiwiLauren;40801 wrote:** Unfortunately, the SAD diet is alive and well here in NZ. Maori and Pacific Islanders in NZ are over-represented in every health risk category, including obesity and all the other diseases of modern life.
>
>
> It seems to be the same situation in North America with respect to the native peoples. There are reports of early explorers of people in superb physical condition and health. Now they're some of the least healthy people.
>
>
>
> Canada:
>
>
>
>
> > ... obesity, metabolic syndrome and type 2 diabetes in First Nations
> >
> >
> > [http://www.cbc.ca/thelens/bigfatdiet/wortman.html](http://http://www.cbc.ca/thelens/bigfatdiet/wortman.html)
> >
> >
> >
> > U.S.:
> >
> >
> >
> >
> > > The Indians are hit harder and faster than the rest of us because they are only two generations away from the "old way" of life, based on game animals and fish. Uncle Sam will never admit that the Indians were tall, lean and healthy just two generations ago. If ever someone wanted proof that humans weren't designed to eat a grain-based diet, look at the American Indian population-almost all of them are battling overweight, diabetes, and heart disease.
> >
> >
> >
> > [http://www.westonaprice.org/traditional-diets/guts-and-grease](http://http://www.westonaprice.org/traditional-diets/guts-and-grease)
> >
> >
> >
> >
> >
> > And come to think of it, the Australian Aborigines are in much the same plight. They're not only unhealthy; their societies are riddled with violence, endemic crime, alcoholism, and sexual assault:
> >
> >
> >
> > [http://www.smh.com.au/national/alcohol-primary-cause-of-aboriginal-violence-20100408-rv67.html](http://http://www.smh.com.au/national/alcohol-primary-cause-of-aboriginal-violence-20100408-rv67.html)
> >
> >
> >
> > Yet, according to Price:
> >
> >
> >
> > Their social organization is such that almost every person who had been in intimate contact with them, testified that they had never known any of the Aborigines to be guilty of the theft of anything.
>
>
>
>
>
> Price notes:
>
>
>
> The rapid degeneration of the Australian Aborigines after the adoption of the government's modern foods provides a demonstration that should be infinitely more convincing than animal experimentation. It should be a matter not only of concern but deep alarm that human beings can degenerate physically so rapidly by the use of a certain type of nutrition, particularly the dietary products used so generally by modern civilization.

Whenever I think about these kinds of issues I find it frightening that Price can have been documenting all this so carefully and in such detail, and yet everyone has gone on in much the same way heading for the edge of the cliff. It's truly mind-boggling.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-24T14:22:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weston-price-on-fish-and-sea-food.2632/post-40635>

That is the definition of an environmental mismatch where you supposedly "smart brain" intellectualizes a concept and screws it up over your gut instinct. Inner guidance should come through our feelings and body wisdom and not via intellectual understanding. Pay attention to your inner guru. When it speaks to you, you need to listen intently. The mind can no longer be thought of confined to the skull. It resides in every cell of our body. Every thought is a biochemical equivalent.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-24T14:30:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weston-price-on-fish-and-sea-food.2632/post-40638>

You can't become who you are meant to be in your future if you continue clinging to who you were yesterday. Lead,dream,inspire, and disrupt! And eat more shellfish.........I think tonights webinar will really help you understand how this all fits lock and key.

### nonchalant — 2012-07-24T15:30:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weston-price-on-fish-and-sea-food.2632/post-40661>

> **Jack Kruse;40825 wrote:** That is the definition of an environmental mismatch where you supposedly "smart brain" intellectualizes a concept and screws it up over your gut instinct. Inner guidance should come through our feelings and body wisdom and not via intellectual understanding. Pay attention to your inner guru. When it speaks to you, you need to listen intently. The mind can no longer be thought of confined to the skull. It resides in every cell of our body. Every thought is a biochemical equivalent.

We have to learn when it is our inner guru speaking, and not the intestinal overgrowth, though. Last night I didn't want to eat what we were having (chicken). I was irritated and decided to wait a bit, then dive into some cerviche. Then I realized I wasn't hungry any more. I looked up, and saw it was too late to eat, so I didn't. I went to bed after another hour.

This is very different from my behavior just a few months ago, when Candida or whatever was influencing my meals. Eating lots of seafood seems to be in the process of redefining my behaviors and tastes, and thoughts.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-24T15:36:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weston-price-on-fish-and-sea-food.2632/post-40663>

its changing your gut flora

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-24T15:37:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weston-price-on-fish-and-sea-food.2632/post-40664>

> **Jack Kruse;40828 wrote:** You can't become who you are meant to be in your future if you continue clinging to who you were yesterday. Lead,dream,inspire, and disrupt! And eat more shellfish.........I think tonights webinar will really help you understand how this all fits lock and key.

I really wish I could join tonight...I'll see if I can swing it and get the kiddos in bed earlier than normal......

### Michael — 2012-07-24T15:51:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weston-price-on-fish-and-sea-food.2632/post-40666>

> **Jack Kruse;40825 wrote:** That is the definition of an environmental mismatch where you supposedly "smart brain" intellectualizes a concept and screws it up over your gut instinct. Inner guidance should come through our feelings and body wisdom and not via intellectual understanding. Pay attention to your inner guru. When it speaks to you, you need to listen intently. The mind can no longer be thought of confined to the skull. It resides in every cell of our body. Every thought is a biochemical equivalent.

IOW, we should also be listening to the Zen/Ch'an Buddhists (and perhaps the Taoists which is probably where this type of thinking made its way into Chinese Buddhism from). The point of those riddles, the koan, I understand, is not "smart alec" responses as Westerners tend to believe -- what "Zen" often seems to mean in the mouth of a Westerner. The point is that you _can't solve them. You think and think over the koan with your analytical mind until you have to give up. Sooner or later, and this seems to be influenced by your meditation practice, an intuitive answer will present itself to you.

It's not the answer itself that matters. So buying something like _The Gateless Gate or The Blue Cliff Record_ and reading some of the traditional koans, responses, and commentary on them is beside the point. It's the process. And the "sitting"_ of course.

I'm sure I should develop my intuitive side more.

### nonchalant — 2012-07-24T16:36:30+0300

And it's so easy! No zen-like powers of self control over what/when/how much you eat. Just ... let it be.

Thank you so much, Jack.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-24T01:26:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-labs.2634/post-40509>

Your thyroid numbers suck! But you know that....

TSH - too high

FT3 - too low (s/b around 4.0-4.2)

FT4 - too low (s/b at least 3.0 but really higher)

rT3 ratio - too low

You are converting your FT4 to rT3 and NOT FT3. I can't remember, of course, do you have adrenal problems? Your iron looks a little weak to me which is another reason our bodies want to make rT3 instead of FT3. IIRC, you want the iron or ferritin to be near 100 and definitely need your saturation to be around 35%.

WOW, Vitamin B12 is great! But your preg and DHEA both look low. DHEA should be around 250-380 according to Dr. Dzugan. Preg s/b 200-230. Aren't you taking those?

### Destiny — 2012-07-24T01:35:16+0300

Hi Patty! Yes, I know the thyroid is not great. I have a bit of adrenal problem since it is OK in the morning, low at noon and evening and high at night.

I started taking Preg only - 7mg since I was really affected by that. I actually did not take any for past two days since I started having more palpitations and headaches and felt really weird and tired. Basically, I started feeling worse on it.

B12 is not a surprise, I give myself an injection every month.

I was going to schedule a consultation with Dr. Kruse for tomorrow morning but then I found out that he only skypes on the weekends. I am travelling this weekend! More waiting ....

Your adrenals, whether high or low, can affect that rT3. I guess it's the light cycles that help it the most so I am pretty good at orange glasses at night. I'm pretty boring so no late night adventures for me! But my adrenals are low across the board so it's been going on for a long time.

Since preg is made from cholesterol how is your cholesterol? Maybe there is a clue there? Just throwing some things out there...

I am a little concerned about the CRH/ACTH connection as my ACTH is very low. I am following the rx for circadian rhythms with orange glasses and waking up with the sun. Way too much sun here in the afternoon and not enough in the early morning. I feel better with the pregnenolone, now up to 150 mg, so I believe that is helping my adrenals too. I am working on getting a saliva test but this last few months hasn't been good for income so hopefully I will be able to retest in the next month to see what is happening.

I'm actually quite amazed that I haven't given up. the answers are slow to come(for me), but my gut tells me I have found the right path.
>
>
>
> This experience has been very humbling..I'm used to picking things up fast and usually quite confident. I feel like a kindergartener, not knowing what room I'm in and crying and peeing my pants too often. Could it just be menopause
>
>
>
> Anybody else??

I get frustrated too but there is no other real option than this. It could be menopause, but wait for your labs. I am sure dr. Kruse will help you with those.

### Destiny — 2012-08-05T14:01:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-labs.2634/post-43026>

So I had my consult and it was great. It gave me a clear direction on what to do first, second, etc. The good news is that my problems are straightforward and fixable.

I need to find a doctor who will prescribe T3. I will try my GP first. Then, my compounding pharmacy is next - to give me a few doctors who prescribe compounded T3.

After that, I will have to concentrate on getting more progesterone, estrogen and testosterone. I am presently on BHRT - progesterone and estrogen but it is not enough. I am seeing my Obgyn next week. If she is not open to that, I will need to find a new one.

I could also benefit from Metformin and Cycloset but that would come after the addressing the first two big issues - thyroid and then my female hormones.

Another unexpected benefit of the consult was that my husband expressed an interest in getting his own labs.

# Thread: journal worth following? Antioxidants  Redox Signaling
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/journal-worth-following-antioxidants-redox-signaling.2639/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-07-24T05:31:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/journal-worth-following-antioxidants-redox-signaling.2639/post-40589>

Powerful class of antioxidants may be potent Parkinsonâ€™s treatment

> A new and powerful class of antioxidants could one day be a potent treatment for Parkinsonâ€™s disease, researchers report.
>
>
>
> A class of antioxidants called synthetic triterpenoids blocked development of Parkinsonâ€™s in an animal model that develops the disease in a handful of days, said Dr. Bobby Thomas, neuroscientist at the Medical College of Georgia at Georgia Health Sciences University and corresponding author of the study in the journal Antioxidants & Redox Signaling.
>
>
>
> Thomas and his colleagues were able to block the death of dopamine-producing brain cells that occurs in Parkinsonâ€™s by using the drugs to bolster Nrf2, a natural antioxidant and inflammation fighter.
>
>
>
> Stressors from head trauma to insecticide exposure to simple aging increase oxidative stress and the body responds with inflammation, part of its natural repair process. â€œThis creates an environment in your brain that is not conducive for normal function,â€ Thomas said. **â€œYou can see the signs of oxidative damage in the brain long before the neurons actually degenerate in Parkinsonâ€™s.â€
>
>
>
> Nrf2, the master regulator of oxidative stress and inflammation, is â€“ inexplicably â€“ significantly decreased early in Parkinsonâ€™s. In fact, Nrf2 activity declines normally with age.
>
>
>
> â€œIn Parkinsonâ€™s patients you can clearly see a significant overload of oxidative stress, which is why we chose this target,â€ Thomas said. â€œWe used drugs to selectively activate Nrf2.â€
>
> **
>
> They parsed a number of antioxidants already under study for a wide range of diseases from kidney failure to heart disease and diabetes, and found triterpenoids the most effective on Nrf2. Co-author Dr. Michael Sporn, Professor of Pharmacology, Toxicology and Medicine at Dartmouth Medical School, chemically modified the agents so they could permeate the protective blood-brain barrier.
>
>
>
> Both in human neuroblastoma and mouse brain cells they were able to document an increase in Nrf2 in response to the synthetic triterpenoids. Human dopaminergic cells are not available for research so the scientists used the human neuroblastoma cells, which are actually cancer cells that have some properties similar to neurons.
>
>
>
> Their preliminary evidence indicates the synthetic triterpenoids also increase Nrf2 activity in astrocytes, a brain cell type which nourishes neurons and hauls off some of their garbage. The drugs didnâ€™t protect brain cells in an animal where the Nrf2 gene was deleted, more proof that that Nrf2 is the drugsâ€™ target.**
>
>
>
> The researchers used the powerful neurotoxin MPTP to mimic Parkinsonâ€™s-like brain cell damage in a matter of days. They are now looking at the impact of synthetic triterpenoids in an animal model genetically programmed to acquire the disease more slowly, as humans do. Collaborators at Johns Hopkins School of Medicine also will be providing induced pluripotent stem cells, adult stem cells that can be coaxed into forming dopaminergic neurons, for additional drug testing.**

# Thread: Telomere Modification Program - Started In February 2008
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/telomere-modification-program-started-in-february-2008.2641/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### LouTheNavi — 2012-07-24T06:40:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/telomere-modification-program-started-in-february-2008.2641/post-40599>

G'day mates,

My name is Lou and I am 168 cm tall and have a chronological age of 61 years.

I'm a new member here and in this thread I'll update you on my progress in a Telomere Modification Program that I began using in February 2008. Prior to starting this program I weighed over 147 kg and had Gout, Asthma, and water retention. I was taking 5 medications for these conditions.

Now I weigh 95 kg and do not have to take any medications at all for Gout, Asthma or water retention, as I no longer have those conditions.

My TMP is based on these two studies:

[http://archinte.jamanetwork.com/article.aspx?articleid=413815](http://http://archinte.jamanetwork.com/article.aspx?articleid=413815)

[http://www.plosmedicine.org/article/info:doi/10.1371/journal.pmed.0050012](http://http://www.plosmedicine.org/article/info:doi/10.1371/journal.pmed.0050012)

I took a biological age test on July 20 and it measured my biological age as 48 years.

I'll explain more in my next post.

Cheers!

### fitness@home — 2012-07-24T12:53:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/telomere-modification-program-started-in-february-2008.2641/post-40622>

Hey Lou,

Just wanted to say hi. We will be heading your way next month for vacation (Corpus, then South Padre Island). Lots of seafood will be consumed

Will be following your thread...

### nonchalant — 2012-07-24T15:18:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/telomere-modification-program-started-in-february-2008.2641/post-40657>

"A sedentary lifestyle (in addition to smoking, high body mass index, and low socioeconomic status) has an effect on LTL and may accelerate the aging process." I was always irritated when they mentioned 'low socioeconomic status' as a reason for poor health. It seems like a vague catch-all for unknown factors. I couldn't see how the contents of your wallet determined your health. But now I think it could be mostly food ingested, along with other things like living in a high crime neighborhood, stress and whatever.

### [email redacted] — 2012-07-24T18:48:29+0300

How did you first get involved in this program? Is this a Spectracell intiative because I know they are based in Texas? I'm based in the UK and on the odd occasion I have mentioned the word "telomere" peoples eyes sort of glaze over...interesting that they have based part of your protocol on a UK study.

At your current rate of progress you should be a biological teenager by the time your reach your chronological 80's...

### [email redacted] — 2012-07-24T21:34:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/telomere-modification-program-started-in-february-2008.2641/post-40799>

yes, you have my interest!

### LouTheNavi — 2012-07-24T22:56:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/telomere-modification-program-started-in-february-2008.2641/post-40831>

G'day mates,

Before I started the Telomere Modification Program I had severe hypertension. I had to take 2 high powered combination pills along with two pills for water retention and even then my BP was usually too high. Two years ago my BP began to improve and now I only take one pill a day (Lotensin 20mg) and that keeps my BP well within the normal range.

Also before I began the TMP regimen I had trouble sleeping and took Melatonin and Benadryl to help me sleep. Now I don't take either one and I sleep as good as I did when I was 27, which is interesting as that is the last time in my life that I weighed 95 kg as I do now.

This next item about sleeping also has changed during the TMP, it seems like a small thing but perhaps it is another good sign. Before starting the TMP I always used 2 pillows to sleep. Now I just need one and sleep the best I have in years.

Besides Telomere modification, they have done a few enhancements to my body. Just over a year ago they replaced my natural lenses in my eyes with artificial ones. Also I have 6 steel alloy teeth permanently implanted also, so no need for dentures.

I'm leaving in the morning to spend 3 days in Fiesta Texas, a theme park in San Antonio. I celebrated my 61st chronological birthday by spending 3 evenings at "Buc Days" which had some nice roller coasters, thrill rides and a launched freefall ride. I have a lot more energy than before, and I rode 96 rides and coasters. Perhaps I'll do a few more on this trip. Before modifying my telomeres I could not have done all those rides.

Cheers!

### LouTheNavi — 2012-07-31T05:43:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/telomere-modification-program-started-in-february-2008.2641/post-42016>

G'day mates,

Today I have been in the TMP for 54 months.

On 9 August and 11 August I will have a battery of tests to analyze my latest progress and additional lifestyle changes that can be made as this is similar to a building program where you rebuild a section at a time.

On 22 August there will be an evaluation of the results of the latest tests.

Cheers!

### LouTheNavi — 2012-07-31T22:16:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/telomere-modification-program-started-in-february-2008.2641/post-42105>

G'day mates,

TMP is really a hybrid of 4 sets of protocols. The Telomere Modification Program involves doing all the protocols in the 2 studies I mentioned in the first post in this thread. These protocols are combined with some other activities and supplements from other sources. Doctors monitor my progress though tests every few months.

Cheers!

### vkiernan — 2012-07-24T20:12:05+0300

How do you know you are not LS? You just posted that you were losing weight or a size or so anyway. You become LS, then you start losing weight. You have to get the leptin going in the right direction first then amazing things start to happen. That is all part of the process. How many signs do you have? You might be surprised.

### ealachan — 2012-07-24T20:38:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tmi-alert.2642/post-40764>

> **vkiernan;40949 wrote:** How do you know you are not LS? You just posted that you were losing weight or a size or so anyway. You become LS, then you start losing weight. You have to get the leptin going in the right direction first then amazing things start to happen. That is all part of the process. How many signs do you have? You might be surprised.

I thought the deal was that if you were obese, you couldn't be leptin sensitive? It's quite possible I somehow misinterpreted that.

signs I have:

hair and nails greatly improved and growing super fast

feet used to be super rough / dry all the time, are now smooth as a baby's backside

skin is clearer, with better coloring

generally calmer, less prone to anxiety attacks

thinking far more clearly (though my posts don't always show it...haha)

libido has returned from a very long hiatus

no carb cravings - haven't TRULY craved much of anything besides shrimp and steak for months now

can easily get by on two meals a day most days

usually sleep very well now (used to wake up a lot in the night, tossing and turning)

wake up before alarm most days, able to get up and get going with much more ease than previously

losing weight, though not rapidly by any means

> A. Men will notice quick weight loss.

# Thread: Ceviche recipes
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-recipes.2647/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 20
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### nonchalant — 2012-07-24T15:42:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-recipes.2647/post-40665>

I'm interested in your favorite ceviche ideas.

I've been making mine with raw seafood, lime juice, and cilantro, sit overnight.

I add stuff like avocado, cucumber, carrots, onion, etc, but I find myself just fishing (haha) out the seafood mostly anyway.

I've decided I don't like adding red cabbage, it ends up making a pink liquid, yuk.

How long does this last in the fridge? Seems to sit a couple days anyway with no problems, for me.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-24T18:17:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-recipes.2647/post-40700>

This is my favorite scallop ceviche recipe: [http://www.epicurious.com/recipes/food/views/Scallop-Ceviche-on-Black-Pasta-Cakes-with-Cilantro-Salsa-104384](http://http://www.epicurious.com/recipes/food/views/Scallop-Ceviche-on-Black-Pasta-Cakes-with-Cilantro-Salsa-104384) I have never made the rice cakes so ignore that part. I have made this both where I par-boil the scallops and used raw ones. It's easier raw so that is what I do these days. I'm nightshade free these days so don't use the tomatoes or tomatillos and add avocado instead. It's really good!

### nonchalant — 2012-07-24T19:20:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-recipes.2647/post-40725>

Thanks for the recipe, Patty. Sea salt! I haven't tried that yet.

### nonchalant — 2012-07-26T15:35:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-recipes.2647/post-41187>

Every recipe I see that calls for coconut water in the ceviche also calls for lime juice. Wonder if I need a second coconut?

### nonchalant — 2012-07-26T15:59:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-recipes.2647/post-41191>

Put the lime in the coconut, drink them both together,

Put the lime in the coconut, then you feel better.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-26T19:02:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-recipes.2647/post-41236>

when you make ceviche, does it "cook" the seafood all the way through or just on the outside? My sea scallops were only white on the outside after overnight soaking in lime juice and a bit of apple cider vinegar, the insides were still entirely raw.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-26T19:18:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-recipes.2647/post-41243>

The time you leave the fish in the juices denotes how "cooked" they will be. I've made a white fish ceviche, took some to a party and left the rest in the fridge overnight. It was a huge success at the party but the next morning what I had left in the juices in the fridge was almost rubbery. It had over "cooked". So if you want it more white you'll need to leave it in the juices longer. Also you could try maybe cutting the scallops into fourths.

Rick Bayless has a good ceviche recipe too. Just leave out the items you cannot have and you still have a great dish. I am a huge fan of ceviche. I usually end up making it with juice the lemon and lime juice, onions and fish. Sometimes I add the raw onion during the cooking then take it out when I drain off the juices and add caramelized onion for flavor.

### [email redacted] — 2012-07-26T19:32:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-recipes.2647/post-41248>

I like ceviche too. I think I like the bay scallops the best. I just use lime juice, white wine, basil and salt. Haven't been brave enough to use white fish yet...

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-26T19:40:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-recipes.2647/post-41256>

Thanks Patty. I guess I should have cut up the scallops. Was thinking of it and then felt too bad about chopping them b/c these were my first ever big sea scallops and not the little bay scallops. I wanted for them to look nice considering the price I paid for them

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-26T19:41:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-recipes.2647/post-41258>

> **Jennifer;41465 wrote:** I like ceviche too. I think I like the bay scallops the best. I just use lime juice, white wine, basil and salt. Haven't been brave enough to use white fish yet...

I'll try it with the little scallops next time. These were.... raw  I was wondering if sole fillets might work well since they are so thin?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-26T19:43:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-recipes.2647/post-41259>

I think the size is why the recipe I posted has the person par boil them. I have done it both ways but that is where I first learned my lesson on timing. Sea scallops are so beautiful they are hard to cut up. Also be careful if you mix and match fishies. Different types can "cook" faster than others. I have even gone as far as mixing the juice then dividing it up into separate containers and use one for each of the different fish. Then mix after draining. A lot of work but it realty does make a perfect ceviche.

### [email redacted] — 2012-07-26T19:57:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-recipes.2647/post-41262>

I just can't use sea scallops for ceviche because they are SO DELICIOUS pan fried in CO! Just too good. Plus they are more expensive. I made a note to myself that grouper, halibut and sea bass are good for ceviche and to avoid cod because they tend to parasites (they have a worm named after them). Although, if you cut up the fish into small pieces, you should be cutting up the buggies and killing them. OR at least that's what I read.

[http://fishcooking.about.com/od/rawfish/a/sushi\_fish.htm](http://http://fishcooking.about.com/od/rawfish/a/sushi_fish.htm)

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-26T20:02:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-recipes.2647/post-41266>

well, therefore, mackerel head smoothies must be perfectly safe

Parasites can be very small and they lay eggs....

### nonchalant — 2012-08-20T03:31:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-recipes.2647/post-45777>

I wonder about the lime juice come Winter time. Sounds like a mismatch. Guess we could rinse the lime juice off before consuming?

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-08-20T15:20:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-recipes.2647/post-45836>

Nonchalant: For winter you'd most likely want to increase high meat and fermented 'anything'. I mean for daily living. Of course if you're out and even choosing seafood at restaurant...then just get 'whatever' or still, sushi is always easy option.

Ooooooo winter...my fermented use shall increase 10000x fold. For now, I'm looking forward to fresh apples in season. The quercetin in them is nice and having them at the right time is just so ideal, not to mention delicious! End of season harvest---I'm taking advantage!!

### nonchalant — 2012-08-20T17:55:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-recipes.2647/post-45850>

Good point, Gladina. I need to check on the pickles I fermented earlier, see if they are still good. They have been sitting in the back of the fridge, I'm ashamed to say. But I fermented most of them with grass-fed whey, so I might have to just check out the salted ones.

I'm looking forward to some more homemade sauerkraut. This year I'll use organic cabbage. And no dairy. No whey! \[Sorry for the pun.\]

About the apples, perhaps I'll eat a slice or two when DH indulges. Lots of carbs there. One of these days I'll be able to take fuller advantage of the fruit seasons.

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-05T00:52:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-recipes.2647/post-49474>

Was making ceviche with scallops - lime juice and basil when I remembered I had some mago kevita in the fridge and so I threw some in....Pretty good. Don't know if it does anything to "cook" or not, but tasted ok. I'll let you know if I get deathly ill.

### Stavity — 2012-09-05T15:22:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-recipes.2647/post-49573>

I won't worry about the lime or lemon juice being a "mismatch" because it's the winter time that citrus fruit is available for me. The off season for citrus fruit here is in the summer. I am eating fish everyday, and to think I need to worry about the minutia is not going to happen, as I have a tendency towards being OCD as it is, and will hold that in check. My eating is pretty limited now and my body will tell me when something is amiss.

Though, everything about me is changing, and I have think it is eating seafood and taking vitamin D3. Even my gray hairs are looking blonde and I am getting asked everyday what I am doing to my hair. Well...I am doing nothing, chemically--I am eating fish. I have a calmness about me that has never been there, and I am thinking things out rather then reacting to them. I should also credit the Great Doc for his recommendations of certain vitamins and spices, and feel maybe I should be tested again for Vitamin D to see where I stand. That is after Oct 17 when I see the hepatologist?? again. He is doing extensive blood work and I need to take one thing at a time. Never been this excited about seeing a doctor before, but he had a sense about him I liked. Not much of a personable guy but someone with confidence and who did listen to me and take some of my feelings and ideas into consideration.

Hmmmmm, seems I am rambling. Sorry

### Stavity — 2012-09-05T15:39:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-recipes.2647/post-49575>

> **Gladina;46219 wrote:**
>
> Ooooooo winter...my fermented use shall increase 10000x fold. For now, I'm looking forward to fresh apples in season. The quercetin in them is nice and having them at the right time is just so ideal, not to mention delicious! End of season harvest---I'm taking advantage!!

Our apples have suffered huge losses do to historic weather events in winter and spring this past year. This will really have an impact on our agriculture industry. I will miss making huge amounts of apple sauce this year. It's one of my favorite foods.

I also have mixed feelings about eating loads fermented veggies, so have decided to eat them sparingly and stick to mostly, broccoli and spinach in small amounts during the winter. I feel that eating fish alone is giving me the all the vegetables I need.

### nonchalant — 2012-09-06T01:24:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-recipes.2647/post-49625>

Stavity, I know what you mean about the calmness. It's hard to describe to others. I think they just figure its all in my mind. And it is!

# Thread: the effect of protein on blood sugar
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-effect-of-protein-on-blood-sugar.2648/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ealachan — 2012-07-24T16:09:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-effect-of-protein-on-blood-sugar.2648/post-40669>

Question for the far more educated than I:

Is it true that if one were to eat a very large portion of protein in a sitting, that it would potentially raise the level of blood glucose, especially in an insulin-deficient person?

Obviously it wouldn't likely spike the BG in the way that carbs would - that I understand.

Thank you!

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-24T23:14:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-effect-of-protein-on-blood-sugar.2648/post-40838>

yes, it can cause a spike.... it does or used to for me

### ealachan — 2012-07-24T23:25:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-effect-of-protein-on-blood-sugar.2648/post-40845>

So it's basically a case of trial and error to find what amount of protein one might be able to eat without it causing a spike, then?

Man, diabetes is exhausting already, and I'm not even the one with the disease...

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-24T23:31:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-effect-of-protein-on-blood-sugar.2648/post-40848>

protein tends to cause a spike in diabetics but that doesn't mean you shouldn't eat it.... path to health. Medicating it might be best.

### ealachan — 2012-07-24T23:43:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-effect-of-protein-on-blood-sugar.2648/post-40853>

> **Souldanzer;41039 wrote:** protein tends to cause a spike in diabetics but that doesn't mean you shouldn't eat it.... path to health. Medicating it might be best.

Doctor's appt is next tuesday. Just bumbling blindly with a blood glucose monitor and info from the internet until then.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-24T23:47:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-effect-of-protein-on-blood-sugar.2648/post-40860>

traditional treatment for diabetes was fasting until BG is acceptable then slowly introducing fat and protein at a rate that BG is controlled

### Cody — 2012-07-26T18:42:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-effect-of-protein-on-blood-sugar.2648/post-41220>

Check out Jimmy Moore's N=1 experiments on dietary ketosis.

I've never been diagnosed as T2D, however, I used to weight 456 pounds, and I'm pretty sure I was T2D or pre T2D. I now weigh around 330 lbs.

I cannot eat large amounts of protein without feeling super sleep afterwards, which I'm assuming means that my body is producing quite a bit of insulin in response to the protein intake.

I have to keep my fat pretty high (upwards of 75% of calories) to get into and stay in ketosis.

I haven't tested my BG or BK yet, as I'm in the middle of a move, but I have a monitor, and plan on testing everything I put into my mouth to figure out what is going on.

### Petra — 2012-07-27T12:45:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-effect-of-protein-on-blood-sugar.2648/post-41475>

Feels to me, that in this cases it matter if you are leptin sensitive.

And indeed if your body is looking for fat as fuel or sugar, will determine if is converts protein into sugar.

But please correct me if I see this wrong.

It is a gut feeling of mine

# Thread: Circadian T3 Method for Healing Adrenals
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-t3-method-for-healing-adrenals.2649/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 30
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### bigknitwit — 2012-07-24T16:25:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-t3-method-for-healing-adrenals.2649/post-40671>

[http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/t3-circadian-method-for-adrenals/](http://http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/t3-circadian-method-for-adrenals/)

Has anybody tried this method of dosing T3 (or Armour) early in the AM? This guy says most cortisol for the day is made in the last 4 hours of sleep. He started taking T3 in those hours (you have to play with the timing and dosing) to produce a better adrenal response (measured by blood pressure, heart rate, and temperatures). People are no longer needing their hydrocortisone as a result...

I am thinking of trying it but I am hesitant because I will have a hard time gonig back to sleep after waking to take the dose. And especially because of all that Jack says about the greatness of restful sleep, etc, which I finally seem to be getting. So, does the potential benefit out weigh the cost?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-24T18:13:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-t3-method-for-healing-adrenals.2649/post-40698>

I've been taking my meds this way since the early part of this year. I tried it with the natural thyroid meds (NDT) and it made an immediate difference with my energy upon waking; I had a lot more! I am now on T3 and still doing it. It takes some work, with monitoring your heart rate, BP, temp, etc. I personally didn't have any problem going back to sleep with either the NDT or T3, but I know some people do. Get Paul's book and read on how he did it so you will know all the details of the program. I really like it and so do mnay others but it's not just a "take a pill" type program. Also it doesn't interfere with the BAB in the morning!!!

The only way to know if it will interfere with you falling back to sleep is to try it. Some people just cannot do it because it keeps them awake. I am quite a light sleeper and surprisingly I barely open my eyes when I take the pills and I am back to sleep almost immediately.

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-24T18:21:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-t3-method-for-healing-adrenals.2649/post-40702>

I take my armour 1st thing. But I'm pretty positive it's not at the correct dosage. I'm only on a 1/2 grain. However I think the cycloset rx I'll be picking up today will help some of that. Got to fix the circadian mismatch first other wise the thyroid won't get fixed.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-24T18:27:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-t3-method-for-healing-adrenals.2649/post-40704>

Shinjin do you have high FBG too? Yes, that is a tiny amount of Armour in the scope of things. Adrenals are supposed to be fixed first so it sounds like you are on the right track! My adrenals have long been my nemesis but I am starting to call them my little friends hoping they will change faster! LOL

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-24T18:34:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-t3-method-for-healing-adrenals.2649/post-40707>

> **Patty Cakes;40894 wrote:** Shinjin do you have high FBG too? Yes, that is a tiny amount of Armour in the scope of things. Adrenals are supposed to be fixed first so it sounds like you are on the right track! Yup. It ranges btwn 88-98 1st thing in the am. Not to mention my last IGF-1 was In the toilet & my TNF alpha is high And my adrenals are at the low end of normal and they get worse in summer. I'm a Winter Woman!! The joy of having both PCOS & being E- dominant coupled to circadian mismatches. Working on fixing those.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-24T18:39:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-t3-method-for-healing-adrenals.2649/post-40709>

It's weird how all these things interconnect and most docs have no idea. My IGF-1 is in the toilet too and no one ever even tested me for that before Dr. K. I love reading about the connections people are making with their individual issues even if they don't mimic mine. It's like a bunch of light bulbs all going off to further illuminate the dark we live in. Thanks for sharing!

### chocolate — 2012-07-24T18:45:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-t3-method-for-healing-adrenals.2649/post-40710>

I'm wondering about exactly that. I think my daughter might just be depleted on a bunch of stuff from stress. I can't figure how iodine plays exactly into it, but whenever she takes it... 15 mg... the day before, she's fine all day goes to sleep and practically convulses awake, not the first wake-up, but the morning wake up. I'm sure the selenium must not be up to par, but the cortisol timing seems right. Of course the iodine isn't an option yet, at least orally. We were hoping to use it for an anti-fungal. I have hoping someone would have some quick ideas about what might be happening in relation to the cortisol.

### bigknitwit — 2012-07-25T14:21:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-t3-method-for-healing-adrenals.2649/post-40962>

I did my first 2am Armour dose last night. Stayed awake until 3am, but then right back to sleep until 7am with lots of dreaming. Can't say I feel any better this morning though, and my stats are still similar to other mornings (92/60, 84bpm), mayb even a little worse (higher heart rate). I'm beginning to think I can't tolerate the Armour at all, and need to go back to synthroid, re-stabilise, and then gradually ease off. But I'll be giving this a longer try before that kind of a drastic move.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-25T20:47:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-t3-method-for-healing-adrenals.2649/post-41052>

When you start the T3 CM method you should take the dose about two hours before you usually wake up. You adjust the timing back as well as the dose, as you see how your body reacts. There is a lot too this method but I think you took it too early to start. Also because of the fillers in Armour you should break it up, chew it if you will, before ingesting. It helps with the absorption. I personally do not like the brand Armour and much prefer NP Thyroid by Acella for a natural product.

Thyroid-S was my fav of all but it had some weird stuff in it so I stopped taking it and moved to NP. I'm hoping the fish and sea veggies are my ticket as it seems I've tried everything else.

### JackStapleton — 2012-09-23T16:31:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/for-those-that-count-grams-of-protein-for-bab-best-practice.2650/post-53584>

I was simply eating a REALLY BIG breakfast and assumed I had it covered. I went and looked it up this morning. Looks like I was wrong. An egg has 6 grams of protein. Two slices of bacon have 3. So if I ate **6 eggs and 6** slices of bacon I would only get to 45 grams of protein, not enough if 50 grams is the goal.

Apparently my idea of big was not nearly big enough. Are people really eating more than 6 eggs and 6 slices of bacon for breakfast??? Wow.

I thought I was already pigging out with 4 eggs and 5 slices of bacon, which seems already double the "norm". So I added a cup of chili. Still not at 50 grams. Only 47.

# Thread: CT helps Control Migraine pain!!!!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-helps-control-migraine-pain.2651/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Shijin13 — 2012-07-24T16:57:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-helps-control-migraine-pain.2651/post-40681>

So I woke up feeling a bit off this am (I was up at 515) then around 8am BLAM! a MIGRAINE launched a sneak attack on me....

I waited a bit to see if it truly was a migraine - thinking it away - no such luck.

so about 30mins ago I put ICE Packs on my carotid artery, and held them in place w/an ACE Bandage -

CTing my carotid arteries w/ICE packs to help w/my Migraine... OMG w/in 15mins the pain has decreased measurably ! I still have an aura, and some pain - but I can function!!! I still have a migraine but the pain is bearable!

### Lyndra — 2012-07-24T19:03:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-helps-control-migraine-pain.2651/post-40716>

Wow, that's awesome. I'm going to try that myself.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-24T19:29:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-helps-control-migraine-pain.2651/post-40729>

Cold will constrict your arteries just like caffeine will. Most migraines are due to unnaturally relaxed blood vessels. My neurologist refused me migraine meds b/c I apparently am doing very well managing things myself  I told him I dunk my head in buckets of ice water and then pack my entire head in bags of ice including carotid arteries.

He missed the point that I want to \*function\* not be tied to a bucket and a bag of ice all day long. But yes, he was thrilled about the ice and knew that it works.

My mother always treats her migraines with a hefty dose of caffeine.

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-24T19:38:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-helps-control-migraine-pain.2651/post-40740>

# Thread: How far down the food chain did we go for our big brains?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-far-down-the-food-chain-did-we-go-for-our-big-brains.2654/>
**Subforum:** Factor X
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### MJ* — 2012-07-24T19:07:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-far-down-the-food-chain-did-we-go-for-our-big-brains.2654/post-40717>

Here is a very interesting [article](http://http://www.algaeindustrymagazine.com/algae-101-part-eleven-did-algae-make-us-human/) on alge and it's potential role in encephalation. Could alge be something we should include in our diets? Is anyone supplementing with Spirulina?

"Down the food chain:

Rather than moving up the food chain to game meat, early hominoidsâ€™ first step may instead have been down the food chain when they ingested algae in their drinking water. Consuming algae may have been intentional but more likely was incidental because the tiny algal cells were visible only in the sense that they turned the water slightly green. The lakes and wetlands in the Rift Valley where humans developed larger brains are home to some of the oldest lakes and wetlands on Earth that produce plentiful natural stands of the high protein and nutrient rich spirulina algae. Spirulina is the best selling algal nutritive supplement on the market today because it provides a complete set of essential nutrients. A hominoid tribe on the lee side of an algae lake may have ingested several grams of algae daily in their drinking water. These few grams of algae would not have provided sufficient roughage or protein for a full diet. Algae would have acted as a natural food supplement to supply the essential nutrients, vitamins and antioxidants that provided the green spark for encephalation."

### WannaBeOptimal — 2012-07-25T18:08:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-far-down-the-food-chain-did-we-go-for-our-big-brains.2654/post-41014>

Very high in protein...most of the lipid content is GLA.

DHA content?

### David — 2012-07-25T23:13:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-far-down-the-food-chain-did-we-go-for-our-big-brains.2654/post-41086>

WiKiPedia sez: "Spirulina's lipid content is about 7% by weight, and is rich in gamma-linolenic acid (GLA), and also provides alpha-linolenic acid (ALA), linoleic acid (LA), stearidonic acid (SDA), eicosapentaenoic acid (EPA), docosahexaenoic acid (DHA) and arachidonic acid (AA)."

### WannaBeOptimal — 2012-07-26T01:13:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-far-down-the-food-chain-did-we-go-for-our-big-brains.2654/post-41108>

Looks like DHA is pretty far down on the totem pole.

### chocolate — 2012-07-26T03:08:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-far-down-the-food-chain-did-we-go-for-our-big-brains.2654/post-41131>

I have some and take it if it crosses my path. I don't take it regularly.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-27T16:09:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-far-down-the-food-chain-did-we-go-for-our-big-brains.2654/post-41516>

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/farmers-forestry-workers-and-fishermen-happier-than-the-rest-of-us-ons-study-to-fin.2655/post-40731>

Farmers, forestry workers and fishermen happier than the rest of us, ONS study to find

[http://www.bna.org.uk/news/view.php?permalink=KQSP72P2YJ](http://http://www.bna.org.uk/news/view.php?permalink=KQSP72P2YJ)

> Those who work outdoors in the countryside are happier than the rest of us Photo: Alamy
>
>
>
> Results from the Governmentâ€™s first ever survey of National Well Being, published by the Office for National Statistics, are likely to show that people who work in farming, forestry and fishing are happier than others.
>
>
>
> The ONS â€“ more used to weighing up public sector finances or crime statistics â€“ was given the rather subjective task of asking how happy Britons are by Prime Minister David Cameron in 2010.
>
>
>
> Ministers want to use the information to allow young people to make informed choices about their jobs â€“ asking whether they will be happy, rather than whether they will earn a lot of money.
>
>
>
> The prime mover behind the 'happiness index' has been Mr Cameron who said when he became Conservative leader in 2005 that gauging people's wellbeing was among the "central political issues of our time".
>
>
>
> He said then: "It's time we focused not just on GDP but on GWB â€” general well being." Previous surveys have suggested that Britons' happiness has remained static for 25 years
>
>
>
> According to provisional data from the ONS's Well Being study - which have been seen by The Daily Telegraph â€“ 20 different industry sectors were ranked according to â€œmean life satisfactionâ€.
>
>
>
> The study - which is published in full on Tuesday morning - found that at the top of the satisfaction list were people who work in â€œagriculture, forestry and fishingâ€.
>
>
>
> **That was followed in the top third of the satisfaction index - perhaps surprisingly - by â€œpeople working in mining, quarrying, â€œreal estate activitiesâ€, electricity and gas supply and â€œwater supply, sewerage and wasteâ€.
>
> **
>
> Propping up the list were those working in â€œadmin and support servicesâ€, transport and storageâ€, â€œaccommodation and food servicesâ€ and â€œwholesale repair of vehiclesâ€.
>
>
>
> Even people who are paid to cheer us up did not appear to be a very happy with their lot, with those working in â€œarts, entertainment and recreationâ€ ranked below bankers and insurers for job satisfaction.
>
>
>
> The study covered the quality of life of people in the UK, environmental and sustainability issues, as well as the economic performance of the country.

WFM gave me some beef fat for free. I asked for the grass-fed beef fat, but they didn't have any at the time.

When I rendered lard from a local farm, I tried cutting up the first batch. Lots of work! The second batch I just tossed into the pot as a lump. It came out fine. Probably took a little longer, but I wasn't standing around watching it anyway. As it started cooking a bit, I used a metal spatula and 'cut' it into smaller pieces.

### Dali Dula — 2012-07-25T05:25:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/made-my-own-lard-from-my-pastured-piggy.2657/post-40926>

> **Souldanzer;41040 wrote:** slow cookers work great if you don't feel like watching it

slow cooker is the way to go. put the fat in whole and slice it up right in the pot as it softens. 3-4 hours. pull it before the proteins start browning. strain or filter. pour into jars. freeze.

### [email redacted] — 2012-07-26T03:00:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/made-my-own-lard-from-my-pastured-piggy.2657/post-41128>

So on my lard post, I was posed this question in the comments:

I’d love to see a comparison of pasture raised lard to regular lard to margarine. Is conventionally raised pork healthier than margarine? If you can’t afford pasture raised it helps to know these things.

I don't know the answer definitively, but I would guess the lard from a conventionally raised pig would beat out the nutritional profile of margarine any day. But then... Anyone want to go weigh in on this or have a good study or links to share?

# Thread: Optimizing Methlyation
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimizing-methlyation.2658/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 90
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-07-24T19:57:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimizing-methlyation.2658/post-40749>

The purpose of this thread is to have a consolidated place to discuss our attempts to optimize methlyation and related biochemical pathways. Many of us have done the 23andMe test and/or methlyation panels and compared the results to information available on-line based on the work of Dr. Amy Yasko (who primarily uses this info to treat autistic children). Use this thread to discuss test results, share information and articles, and to talk about the dietary and supplementation strategies.

If you are interested in reading up on this topic here are some great links over on this thread:

[http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?1866-Methylation-issues-and-the-Leaky-Gut&highlight=methylation](http://http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?1866-Methylation-issues-and-the-Leaky-Gut&highlight=methylation)

### freshveggies — 2012-07-24T23:44:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimizing-methlyation.2658/post-40856>

HI, I have been searching for 6 years as to why my health has been going down hill and I think it is pointing to my methylation defects expressing themself. I have run the 23 and me panel and a methylation panel from Health Diagnositics. Here are my methylation snips from 23 and me:

ACE--Not available

CBS A360 -/-

CBS C699T +/- (CBS generates sulfur breakdown products (sulfite and sulfate, which stimulate the stress/cortisol “fight or flight” response)

COMT H62H -/-

Comt V158M -/-

COMT L136L

COMT-61 -/-

MOA A +/+

MTHFR C677T +/-

MTHFR A1298C -/-

MTHFR 03 P39P -/-

MTR A2756G -/-

MTRR H595Y -/-

Mtrr K350A -/-

MTRR R415T -/-

MTRR S257T N/A

MTRR A919G -/+

MTRR-11 A664A +/-

NOS 3 N/A

SOUX S370S N/A

SOUX A628G n/a

VDR BSM TAQ -/-

VDR Fok I N/A

ACAT1-02 -/-

AHCY-01 -/-

AHCY-02 -/-

AHCY-19 -/-

BHMT-01 n/a

BHMT -02 -/-

BHMT -04 -/-

BHMT-08 -/+

SHMT C1420T -/-

The ones that are +/+ are Homozygous and there is 2 copies of the mutation. One from each parent.

\-/+ is Heterozygous one copy of the mutation

\-/- no mutation

Here is my Health Diagnostics test:

I did Health Diagnostics test: March 2012

Gutathione oxidized .51 range .16-.50

Glutathione redued 3.3 range 3.8-5.5

s-adenosylmethionine 237 range 221-256

s-adensoylhomocysteiine 56.8 range 38-49

folic acid derviates

5-CH3-THF 15.0 range 8.4-72.6

10-Formyl-THF 4.3 range 1.5-8.2

5-Formyl-THF 4.90 range 1.2-11.70

THF 1.15 range 06-6.80

Folic Acid 18.5 range 8.9-24.6

Folic Acid (WB) 17-3 range 9.0-.35.5

Folic Aci active RBC 417 range 400-1500

Since I have a CBS and BHMT defect, it is recommended that I start with working on them. My diet is suppose to be low sulfur and I will be adding in TMG, acetyl L-Carnitine, Yucca, gaba.

### nonchalant — 2012-07-24T23:51:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimizing-methlyation.2658/post-40862>

Extra Vitamin B complex is needed, right? Plus PQQ?

### freshveggies — 2012-07-24T23:55:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimizing-methlyation.2658/post-40865>

> **Nonchalant;41053 wrote:** Extra Vitamin B complex is needed, right? Plus PQQ?

Right now because of a CBS defect, I need to stay away from B6 and can have a little bit of B6 in the P5P form. So I am not on any b-complex right now.

### [email redacted] — 2012-07-25T03:07:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimizing-methlyation.2658/post-40897>

From the anecdotal evidence that I have read only having 1 copy of CBS is not too too bad. That said there is some stuff below on your CBS issue. I like your idea of the sulfur dip strip testing and monitoring to see how your diet impacts those levels. I think that will help you know if you need to focus on a low sulfur diet or not.

I too have two MTRRs, but I only share one with you - MTRR-11. From what I have read 415 is the tough one, so we both dodged the bullet on that one! But MTRR means you do not recycle B12 very well. Since you are COMT-/- you can go with Mb12 or a mix of methly, hydroxy and andenoyl. Some people recommend working up to 3-8 mg/day.

What have you been using for B12 and how much?

MTHFR C677T one means you will not use folic acid very well, and need to take folinic & mthf folate (Yasko says 200mg of each a day, others have used more and seen benefit).

CBS C699T. This one means you will end up with high ammonia and high taurine if you eat "too much" protein. That is where the urine amino acid test might come in handy.

### [email redacted] — 2012-07-25T03:18:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimizing-methlyation.2658/post-40901>

Oh I also read that alpha ketoglutarate will help lower the ammonia levels. Personally I have not tried this one.

From: [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alpha-Ketoglutaric\_acid](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alpha-Ketoglutaric_acid)

"...studies that show excess ammonia in the body can combine with alpha-ketoglutarate, reducing problems associated with ammonia toxicity"

### [email redacted] — 2012-07-25T03:52:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimizing-methlyation.2658/post-40906>

Just a little bit more....

COMT-- = needs lots of methyl groups. So methyl B12 is a very good thing. SAMe and DMG would be two others to consider adding.

BHMT 8 +- is not too severe, but DMG would probably be a good supp to consider adding if there are any attention/focus issues (and only after you have a good amount of B12 in place first).

MAO ++. That is the enzyme that breaks down serotonin. Can means either high serotonin (aggressive, sometimes short tempered or "prickly") or serotonin swings (moody, with some of those moods aggressive/prickly). Support for this is frequent dosing of very low dose 5htp and/or st johns wort. Some people recommend trying 1/2 cap of a 50mg 5htp capsules 4x a day. I know it sounds counterintuitive to take 5htp for high serotonin (5htp turns into serotonin), but it helps regulate the body's biofeedback (high serotonin turns off serotonin production, which causes swings).

### freshveggies — 2012-07-25T04:20:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimizing-methlyation.2658/post-40908>

> I too have two MTRRs, but I only share one with you - MTRR-11. From what I have read 415 is the tough one, so we both dodged the bullet on that one! But MTRR means you do not recycle B12 very well. Since you are COMT-/- you can go with Mb12 or a mix of methly, hydroxy and andenoyl. Some people recommend working up to 3-8 mg/day.
