>
> Have any of you ever heard of Apple Cider Vinegar for UTI's? Lots of anecdotal evidence on web that it works by changing ph of urine.

AKMan, I'm really sorry to hear about your wife's struggles. +1000 on getting Knock Out. You don't have to have cancer to benefit from this book.... and it's very empowering when dealing with the medical system because you can a) support yourself and b) know what good care looks like. (You definitely need to prepare the body for surgery and surgery-recovery, too, even if it isn't cancer... and one of the last chapters talks about supporting the body when choosing conventional means, too, so something for everyone.)

ACV is great stuff and I'm sure it's helpful. As are probiotics, fermented veggies, etc. The thing is, once you get to the point she is at (and I can relate, even though my issues presented differently), the small helpful things that heal others won't get you there. I pretty much had to eliminate all green veggies and cruciferous ones for a long while. I concentrated on good fat and good protein, now with a focus on seafood.

Has she had any blood testing? A basic work up (and a consult with Dr K) would likely be a huge wake up call.

In my case, getting sick was exactly the wake up call I needed. When you can 'manage' and get by you don't tend to do the radical shifts you need to really address the problems. I hope this illness can be what she needs to light the proverbial fire. Especially as she's already had skin cancer.

Good luck to you both!

### Joann — 2012-07-26T23:57:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kidney-tumor-recurrent-bladder-infections.2697/post-41355>

Akman, this sounds so familiar to me what you are saying about your wife.

I am assuming she does not use hormones. A hysterectomy = no estradiol which is needed in bladder area ....without enough estrogen the urethra gets too thin and can cause uti.

estriol is a weak estrogen but works well.

Susanne Somers definitely uses BHRT, just saying

### AKMan — 2012-07-27T00:16:01+0300

She had a really bad case of wrist tendonitis and did two rounds of cortisone shots in the past year, wonder if there's a connection since that's about when the UTIs started showing up...

She was in the hospital getting a cystoscopy a couple weeks ago to check for bladder cancer (all clear), they started an IV of Cipro antibiotics and as soon as it hit her vein, she started breaking out in big red welts up her arm. They stopped that quickly and used a different antibiotic, not sure what, but ever since when she takes the antibiotics they gave her for UTI, she gets hives all over and must take Benedryl along with it.

Thanks for talking this out with me, she is under doctor care and has tons of labwork, maybe someday when this is all under control (or not) we can set something up with Jack.

### AKMan — 2012-07-27T00:53:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kidney-tumor-recurrent-bladder-infections.2697/post-41367>

> **indigogirl;41583 wrote:** Akman,
>
> Please take this with a grain of salt as only your wife and you know what is best for her...
>
>
>
> If you can afford it the time to talk to Jack is now, before her surgery...not "when this is all under control". You need him now to get things under control!
>
>
>
> Also is Estratest a bioidentical hormone? There is a huge difference between synthetic and bioidentical hormones.

According to their website, Estratest is composed of:

Esterified Estrogens

Esterified Estrogens, USP is a mixture of the sodium salts of the sulfate esters of the estrogenic substances, principally estrone, that are of the type excreted by pregnant mares. Esterified Estrogens contain not less than 75.0 percent and not more than 85.0 percent of sodium estrone sulfate, and not less than 6.0 percent and not more than 15.0 percent of sodium equilin sulfate, in such proportion that the total of these two components is not less than 90.0 percent.

Methyltestosterone

Methyltestosterone, USP is an androgen. Androgens are derivatives of cyclopentano-perhydrophenanthrene. Endogenous androgens are C-19 steroids with a side chain at C-17, and with two angular methyl groups. Testosterone is the primary endogenous androgen. Fluoxymesterone and methyltestosterone are synthetic derivatives of testosterone.

So can anyone tell me if it's considered real or synthetic?

As to getting her to talk to Jack, she has no clue who Jack is other than me saying, "I'm in a tub of ice because Jack says it good", just kidding, kinda, but she isn't a blog-reading, web crawler like us--she works with her doctors, does about half of what they say and chalks the rest up to genetics. She's a big girl and uses the system like 98% of other Americans. I have heard Jack say he gives advice and only a portion take it at first, later they come crawling back and are more open to his advice.

AKMan, I know it's been said repeatedly, but I want to suggest again a Skype consult with Dr K. Get whatever labs she has, send them to Dr K, and book a consult... before her surgery. First off, he IS a doctor and she listens to doctors. (His is not only a neurosurgeon, but has been treating people and consulting with people along his paradigm for years now.) Secondly, as someone who has spent a lot of time in front of doctors lately, she will have an amazing experience listening to Dr K. He really speaks to you, in a synthesized way, with YOUR information in mind. He doesn't just give general recommendations for 'conditions like this' he talks about YOU and your specific information. It is totally rare to have someone pay that close attention to you medically... and it is powerful. So even when he gives you the proverbial kick in the pants, it has meaning that other doctors don't get close to.

I totally know what you mean about opening up personally. I feel that way, too. But I found with my consult (and on the forum) that the most sensitive details of my life were not only handled skillfully, but in ways that OPENED up doors for me to do something real and dramatic about it - for the better. She does not have to be a blog reader or forum-poster to get the full benefit. And you will also get the full benefit of the big picture too. Which will give you good information and also help you determine how committed your wife is going to be at this point.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-27T02:56:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kidney-tumor-recurrent-bladder-infections.2697/post-41412>

PS, some research that ties Dr K's work to your wife's (and all of us like her!) desire to eat comfort foods when she doesn't feel well....

**Leptin concentrations in response to acute stress predict subsequent intake of comfort foods**

_Physiology and Behaviour, 2012_

Both animals and humans show a tendency toward eating more “comfort food” (high fat, sweet food) after

acute stress. Such stress eating may be contributing to the obesity epidemic, and it is important to understand

the underlying psychobiological mechanisms. Prior investigations have studied what makes individuals eat

more after stress; this study investigates what might make individuals eat less. Leptin has been shown to

increase following a laboratory stressor, and is known to regulate satiety. This study examined whether

leptin reactivity accounts for individual differences in stress eating. To test this, we exposed forty women

to standardized acute psychological laboratory stress (Trier Social Stress Test) while blood was sampled

repeatedly for measurements of plasma leptin. We then measured food intake after the stressor. Increasing

leptin during the stressor predicted lower intake of comfort food. These initial ﬁndings suggest that acute

changes in leptin may be one of the factors modulating down the consumption of comfort food following

stress.

### chocolate — 2012-07-29T18:45:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kidney-tumor-recurrent-bladder-infections.2697/post-41807>

[http://www.nlm.nih.gov/medlineplus/druginfo/natural/875.html](http://http://www.nlm.nih.gov/medlineplus/druginfo/natural/875.html)

> Improving recovery after surgery. Taking L-arginine with ribonucleic acid (RNA) and eicosapentaenoic acid (EPA) before surgery or afterwards seems to help reduce the recovery time, reduce the number of infections, and improve wound healing after surgery.
> The following doses have been studied in scientific research:
>
>
>
> BY MOUTH:
>
>
>
> For congestive heart failure: doses range from 6-20 grams per day, as three divided doses.
>
> For chest pain associated with coronary artery disease (angina pectoris): 3-6 grams three times per day for up to one month.
>
> For preventing the loss of the effectiveness of nitroglycerin in relieving pain in people with chest pain due to coronary artery disease (angina pectoris): 700 mg four times daily.
>
> For organic erectile dysfunction (ED): 5 grams per day. Taking lower doses might not be effective.
>
> For preventing inflammation of the digestive tract in premature infants: 261 mg/kg added to oral feedings daily for the first 28 days of life.

### PrimalPam — 2012-07-29T18:59:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kidney-tumor-recurrent-bladder-infections.2697/post-41811>

AKMan, I used to get UTI's all the time, starting in my early 20's. So often that I'd just call my doctor and they'd write Rx for anti-b's over the phone.

Cranberry, apple cider, washing thoroughly, etc....zero help.

So bad that I carried AZO Standard (pain relief for UTI's) in my purse constantly in case I got a UTI before I could get anti-b's.

I know your wife doesn't want to hear it, but what stopped them (and I haven't had one now in over 2 years)...Paleo diet. Cutting out sugar & grain.

Your wife saying because she's thin she can eat whatever she wants is the problem. As Dr. K. says, looks like a rockstar on the outside, but a mess on the inside.

If she can't see that sugar & grain are the source, and the anti-b's have basically ruined her gut flora and are keeping the problem going, then you have to find a way to get her to see this.

Leave her because she won't eat epi-Paleo? lol, of course not. But you care about her and don't want to see her further down the hole.

Shoot, I just had this huge talk with my parents about all this stuff, but they say "We're in our late 70's, what's a little pasta going to hurt us now?" So I hear you.

Gave my mom a bag of Organic unsweetened coconut and told her to eat a spoonful of it with every meal. Guess what she tells me last night..."Oh I love the coconut! I put it on my cereal, on my fruit, in my ice cream". Sheesh.

### AKMan — 2012-09-20T19:33:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kidney-tumor-recurrent-bladder-infections.2697/post-52938>

> **AKMan;41464 wrote:** Hey guys, just wanted to talk this out with ya'all since you guys are like family...
>
>
>
> My wife, 55, 5'2, 120lbs. Skinny her whole life. Lots of medical issues through the years. Interstitial Cystitis, Endometriosis, total hysterectomy 6 years ago due to large teratoma, GERD, IBS, lactose intolerant, stomach ulcers a few years ago.
>
>
>
> Around December, she got a UTI and they gave her antibiotics. After 6 months of rounds, she is still getting UTI's and bladder infections. At my urging, she got an MRI a couple weeks ago and they found a 1.7cm tumor on her kidney. She's got a surgery date of 15 Sep to get it removed via DaVinci surgery at the Swedish Medical Center in Seattle.
>
>
>
> She's not on board with paleo or CT, but since I'm the cook, I make sure she eats pretty good. She has always had a sweet tooth and gets lots of calories from sugar. I can't control everything she eats, but I do dog her a bit hoping she will see the folly of her ways.
>
>
>
> I was reading about Apple Cider Vinegar for bladder infections and bought her a bottle yesterday. I think she needs to get over this infection problem before her surgery, but I really don't see how she can. We have bone broth a couple times a week, but she is such a picky eater with her Lactose Intolerance, GERD and IBS that anything out of her comfort zone is hard for her to eat. Most green veggies are out, she hates seafood, she eats lots of chicken, rice, and venison.
>
>
>
> It's hard for me to watch this all unfold, because I can't see it ending well. Any thoughts?

Well, we just got back from Seattle. Her surgery went well, they removed the tumour from her kidney and found it to be a non-cancerous oncocytoma. So now several weeks of down-time for her with lots of bone broth and as much seafood as I can get her to eat, which won't be much.

It was an incredible experience seeing a huge medical complex like the Swedish Center in Seattle. It's in an area called First Hill in downtown Seattle, block after block of high-rise hospitals, cancer centers, transplant centers..amazing, and boggles the mind to think how much money goes through an area like this. Hospital food was pathetic, sleep impossible for recovering surgery patients.

Anyway, just wanted to follow up on wife's status.

### tweety — 2012-07-26T20:04:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/saliva-test-update-help-please.2698/post-41267>

So I did a new saliva test. My 12noon cortisol came up, 4pm cortisol went down some, but my hormones just further went down. I have been using adrenal support since then, and progesterone cream since Nov. I started DHEA keto (50mg) a few months ago. Clearly what I am currently doing is not really making much of an impact. Interestingly, I think the progesterone cream I am on is all being converted to cortisol, not progesterone. I am gaining roughly 1-2lbs a month, (on my waist) and the thought is bcs my progesterone is becoming cortisol. What things should I consider next? I review this with my new dr in less than 2 weeks, but I want to be prepared.

**Cortisol****

**6am:

Jan12 11 (range 13-24) July12 1.7 (1.8-3.8)

**12pm:

Jan12 4 (range 5-10) July12 .32 (.3-1.4)

**4pm:

Jan12 4 (range 3-8) July12 .24 (.1 - 1.1)

**10pm:**

Jan12 2 (range 1-4) July12 .22 (.1-.8)

Hormones**

DHEA-S**

Jan12 6 (range 3-10) July12 .07 (.1 - 1.3)

17OH Progesterone**

Jan12 25 (22-100) July12 14.7 (15.4-60.5)

Also tested this time:

E1** .6 (range

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-26T20:24:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/saliva-test-update-help-please.2698/post-41277>

Maybe you need some pregnenolone. You may have preg steal going on where all your pregnenolone is going to cortisol and nothing to progesterone. Also how are your light cycles? Cortisol seems to be a big issue for you (and me!). The weight gain may be due to the cortisol too. Especially if it's around the belly.

Where did you do your testing?

### tweety — 2012-07-26T21:03:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/saliva-test-update-help-please.2698/post-41300>

The first time was Diagnos-techs Saliva test. This time was Access.

I am pretty good on my light cycles. I usually go to bed around 9 or 9:30. I do read a little and will be switching to an orange bulb by the bedside. But I wake without an alarm, usually at the same time every day.

Yes, cortisol is my big issue. I am so tempted to tweak supplements, but I suppose I will wait until my appointment. Pregnenolone would probably be good, maybe some PS to reduce my cortisol?

### tweety — 2012-07-26T21:44:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/saliva-test-update-help-please.2698/post-41310>

### nonchalant — 2012-07-26T22:15:13+0300

I never really looked at the results you posted but it looks like you need more hormones period! I'd add pregnenolone and up the DHEA. Do you have a pregnenolone level? And more saturated fat and cholesterol, vitamin A and CT to get your thyroid to make some T3 for conversion, iodine, seaweed and seafood.

Basically, do what we're all doing to save our hormones

### tweety — 2012-07-26T23:30:24+0300

### vkiernan — 2012-07-27T01:32:19+0300

Not sure. I was in your boat but that was due to detox from Ct but I was in 53 degree water too. I was always that way every month before I had thermal ablation done. It was due to est. dominance. I am not really sure but it seems you are not that dominant but who is to say that things don't go awry at that time. I don't know when you did the assay in relation to cycle day. Gotta know that. I am also confused as to which day you should do it. I have heard day 21 and I thought Jack said day 2 or 3 but I can't find it anymore. You appear to be low in prog. and perhaps the good E too. Maybe you need both. Colleen C. is the master of this. Maybe you could hijack her HCG thread and ask her. Hang in there another month and see what happens. I am not sure that progesterone fixes things in a month or two. Might take more time and different dosing. If you could do blood tests at the right cycle day, I think you would know if that is the problem or not.

### Tizawee — 2012-07-27T15:57:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/saliva-test-update-help-please.2698/post-41511>

The body makes between 20 - 40 mg progesterone daily. Supplementing with cream, you would use that much. (I prefer twice daily doses because it's more natural than dumping it on all at once.) Supplementing orally, you need to use 200 - 400 mg because it passes through the liver and bioavailability goes down to 10%.

I know it sounds counter-intuitive, but you might need some estrogen. I had estrogen dominance symptoms like crazy. PCP put me on sublingual Biest/Prog/DHEA and symptoms have subsided somewhat (we still need to tweak the dose). I was skeptical, to say the least, but it's working.

It's probably a good idea. My salivary estradiol was at 0.7. I was convinced I didn't need any because of my symptoms. After awhile with just the progesterone cream, my estrogen dominance symptoms returned. I have been on the combination for 2 months now & so far, so good.

My labs are here: [http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?1833-Tis-A-Wee-Optimal-Journey](http://http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?1833-Tis-A-Wee-Optimal-Journey) . Salivary hormones have their own section. When looking at my labs, please bear in mind that I'm 3 years post-menopause.

# Thread: how to interpret my thyroid labs

**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### JimG — 2012-07-26T20:52:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-interpret-my-thyroid-labs.2699/post-41293>

can some one point how to calculate the ratios and if some parameter is missing? Thanks

Reverse T3, Serum - 29.4 ng/dL

TSH - 1.65 uIU/mL

Triiodothyronine,Free,Serum - 3.4 pg/mL

T4,Free(Direct) - 1.27 ng/dL

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-26T23:04:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-interpret-my-thyroid-labs.2699/post-41336>

Here is the ratio calculator for the rT3: [http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/rt3-ratio/](http://http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/rt3-ratio/)

If you want help interpreting, please post the ranges.

### vkiernan — 2012-07-27T01:40:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-interpret-my-thyroid-labs.2699/post-41392>

I get 11.6 for a ratio, needs some work to get your RT3 down and your FT3 up.

### JimG — 2012-07-27T06:46:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-interpret-my-thyroid-labs.2699/post-41468>

i get that too from the above calculator, i guess i need a eat of seafood now...

### vkiernan — 2012-07-27T14:44:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-interpret-my-thyroid-labs.2699/post-41489>

> **jimg;41686 wrote:** i get that too from the above calculator, i guess i need a eat of seafood now...

I've been working on my ratio for a year. I started with 7.8 and am now 17.8 or the near vicinity. Anyway, what are you currently doing for it?

### JimG — 2012-07-27T20:31:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-interpret-my-thyroid-labs.2699/post-41561>

> **vkiernan;41710 wrote:** I've been working on my ratio for a year. I started with 7.8 and am now 17.8 or the near vicinity. Anyway, what are you currently doing for it?

o thats good, congrats! quite a jump but a year sounds long to me, I need to ask you what you have been doing to help the ratio, I mean I know seafood will be the obvious response but any supplements ?

I recently added seafood to my template from being a vegetarian so its a rough patch for me, I have eating butt load of eggs for a year now with the top supplements, like vit-B, minerals, fish oil and many more & yes some small time CT... I am wondering any more different kind of testing I need to do to zero in into any underlying issues...

### vkiernan — 2012-07-27T23:14:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-interpret-my-thyroid-labs.2699/post-41586>

> **jimg;41782 wrote:** o thats good, congrats! quite a jump but a year sounds long to me, I need to ask you what you have been doing to help the ratio, I mean I know seafood will be the obvious response but any supplements ?
>
>
>
> I recently added seafood to my template from being a vegetarian so its a rough patch for me, I have eating butt load of eggs for a year now with the top supplements, like vit-B, minerals, fish oil and many more & yes some small time CT... I am wondering any more different kind of testing I need to do to zero in into any underlying issues...

Yes, you need to figure out why your T4 is converting to RT3 instead of T3. A lot of people go on T3 only to clear out the RT3 but you still have to know why it is not converting to what it is supposed to otherwise you just treating the thyroid and not the real problem.

Unfortunately it has been a year, well over a year now. I had to figure out why mine was converting to RT3. but I didn't know this out of the gate. I knew something was wrong and started reading 2 years ago and last year had sinus/reconstructive nose surgery and I didn't recover very fast. This was a huge red flag for me. I realized I had a thyroid problem. Started on T4 - of course, CW! I knew that wasn't right and begged for armour but it was a no go. Found a new doc and she put me on Naturethroid. She also gave me progesterone. I was having a rough summer, 2nd one in a row and this I found strange. Injured the summer before along with bizarre rashes and bee sting that swelled my whole arm up. The next summer (last) I got into poison ivy 2x along with whatever else happened that I can't remember. The first time I had it OMG bad. Ended up on prednisone for awhile. In the mean time, the new doc did a ASI test and my cortisol was whacked but didn't show fatigue per say. But life goes on and you believe what you are told even though you know better. She should have listened to me and learned my history instead of assuming it was nothing. I had been on prednisone which probably screwed up my ASI results. Got fed up with "here, if you are cranky put more on (progesterone) and take L-theanine for the adrenals. Found another new doc who knew immediately that I had severe adrenal fatigue. This doc ended up putting me on HC for the adrenals but doesn't believe in bio's and I needed them. So I am seeing yet another new doc in 1.5 weeks and took myself of the HC. Don't know what will come of that, but I will be seeing Dr. K when he sets up his new deal. Tired of messing around and wasting my life, my time and my dollars.

Sorry about the life story. My point is you need to test further. There is a problem cuz you are converting to RT3. Could be a mulitude of things. I'm not sure seafood or supps is going to fix the problem or at least probably not for awhile. I CT as much as I can. I can get my water to 53 but only last 30 minutes. I have been CT'ing since February and don't feel like I am making progress with length of time but it is helping me for sure. I think CT'ing and eating this way is what is working for me and paying attn. to my circadian rhythm. I have to get my hormones balanced and my adrenals healed if I am ever going to get to optimal. You cannot fix this stuff overnight but you can fix it. First though you have to know what is wrong. If you can get tests, go for as many as you can and start solving your problem today. Don't wait for seafood and supps to solve it all. Remember, food always trumps supps per Dr. K. If you can, get labs done and do a consult with Jack if you haven't. Money well spent.

Did you read the lab blog? It will tell what you need if you can get them. ASI with DHEA, HS-Crp, VAP these are a good place to start. Tells you your circadiam rhythm, how much inflammation and your cholesterol along with particle size which is very important. I'm guessing your o6/03 ratio is bad from being a veg. By having a ratio under 20 means you are LR; didn't know if you knew that. You can read the hormone 101 blog, lots of good info. Keep us informed of how you fare. Good luck on that ratio.

# Thread: Probiotics and our gut flora
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/probiotics-and-our-gut-flora.2700/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Souldanzer — 2012-07-26T21:27:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/probiotics-and-our-gut-flora.2700/post-41308>

So I learned that our gut flora will change with the location we live at. I.e. I was born in Germany but have been living in the U.S. for 13 years in August )). Therefore, I have an American gut flora and have to eat like an American (lol, not!).

But b/c I live in CO, I have to eat things seasonal and indigenous to CO b/c that's what does my gut flora good. How does this fit with taking supplemental probiotics? They're sort of one size fits all.

Should probiotics be "local" specific? Maybe another reason (other than that probiotic ## are way too low in supplements) to choose locally fermented veggies over supplements... b/c then the bacteria are the correct ones?

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-26T21:40:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/probiotics-and-our-gut-flora.2700/post-41309>

Can you make your own ferments? That's what I'm doing - I've got 2 heads of cabbage in a fermentation crock to make kraut? that way your environment is populating the kraut...

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-07-26T21:52:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/probiotics-and-our-gut-flora.2700/post-41312>

Grow your own garden and make sure you eat plenty of raw dirty produce from it - so you get the soil microbes. You can also make your own homebrews from wild yeasts. I used to make hard cider that way all the time. Every batch is different!

### kathylu — 2012-07-26T21:58:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/probiotics-and-our-gut-flora.2700/post-41314>

Yup. Make your own kraut from local organic cabbage. Don't wash it. It's full of local soil sourced buggies.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-26T22:11:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/probiotics-and-our-gut-flora.2700/post-41316>

I don't take probiotics  this was a theoretical question.... more like if you take supplements, maybe you'd have to get the ones specific to your location, like raw honey it's better to eat locally produced.

I never quite understood why people spend the money on pills if you can grow it in your kitchen. I'm a bit lazy with the kraut lately (I'm embarrassed to say that since I'm German ) b/c the chopping is so time consuming (and boring). I have some cucumbers that are ready to eat... but don't like them. So I stick with the fermented fizzy drinks. Love those.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-26T22:13:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/probiotics-and-our-gut-flora.2700/post-41318>

Have my own garden and eat plenty of the dirt. Intentionally or not

### Huck — 2012-07-26T23:22:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anything-safe-to-take-for-headache-with-leaky-gut.2704/post-41341>

### nonchalant — 2012-07-26T23:29:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anything-safe-to-take-for-headache-with-leaky-gut.2704/post-41342>

+1 on the ice. It works for everyone. Try putting it on the sides of the neck.

Also krill oil worked wonders for me, when I used to have headaches. Takes about 45 minutes, same as ibuprophen.

Since I've been seeing a chiropractor, as soon as I sense a headache coming on, I "lift" my head, from behind the ears or the back of the head, to stretch the neck a bit. Stops the headache in its tracks for me. Guess mine are blood circulation related.

Good luck!

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-26T23:46:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anything-safe-to-take-for-headache-with-leaky-gut.2704/post-41349>

### CarolMorris — 2012-07-27T00:15:12+0300

### Eve — 2012-07-27T04:26:57+0300

### CarolMorris — 2012-07-27T20:04:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anything-safe-to-take-for-headache-with-leaky-gut.2704/post-41546>

To help with the food reactions I use Allergy Research/Nutricology powdered Vit C. It helped with food sensitivities for a few years, but my list of foods causing a problem increased. Then i found this forum and bingo...leaky gut...it began to make sense!

Here's the list of ingredients...could there be corn??--ugh!

Buffered Vitamin C Powder contains high-purity ascorbic acid buffered with carbonates of potassium, calcium and magnesium. Buffered C has an acid-alkaline buffering action that can help control the increased acidity often associated with allergic reactions. Developed by Stephen A. Levine, Ph.D.

These nutrients are of the highest quality and purity obtainable, and do not contain preservatives, diluents, or artificial additives.

Supplement Facts

Serving Size 1 teaspoon (4.7 g)

Servings per container 51

Amount per serving:

Vitamin C (as Ascorbic Acid) 2135 mg

Calcium (as Calcium Carbonate) 405 mg

Magnesium (as Magnesium Carbonate) 215 mg

Potassium (as Potassium Carbonate) 90 mg

Other ingredients: Cellulose.

### kathylu — 2012-07-28T14:55:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anything-safe-to-take-for-headache-with-leaky-gut.2704/post-41656>

> **Joann;41860 wrote:** Where is calcium coming from? I get confused with calcium issue. rda says 1000 mg calcium a day. without dairy I can't figure out where we are getting it from.
>
> then there is the vitamin d and vit k issue.....taking vit d and not getting enough calcium.....wondering if that could cause an issue...

Lots of calcium in meat and seafood...it's essential for cell membrane function and structure...bones too. Many plants have significant calcium, broccoli for instance.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-28T17:06:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anything-safe-to-take-for-headache-with-leaky-gut.2704/post-41665>

> **Joann;41860 wrote:** Where is calcium coming from? I get confused with calcium issue. rda says 1000 mg calcium a day. without dairy I can't figure out where we are getting it from.
>
> then there is the vitamin d and vit k issue.....taking vit d and not getting enough calcium.....wondering if that could cause an issue...

The problem is actually opposite, taking vitamin d will reduce the need for calcium. Vitamin K tells the calcium where to go (NOT to your heart and blood vessels). You might not need the RDA of calcium if your diet is dialed in. Now if you drink tons of cola or other stuff like that that leaches the calcium from your bones.... you might want to follow the RDA. Mg / calcium ratio is actually more important than total calcium intake. Dairy gives you way too much calcium with too little mg and in the process leaches calcium from your body.

I focus on the best foods I can find and dialing in my diet. Bone broths have calcium. Any fish eaten with bones has tons of calcium. Veggies, especially leafy green ones. I doubt transitional apes ever counted their calcium intake.

### vlynnb — 2012-07-28T19:48:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anything-safe-to-take-for-headache-with-leaky-gut.2704/post-41692>

Thanks to all for this thread. I rarely have headaces anymore but had one last night. Simply splashed cold water on face for a couple of minutes, it was feeling so much better but not completely gone so I used an flexible ice pack. I could only stand it for 10 seconds or so at a time on the side of my forehead and cheek bone but it took the ache away almost immediately. I am so amazed. My mom used to use ice packs for her migraines but when I had one I only wanted warm wash cloths. No more warm washcloths for me.

# Thread: fish ain't people, but this is interesting
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-aint-people-but-this-is-interesting.2707/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-07-27T01:28:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-aint-people-but-this-is-interesting.2707/post-41389>

[http://www.springerlink.com/content/n2102rpw13436834/](http://http://www.springerlink.com/content/n2102rpw13436834/)

> 11 (2008), 989-997, DOI: 10.1007/s11745-008-3228-8
>
>
>
>
>
> the American Oil Chemists' Society
>
>
>
> Original Article
>
> Sesamin Supplementation Increases White Muscle Docosahexaenoic Acid (DHA) Levels in Rainbow Trout ( Oncorhynchus mykiss ) Fed High Alpha-Linolenic Acid (ALA) Containing Vegetable Oil: Metabolic Actions
>
> Abstract
>
> The effects of including an equi-mixture of sesamin and episesamin in fish diets based on vegetable oils of different fatty acid composition were examined. Sesamin/episesamin (hereafter named sesamin) was included at 0.58 g/100 g diet. The oil used in the feed was either a mixture of linseed and sunflower oils (6:4, by vol) or 100% linseed oil. **Addition of sesamin increased the percentages of docosahexaenoic acid (DHA) in white muscle phospholipid and triacylglycerol fraction **by up to 37% but the fatty acids in red muscle and liver were not affected**. The expression of the peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor PPARα was significantly down regulated in the liver of the fish fed sesamin and mixed oil diet (P < 0.05). Sesamin and episesamin were detected in liver and muscle tissues of the fish that had been fed sesamin. Fish fed sesamin had elevated levels of total cytochrome P450 (CYP) enzymes and EROD activity in the liver, indicating an induction of CYP1A in this tissue.** Our conclusion was that supplementation of fish feed with sesamin increased the proportions of DHA in the white muscle.

### chocolate — 2012-07-27T02:23:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-aint-people-but-this-is-interesting.2707/post-41397>

Sesamin ameliorates oxidative stress and mortality in kainic acid-induced status epilepticus by inhibition of MAPK and COX-2 activation

> Abstract
>
> Background
>
>
>
> Kainic acid (KA)-induced status epilepticus (SE) was involved with release of free radicals. Sesamin is a well-known antioxidant from sesame seeds and it scavenges free radicals in several brain injury models. However the neuroprotective mechanism of sesamin to KA-induced seizure has not been studied.
>
> Methods
>
>
>
> Rodents (male FVB mice and Sprague-Dawley rats) were fed with sesamin extract (90% of sesamin and 10% sesamolin), 15 mg/kg or 30 mg/kg, for 3 days before KA subcutaneous injection. The effect of sesamin on KA-induced cell injury was also investigated on several cellular pathways including neuronal plasticity (RhoA), neurodegeneration (Caspase-3), and inflammation (COX-2) in PC12 cells and microglial BV-2 cells.
>
> Results
>
>
>
> **Treatment with sesamin extract (30 mg/kg) significantly increased plasma α-tocopherol level 50% and 55.8% from rats without and with KA treatment, respectively. It also decreased malondialdehyde (MDA) from 145% to 117% (p = 0.017) and **preserved superoxide dismutase from 55% of the vehicle control mice to 81% of sesamin-treated mice, respectively to the normal levels (p = 0.013). The treatment significantly decreased the mortality from 22% to 0% in rats. Sesamin was effective to protect PC12 cells and BV-2 cells from KA-injury in a dose-dependent manner. It decreased the** release of Ca2+, reactive oxygen species,** and MDA from PC12 cells. Western blot analysis revealed that sesamin significantly reduced ERK1/2, p38 mitogen-activated protein kinases, Caspase-3, and COX-2 expression in both cells and RhoA expression in BV-2 cells. Furthermore, Sesamin was able to reduce PGE2 production from both cells under KA-stimulation.
>
> Conclusions
>
>
>
> Taken together, it suggests that sesamin could protect KA-induced brain injury through anti-inflammatory and partially antioxidative mechanisms.

### chocolate — 2012-07-27T02:28:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-aint-people-but-this-is-interesting.2707/post-41398>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/19794909](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/19794909)

> Sesamin modulates tyrosine hydroxylase, superoxide dismutase, catalase, inducible NO synthase and interleukin-6 expression in dopaminergic cells under MPP+-induced oxidative stress.
>
> Lahaie-Collins V, Bournival J, Plouffe M, Carange J, Martinoli MG.
>
> Source
>
>
>
> Department of Biochemistry, Neuroscience Research Group, UniversitÃ© du QuÃ©bec, Trois-RiviÃ¨res, QuÃ©bec, Canada.
>
> Abstract
>
>
>
> Oxidative stress is regarded as a mediator of nerve cell death in several neurodegenerative disorders, such as Parkinson's disease. Sesamin, a lignan mainly found in sesame oil, is currently under study for its anti-oxidative and possible neuroprotective properties. We used 1-methyl-4-phenyl-pyridine (MPP(+)) ion, the active metabolite of the potent parkinsonism-causing toxin 1-methyl-4-phenyl-1,2,5,6-tetrahydropyridine, to produce oxidative stress and neurodegeneration in neuronal PC12 cells, which express dopamine, as well as neurofilaments. Our results show that picomolar doses of sesamin protected neuronal PC12 cells from MPP(+)-induced cellular death, as revealed by colorimetric measurements and production of reactive oxygen species. We also demonstrated that sesamin acted by rescuing tyrosine hydroxylase levels from MPP(+)-induced depletion. Sesamin, however, did not modulate dopamine transporter levels, and estrogen receptor-alpha and -beta protein expression. By examining several parameters of cell distress, we found that sesamin also elicited a strong increase in superoxide dismutase activity as well as protein expression and decreased catalase activity and the MPP(+) stimulated inducible nitric oxide synthase protein expression, in neuronal PC12 cells. Finally, sesamin possessed significant anti-inflammatory properties, as disclosed by its potential to reduce MPP(+)-induced interleukin-6 mRNA levels in microglia. From these studies, we determined the importance of the lignan sesamin as a neuroprotective molecule and its possible role in complementary and/or preventive therapies of neurodegenerative diseases.

### chocolate — 2012-07-27T02:44:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-aint-people-but-this-is-interesting.2707/post-41406>

[http://www.lipidworld.com/content/8/1/34/](http://http://www.lipidworld.com/content/8/1/34/)

Skeletal muscle structural lipids improve during weight-maintenance after a very low calorie dietary intervention

Steen B Haugaard1,2,3\*â€ , Allan Vaag4\*â€ , Huiling Mu5 and Sten Madsbad1

\* Corresponding authors: Steen B Haugaard [[email redacted]](mailto:[email redacted]) - Allan Vaag [[email redacted]](mailto:[email redacted])

â€  Equal contributors

> Abstract
>
> Background
>
>
>
> The objective was to investigate in a group of obese subjects the course in skeletal muscle phospholipid (SMPL) fatty acids (FA) during a 24-weeks weight maintenance program, which was preceded by a successful very low calorie dietary intervention (VLCD). Special focus was addressed to SMPL omega-3 FA, which is a lipid entity that influences insulin action.
>
> Methods
>
>
>
> Nine obese subjects (BMI = 35.7 Â± 1.0 kg/m2), who had completed an 8 weeks VLCD (weight-loss = -9.7 Â± 1.6 kg, P < 0.001), had obtained skeletal muscle biopsies (vastus lateralis) before and after a dietician-guided 24-weeks weight-maintenance program (-1.2 Â± 1.5 kg, P = ns). SMPL FA composition was determined by gas liquid chromatography. During the preceding VLCD, insulin sensitivity (HOMA-IR) and glycemic control (HbA1c) improved but no change in SMPL omega-3 FA was observed. During the weight-maintenance program five subjects received the pancreas lipase inhibitor Orlistat 120 mg t.i.d. versus placebo.
>
> Results
>
>
>
> HOMA-IR and HbA1c stabilized and SMPL total omega-3 FA, docosahexaenoic acid and ratio of n-3/n-6 polyunsaturated FA increased by 24% (P < 0.01), 35% (P < 0.02) and 26% (P < 0.01), respectively, whereas saturated and monounsaturated FA did not change. Plasma total-cholesterol and LDL-cholesterol, which decreased during the VLCD, reverted to pre-VLCD levels (P < 0.01). Orlistat therapy was associated with weight-loss (P < 0.05), trends for better glycemic control (P = 0.15) and greater increase in SMPL docosahexaenoic acid (P = 0.12) but similar reversal of plasma cholesterols compared to placebo.
>
> Conclusion
>
>
>
> The data are consistent with the notion that greater SMPL omega-3 FA obtained during a weight-maintenance program may play a role for preserving insulin sensitivity and glycemic control being generated during a preceding VLCD.

### chocolate — 2012-07-27T17:47:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-aint-people-but-this-is-interesting.2707/post-41527>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/11074268](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/11074268)

> Kainate, a double agent that generates seizures: two decades of progress.
>
> Ben-Ari Y, Cossart R.
>
> Source
>
>
>
> The INMED, INSERM U29, Parc scientifique de Luminy, BP 13, 13273, Marseille, France.
>
> Abstract
>
>
>
> Studies using kainate, an excitatory amino acid extracted from a seaweed, have provided major contributions to the understanding of epileptogenesis. Here we review pioneering and more recent studies aimed at determining how kainate generates seizures and, in particular, how inhibition is altered during seizures. We focus on target and subunit-specific effects of kainate on hippocampal pyramidal neurons and interneurons that lead to an excitation of both types of neurons and thus to the parallel increase of glutamatergic and GABAergic spontaneous currents. We propose that kainate excites all its targets, the net consequence depending on the level of activity of the network.
>
>
> Sesamin ameliorates oxidative stress and mortality in kainic acid-induced status epilepticus by inhibition of MAPK and COX-2 activation.
>
> Hsieh PF, Hou CW, Yao PW, Wu SP, Peng YF, Shen ML, Lin CH, Chao YY, Chang MH, Jeng KC.
>
> Source
>
>
>
> Division of Neurology, Department of Medical Research, Taichung Veterans General Hospital, Taichung, Taiwan. [[email redacted]](mailto:[email redacted])
>
> Abstract
>
> BACKGROUND:
>
>
>
> **Kainic acid (KA)-induced status epilepticus (SE) was involved with release of free radicals.** Sesamin is a well-known antioxidant from sesame seeds and it scavenges free radicals in several brain injury models. However the neuroprotective mechanism of sesamin to KA-induced seizure has not been studied.
>
> METHODS:
>
>
>
> Rodents (male FVB mice and Sprague-Dawley rats) were fed with sesamin extract (90% of sesamin and 10% sesamolin), 15 mg/kg or 30 mg/kg, for 3 days before KA subcutaneous injection. The effect of sesamin on KA-induced cell injury was also investigated on several cellular pathways including neuronal plasticity (RhoA), neurodegeneration (Caspase-3), and inflammation (COX-2) in PC12 cells and microglial BV-2 cells.
>
> RESULTS:
>
>
>
> Treatment with sesamin extract (30 mg/kg) significantly increased plasma α-tocopherol level 50% and 55.8% from rats without and with KA treatment, respectively. It also decreased malondialdehyde (MDA) from 145% to 117% (p=0.017) and preserved superoxide dismutase from 55% of the vehicle control mice to 81% of sesamin-treated mice, respectively to the normal levels (p=0.013). The treatment significantly decreased the mortality from 22% to 0% in rats. Sesamin was effective to protect PC12 cells and BV-2 cells from KA-injury in a dose-dependent manner. It decreased the release of Ca2+, reactive oxygen species, and MDA from PC12 cells. Western blot analysis revealed that sesamin significantly reduced ERK1/2, p38 mitogen-activated protein kinases, Caspase-3, and COX-2 expression in both cells and RhoA expression in BV-2 cells. Furthermore, Sesamin was able to reduce PGE2 production from both cells under KA-stimulation.
>
> CONCLUSIONS:
>
>
>
> Taken together, it suggests that sesamin could protect KA-induced brain injury through anti-inflammatory and partially antioxidative mechanisms.

### chocolate — 2012-08-27T05:43:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-aint-people-but-this-is-interesting.2707/post-47432>

[http://stemcell.childrenshospital.org/treating-patients/a-stem-cell-boosting-drug-goes-from-fishtank-to-bedside-2/](http://http://stemcell.childrenshospital.org/treating-patients/a-stem-cell-boosting-drug-goes-from-fishtank-to-bedside-2/)

> Several years ago, the ancestors of some of these rapidly-breeding fish helped hematologist Leonard Zon, MD, Director of the Stem Cell Program at Boston Childrenâ€™s Hospital, discover a way to help patients with cancer. Zonâ€™s team placed tiny zebrafish embryos into laboratory dishesâ€”five fish to a well, 48 wells to a plate. The fish were bathed in more than 2,500 different test chemicalsâ€”in the hopes that at least one chemical would increase their numbers of blood stem cells.
>
>
>
> Zon had long sought a treatment to help patients recover their immune function more quickly after chemotherapy or bone marrow transplant. Currently, when patients canâ€™t find a suitable marrow donor to replenish their blood stem cells, the only other option is umbilical cord blood. But the number of blood stem cells in a single cord often isnâ€™t enough for older children and adults, and using two cords increases the risk of immune complications.
>
>
>
> â€œTwo cords go into a person and their immune systems are different, because they come from two different babies,â€ Zon explains. â€œThe immune systems start fighting each other.â€ Cords are also expensive and in limited supply. Could a drug be found that would help blood stem cells multiply and eliminate the need for a second cord?
>
>
>
> The zebrafish on the second floor provided an excellent platform for answering the call. The embryos can take up chemicals through their skin, and because theyâ€™re transparent, their blood stem cell numbers can be easily counted under the microscope. Before long, they gave Zon and former research fellows Trista North, PhD, and Wolfram Goessling, MD, PhD, a potent â€œhit.â€ A derivative of prostaglandin E2 (PGE2), originally developed to fight stomach ulcers but then abandoned, boosted blood stem cell formation three- to four-fold. As reported in the Journal Nature in 2007, PGE2 helped both adult zebrafish and mice recover their blood cell populations after injury.
>
>
>
> â€œWe werenâ€™t specifically looking for prostaglandins,â€ says Zon. â€œThis was a surprise finding.â€
>
>
>
> Because PGE2 is already approved by the FDA for other uses, the drug moved swiftly to testing in patients. In May, 2009, a Phase I clinical trial of PGE2 began at the Dana-Farber Cancer Institute and Massachusetts General Hospital, led by Corey Cutler M.D., M.P.H., of Dana-Farber and sponsored by Fate Therapeutics.
>
>
>
> In the two-year Phase I trial, which is still open for enrollment, 12 adults undergoing treatment for leukemia or lymphoma will receive blood stem cells from two donor umbilical cords, one or both of which will be pre-treated with PGE2.
>
>
>
> If the drug works as hoped, a single cord may provide enough stem cells to successfully engraft in patientsâ€™ bone marrow and produce the range of healthy blood cells needed. This could make cord blood a more viable option for patients who lack a matched donor for a marrow transplant. Zon hopes to move to Phase II testing, which would involve 40-80 patients at multiple centers.
>
>
>
> PGE2 is a first-of-a-kind discovery in several ways. Previous studies, including one from Zonâ€™s own lab, had found ways to boost formation of blood stem cells, none of them lent themselves to broad medical use. PGE2 is the first
>
> small-molecule drug found to induce stem-cell production, the first drug discovered using zebrafish to go to clinical trial, and the first drug discovered through stem cell research to reach patients. Interestingly, PGE2 also seems to stimulate regeneration in other tissues, such as the liver.

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-27T03:43:49+0300

# Thread: Thinking about ct in the fall/winter in Michigan
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thinking-about-ct-in-the-fall-winter-in-michigan.2712/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-07-27T05:25:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thinking-about-ct-in-the-fall-winter-in-michigan.2712/post-41450>

So, I'm really bad. Ever since we got back from. Action I haven't gotten back into ct. Thats a little less than two months. BUT I am thinking ahead and looking forward to the weather cooling off. I'd like to utilize my in ground pool for ct, but wondering how it will logistically work. Do I close up my pool and blow the lines out like normal in the fall? Would it be alright to keep using the pool? Should I at some point put on the safety cover and call it done for the season and just use the tub?

On a side note, we homeschool and are part of a co-op. My husband thinks we should start a polar club and invite kids and parents from the co op over to ct with us and to talk about the benefits of the cold! He's totally gung ho about this.

### fitness@home — 2012-07-28T03:29:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thinking-about-ct-in-the-fall-winter-in-michigan.2712/post-41611>

Aren't you in the upper lower part of Michigan? Kinda remember somewhere by Indian River.

A polar bear club sounds fun. What about lakes in the area - like Burt Lake. When does it freeze over - December? I didn't have a pool when we lived in Michigan. Is your built in or above ground?

Using it in fall/winter would extend your swim season. Getting more out of your investment that way

### [email redacted] — 2012-07-29T04:51:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thinking-about-ct-in-the-fall-winter-in-michigan.2712/post-41752>

We live in Mid-MI (near Lansing). We alreadyhave extended the swimming season by starting early! We got in the pool March 15 for the firsst time and it sure was cold! We had flurries a few times after that. That started the swimming season a good 2.5 months early. It would be cool to extend it that much on the other side too

Our pool is in-ground, gunite. Wondering what we should do about blowing out the lines?

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-07-31T16:14:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thinking-about-ct-in-the-fall-winter-in-michigan.2712/post-42042>

I kind of stalled out after my vacation from Orlando, but I keep doing as much CT as I can.

However, I am definitely looking forward to this fall/winter - I'm hoping for lots of snow so I can have a cheap source of "ice"

### WestmorelandGrape — 2012-08-01T04:47:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thinking-about-ct-in-the-fall-winter-in-michigan.2712/post-42202>

Hi, Trisha, I'm in Midland, Michigan!

When the temps start getting to the freezing point at night, I would make sure the pipes on your pool are blown out. You don't want the pipes to crack from freezing, leading to a costly repair. Can you still swim when the pool is winterized - I don't see why not, the water level may be lower but you should still be able to get in.

After this brutal summer, I am looking forward to rolling naked in the snow! That will be my CT!

# Thread: User experience between Wordpress site and the forum can be seamless...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/user-experience-between-wordpress-site-and-the-forum-can-be-seamless.2713/>
**Subforum:** Feedback/Suggestions
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### lee — 2012-07-27T05:30:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/user-experience-between-wordpress-site-and-the-forum-can-be-seamless.2713/post-41452>

It's lovely, but as a brand new user, I was confused for a little while about why I couldn't post on the forum. Then I needed to create a new account?

You can have Wordpress and the forum share a user table and have the experience be seamless - so double logging in isn't required. There are ways to do this and people who can help you. Your user experience can be seamless.

Thanks,

Lee

### lee — 2012-07-28T12:42:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-sequenced-posts-have-links-to-prior-posts-in-the-series-ex-cpc-5.2714/post-41648>

Thanks all of you! I really appreciate your taking the time to explain this. I just found the site yesterday and kept landing on amazing articles that were #3 or #5. I'm addicted now, and hope I don't get resistant from overloading. I've foresaken most of the day for your site, it's so good.

I work in the web industry, and newbie first impressions are kind of precious to our company because once we've learned a site, we're less able to provide usability insight. We call it the curse of knowledge, and try to pay it forward when we land on a site we like and are new and can see how someone can save users a few clicks here and there. (Others do the same for us and we've benefitted greatly from their suggestions.) I mean no disrespect.

There's are several plugins for Wordpress for related content that might provide user experience benefit. Related content could show up in a sidebar. Might be helpful, or at least a user interface enhancement since there are so many blog sequences in this extraordinary site.

The Wordpress plugin directory has 2 pages of plugins for related content:

[http://wordpress.org/extend/plugins/tags/related-content](http://http://wordpress.org/extend/plugins/tags/related-content)

Perhaps a future version of the site could use a feature like this. FWIW, it's probably very easy to install.

Love the quilt!

Thanks everyone. I'll use what you showed me.

# Thread: Circadian tutorial
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-tutorial.2715/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### nonchalant — 2012-07-27T06:40:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-tutorial.2715/post-41467>

I wish someone would create a video to explain the circadian rhythm, and some of the hormones that are triggered by it.

Not someone standing in front of a chart, but something with music, and interesting video. Pictures of the sunrise and sunset, the cortisol and melatonin curves moving with the time of day, and the effect of meals and such.

### nonchalant — 2012-07-27T23:58:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-tutorial.2715/post-41590>

Yes, CT 7 set to music and video. Precisely.

### nonchalant — 2012-07-28T03:56:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-tutorial.2715/post-41618>

What better way to get this out to everyone? Youtube.

The circadian cycle is of prime importance. Most people know that they need good sleep, but they don't know what a marvel of coordination it all is. Most people know know eating a huge meal just before bed can cause problems, but not that they should go to bed long after their last meal. They don't know that cooler temps are important, and darkness is essential. They know that "breakfast is the most important meal of the day" but they don't know why, so they dismiss it.

We need some people with some creativity. Dr K could narrate.

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-02T06:17:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-tutorial.2715/post-42448>

Nonchalant, you are too, too funny and of course I agree with you. Music, video, birds chirping, sun shining, then quiet stillness of the evening twilight, the last twittering of the birds, then the faint glow of the brightest stars, then a hush as the sky is aglow with the ribbon of the milky way and we can stop thinking of actually wanting to eat one!

# Thread: Epi-Paleo detox symptoms

### Petra — 2012-07-27T12:39:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-detox-symptoms.2716/post-41474>

For 2 weeks I'm into to epi paleo fish eating mode. I feel great. But every night I feel really tired. In the afternoon I get foggy in my head. And I'm noticing my focus is shifting more often.

Are these Epi-Paleo detox symptoms ?

Anybody else experiencing this?

Please share,

Petra

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-27T13:17:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-detox-symptoms.2716/post-41479>

there are no detox symptoms from fish. If you get tired then it could be because hormones are not optimal.

### Petra — 2012-07-27T14:45:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-detox-symptoms.2716/post-41490>

Thanks for your reply Jack. Most appreciated.

Back to the lab it is for me.

Leptin and Cortisol, melatonin seem ok, maybe the estrogen came to play.

Take care,

Petra

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-27T20:03:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-detox-symptoms.2716/post-41545>

> **Jack Kruse;41700 wrote:** there are no detox symptoms from fish. If you get tired then it could be because hormones are not optimal.

well something is happening. And not from tons of seafood b/c I've been doing that for months now. It's from switching to \*raw\* seafood.

My digestive system has decided to completely stop working. I had to up my mg to ridiculous levels that I could never have tolerated before. I get the worst cravings. For the weirdest things (like fish heads.....). And no, I don't like the taste. But I want it anyways. And then nothing will move out of my stomach. It just sits there forever. My stomach is so bloated after a meal that I can't fit into any pants without spanx. I get nauseous after meals, partially b/c I feel too full but even if I eat less I still feel sick to the point of throwing up a few times by now. NOT self-induced, as that might need to be declared in my case  Sometimes I feel sick right away, sometimes up to 2hrs later. This isn't food poisoning either. It goes away a few hours after a meal. I eat too much and I'm constantly hungry. Basically, I feel like I'm back to my first year or so of re-feeding. I feel like cr\*p.

And yes, I'm tired. So tired I am spending half my day in bed. But then I also upped my Lugol's to 150mg/day b/c my fibrocystic symptoms are out of control and I'm scared.

I don't doubt I'm a hormonal mess right now. And maybe it's not detox. But there's \*something\* happening. I guess I could just go back to cooking all my food but that's kinda not my style  And I used to be all into kirlian images as a raw foodie......

### janagram — 2012-07-27T20:08:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-detox-symptoms.2716/post-41551>

do u need enzymes? I would, and do.

Petra

### Inger — 2012-07-28T11:17:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-detox-symptoms.2716/post-41644>

I am having no detox symptoms from the MHS or raw fish. Just feeling great..!

But I am not stuffing myself like mad eather.. I take 1-3 heads in one setting (makes 1-2 glas full of smoothie), and after I eat a very enjoyable meal of maybe 0,5 pounds of delicious raw mackerel-tartar made with fresh grated ginger, wild raw honey and vinegar (maybe some algae on the top) to that. Some raw wild berries for dessert. Perfect meal. No abdominal discomfort at all. But I have been eating raw fish for maybe 8 years...

Maybe start slowly if you want it to go smooth..? Your body needs time to heal and change! Like the CT. You need to start with the facedunks.. not laying in the ice for one hour.. You could maybe but sure you will suffer...

(could be parasites too.. just sayin. Have you looked up on it? Better do, knowledge is always good)

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-07-30T17:17:23+0300

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-31T22:06:19+0300

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-31T23:03:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-detox-symptoms.2716/post-42125>

> **Jack Kruse;42374 wrote:** yep......google ADH.

ok...

is this why when we eat seafood I'm not as thirsty?????

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-31T23:42:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-detox-symptoms.2716/post-42135>

> **Jack Kruse;42374 wrote:** yep......google ADH.

wow.... most interesting. There's my answer to why I'm drinking so much Dr Cardiologist and maybe it's not all mental. I can go way beyond 4L/day if I allow myself to.... this has nothing to do with AN behaviors/hunger suppression me thinks.

So many severe AN's have issues with bladder control/frequent urination. So many of them drink excessive amounts. The standard physician answer is that at a certain level of emaciation the bladder muscle will atrophy and that's why we have to pee every 5min. My level of dehydration from chronic purging limited this effect somewhat during my own AN but boy, once I was re-feeding.... I couldn't go too far from access to bathroom. I'd have some fluids and it would go right through me. It got a bit better with continued recovery but I'm still the one person who needs to pee first out of a group. Constantly thirsty. I suspected diabetes at some point.

Still studying up on it....haven't quite found how it fits with the other hormones. I think someone said it's part of the HPA axis somewhere... at the bottom?

Thanks  Besides the ugh, gotta go back to my PCP it looks like....

Now I cannot eat any nuts, seeds, eggs, dairy, mushrooms, avocados, and grains without putting on a few pounds overnight. And the weight accumulates with each infraction. I wonder if these foods are somehow messing with this hormone. I have no symptoms of intolerance other than uncontrollable weight gain.

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-01T05:20:12+0300

I thought this was interesting about the ADH:

however, some AVP is also released directly into the brain, where it plays an important role in social behavior and bonding.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-01T06:12:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-detox-symptoms.2716/post-42215>

I thought this was pretty cool

> Vasopressin released within the brain has many actions:
>
> It has been implicated in **memory formation, including delayed reflexes, image, short- and long-term memory, though the mechanism remains unknown; these findings are controversial. However, the synthetic vasopressin analogue desmopressin has come to interest as a likely **nootropic**.
>
> Vasopressin is released into the brain in a circadian rhythm by neurons of the supraoptic nucleus.**
>
> Vasopressin released from centrally projecting hypothalamic neurons is involved in aggression, blood pressure regulation and temperature regulation.
>
> Selective AVPr1a blockade in the ventral pallidum has been shown to prevent partner preference, suggesting that these receptors in this ventral forebrain region are crucial for pair bonding.\[3\]
>
> Recent evidence suggests that vasopressin may have analgesic effects. The analgesia effects of vasopressin were found to be dependent on both stress and gender.\[5\]

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-08-04T17:13:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-detox-symptoms.2716/post-42837>

> **Souldanzer;41766 wrote: well something is happening. And not from tons of seafood b/c I've been doing that for months now. It's from switching to \*raw\* seafood.
>
>
>
> My digestive system has decided to completely stop working. I had to up my mg to ridiculous levels that I could never have tolerated before. I get the worst cravings. For the weirdest things (like fish heads.....). And no, I don't like the taste. But I want it anyways. And then nothing will move out of my stomach. It just sits there forever. My stomach is so bloated after a meal that I can't fit into any pants without spanx. I get nauseous after meals, partially b/c I feel too full but even if I eat less I still feel sick to the point of throwing up a few times by now. NOT self-induced, as that might need to be declared in my case  Sometimes I feel sick right away, sometimes up to 2hrs later. This isn't food poisoning either. It goes away a few hours after a meal. I eat too much and I'm constantly hungry. Basically, I feel like I'm back to my first year or so of re-feeding. I feel like cr\*p.
>
>
>
> And yes, I'm tired. So tired I am spending half my day in bed. But then I also upped my Lugol's to 150mg/day b/c my fibrocystic symptoms are out of control and I'm scared.
>
>
>
> I don't doubt I'm a hormonal mess right now. And maybe it's not detox. But there's \*something\* happening. I guess I could just go back to cooking all my food but that's kinda not my style  And I used to be all into kirlian images as a raw foodie......**

**

Hi Souldanzer,

When you say: _"And then nothing will move out of my stomach."_ I don't know your whole story but this symptom describes a common problem that is seen in some people at the **Weston A Price Foundation** that go from high carbohydrate to high protein. Of course, Sally Fallon would say don't go high protein, actually its dangerous, go high fat.

Some people cannot digest protein because of the lack of HCL in the stomach. If this is your problem your digestion will never work properly. You can easily test at home to see if this is your problem. Here is an WAPF essay to read and a betaine HCL home test called the Heidelberg Capsule Test**:

[http://www.modernherbalist.com/betaine.html](http://http://www.modernherbalist.com/betaine.html)

[http://www.westonaprice.org/digestive-disorders/acid-reflux-a-red-flag](http://http://www.westonaprice.org/digestive-disorders/acid-reflux-a-red-flag)

If I were you, during the day, I would get out of bed and slowly walk around outside. Get some sun. After a short walk sit in the sun with your eyes closed and let the light filter through your eye lids. Do as much as you can. Think good thoughts; do not let poisonous thoughts enter your mind. Sleep in as dark as possible during the night. Try not to sleep during the day.

If you are not digesting your food properly, you are doing yourself harm every time you eat.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-04T18:28:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-detox-symptoms.2716/post-42842>

After a short walk sit in the sun with your eyes closed and let the light filter through your eye lids. Do as much as you can. Think good thoughts; do not let poisonous thoughts enter your mind. Sleep in as dark as possible during the night. Try not to sleep during the day.

Thanks, Caroline

I'm actually feeling better by the day  But I appreciate your input! I've tried HCL and I can't tolerate it, not b/c of the acid but b/c of what is added to the capsules as a filler.... allergic .... can't take Betaine HCL without capsule and filler so that's a nogo for me. I'm unfortunately very familiar with delayed stomach emptying b/c I'm recovering from anorexia. I"ve been doing pretty well with it though. I think my gut has adjusted except for that I still need a little more mg than before to keep things moving. I don't monitor my food, I eat intuitively, but there definitely is a lot of fat involved!!

I hear you on trying not to stay in bed. There's some days I literally cannot get out of bed (or off the couch) for most of the day. I have really bad headaches from a head injury and probably a congenital heart defect, I just can't do it. I need to be horizontal, quiet, I do try to not make it dark.... but boy some days just getting up to make my food appears like a major exertion. I've been able to go out a bit more again lately, so I think that's helping. I tend to "wake up" when the sun hits my eyes and I have a bit more energy then. I also go to the pool and rest there in the grass or on a lounge chair.

I still have a problem with my stomach and brain not agreeing on food intake. Brain asks for more than stomach can handle. But that's been ongoing for a long time now.

### shilohman — 2012-08-04T18:33:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-detox-symptoms.2716/post-42844>

Souldanzer, I had the exact same problem when I increased my raw fish intake. Increase your water/green tea consumption. This seems to be what did the trick for me.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-04T18:52:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-detox-symptoms.2716/post-42856>

> **shilohman;43123 wrote:** Souldanzer, I had the exact same problem when I increased my raw fish intake. Increase your water/green tea consumption. This seems to be what did the trick for me.

Thanks, Shiloman. It appears this is rather common so I'm not worried at all anymore.

I'm at 4+ liters/day most of it green tea (decaf).... not counting water in my food. I'm spending half my day peeing, I'm not kidding either. I've been trying to reduce my fluid intake for years now b/c my AN docs say it's an eating disorder behavior but I threw that out the window after Dr K clued me in on what might be really going on (low ADH). I'm thinking of getting it tested via my PCP soon. The one who complains about me drinking too much.... lol. I guess my question isn't so much if that's my problem but what do you do about low ADH?? (yes, other than getting your PFO fixed..... )

The digestion problems have been sorted except for the mg but even that's come down a bit now... I'm taking around 1200mg including 600mg citrate, 400mg chloride, and 2000mg L-threonate. I'll continue to keep dropping it as my gut adjusts.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-04T19:17:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-detox-symptoms.2716/post-42865>

Anti Diuretic hormone. I can't quite fit it in with the other hormones.... comes from your pituitary if I remember correctly. It regulates your water retention/excretion. Too little and you just keep peeing... and therefore get thirsty. A lot.

### shilohman — 2012-08-04T19:22:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-detox-symptoms.2716/post-42867>

I found it in "CPC #4 EVOLUTIONARY FRIEND OR FOE", hormone vassopressin (ADH) from the posterior pituitary

Was reading about diabetes insipidus after Dr k's comment. Does sound a lot like me. It gets better at times though. Some days I don't have to pee every 15 min... only every hour or so... lol. My family wouldn't take me on vacation once b/c I needed to pee so often and drink all the time. Got blamed for that one. Just stop doing it, Soul. It's all mental. Right.

I think it also co-incides with chronic brain injuries. And probably PFOs... gah. Double whammy. And lots of my AN/BN friends struggle with this.... another score  In those cases it always gets blamed on the person, though.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-04T20:39:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-detox-symptoms.2716/post-42883>

> **hebsie;43162 wrote:** ...FWIW, I've had it thrown around in many doctor's appts, diabetes insipidus that is. Testing wise, for me they did a 24 hour urine osmolality (it should average between 500 and 800 mOsm/kg), and then ran a (in my case botched) [water deprivation test](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fluid_deprivation_test) (where they inject you with DDAVP)...hebs

water deprivation test...... that sounds horrific to me....  My cards tested my electrolytes with osmolality (but that was blood?) and it was fine..... but that was after overnight fasting and I have to stop drinking a few hours before bed or I have to pee all night long. And I tend to restrict my fluid intake when there is testing coming up.... b/c I know I drink excessively. Water, that is  And stop drinking a while before I go anywhere, really.... lol. Unless there's bathrooms around.

Well, considering how many people with EDs I know that have this issue.... not that surprising really, hebs. it's just kinda cruel to think about all the IP/IOP places that put restrictions on your fluid intake  b/c it's considered an ED behavior. I highly doubt they look for low ADH before they put people through that. They never tested for me.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-24T07:22:02+0300

>

Reviving this thread. After listening to tonight's webinar I realized that the mag thing is starting to happen to me. I had to take close to 2000mg just to get things moving after switching to raw epi paleo. Since then, my needs have slowly been dropping. The last few days, I'm not able to tolerate even 300mg of mg anymore..... so I"m assuming this is a good thing? It means I cleared out my biofilms and can start rebuilding?

I unintentionally induced the 2-3 days of number 6/7 a few times lately b/c my mg needs are dropping and I don't realize until I already got the runs

Will drop my dose to 200mg tomorrow

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-24T18:26:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-detox-symptoms.2716/post-53814>

I always think that dreams gone bad have something to do with hypoglycemia at night, followed by a release of cortisol/adrenaline to bring up the blood sugar. At least that's what seems to be the pre-curser for mine.

The other night I pulled my dead daughter out of the bottom of a (cloudy) swimming pool, where I had been watching all the kids swimming. That one still shakes me to the bone. It was the daughter I sent to first grade on minimal rations a few days prior, because she refused to help make her lunch!!

### ealachan — 2012-09-24T18:54:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-detox-symptoms.2716/post-53817>

> **bigknitwit;54474 wrote:** I always think that dreams gone bad have something to do with hypoglycemia at night, followed by a release of cortisol/adrenaline to bring up the blood sugar. At least that's what seems to be the pre-curser for mine.
>
>
>
> The other night I pulled my dead daughter out of the bottom of a (cloudy) swimming pool, where I had been watching all the kids swimming. That one still shakes me to the bone. It was the daughter I sent to first grade on minimal rations a few days prior, because she refused to help make her lunch!!

In my case, hypoglycemia shouldn't be an issue...but that certainly sounds like a legitimate explanation for some.

In one of my dreams, my beloved Nana had dropped dead out of nowhere (which, given I was reading Destiny's thread on here about her friend dropping dead, I can kind of see where that one came from in retrospect). The other two, though...were just bizarre and violent and unsettling. People very close to me trying to hurt me in sick ways, people who have been in the background of my life since childhood (in real life), chasing me through a dream, trying to hurt me and a friend, and me being ultra-violent fighting him off. Total unadulterated weirdness.

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-24T20:39:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-detox-symptoms.2716/post-53862>

> **ealachan;54477 wrote:** In my case, hypoglycemia shouldn't be an issue...but that certainly sounds like a legitimate explanation for some.
>
>
>
> In one of my dreams, my beloved Nana had dropped dead out of nowhere (which, given I was reading Destiny's thread on here about her friend dropping dead, I can kind of see where that one came from in retrospect). The other two, though...were just bizarre and violent and unsettling. People very close to me trying to hurt me in sick ways, people who have been in the background of my life since childhood (in real life), chasing me through a dream, trying to hurt me and a friend, and me being ultra-violent fighting him off. Total unadulterated weirdness.

I used to get really weird, horrendous dreams like that when I was getting off (or on) zoloft. So changes in neurochemistry were a real trigger here. They are very, very unsettling, I remember some of mine, and they were much like yours. I wonder if in your case it's a good sign that you're upregulating some of your neurotransmitters..? I really don't know. I didn't like pulling my dead 6 year old out of the pool though. Still takes my breath away.

### ealachan — 2012-09-24T21:00:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-detox-symptoms.2716/post-53872>

> **bigknitwit;54528 wrote:** I used to get really weird, horrendous dreams like that when I was getting off (or on) zoloft. So changes in neurochemistry were a real trigger here. They are very, very unsettling, I remember some of mine, and they were much like yours. I wonder if in your case it's a good sign that you're upregulating some of your neurotransmitters..? I really don't know. I didn't like pulling my dead 6 year old out of the pool though. Still takes my breath away.

Yeah can't say as I blame you there. The idea of that makes me shiver, and I don't even have kids.
