> Causes
>
>
>
> Gallstone risk increases for females (especially before menopause) and for people near or above 40 years;\[8\] the condition is more prevalent among both North and South Amerindians and among those of European descent than among other ethnicities. **A lack of melatonin could significantly contribute to gallbladder stones, as melatonin inhibits cholesterol secretion from the gallbladder, enhances the conversion of cholesterol to bile, and is an antioxidant, which is able to reduce oxidative stress to the gallbladder.\[9\] Researchers believe that gallstones may be caused by a combination of factors, including inherited body chemistry, body weight, gallbladder motility (movement), and perhaps diet\[citation needed\]. The absence of such risk factors does not, however, preclude the formation of gallstones.
>
>
> Pathophysiology
>
>
>
> This obstruction may lead to jaundice, elevation in alkaline phosphatase**, increase in conjugated bilirubin in the blood and increase in cholesterol in the blood. It can also cause acute pancreatitis and ascending cholangitis.

### chocolate — 2012-10-22T00:42:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-im-thinking-about-going-over-to-the-dark-side.2766/post-59533>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/15813612](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/15813612)

> Autoimmune thyroiditis with associated proteinuria: report of two patients.
>
> Agras PI, Kinik ST, Cengiz N, Baskin E, Saatci U.
>
> Source
>
>
>
> Pediatric Nephrology, Baskent University Faculty of Medicine, Ankara, Turkey. [[email redacted]](mailto:[email redacted])
>
> Abstract
>
>
>
> The association of renal disease and autoimmune thyroid disorders has been reported previously. Renal findings associated with autoimmune thyroiditis present more commonly as proteinuria ranging from mild to nephrotic levels. We report here two adolescent girls with hyperthyroidism associated with transient proteinuria correlated with thyroid hormone levels. They had positive antithyroid peroxidase and antithyroglobulin antibodies. Ultrasonographic and scintigraphic findings of the thyroid gland were consistent with Graves' disease in both. Their renal functions were normal except proteinuria (daily protein excretion of 13.5 mg/m2/h in patient 1 and 11 mg/m2/h in patient 2). When they became euthyroid on antithyroid treatment, proteinuria decreased without associated hematuria and/or hypertension. **In conclusion, patients with autoimmune thyroid disease should be assessed for the possibility of proteinuria and the etiological investigation of proteinuria should include evaluation of thyroid functions.**
>
>
>
> PMID:
>
> 15813612
>
> \[PubMed - indexed for MEDLINE\]

>
> Conclusions— NADPH oxidase is potentially an important source of ROS that may upregulate NF-κB in preeclampsia. We suggest that AT1-AA through activation of NADPH oxidase could contribute to ROS production and inflammatory responses in preeclampsia.

>
>
>
> [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anti-cardiolipin\_antibodies](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anti-cardiolipin_antibodies)

>
> > Anti-cardiolipin antibodies
> >
> > From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
> >
> > Jump to: navigation, search
> >
> > schematic representation of antibody.
> >
> >
> >
> > Anti-cardiolipin antibodies (ACA) are antibodies often directed against cardiolipin and found in several diseases including syphilis\[1\], **antiphospholipid syndrome, livedoid vasculitis, vertebrobasilar insufficiency, BehÃ§et's syndrome\[2\], **idiopathic spontaneous abortion,\[3\] and systemic lupus erythematosus(SLE).\[4\] They are a form of anti-mitochondrial antibody. In SLE, The anti-DNA antibodies and anti-cardiolipin act independently.\[5\] In rheumatoid arthritis\[6\] with systemic sclerosis (scleroderma)\[7\] these antibodies may tie two conditions together.
> >
> >
> >
> > Anti-cardiolipin antibodies can be classified in two ways.
> >
> >
> >
> > As IgM, IgG or IgA
> >
> > As β2-glycoprotein dependent or independent
> >
> > In autoimmune disease ACA are beta-2 glycoprotein dependent
> >
> > syphilis ACA\[1\] are beta-2 glycoprotein independent and can be assayed using the Venereal Disease Research Laboratory test
> >
> >
> >
> > Apolipoprotein H involvement
> >
> >
> >
> > β2-glycoprotein I has been identified as Apolipoprotein H and is required for the recognition of ACA in autoimmune disease.\[8\] Only a subset of autoimmune anti-cardiolipin antibodies bind Apo-H, these **anti-apolipoprotein antibodies are associated with increased thrombosis******
>
> ****
>
>
>
>
> [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21099614](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21099614)
>
> Association of anticardiolipin antibodies with preeclampsia: a systematic review and meta-analysis.CONCLUSION:
>
>
>
> Moderate-to-high levels of anticardiolipin antibodies are associated with preeclampsia, but there is insufficient evidence to use anticardiolipin antibodies as predictors of preeclampsia in clinical practice**.**

### chocolate — 2012-10-24T01:43:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-im-thinking-about-going-over-to-the-dark-side.2766/post-59909>

I'm pondering whether sticky blood gets helped by alcohol.... Maybe there is an evolutionary purpose for candida. Actually, it looks like candida causes sticky blood.

[http://www.fin.ucar.edu/polpro/section2/2-9\_appendix.html](http://http://www.fin.ucar.edu/polpro/section2/2-9_appendix.html)

> Health Effects of Alcohol Use
>
>
>
> Ninety-five percent of all alcohol consumed is absorbed into the body through the stomach, small intestine and colon. Complete absorption into the blood requires 2-6 hours or more. The rate of absorption into the blood stream is influenced by the presence of food in the system, the time period of consumption, the driver's body weight and metabolism. Once alcohol is in the bloodstream, alcohol quickly goes to every cell and tissue in the body. Alcohol causes red blood cells to coagulate together in sticky wads, slowing circulation and depriving tissues of oxygen. Alcohol in the blood can cause anemia by reducing the production of red blood cells. Alcohol decreases the ability of white blood cells to destroy bacteria and degenerates the clotting ability of blood platelets. Alcohol kills brain cells, which is permanent damage. Long-term alcohol use causes loss of memory, impaired judgment, and learning ability due to the damage done to the brain cells.
>
>
>
> Alcohol affects the central nervous system of the body more than any other bodily function. Because alcohol is a depressant, it inhibits the control mechanisms of the brain, which results in unrestrained activities in various parts of the brain. An extremely high dose of alcohol can depress the central nervous system to a point where breathing may stop completely, resulting in death.
>
>
>
> Besides the effects alcohol has on the central nervous system, it causes damage and destruction to the tissue cells in the body including brain cells. Excess alcohol use can depress the appetite and prevent the absorption of amino acids, vitamins and other nutrients, which contribute to malnourishment of the body. Alcohol hampers the liver's ability to metabolize fat which leads to fatty liver disease and cirrhosis of the liver. Alcohol increases the blood pressure in people with hypertension, which can lead to life threatening heart problems.

### chocolate — 2012-10-28T04:39:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-im-thinking-about-going-over-to-the-dark-side.2766/post-60511>

And it looks like candida makes oxalates and vitamin c. Candida likes it colder, the subterranean temperature actually. And candida slows down the thyroid. Oxalates are anti-freeze, and something that clicked was the Andres 3 to 4 grams proportional to dogs and a study showed that similar amounts wouldn't break down to oxalates. Maybe the candida is where we get our vitamin c.

> Evaluate vitamin C intake. Vitamin C can break down to form oxalates. However, in adults, the amount of oxalate formed did not increase until the amount exceeded 4 g of vitamin C per day (26). A large study of more than 85,000 women found no relation betwen vitamin C intake and kidney stones (27). In addition, an evaluation of 100 children on the autistic spectrum at The Great Plains Laboratory revealed that there was nearly zero correlation between vitamin C and oxalates in the urine (Table 2). Megadoses (more than 100 mg/Kg body weight per day) of vitamin C were shown to markedly reduce autistic symptoms in a double blind placebo controlled study (28) so any restriction of vitamin C needs to be carefully weighed against its significant benefits.
>
>
>
> > Vitamin C (ascorbic acid or ascorbate) can be coverted to oxalates but apparently the biochemical conversion system is saturated at low levels of vitamin C so that no additional oxalate is formed until very large doses (greater than 4 g per day) are consumed. It is interesting that fungi can also produce vitamin C which may explain why many children with autism have high vitamin C even though they do not take supplements containing vitamin C. The high correlation between arabinose and oxalates indicate that intestinal yeast/fungal overgrowth is likely the main cause for elevated oxalates in the autistic spectrum population. The deposition of oxalates in critical tissues such as brain and blood vessels, the oxidative damage caused by oxalate salts, and the deposition of oxalate mercury complexes in the tissues may all be important factors in the core etiology of autism.
> >
> >
> > It sure would be nice to figure out if ct elevated vitamin c.
> >
> > [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/11532105](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/11532105)
> >
> > CONCLUSIONS:
> >
> > Previous studies have shown that components of the endogenous digestive microflora, in particular Oxalobacter formigenes, utilize oxalate in the gut, thus limiting its absorption.
>

### chocolate — 2012-10-28T17:32:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-im-thinking-about-going-over-to-the-dark-side.2766/post-60572>

[http://pubs.acs.org/doi/abs/10.1021/ed080p71](http://http://pubs.acs.org/doi/abs/10.1021/ed080p71)

Article

> **Competitive Sorption between Oxalate and Phosphate in Soil: An Environmental Chemistry Laboratory Using Ion Chromatography
>
> A laboratory experiment was developed to introduce students to an important chemical phenomenon, anion sorption and desorption in the soil. In this lab exercise, competitive sorption between oxalate and phosphate was evaluated on aluminum oxide and a soil from a temperate region. Testings were done at two pH levels using a batch equilibrium technique. Ion chromatography was used for simultaneous measurement of oxalate and phosphate in water samples collected from the experiment. The results from this experiment clearly illustrated that oxalate exudation by the roots of certain plants may help them obtain phosphorus in phosphorus-deficient soils.** Students gained hands-on experience in ion chromatography and knowledge about important chemical factors affecting the behavior of anions in the soil environment and their potential effects on plants. This lab exercise would be appropriate for use in undergraduate or master-degree courses in chemistry, environmental chemistry, and instrumental analysis.

### chocolate — 2012-10-28T21:44:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-im-thinking-about-going-over-to-the-dark-side.2766/post-60626>

[http://www.healthaliciousness.com/articles/high-phosphorus-foods.php](http://http://www.healthaliciousness.com/articles/high-phosphorus-foods.php)

> . Phosphorus is found in almost every food, and as such, deficiency is rare. Conversely consuming too much phosphorus causes the body to send calcium from the bones to the blood in an attempt to restore balance. This transfer of calcium weakens bones, and can cause calcification of internal organs, increasing risk of heart attack and other vascular diseases. Some scientific research suggests that phosphorus is more easily absorbed from meat products,4 and you can only absorb half of the phosphorus contained in plant foods.
>
> Read more at [http://www.healthaliciousness.com/articles/high-phosphorus-foods.php#DQSvXQr6Yut6RsVK.99](http://http://www.healthaliciousness.com/articles/high-phosphorus-foods.php#DQSvXQr6Yut6RsVK.99)
>
>
>
>
> People at Risk of a Phosphorus Deficiency
>
>
>
> **Alcoholics - Alcohol can draw phosphorus supplies from the bones, lowering overall levels.3
>
> People Taking Salt Substitutes - Salt substitutes which contain potassium may reduce phosphorus levels in the long term.3
>
> Drugs which may Lower Phosphorus Levels3
>
> **Antacids - Antacids with aluminum, calcium, or magnesium, can hamper phosphorus absorption in the digestive system.
>
> Anticonvulsants - Anticonvulsants, such as phenobarbital and carbamazepine or Tegretol, can lower levels of phosphorus in the body, and create enzymes which hamper absorption.
>
> Bile Acid Sequestrants - Bile acid sequestrants used to lower cholesterol can prevent phosphorus being absorbed by the body.
>
> Corticosteroids - Corticosteroids, such as prednisone or methylprednisolone (Medrol), can increase excretion of phosphorus in unrine**.
>
> Insulin - Insulin in high doses may lower phosphorus absorption.**
>
> ACE Inhibitors (Blood Pressure Medication) - ACE Inhibitors such as Benazepril (Lotensin), Captopril (Capoten), Enalapril (Vasotec), Fosinopril (Monopril), Lisinopril (Zestril, Prinivil), Quinapril (Accupril), and Ramipril (Altace) may lower levels of phosphorus.
>
> Cyclosporine (Immune Supressant)
>
> Cardiac Glycosides (Digoxin or Lanoxin)
>
> Heparins (Blood-thinning Drugs)
>

### chocolate — 2012-10-29T14:19:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-im-thinking-about-going-over-to-the-dark-side.2766/post-60689>

Candida study... pretty neat... it talks about dha and candida manufacturing resolvins identical to the ones humans make to quiet inflammation. I think candida isn't cancer, so .... idk... its taking up the slack for something...those deer guts with all the juices are starting to look pretty good. freeze dried without the maggots, please

[http://www.plosone.org/article/info%3Adoi%2F10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0001316](http://http://www.plosone.org/article/info%3Adoi%2F10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0001316)

> Candida albicans Modulates Host Defense by Biosynthesizing the Pro-Resolving Mediator Resolvin E1
>
> Abstract Top
>
>
>
> Candida albicans is an opportunistic fungal pathogen of humans that resides commensally on epithelial surfaces, but can cause inflammation when pathogenic. **Resolvins are a class of anti-inflammatory lipids derived from omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (PUFA) that attenuate neutrophil migration during the resolution phase of inflammation. In this report we demonstrate that C. albicans biosynthesizes resolvins that are chemically identical to those produced by human cells. In contrast to the trans-cellular biosynthesis of human Resolvin E1 (RvE1), RvE1 biosynthesis in C. albicans occurs in the absence of other cellular partners. C. albicans biosynthesis of RvE1 is sensitive to lipoxygenase and cytochrome P450 monoxygenase inhibitors. We show that 10nM RvE1 reduces neutrophil chemotaxis in response to IL-8; 1nM RvE1 enhanced phagocytosis of Candida by human neutrophils, as well as intracellular ROS generation and killing, while having no direct affect on neutrophil motility. In a mouse model of systemic candidiasis, RvE1 stimulated clearance of the fungus from circulating blood. These results reveal an inter-species chemical signaling system that modulates host immune functions and may play a role in balancing host carriage of commensal and pathogenic C. albicans.**

so, now we need to figure out how to signal candida in a dish to grow these resolvins. I think the fungus is smarter than us.

### chocolate — 2012-11-01T00:43:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-im-thinking-about-going-over-to-the-dark-side.2766/post-61180>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/15469436](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/15469436)

> Candida albicans and Streptococcus salivarius modulate IL-6, IL-8, and TNF-alpha expression and secretion by engineered human oral mucosa cells.
>
> Mostefaoui Y, Bart C, Frenette M, Rouabhia M.
>
> Source
>
>
>
> Groupe de recherche en Ã©cologie buccale, FacultÃ© de mÃ©dicine dentaire, UniversitÃ© Laval, Quebec City, Quebec, Canada G1K 7P4.
>
> Abstract
>
>
>
> We investigated the involvement of oral epithelial cells via two cytokines (IL-6 and TNF-alpha) and one chemokine (IL-8) in local defences against live yeast (Candida albicans) and bacteria (Streptococcus salivarius) using an engineered human oral mucosa model. We report that the yeast changed from the blastospore to the hyphal form and induced significant tissue disorganization at later contact periods (24 and 48 h) compared to the bacteria. However, this effect did not reduce the viability or total number of epithelial cells. Gene activation analyses revealed that IL-6, IL-8 and TNF-alpha mRNA levels rose in tissues in contact with live C. albicans or S. salivarius. Gene activation was followed by an upregulation of protein secretion. **IL-6 levels were higher after contact with C. albicans than with S. salivarius.** IL-8 levels after contact with S. salivarius were higher than with C. albicans. Our study suggests that S. salivarius is more efficient at inducing proinflammatory mediator release than C. albicans. These results provide additional evidence for the contribution of oral epithelial cells to the inflammatory response against fungi and bacteria.

### chocolate — 2012-11-04T18:40:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-im-thinking-about-going-over-to-the-dark-side.2766/post-62029>

In the candida world of mice..... candida is the thing here, not the mice...

The UVC rays kill candida. Not the darn tanning bed rays.

> Abstract:
>
>
>
> [http://www.ingentaconnect.com/content/bpl/php/2011/00000087/00000002/art00012](http://http://www.ingentaconnect.com/content/bpl/php/2011/00000087/00000002/art00012)
>
>
>
> Burn patients are at high risk of invasive fungal infections, which are a leading cause of morbidity, mortality, and related expense exacerbated by the emergence of drug resistant fungal strains. In this study, we investigated the use of UVC light (254 nm) for the treatment of yeast Candida albicans infection in mouse third degree burns. In vitro studies demonstrated that UVC could selectively kill the pathogenic C. albicans compared with a normal mouse keratinocyte cell line in a light exposure dependent manner. A mouse model of chronic C. albicans infection in non-lethal third degree burns was developed. The C. albicans strain was stably transformed with a version of the Gaussia princeps luciferase gene that allowed real-time bioluminescence imaging of the progression of C. albicans infection. UVC treatment with a single exposure carried out on day 0 (30 min postinfection) gave an average 2.16-log10-unit (99.2%) loss of fungal luminescence when 2.92 J cm−2 UVC had been delivered, while UVC 24 h postinfection gave 1.94-log10-unit (95.8%) reduction of fungal luminescence after 6.48 J cm−2. **Statistical analysis demonstrated that UVC treatment carried out on both day 0 and day 1 significantly reduced the fungal bioburden of infected burns. UVC was found to be superior to a topical antifungal drug, nystatin cream. UVC was tested on normal mouse skin and no gross damage was observed 24 h after 6.48 J cm−2. DNA lesions (cyclobutane pyrimidine dimers) were observed by immunofluorescence in normal mouse skin immediately after a 6.48 J cm−2 UVC exposure, but the lesions were extensively repaired at 24 h after UVC exposure.**

I guess this is why a systemic candida infection is like AIDS wasting.

> Alcohol boosts cortisol, a fat-creating hormone
>
>
>
> **Drinking heavily or even occasionally increases the body's release of cortisol - the hormone that breaks down muscle and retains fat. This loss of muscle can mean a huge slowdown in one's metabolism, making it easier to gain weight. In addition, alcohol causes a drop in testosterone in men, a hormone which helps burn fat.
>
>
>
> Alcohol also blocks the body from burning fat. Research published in the American Journal of Clinical Nutrition reported that fat metabolism can be reduced by as much as 73 percent after only two drinks of vodka and lemonade in a one-hour time period. In effect, alcohol shuts down the body's ability to access fat stores for energy. The body needs to be well-hydrated in order to build muscle and burn fat. Alcohol has the effect of dehydrating the body.
>
>
>
> Learn more: [http://www.naturalnews.com/035319\_alcohol\_body\_fat\_calories.html#ixzz2BsLaI0YB](http://http://www.naturalnews.com/035319_alcohol_body_fat_calories.html#ixzz2BsLaI0YB)
>
>
>
>
>
>
> Once considered fringe, "health nut" advice, scientists have recently documented multiple ways probiotics can benefit health. **Now, at the American Heart Association's Scientific Sessions 2012 currently underway in Los Angeles, researchers have announced taking a probiotic supplement might help prevent heart disease.**
>
>
>
> Learn more: [http://www.naturalnews.com/037884\_probiotics\_bad\_cholesterol\_heart\_disease.html#ixzz2BsNB0siD](http://http://www.naturalnews.com/037884_probiotics_bad_cholesterol_heart_disease.html#ixzz2BsNB0siD)
>
>
>
>
>
> In earlier studies, a strain of live probiotic microorganisms (Lactobacillus reuteri) had shown promise in lowering blood levels of LDL or "bad" cholesterol. For the new study, the investigators looked to see if the same probiotic could lower LDL and reduce molecules of cholesterol attached to fatty acids known as cholesterol esters. A combination of elevated cholesterol esters plus high LDL has been tied to dangerous plaque buildup in the arteries, resulting in a heightened risk of heart disease.
>
>
>
> The study involved 127 adult research subjects who all had high cholesterol. About half the patients took the probiotic two times a day, while the rest were given placebo capsules. After just nine weeks, those taking the probiotic had LDL levels 11.6 percent lower than those on placebos. In addition, cholesterol esters were reduced by 6.3 percent and cholesterol ester saturated fatty acids fell by almost nine percent, compared with the placebo group**.
>
>
>
> Learn more: [http://www.naturalnews.com/037884\_probiotics\_bad\_cholesterol\_heart\_disease.html#ixzz2BsMuJ8Zw](http://http://www.naturalnews.com/037884_probiotics_bad_cholesterol_heart_disease.html#ixzz2BsMuJ8Zw)

> Pancreas May ‘Taste’ Fructose, Hinting at Links to Diabetes
>
>
>
> (Excerpt)
>
>
>
> HeConnection-InformedNew research shows that the pancreas has sweet-taste receptors — like those found on the tongue — that can “taste” fructose. Fructose is a common ingredient in many foods and beverages. Nutrition experts have warned that Americans take in far too much fructose and the new findings might bolster their concerns.
>
>
>
> In lab studies of pancreas cells from both humans and mice, the researchers found that when the pancreas tastes fructose from foods, it responds by producing more insulin. Insulin is a hormone that allows sugar from the blood to enter the body’s cells. However, excess levels of insulin, or an inefficient use of insulin, have been linked to obesity and type 2 diabetes, the researchers said.

### chocolate — 2012-11-19T01:30:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-im-thinking-about-going-over-to-the-dark-side.2766/post-65233>

[http://ajcn.nutrition.org/content/87/4/1028.full](http://http://ajcn.nutrition.org/content/87/4/1028.full)

Luminal fructose inhibits **rat intestinal sodium-phosphate cotransporter gene expression and phosphate uptake1,2,3,4,5

> Background: While searching by microarray for sugar-responsive genes, we inadvertently discovered that sodium-phosphate cotransporter 2B (NaPi-2b) mRNA concentrations were much lower in fructose-perfused than in glucose-perfused intestines of neonatal rats. Changes in NaPi-2b mRNA abundance by sugars were accompanied by similar changes in NaPi-2b protein abundance and in rates of inorganic phosphate (Pi) uptake.
>
>
>
> Objective: We tested the hypothesis that luminal fructose regulates NaPi-2b.
>
>
>
> Design: We perfused into the intestine fructose, glucose, and nonmetabolizable or poorly transported glucose analogs as well as phlorizin.
>
>
>
> Results: NaPi-2b mRNA concentrations and Pi uptake rates in fructose-perfused intestines were ≈30% of those in glucose and its analogs. NaPi-2b inhibition by fructose is specific because the mRNA abundance and activity of the fructose transporter GLUT5 (glucose transporter 5) increased with fructose perfusion, whereas those of other transporters were independent of the perfusate. Plasma Pi after 4 h of perfusion was independent of the perfusate, probably because normal kidneys can maintain normophosphatemia. Inhibiting glucose-6-phosphatase, another fructose-responsive gene, with tungstate or vanadate nonspecifically **inhibited NaPi-2b mRNA expression and Pi uptake in both glucose- or fructose-perfused intestines. The AMP kinase (AMPK)–activator AICAR (5-aminoimidazole-4-carboxamide-1-β-D-ribofuranoside) enhanced and the fatty acid synthase–AMPK inhibitor C75 (3-carboxy-4-octyl-2-methylenebutyrolactone trans-4-carboxy-5-octyl-3-methylenebutyrolactone) prevented fructose inhibition of NaPi-2b but had no effect on expression of other transporters. NaPi-2b expression decreased markedly with age and was inhibited by fructose in all age groups.**
>
>
>
> Conclusions: Energy levels in enterocytes may play a role in NaPi-2b inhibition by luminal fructose. Consumption of fructose that supplies ≈10% of caloric intake by Americans clearly affects absorption of Pi and may promote Pi homeostasis in patients with impaired renal function**

> .

### chocolate — 2012-12-16T11:24:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-im-thinking-about-going-over-to-the-dark-side.2766/post-67633>

I've been reading up on buccally absorbed vitamins the past few days and prolactin and a little of this and that. It was kind of neat to learn that copper is buccally absorbed, as is vitamin c. There is a group of folks in India that have too much collagen in their mouths from chewing on some nut. Copper is also buccally absorbed and you can get it through your skin. Copper and vitamin c can disrupt each other. After this long trip around the flagpole, I learn that vitamin c is required to make carnitine, and has a saturation point for vitamin c in the gut, but not really in the mouth. I think of the pregnant women that destroy their teeth sucking lemons. I wonder if they have too much copper? Who knows? A little pub med article talks about a woman that had a copper iud and started lactating. They removed it and she was fine. Three years later they replaced it and she started lactating again. I guess since copper pools down there and rushes to the breasts when you are pregnant, its absorbs down there, too. It misses the vitals that way. I've about come to the conclusion that these things are circadian as well. Selenium is a copper antagonist, and you can't absorb iodine without selenium, there is no way to store it.... what the heck?

I just want to conquer the fat solubles.

### chocolate — 2012-12-22T18:31:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-im-thinking-about-going-over-to-the-dark-side.2766/post-68212>

I've spent every spare minute the past week reading about taurine. I guess, it goes hand in hand with iodine to protect the brain. It keeps potassium in cells and magnesium needs it for regulation.... gee, everything I need. It prevents fructose induced insulin resistance, its an appetite suppressant, and it hedges the alcohol/candida disruption of stage four sleep. Candida disrupts taurine. I guess its the real darling of the supplement world. It is part of a huge study and I just read a really neat MITÂ powerpoint from a couple months back on taurine. It talks about how it is used in the brain to make bleach somehow to kill germs in encephalitis. The iodine is needed as a cofactor to prevent damage, according to another study. It heals alcoholic liver and it busts up the bile and cholesterol so your fats are metabolized properly and your cholesterol is available to do its good deeds. I feel much better and have a boatload of supps to use up. Kind of stupid, I should be down to just a few. I think I understand why muscle gets spent for no apparent reason now. If you are taurine deficient in your diet or your overloaded on candida, that is the only way to get taurine. anyway, studies have been done in warm adapted rats with taurine iodine and phosphlipids and the brain mass returns. The taurine needs methionine and cysteine to be produced and we all seem to have our methionine problems. Supposedly, if you have the taurine, you can just ditch all of the sulphur based aminos.

I'm using my husband's computer and don't want to junk it up so I can't post articles. I've mailed them all to myself for later. I was also reading a compilation of iodine uses and treatments from last century (pre-penicillin era) and one of the things that came screaming off the page to me was about how parts of Manitoba supplied children with potassiumm iodide to prevent goiter and not one child receiving the SSKI contracted polio. pretty fabulous if you ask me. I had an aunt that was in her twenties mowing the lawn one evening and couldn't walk the next. For want of a little pill.

### janagram — 2012-12-22T22:11:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-im-thinking-about-going-over-to-the-dark-side.2766/post-68225>

Wow that SSKI is great...

good to learn about taurine...so much interesting stuff in your journal...

### chocolate — 2012-12-24T12:35:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-im-thinking-about-going-over-to-the-dark-side.2766/post-68302>

http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/12/121221123504.htm?utm\_source=feedburner&utm\_medium=feed&utm\_campaign=Feed%3A+sciencedaily+%28ScienceDaily%3A+Latest+Science+News%29

**A New Type of Nerve Cell Found in the Brain**

Dec. 21, 2012 â€” Scientists at Karolinska Institutet in Sweden, in [collaboration](http://http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/12/121221123504.htm?utm_source=feedburner&utm_medium=feed&utm_campaign=Feed%3A+sciencedaily+%28ScienceDaily%3A+Latest+Science+News%29) with colleagues in Germany and the Netherlands, have identified a previously unknown group of nerve cells in the brain. The nerve cells regulate cardiovascular functions such as heart rhythm and blood pressure. It is hoped that the discovery, which is published in the _Journal of Clinical Investigation_, will be significant in the long term in the treatment of cardiovascular diseases in humans."

"This is, however, still far in the future. A more immediate conclusion is that it is of utmost importance to identify and treat pregnant women with hypothyroidism, since their low level of thyroid hormone may harm the production of these neurons in the fetus, and this may in the long run cause cardiovascular disorders in the offspring."

I don't know if these are thyroid controlled or vice versa.... the thyroid hormone is involved in their development.Â  I'm sure there is a much better place for this article... just haven't been able to muddle through yet.

I found another study about schizophrenia and cortical cells in white matter, when they are normally only in gray. I guess that's a good enough reason for mainstream medicine to start treating functional adrenal problems. Its just a little to individualized, I guess.

"

# Thread: Breast Feeding promotion = Nanny State?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breast-feeding-promotion-nanny-state.2769/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Raiken3712 — 2012-07-30T22:50:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breast-feeding-promotion-nanny-state.2769/post-41965>

[http://www.foxnews.com/politics/2012/07/29/nyc-mayor-wants-hospitals-to-lock-up-baby-formula-to-encourage-breast-feeding/?test=latestnews](http://http://www.foxnews.com/politics/2012/07/29/nyc-mayor-wants-hospitals-to-lock-up-baby-formula-to-encourage-breast-feeding/?test=latestnews)

I personally don't see it as all that bad....they compare it to Bloomberg outlawing sodas over a certain size and yet here we are talking about just putting the formula out of sight...they can still get it if they want it. Their reasons for encouraging breast feeding over formula are probably not as thorough as mine or Dr. Mercola's or anyone else who knows why Formula at least standard formula is so bad, but I think its a positive overall to let people know of the benefits.

Banning things is not the same as saying one thing has benefits but still allowing you to get the other.

Thoughts?

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-30T23:10:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breast-feeding-promotion-nanny-state.2769/post-41967>

This is being pushed for nationwide - by moms who didn't receive appropriate breastfeeding support while in the hospital -

With both kids I was given a "Breastfeeding support" bag sponsored by similac - each back had formula samples. WFT? If I'm bfing why would I want a bag w/formula samples.

Formula companies offer lactation support counseling on 800# - and they are staffed by formula companies employees who are not trained IBCLCs! They regularly recommend supplementing instead of nursing.

WHO and CDC both recommend exclusive breastfeeding for the first 6mths (Doesn't matter if its nursing, eping, or via donor milk). However of the two only the WHO recommends nursing until 2yr of age for all children.

I remembered when I was 1st pregnant - I kept getting formula samples - I actually wrote on them return to sender - they stopped showing up. Unfortunately w/dd she was unable to nurse and I was unable to produce enough milk through expression to exclusively feed her breastmilk - so she got formula - this was before HumanMilk4HumanBabies got going - if it had been around I would have feed her that over formula - I did have friends who donated to me as well... the problem w/formula - is so many practitioners recommend it to help mom recover, give her a chance to rest or to give dad an opportunity to feed the baby - all that does is make exclusive breastfeeding that much harder.

Breastfeeding is the hardest thing I've ever done. I have a love hate relationship with my pump. I love nursing my son, and I wished dd could have nursed as well. only about 1% of women has a condition called IGT - where they cannot produce milk at all, the rest have chosen lazy route when they've been educated on the benefits, but choose formula. Its heart wrenching for those who do more than try to exclusively breastfeed not be able to do that - you can feel like a failure - even when you're not. Formula is a good thing in those instances where there are no other options.

But there are other options - milk from other women, for thousands of years this is how we supported infants who's mothers died or couldn't produce milk. Its how mothers who had a loss of a child at birth recovered from their grief.

When you look at the recommendations from the WHO the first choice is the milk from the mother of a child, 2nd is milk via direct nursing from another woman, 3rd is donor milk from multiple women, 4th is a WAP formula made w/goats milk. and 5th is Formula

That should say something but the marketing pushed by the food and drug mfrs who make this stuff - have sold americans on the idea that breasts aren't for nurturing offspring...

I could go on for hours about this....

when hospitals remove formula from their maternity wards - it is b/c the women using the facility are requesting it. some hospitals require you to bring your own if you don't plan on breastfeeding.

### Huck — 2012-07-30T23:24:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breast-feeding-promotion-nanny-state.2769/post-41968>

Just because the government happens to get it right every once in a while, doesn't mean they should involve themselves like this. Seems like Bloomberg wants to be health commissioner, not the Mayor.

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-30T23:28:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breast-feeding-promotion-nanny-state.2769/post-41969>

> **Huck;42212 wrote:** Just because the government happens to get it right every once in a while, doesn't mean they should involve themselves like this. Seems like Bloomberg wants to be health commissioner, not the Mayor.

maybe he's auditioning for the cabinet position of HHS...

### kathylu — 2012-07-30T23:49:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breast-feeding-promotion-nanny-state.2769/post-41971>

Well, I do agree with the intention but I think the implementation is off base. Education is the answer, but ultimately the decision rests with the mother.

I was only able to breastfeed for 3 months, and only partially at that. I just didn't produce enough milk, too old I guess. Fortunately I had a girlfriend with a new baby who generously donated breast milk for almost 6 months. Between the two of us, my baby did well. Then I switched to the WAP formula (Fallon formula). My son really thrived on it, so all is well.

I think the use of bf first, then milk donation options then the Fallon formula would be the best path. No commercial formula unless there is no other option to keep the baby from starving.

### nonchalant — 2012-07-31T00:04:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breast-feeding-promotion-nanny-state.2769/post-41973>

The government should have no place in this.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-31T01:07:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breast-feeding-promotion-nanny-state.2769/post-41991>

In an Australian hospital I consulted with, formula was treated as medicine because it has so many known side effects. The hospital gave it to any mother who requested it, but she had to sign a consent saying this was what she wanted. Personal choice cannot be interfered with; however neither can informed consent. If you look at some of the recent research, a day's worth of soy formula has as much aluminum as a year's worth of vaccines. This cannot be taken lightly. (As well as issues with gut permeability from birth, leptin in breastmilk, etc.) However, the problem also cannot rest solely in the mother's hands. A mother with a newborn is exquisitely vulnerable. If she chooses not to breastfeed (or cannot...), then finding human milk banks/donations and/or using a homemade recipe like kathylu mentioned is obviously the next best step... but mothers need help receiving this information and accessing these things.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-07-31T01:47:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breast-feeding-promotion-nanny-state.2769/post-41999>

> **Nonchalant;42217 wrote:** The government should have no place in this.

This one is hard for me...on the one hand I generally don't like government being involved...but if the choice is government involvement = more breast feeding or no government involvement = more formula use...

Ideally the hospitals would know whats best and be doing it without government even talking about it...

Sigh rghglllghghlghgl

### kathylu — 2012-07-31T01:47:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breast-feeding-promotion-nanny-state.2769/post-42000>

This is an aside, but I never told any health care provider (and not much of anyone else either) that I was making my own baby formula from 6 months onward. I was really afraid someone would call Protective Services and report it and then I might have legal issues, or possibly even have the baby taken into custody for his own protection. Most people would think I was deliberately malnourishing my child. Then of course, there is the "raw milk is illegal" issue too. Stupidity abounds.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-07-31T17:26:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breast-feeding-promotion-nanny-state.2769/post-42048>

yeah...telling people you feed your baby raw milk...it might actually happen that way...sad

I've seen some absurd stories about babies being taken away ....for some really stupid stuff I wouldn't put it past them.

>

>
>
>
> \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_ Phosphatidylcholine 250 ml Ethanol 75 ml Cetyl alcohol 10 ml Distilled water 60 ml Medium chain triglycerides 10 ml DHEA 1000 mg \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
>
>
>
> BJR returned every third day for analysis.
>

### kathylu — 2012-08-01T20:51:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dhea-cream-making-using-ordering-helpful-hints-in-this-thread-pls.2777/post-42313>

Hi girls (and guys). First, most if not all liquor stores carry grain alcohol (95% ethanol), but you have to ask for it. Cheaper than vodka and no additives, so that would be my choice for an organic solvent.

Now, how are you going to know what effect the dhea cream will have? Are you going to make a certain concentration (maybe 50 mg/ml?) and then draw blood for labs? Or are you simply going to "put some on" thinking it will be well absorbed and will help you? I am quite curious because I have realized after testing that oral dhea is mostly inactivated by first pass metabolism through the liver. So, I am swallowing 100mg per day and only a fraction of that actually makes it into my bloodstream. Since I am already doing estrogen, progesterone and testosterone as transdermal creams, adding one more wouldn't be a problem, but I am just wondering how best to monitor the situation. For instance, many people buy OTC progesterone cream, but the concentration is so low that it has very little effect and is really just a waste of money.

bypass the liver and take your DHEA sublingually.... micronized works best for this

### kathylu — 2012-08-01T21:19:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dhea-cream-making-using-ordering-helpful-hints-in-this-thread-pls.2777/post-42323>

> **janagram;42570 wrote:** hmm, kathylu, so my gyno, who said the P-cream was just a placebo was right? grrr....hoping it does something...following this thread avidly as I would like to use dhea that way....

Yup. The OTC progesterone has a couple of problems. First the concentration is so low that it would be incredibly expensive to put enough on to get an effect. Second, many of them are formulated from wild mexican yam which supposedly converts to progesterone in the body...only most people can't make the conversion. If it contains "USP progesterone" that's the real stuff (like Emerita cream), but then you have the pesky concentration problem again. Cheaper and better to get progesterone cream from a compounding pharmacist by prescription.

### kathylu — 2012-08-02T02:20:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dhea-cream-making-using-ordering-helpful-hints-in-this-thread-pls.2777/post-42391>

> **Cavemam;42605 wrote:** ok, so do we think for topical we can skip the dissolving it? This is gonna be a crowed sourced product!
>
>
>
> And, what should the ratio of dhea to cream be?

This is just a suggestion, but I'd make a 1% cream. So the ratio would be 1 gram dhea in 100 ml of carrier base. Then each ml would contain 10 mg of dhea. I would start out using no more than 2 ml per day (20 mg) unless you plan to monitor blood levels (better to be safe!). I think you may have some issues with the dhea separating out if you just use coconut oil. I think you will need an emulsifier, such as 1 gram of cosmetic grade plant wax (not beeswax or paraffin!), although trying 95 % ethanol might be worthwhile.

Also, I wonder if using it directly on the face is good or not...dhea can cause hair growth. Just sayin.

For me, I can go through some amazing amounts of "cream" on my skin. I want to do my face + entire torso and upper thighs. . frankly I look like a burn victim for the most part - so I think my best tact is ratio of DHEA to cream.

My understanding is there isn't much chance of real absorption this way through skin. . .

Right now my evil scientists plan (pulling out the breville super whipper) is to make a solution of DHEA/Alcohol and try to make an emulsion that is a 2% DHEA/98% other stuff that I toss in. With CO, Vanilla, Shea Butter and Vit E capsule if I have some or get some while out.

I still feel like I'm flying blind and a wastin time - I was hoping more people would chime in. . .or you know, you go play for the other team and start dating a cute lady compounding pharmacist! Think how helpful that would be for all of us!

### chocolate — 2012-08-05T18:24:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dhea-cream-making-using-ordering-helpful-hints-in-this-thread-pls.2777/post-43074>

[http://zrtdocsblog.blogspot.com/2010/05/dhea-as-vaginal-treatment.html](http://http://zrtdocsblog.blogspot.com/2010/05/dhea-as-vaginal-treatment.html)

dhea as a vaginal treatment

> Until then, what can you do if you want to try it out? DHEA is an OTC supplement and any compounding pharmacy should be able to create a vaginal suppository or vaginal cream similar to those used in the studies. The studies have used dosages of 3.25mg to 13mg. Since the 3.25 mg dosage worked as well as the 13 mg I would not see any reason for using the higher dosage. In fact, since you are going for a localized effect, my educated guess is that even Â½ that dosage – 1.5 would work well for many women. Using DHEA may also have a higher safety rating than using vaginal testosterone since you are unlikely to trigger irreversible clitoral hypertrophy which maybe caused rarely by testosterone especially at higher dosages.

### Glamazon — 2012-08-12T00:20:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dhea-cream-making-using-ordering-helpful-hints-in-this-thread-pls.2777/post-44411>

I think I am going to try my own compounding. Going to the science supply store tomorrow. It is filled with wonderful things like beakers and glass stirring rods and cobalt blue bottles. They cater to the artisanal beauty crowd who do their own creams and aromatherapy.

Found this today: DHEA will dissolve in fats, oils and alcohol. Liquid DHEA, which is usually DHEA dissolved in alcohol, is now available and is is claimed to be up to 4-5 times as well absorbed as DHEA capsules. (My own personal experiments indicate that DHEA dissolved in alcohol is twice as well absorbed as capsules.) DHEA capsules are somewhat better absorbed if taken with food.

[http://www.futurescience.com/dhea.html](http://http://www.futurescience.com/dhea.html)

### Glamazon — 2012-08-12T02:13:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dhea-cream-making-using-ordering-helpful-hints-in-this-thread-pls.2777/post-44424>

I also found this simple compounding formula on a pheromone forum:

To dissolve the DHEA, I emptied the capsule into a dropper bottle, added the oil, shook it very well, sat the bottle in a hot water bath, and continued to shake periodically. For best results I'd say give it a day or two of sitting around, and just shake it for a few seconds whenever you walk by, and re-heat your hot water bath whenever you get a chance.

There are extra fillers in the capsule that you will have to filter out after you have given the DHEA time to dissolve. I used a coffee filter, and it worked okay but not great. I'm hoping to think of a better solution for filtering the schmutz, so let me know if you think of one.

Also, if you use coconut oil make sure its fractionated coconut oil, not the whole coconut oil you can buy at the grocery store. That stuff is good to eat but will be greasy on the skin and probably wouldn't dissolve in alcohol very well. You could also dissolve the DHEA into DPG, jojoba, or perfumer's alcohol (or everclear if it's legal where you live. I can't get it in Chicago.)

[http://pherotruth.com/Thread-DHEA--4574](http://http://pherotruth.com/Thread-DHEA--4574)

> **Cavemam;44755 wrote:** What's this store? That sounds like fun. Want to go out for coffee when I come down!

I was going to go today but I spaced that it is closed on Sunday. [http://www.sciencecompany.com/](http://http://www.sciencecompany.com/)

It is on 1st and Lincoln. Lincoln is one way at that point, going North. Best would be to come down I 25 and get off at the Alameda exit (so much construction there so be careful) and go North on Lincoln. It is on the west side of Lincoln and parking is in the back. It's a little building on the corner of a parking lot. It's very close to downtown. Avoid rush hour at all costs. Don't want to spike that Cortisol.

I will PM you my cell today. Just make sure your in box has room! Let me know when to PM.

If you kid is a sciency kind of guy there's a lot stuff for kids to buy like weather stations and chemistry kits.

You could outfit a right good lab if you so desired.

### Glamazon — 2012-08-13T04:53:17+0300

### Glamazon — 2012-08-13T04:54:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dhea-cream-making-using-ordering-helpful-hints-in-this-thread-pls.2777/post-44623>

I am wondering, for those of you who are having a granular product after mixing and warming, if you need to get additives out by straining through a coffee filter.

### Glamazon — 2012-08-19T07:03:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dhea-cream-making-using-ordering-helpful-hints-in-this-thread-pls.2777/post-45662>

I used one gram of no additive DHEA mixed with 100 mls of warm melted Coconut oil. The DHEA didnt go into total solution. Some particulate remained. But, I put the beaker in ice bath still it started to get thick like maple syrup. I kept stirring it while it sat in the ice bath. Then I poured it into a glass covered container and put it in the freezer to finish the thickening process. This way I hoped to keep all the particles suspended as evenly as I can. When I put in on my skin there was no grainy feeling.

# Thread: Choline  Epigenetics
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/choline-epigenetics.2783/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-07-31T19:57:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/choline-epigenetics.2783/post-42085>

Choline found mainly in eggs, offal, and seafood!

[http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-07-sick-stress-blame-mom-epigenetics.html](http://http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-07-sick-stress-blame-mom-epigenetics.html)

If you're sick from stress, a new research report appearing in the August 2012 issue of The FASEB Journal suggests that what your mother ateâ€”or didn't eatâ€”may be part of the cause. The report shows that choline intake that is higher than what is generally recommended during pregnancy may improve how a child responds to stress. These improvements are the result of epigenetic changes that ultimately lead to lower cortisol levels. Epigenetic changes affect how a gene functions, even if the gene itself is not changed. Lowering cortisol is important as high levels of cortisol are linked to a wide range of problems ranging from mental health to metabolic and cardiovascular disorders.

### AKMan — 2012-08-01T20:52:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/choline-epigenetics.2783/post-42315>

The salmon I get aren't like what you buy in the grocery store, they've swam hundreds of miles to spawn, their skin is red and has bad patches. They get a big hump of fat on their backs. These are real salmon like our ancestors ate, not caught out in the ocean where their skin is silver. We fillet, then and dry or smoke them. They are usually bristling with eggs ready to be laid--the chickens don't get those! The meat is so oily, you can burn it like a candle when it's dried. I love living in Alaska!

# Thread: leaky gut... leaky brain... leaky skin?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leaky-gut-leaky-brain-leaky-skin.2785/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### hellojtm — 2012-07-31T21:55:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leaky-gut-leaky-brain-leaky-skin.2785/post-42098>

Hey All,

Just posting some spurious thoughts I have had in the last couple of weeks. I have only been able to glance at the blog in passing and not read anything in detail for a couple of months. However I did get the drift from Dr. Kruse that leaky gut potentially is a positive adaptation from a previous stressor that is now being triggered in advertently. I was also thinking about sweating and the fact that I bought thin outerwear that covers my arms and neck so that I wouldn't have to use sunblock while hiking. There were some other random trivial thoughts around like when I jumped in the ocean a couple weeks ago that maybe the salt water was a signal to my body or even being absorbed.

So what about leaky skin? Is this something that humans have, i.e. hormone replacement can be applied as a lotion, that earlier primates don't have? Along with our ability to sweat did we develop the ability to absorb? Or is absorbance the earlier state and other animals have developed a thicker skin barrier?

Here is an article that ultimately likens the skin to the intestines, which triggered me to finally make this post:

[The Skin's Secret Surveillance System](http://http://www.nature.com/news/the-skin-s-secret-surveillance-system-1.11075)

jesse

### nonchalant — 2012-07-31T22:30:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leaky-gut-leaky-brain-leaky-skin.2785/post-42109>

Interesting article, Jesse.

I know that since I've been avoiding low-quality seed oils, I don't burn or peel as easily.

Also since eating seafood daily, my skin quality has changed dramatically. More dense and smooth. I would guess less leaky maybe.

### hellojtm — 2012-09-18T00:10:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leaky-gut-leaky-brain-leaky-skin.2785/post-52221>

Is Chris Kresser reading my posts!?

from:

[http://www.westonaprice.org/2012-conference/2012-conference-presentations](http://http://www.westonaprice.org/2012-conference/2012-conference-presentations)

**Chris Kresser, L Ac The Gut-Brain-Skin** Axis: New Perspectives on an Old Idea

The connection between skin and mental health disorders has been a topic of growing interest in medicine over the past two decades, as evidenced by the rise of new disciplines like psychodermatology and neurodermatology. More recently, researchers are turning their attention to the connection between the skin and the gut. For example, studies have shown that those with acne and other skin problems are more likely to experience digestive issues like constipation, acid reflux and IBS - and vice versa. In this presentation, we'll discuss the relationship between digestive, mental/behavioral and skin disorders, and explore nutritional strategies to preventing and treating them.

### hellojtm — 2012-10-02T20:29:31+0300

[http://www.dovepress.com/comparison-with-ancestral-diets-suggests-dense-acellular-carbohydrates-peer-reviewed-article-DMSO](http://http://www.dovepress.com/comparison-with-ancestral-diets-suggests-dense-acellular-carbohydrates-peer-reviewed-article-DMSO)

### nonchalant — 2012-08-01T00:18:11+0300

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-01T01:51:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/study-supports-brain-gut-axis-as-critical-to-leptin-sensitivity.2786/post-42167>

Is that not just a hypothesis, as in, no experiment has been performed yet to support this? (Don't want the title to mislead people.)

### AKMan — 2012-08-01T02:24:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/study-supports-brain-gut-axis-as-critical-to-leptin-sensitivity.2786/post-42170>

> **Nate R;42419 wrote:** Is that not just a hypothesis, as in, no experiment has been performed yet to support this? (Don't want the title to mislead people.)

Every geek needs to read the full study (only 15 pages), here's the pdf: [http://www.dovepress.com/getfile.php?fileID=13214](http://http://www.dovepress.com/getfile.php?fileID=13214)

It perfectly sums up why paleo 'works'. Also explains why those almond flour pancakes and coconut flour cookies are doing more harm than good. Tie this to the epi-paleo rx and you have a recipe for success!

Here's the conclusion, in case you can't open the link:

"Conclusion

The increased storage life and convenience of some of our oldest agricultural products may come with a hitherto unrecognized metabolic cost. The foods eaten by hunter-gatherers, non-cereal horticulturalists, and those following a modern Paleolithic or â€œprimalâ€￾ diet are sharply delineated from modern foods by their lower carbohydrate densities. Consumption of exclusively low-density carbohydrates is suggested to produce a less inflammatory GI microbiota, and may explain the apparent absence of overweight and metabolic disease in two of these groups, and the promising early data from the third. This hypothesis may also explain (1) why obesity incidence scales with refined food intake, but has such confusing correlatory patterns with macronutrients; (2) why calorie-controlled diets of Westernized foods require a perpetual fight with homeostatic correction mechanisms; (3) the link between periodontal disease and systemic atherosclerotic

disease and obesity; (4) why the benefits of a diet of fruit and vegetables have not been replicated by supplements of the constituent antioxidants, vitamins, minerals, and fiber alongside a Western diet; (5) why low-carbohydrate diets produce ad libitum weight loss, but low-fat diets do not; and (6) the relative resistance of European people to obesity and diabetes from Westernized diets.

We should not settle for the meager improvements attainable

from the consensus dietary advice when it is already clear that so much more might be achieved. Our sights should be set high, to see how close we can move levels of industrialized metabolic health toward those enjoyed by non-Westernized populations. While many will resist making

dietary changes of such magnitude, official advice must nonetheless point in the correct direction, allowing individuals

to make informed decisions.

The social and financial burden of the epidemic of obesity and metabolic syndrome threatens the long-term viability of our health-care systems and perniciously undermines the other benefits of modern civilization, including redirection of scarce financial resources. If this dietary pattern is confirmed to work, practical policy solutions must be sought. Some grain cultivars may be found to produce less inflammation, sprouting techniques may be found to be of benefit, or grain production may be replaced with root-vegetable cultivation where practical. Over time, the interplay between market forces, attainable agricultural yields, and the practicalities of food-distribution networks may allow shifting demand from a newly informed populace to reshape global agribusiness.

A dietary pattern with carbohydrates exclusively from cellular low-density sources may remove the root cause of a range of our most prevalent diseases. The potential savings

in health-care costs should be borne in mind, and the hypothesis tested."

### nonchalant — 2012-08-02T00:47:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/study-supports-brain-gut-axis-as-critical-to-leptin-sensitivity.2786/post-42364>

Thanks for the link, AKMan.

# Thread: Stopping DHEA for baseline ASI?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stopping-dhea-for-baseline-asi.2791/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### SeaJane — 2012-08-01T02:02:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stopping-dhea-for-baseline-asi.2791/post-42168>

So I am doing my first set of labs for a consultation. I am taking 10 mg DHEA and 100mg 7-keto DHEA each day. I started the 100mg based on Jack's post and knowing my DHEA-S is about 45. In looking at my instructions for the ASI, they say to note if taking DHEA as it can affect the results. I am wondering if I should continue to take it or if I should stop it. If I stop it, how many days ahead do I need to stop?

Thanks,

Jane

### James — 2012-08-02T17:02:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stopping-dhea-for-baseline-asi.2791/post-42499>

Good question, I had this lab problem. I looked at one lab manual and it said 4 days to stop supplementation. But in my experience I have to stop for a week, or I will get a crazy lab result that came back saying 1000+ for DHEA-sulfate.

**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Mike H. — 2012-08-01T04:02:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tub-issues.2792/post-42185>

Hello all, So I have been playing with the whole CT thing and am really enjoying the results thus far. Recently I have graduated up to ice baths. I get the water as cold as possible and then place two bags of ice in there. My only issue is I don't fit real well in my tub. Last night I took the ice bath, then took a cold shower immediately after. Is there anything else I can do to help the situation?

Thanks

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-01T04:07:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tub-issues.2792/post-42189>

CT in phases? Left side, right side, front, back.... depending on the degree of the size problem

### Mike H. — 2012-08-01T05:27:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tub-issues.2792/post-42211>

I can try that. Thanks

### Destiny — 2012-08-02T13:18:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tub-issues.2792/post-42474>

> **Mike H.;42463 wrote:** I can try that. Thanks

I flip to my stomach and then back to my back.

### PrimalPam — 2012-08-03T00:08:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tub-issues.2792/post-42575>

> **Destiny;42735 wrote:** I flip to my stomach and then back to my back.

Same. I lay on my back for 20-25 mins, then flip to my stomach for 20-25 mins.

Just get as much of your body immersed in ice cold water for as long as you can take it. For me, it's 45 mins @ 50-53 degrees F.

Do you have a thermometer?

# Thread: Interesting Study - Confirms what we already know!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interesting-study-confirms-what-we-already-know.2794/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jim — 2012-08-01T06:42:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interesting-study-confirms-what-we-already-know.2794/post-42217>

Interesting study that confirms what we already know!

[Mice lacking δ-opioid receptors resist the development of diet-induced obesity](http://http://www.fasebj.org/content/26/8/3483.abstract)

Abstract

Pharmacological manipulation of opioid receptors alters feeding behavior. However, the individual contributions of each opioid receptor subtype on energy balance remain largely unknown. Herein, we investigated whether genetic disruption of the δ-opioid receptor (DOR) also controls energy homeostasis. Mice lacking DOR and wild-type mice were fed with standard diet and high-energy diet (HED). Mice were analyzed in vivo with the indirect calorimetry system, and tissues were analyzed by real-time PCR and Western blot analysis. DOR-knockout (KO) mice gained less weight (P

# Thread: Seniors Optimized living in aged care
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seniors-optimized-living-in-aged-care.2796/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jimmy82 — 2012-08-01T10:04:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seniors-optimized-living-in-aged-care.2796/post-42229>

Many oldies or the senior citizens who don't get proper care and proper attention at home they used to move to an independent living centers or the aged care centers but i must say that many of the seniors care centers do offer very versatile life over there but when some one reaches there the real picture is not exactly like that and i came to know for a fact when i visited my uncle in New York..

No doubt they were providing their very best services but some of the features that were mentioned on their brochure was not really available .....

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-01T23:05:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seniors-optimized-living-in-aged-care.2796/post-42340>

this is a huge issue for elders.......you eat their food you meet your maker faster.

### nonchalant — 2012-08-02T00:07:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seniors-optimized-living-in-aged-care.2796/post-42357>

I heard of one guy doing fairly well in a nursing home. He ate mostly eggs, lots of eggs. I guess they gave him some latitude on what he ate. They probably don't have seafood available very often. People do seem to decline quickly in such places.

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-02T05:48:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seniors-optimized-living-in-aged-care.2796/post-42441>

One of the reasons I went back to Michigan was to investigate housing etc for tribal seniors. yikes what an eye opener! One place had some tribal housing but it was way out in the country and even with their sporadically provided transportation it was bad and the waiting list is 10 years! I don't know if someone was trying to pull my leg but I was told some elders where pretty far down the highway to town pushing their walkers before they got picked up.

The other place was a converted motel for senior housing and in very bad condition.

I went to a couple of "elders lunches." One would think with all the excellent wild white fish available it would be a main staple at these meals. It was all white bread (lots) commercial deli type cole slaw, fake butter and horrors, commodity cheese! (I guess you have to be Indian to fully appreciate that last phrase.) Disgusting! I took a cup of tea and told them I got up late and just ate breakfast. Of course, white frosted cake for desert. The other place I went to was a church pot luck. Typical crap food passed off as "They always provide a nice spread." So much for the so called influence of the tribal diabetes program. By this time of the trip I had even given up drinking any tap water and stuck to a type of purified water I found. We used a combo of an electric hot plate or gas stove and had wild white fish or buffalo steaks with all the trimmings and of course plenty of bacon at the many beautiful parks in the area. I suspected it would be like this but I had to see for myself, still it was very disappointing.

### Pamela — 2012-08-13T17:20:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seniors-optimized-living-in-aged-care.2796/post-44686>

My father is in a nursing home and what they eat is horrible. Seeing these people and how they "live" in these places really gets me working toward optimal for myself. It is not an option to go in one of those places.

### Cyryl — 2013-07-02T21:52:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seniors-optimized-living-in-aged-care.2796/post-89401>

Private well known old age care homes provide the best facilities to old people. There is a nursing staff available 24/7 for the care of old people. So check the good old age care facility for your parents.

### fitness@home — 2013-07-03T00:16:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seniors-optimized-living-in-aged-care.2796/post-89405>

Years ago, I was employed at the nicest nursing home in Monroe County, MI. Worked in their office and often typed up the menus for the head of the Dietary Dept.

I have to say that it was _very_ heavy on carbs at breakfast (cereals, oatmeal, toast, juice). They did a reasonable job of getting protein and veggies in at lunch and dinner, but there was always a carb side dish, bread, and dessert included. They pretty much followed the USDA food pyramid recommendations. Sad...very sad.

Although it is a very nice nursing care facility, I hope to never need such a place.

### caroline — 2013-07-03T05:39:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seniors-optimized-living-in-aged-care.2796/post-89421>

We really need to work very hard not to wind up in a nursing home - trouble is we may not have the option.........

My really dear friend and I are giving her sons explicit instructions on how to care for us if need be....


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Lawsuit challenges anti-ageing claims  Former executive sues manufacturer of pill mea
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lawsuit-challenges-anti-ageing-claims-former-executive-sues-manufacturer-of-pill-mea.2799/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-08-01T17:06:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lawsuit-challenges-anti-ageing-claims-former-executive-sues-manufacturer-of-pill-mea.2799/post-42256>

[http://www.nature.com/news/lawsuit-challenges-anti-ageing-claims-1.11090](http://http://www.nature.com/news/lawsuit-challenges-anti-ageing-claims-1.11090)

> â€œYouâ€™re going to be hearing from my attorney,â€￾ Brian Egan told his boss on his last day of work, nearly a year ago. Last week, Egan filed a class-action lawsuit that accuses Telomerase Activation Sciences (TA Sciences) in New York of engaging in deceptive business practices in promoting a proprietary herbal extract intended to reverse the effects of ageing.
>
>
>
> The lawsuit threatens to put the science of telomeres â€” repetitive nucleotide sequences that protect the ends of chromosomes during DNA replication â€” on trial. But Noel Patton, president of TA Sciences, denies all of the allegations. â€œWe stand by what we say,â€￾ he adds.
>
>
>
> The connection between cellular ageing and telomere length is rooted in solid research. Telomeres become shorter every time a cell divides, and when they are lost cells can no longer reproduce. The enzyme telomerase can lengthen telomeres, possibly slowing or reversing degenerative diseases. In one study1, mice genetically engineered to lack functional telomerase showed brain degeneration and shrunken testes, but those effects were reversed when the enzyme was reactivated.

.

Just an excerpt

If you continue to act as you have always acted, you will continue to get what you have always gotten. If you want different results in your life or your work, all you have to do is change your mind......We do not need an intelligent mind that speaks, but a patient heart that listens. Make your choices and you create your life.

I have to copy, paste it and read it every day!

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-02T20:56:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ode-to-heavy-whipping-cream.2800/post-42542>

> **Cavemam;42797 wrote:** Well, I can't tell if Dr. K was poo-pooing or approving this thread - but it's helping me! I like hearing your stories and that other people are giving up this or that - and that most of you are approaching it from a logical standpoint and not a woo woo.
>
>
>
> I'm doing Kerrygold in coffee today.

I understood his comment as neither. What I got out of it was that if you are not getting what you would like to have, then you need to change your mind first in the process of achieving new results. You're unlikely to achieve something by hanging on to the "old" thing in your mind.... the great thick cream and how you're gonna miss it and if it's \*really\* necessary to eliminate it. Start thinking in terms of the results you're going for..... and the cream will go with not regret b/c you're focused on allowing something new, better, more like what you were looking for. And then it will happen. The cream might never be an issue again whether or not you'll eventually be able to eat it. B/c you're now where you wanted to be.

As for whether or not you should have cream, no matter how thick or buttery, my honest opinion is that things aren't working the way you would like them to, there's quite a few things that suggest that you have an ED-type problem going on, EDs need to follow AI protocol. There is no cream, there is no butter. But that is your choice to make. You can stick with what hasn't been working, or you can move on to new grounds. The new grounds might have nothing to do with cream or no cream.

As usual, YMMV

### Mackay — 2012-08-03T13:23:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ode-to-heavy-whipping-cream.2800/post-42651>

For what it's worth, there is the whole discussion about A1 milk versus A2 milk.

A1 milk has a protein configuration that gives it a similar quality to wheat is messing up your health.

A2 milk does not have this problem and some folks report that they are fine on A2 milk. Products like ghee have almost all of the proteins taken out, so there should be few problems with it.

Butter still has some protein in it, so some folks will have a problem if it is made from A1 milk.

By the way, all goats milk is A2.

### Michael — 2012-08-03T16:30:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ode-to-heavy-whipping-cream.2800/post-42667>

> **Mackay;42930 wrote:** For what it's worth, there is the whole discussion about A1 milk versus A2 milk.

Now I've heard people in paleo (some big names) say that A1/A2 is all a bit airy and there's nothing proved. Makes me wonder if they ever looked into it much. But if you hear Woodforde speak and give him a fair hearing .... I don't think he ever said he had definite proof. But what he has got is a plausible mechanism, some good rodent experiments, some suggestive stuff the Russians have done with autistic kids, and a ton of personal testimony. Any of those might not mean much on its own, but if you put them all together, it really does seem to add up to something.

### kathylu — 2012-08-03T16:51:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ode-to-heavy-whipping-cream.2800/post-42675>

The tack that I and my family are taking is to avoid dairy as much as possible. When we do indulge, I try to make it raw A2 dairy. I am finding I don't really miss it that much and interestingly neither does my 5 year old. He prefers to drink Kevita instead of milk.
