# Thread: Face Dunk Question
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/face-dunk-question.2855/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Arthur Morris — 2012-08-04T18:47:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/face-dunk-question.2855/post-42855>

I have been doing the face dunks for about 6 weeks--30 minutes in 52 degree water. My breath hold times are getting slowly better; they now range from about 35 seconds when I start up to a minute.

Most days when I am done I feel a bit nauseous for a few hours. Is this normal? Has anyone else experienced it? Any remedies?

### Melinda — 2012-08-04T19:48:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/face-dunk-question.2855/post-42873>

Hi Arthur,

I'm a big fan of face dunking. I've never gone past about 5 minutes worth of dunks and don't count how long I hold my breath. It does lengthen over time but I'm not keeping score. I wonder if you are doing it for too long, 30 minutes. I don't remember anyone doing it for that many minutes...I hope someone else with more experience chimes in.

### prAna303 — 2012-08-04T20:44:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/face-dunk-question.2855/post-42884>

Yes itÂ´s normal because you can consider doing that volume of dunking is equal to statictraining in freediving (apnea). One thing that could happend is: the nauseous feeling is caused due to brain tissue becoming hypoxic. What has caused the hypoxia there is a different story (essentially it is restricted blood flow to the brain tissue, caused by hypocapnic brain vessel constriction (hyperventilation) and/or restricted pulmonary circulation (due to high pressures in the lung if packing was involved) or, if combined (packing with hyperventilation) a bit from both). The cold helps to speed up these effects also. If the feeling is there everytime consider doing in for shorter times or make sure you have a spotter with you if you blackout.

### Destiny — 2012-08-04T22:58:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/face-dunk-question.2855/post-42908>

I don't count time either. This is my morning CT protocol. I just do my face dunks for a few minutes, then submerge my arms and last my feet.

Then, I take a cold bath in the evening. I have to flip from my back to my stomach since my bath is not that deep.

### jib — 2012-08-04T23:07:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/face-dunk-question.2855/post-42912>

Arthur, I think you can progress now to the next step in the protocol, and start to shorten your face dunks. Maybe you've already been doing this??

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-04T23:20:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/face-dunk-question.2855/post-42921>

Art, with you I would just make sure to take things slow, and don't be afraid to take a day off for your body to recover. With everything you've said - I wouldn't be suprised if you have some wicked detox symptoms. I was able to hop from face dunks at around 20-30 mins each (I was snorkling) right to a cold bath with no probs. I was really enjoying my face dunks - and my skin looked so awesome I was addicted. So I don't know if it's "too much". In fact, I think more people go wrong with not going slow enough through the initial detox.

I would totally consider adding in spot icing to face dunks, then increase spot ice time then just go for the bath. I quit face dunking when I started baths. . .it just was a time issue.

In fact, I think summer would be a great time to get "used" to a cold bath as the post bath warmup should go a little smoother with it hot outside.

You will quickly learn , taking the bath is easy! It's boredom and then the post bath warmup that are most uncomfortable.

### Arthur Morris — 2012-08-05T16:56:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/face-dunk-question.2855/post-43049>

> **Melinda;43152 wrote:** Hi Arthur,
>
>
>
> I'm a big fan of face dunking. I've never gone past about 5 minutes worth of dunks and don't count how long I hold my breath. It does lengthen over time but I'm not keeping score. I wonder if you are doing it for too long, 30 minutes. I don't remember anyone doing it for that many minutes...I hope someone else with more experience chimes in.

Thanks Melinda. I could have sworn that the 30 minutes was in the Quick Start Guide but I just checked and it is not. Are folks doing the face dunks right before they do the Compression Shirt & Ice or the Full Body Immersion with Ice?

### Arthur Morris — 2012-08-05T16:58:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/face-dunk-question.2855/post-43050>

> **prAna303;43165 wrote:** Yes itÂ´s normal because you can consider doing that volume of dunking is equal to statictraining in freediving (apnea). One thing that could happend is: the nauseous feeling is caused due to brain tissue becoming hypoxic. What has caused the hypoxia there is a different story (essentially it is restricted blood flow to the brain tissue, caused by hypocapnic brain vessel constriction (hyperventilation) and/or restricted pulmonary circulation (due to high pressures in the lung if packing was involved) or, if combined (packing with hyperventilation) a bit from both). The cold helps to speed up these effects also. If the feeling is there everytime consider doing in for shorter times or make sure you have a spotter with you if you blackout.

Thanks for the explanation and the suggestion. I have never come close to blacking out or even feeling dizzy.

### Arthur Morris — 2012-08-05T17:00:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/face-dunk-question.2855/post-43052>

> **Destiny;43189 wrote:** I don't count time either. This is my morning CT protocol. I just do my face dunks for a few minutes, then submerge my arms and last my feet.
>
>
>
> Then, I take a cold bath in the evening. I have to flip from my back to my stomach since my bath is not that deep.

Thanks! I have been doing my face dunking in the mornings after breakfast and doing ice baths in the evening. My tub is so small that if I laid on my tummy I would never get out.... I will start a thread on my cold baths soon.

Where in NY are you?

### Arthur Morris — 2012-08-05T17:12:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/face-dunk-question.2855/post-43053>

> **jib;43193 wrote:** Arthur, I think you can progress now to the next step in the protocol, and start to shorten your face dunks. Maybe you've already been doing this??

Here is where I am at: I went right from the face dunks to a form of full body immersion with ice. I started getting in Florida tap water at about 80 degrees. And was chilled. I started adding ice. After a month I was down to 61 degree water. My tub is small. I stay in for 35 minutes. I keep my legs elevated with my feet up on the wall. My trunk is totally submerged. I read while I am in the tub so my upper arms are in the water for the most part.

The first time that I kept my feet in the water I could barely stand without falling down when I got out; that is why I keep my legs raised and my feet on the wall. I am a well controlled Type II diabetic on insulin. A1C =5.1. I have had very cold feet for the past few decades. The diabetes doc (the renowned Richard K. Bernstein) says that my cold feet and hands are from Reynaud's syndrome not from neuropathy.

Then I went away for two weeks living on a boat in AK and was not able to do any CT. I am home for a while and back in full swing.

The cold baths lower my blood sugar levels dramatically as does my late afternoon swim. Best of all, rather than getting up 6-7 times a night for pit stops I get up only once after a cold bath.

I do travel a lot and will do some CT stuff as I can when on the road.

### Grizz — 2012-08-04T19:15:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iodine-ll.2856/post-42863>

Somehow the original iodine thread got closed by accident, an Youthfuliz could not post her valuable story. Here it is:

> Just a quick outline:
>
>
>
> July 4th: found a lump on my neck while looking in the mirror. Felt it...about the size of a small egg, moved up and down when I swallowed.
>
>
>
> July 5th: went to the health center, got blood test and ultrasound done.
>
>
>
> July 10th: results came back: blood test showed normal thyroid hormones. Ultrasound showed two nodules, size 2.9 and 2.1. Most likely benign but biopsy was suggested due to the size.
>
>
>
> Between July 5th and July 10th: I read online and found that Iodine deficiency is the main cause for thyroid nodules. I read the iodine threads on mda and jackkruse.com (I also post on those forums using the same id), asked questions there (thanks Grizz and others who replied) and started to supplement iodine from Saturday July 7th. I also ate a lot of seaweeds and oysters starting that weekend. I couldn't find Lugol's iodine or iodoral locally so I used potassium Iodide that my local vitaminshoppe carried for nuclear emergency... I was using about 50mg a day. The nodules reacted almost immediately to the iodide... the lump was obviously smaller right away, felt softer too, and became about half the original size within a few days. I ordered iodoral and lugol's online and when my order arrived I had just finished the bottle of potassium iodine so I continued to take iodoral 50mg daily.
>
>
>
> July 12: saw a doctor who again suggested biopsy. The only reason for the biopsy was the size of the nodules. He was also against iodine supplementation. But by then the lump has shrunk so much I knew I'm better off sticking to iodine. I also read online the potential problems with biopsy so I decided not to have the biopsy. Instead I'll take iodine for a few months and repeat the ultrasound to see how much the nodules have shrunk.
>
>
>
> Since then I have been taking 50mg iodoral daily with all the cofactors. There was a day or two I took more than 50mg, and there were days when I added lugol's to my water... I don't think I ever did more than 75mg in any day. Very mild detox, if any, mostly in the forms of stinky sweat, slight headache occasionally, and loose bowels occasionally. The benefits include increased energy, feeling more motivated, and oh I got so much done lately nothing seems difficult any more.
>
>
>
> The lump was not noticeable since a few weeks ago, and now is hardly palpable. I still see a small lump moving when I swallow but it's so much smaller than before... maybe less than 1/3 the size now.
>
>
>
> So this is my one month iodine supplementation summary. Will update again in another month.
>
>
>
> I'm taking 75mg today (37.5 at breakfast, 37.5 later).
>
> [http://curezone.com/forums/fm.asp?i=1970777#i](http://http://curezone.com/forums/fm.asp?i=1970777#i)

Doctors reports, iodine research, testimonials, procedures and more here:

[http://tinyurl.com/iodine-references](http://http://tinyurl.com/iodine-references)

Grizz

Do you think any vit C form is ok? I have just the regular ascorbic acid on hand. Should I get some better C or is that fine?

NWgirl,

Sorry for the delay in answering your message. We just got back from a 9 day Bus Tour of the Western States. Great fun!

Any Vitamin C will probably work. The Iodine Doctors recommend Glycinate because it is most easily absorbed and causes the least amount of indigestion problems. If your Ascorbic Acid does not upset your stomach, it is probably OK.

I have just updated the list of required supplements to show the range of dosage, and the reasons for taking a higher dosage.

\- - - NOTE ON DOSAGES: The range of doses are intended for the amount of Detox problems people have. Smaller dosage is for those with minimal detox symptoms. Large dosage is for people with severe detox symptoms or for those who cannot absorb iodine. Always work with your doctor when taking supplements.

\- - Â½ tsp Natural Celtic Salt, Himalayan Pink Salt, Redmonds Salt or Hawaiin black salt

\- - - If the sea salt is pure white, it is unacceptable (minerals have been stripped out )

\- - 200 to 400mcg selenium (L-selenomethionine preferred)

\- - 400 mg Magnesium - Glycinate preferred

\- - 2,000 to 5000mg Vitamin C - Ester-C preferred - do not take with iodine

\- - ATP Cofactors caplet contains both 100mg B2 & 500mg B3 non-flushing in the correct ratio. Take 1/2 caplet with iodine up to 25mg, then one caplet for each 50 mg iodine,

May I ask for more details on your problems and your recovery since starting iodine supplements?

Our neighbor recently told us how thrilled she was taking iodine with required supplements. Her doctor had told her to learn to live with her painful swollen breasts or else cut them off. She went home crying. Amazingly, her pain was gone within a few days of starting iodine, and her lumps & bumps were GONE in only 1 month. She is now eager to help me present a seminar on iodine at our local church. We plan to fill all 500 seats by word of mouth and email notifications. I'm also planning to write magazine articles on this subject to get the word out. 100 million women are severely suffering from iodine deficiency and the subject of iodine is buried, whitewashed, and downplayed.

Latest Doctors Reports, iodine research, testimonials, procedures and more here:

[http://tinyurl.com/iodine-references](http://http://tinyurl.com/iodine-references)

Very best to your good health,

Salt Flush to clear the kidneys

SALT THE BROMIDE AWAY !

NWgirl,

What are your symptoms? Bloat, pimples & headaches are almost always caused by taking too much iodine too soon, which causes more toxins to come out of our cells than our liver & kidneys can handle. Here is the recommended schedule:

\- - - Mix 1 drop 2% Lugols into many bottles of water, put the caps back on.

\- - - Week 1 drink 1 oz (~30ml) daily (about 380mcg iodone)

\- - - Week 2 drink 2 oz (~60ml) daily (about 775mcg iodine)

\- - - Week 3 drink 4 oz (~120ml) daily ( about 1.5mg iodine)

\- - - Week 4 drink 6 oz (~180ml) daily ( about 2.3mg iodine)

\- - - Week 5 drink 8 oz (~220ml) daily ( about 3.1 mg iodine or about 1 drop per day),

\- - - Week 6 drink 2 drops per day in a glass of water

\- - - Week 7 drink 3 drops per day in a glass of water

\- - - Week 8 drink 4 drops per day (about 12.5mg = to 1 iodoral)

\* Next, Titrate up with iodoral 12.5mg iodine tablets available at Amazon.Com

\- - - add 1/2 tablet each month to eventually reach 25mg daily-refer to Dr. Brownstein’s Book

\- - - 25-50mg Daily is the dosage target recommended by Dr. Brownstein to overcome toxins

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-20T01:36:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iodine-ll.2856/post-45766>

[http://jackkruse.com/what-are-the-top-ten-paleo-supplements/](http://http://jackkruse.com/what-are-the-top-ten-paleo-supplements/)

### chocolate — 2012-08-20T06:15:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iodine-ll.2856/post-45816>

[http://ithyroid.com/copper.htm](http://http://ithyroid.com/copper.htm)

> In a study on rats there is evidence that copper is necessary for proper iodine metabolism and consequently of proper thyroid hormone synthesis.
>
>
>
> Title \[The effect of copper on the metabolism of iodine, carbohydrates and proteins in rats\]
>
>
>
> Author
>
> Esipenko BE; Marsakova NV
>
> Source
>
> Fiziol Zh, 36(2):35-43 1990 Mar-Apr
>
> Abstract
>
>
>
> Experiments on 156 rats maintained at ration with copper deficiency have demonstrated a decrease in the values of iodine metabolism in organs and tissues excluding the liver where a sharp increase in the concentration and content of inorganic iodine was observed. A disturbance in indices of carbohydrate and proteins metabolism in the organism of animals is marked. A direct relationship with a correlation coefficient equaling 0.87-1.00 is determined between changes in the concentration of protein-bound iodine in blood and concentration of glycogen in the liver, skeletal muscles, albumins, alpha 1-, alpha 2-globulins, urea concentration; an inverse relationship with glucose, activity of blood lipo-dehydrogenase and liver mitochondria, aldolase, concentration of pyruvic and lactic acids is established as well. It is concluded that copper deficiency can exert both a direct effect on metabolic processes (as data from literature testify) and an indirect one disturbing iodine metabolism, i. e. sharply decreasing protein-bound iodine production by the thyroid gland.

Jack,

My wife never had fibrocystic breast disease, but I can say that her breasts have improved dramatically since she started on iodine. # Thread: Blood Glucose Monitor for fun and profit?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blood-glucose-monitor-for-fun-and-profit.2857/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-08-04T20:29:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blood-glucose-monitor-for-fun-and-profit.2857/post-42882>

I don't think I've got blood glucose probs, my fasting numbers are always fine. Have you bought one "just to test" and see whats going on and to see how things are effecting you? What's your experience? Reccomendations on what/how to buy one to just test for observation? Is blood glucose that useful?

### Melinda — 2012-08-04T20:56:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blood-glucose-monitor-for-fun-and-profit.2857/post-42885>

I bought the Righton Micro from Walmart. It was around ten bucks and a batch of matching test strips was ~50 for $30. I think that is the best deal around. I found that my fasting was in the 80's and that eating certain carbs even in a 20gram serving could raise my 1 hour postprandial BS to the 140 area, no, no. If I wrapped those carbs in lots of fat I would not get a rise---that I could catch. Some ppl say it goes up eventually, can't fool the body. I'm epi paleo now so I'm very low carb and not fooling with finding 50 carbs a day anymore. It is a useful home test metric but I use my returning hunger organ more. If I get hungry before 4-6 hours have gone by then I know that I ate or thought in a way that caused too much insulin to be released and therefore I quit fat burning.

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-04T21:55:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blood-glucose-monitor-for-fun-and-profit.2857/post-42891>

> **Melinda;43166 wrote:** I bought the Righton Micro from Walmart. It was around ten bucks and a batch of matching test strips was ~50 for $30. I think that is the best deal around. I found that my fasting was in the 80's and that eating certain carbs even in a 20gram serving could raise my 1 hour postprandial BS to the 140 area, no, no. If I wrapped those carbs in lots of fat I would not get a rise---that I could catch. Some ppl say it goes up eventually, can't fool the body. I'm epi paleo now so I'm very low carb and not fooling with finding 50 carbs a day anymore. It is a useful home test metric but I use my returning hunger organ more. If I get hungry before 4-6 hours have gone by then I know that I ate or thought in a way that caused too much insulin to be released and therefore I quit fat burning.

Thanks Melinda! That's some good input. Seems like a good mental exercise to make a logical connection to hunger and bs. . .like if everytime I eat X, My BG goes up hr later. . .and tie that to hunger. . . and figure out what DOESNT do that.

Also, I wonder if it will help me figure out how fast I am processing things with the bypass I had. Like how fast after eating does my BG change. Any thoughts on this anyone?

### Destiny — 2012-08-04T22:09:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blood-glucose-monitor-for-fun-and-profit.2857/post-42894>

> **Cavemam;43163 wrote:** I don't think I've got blood glucose probs, my fasting numbers are always fine. Have you bought one "just to test" and see whats going on and to see how things are effecting you? What's your experience? Reccomendations on what/how to buy one to just test for observation? Is blood glucose that useful?

I got a Freestyle Lite monitor for $20.00. I buy 100 strips for $57.00 on Amazon. I am happy with that since you only need a very little blood for testing.

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-04T22:14:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blood-glucose-monitor-for-fun-and-profit.2857/post-42895>

> **Destiny;43175 wrote:** I got a Freestyle Lite monitor for $20.00. I buy 100 strips for $57.00 on Amazon. I am happy with that since you only need a very little blood for testing.

Did you start testing because of Diabetes or BG problems that came on a test? Or why did you start and what use is it to you?

### Destiny — 2012-08-04T22:44:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blood-glucose-monitor-for-fun-and-profit.2857/post-42902>

> **Cavemam;43176 wrote:** Did you start testing because of Diabetes or BG problems that came on a test? Or why did you start and what use is it to you?
>
>
>

I started back in November. It was recommended on many health oriented/low carb diet sites. It was also mentioned on Dr. Davies' Wheat Belly site and dr. K's site. It was a bad surprise that my FBG and 1-2 hr sugar levels were in high 90's and low 100's. Then, I started supplementing estrogen in February and that brought it down to 80's but it was only temporary. I guess my body was shocked with the hormones and then got used it.

Maybe, you want to know how you react to some foods that are a bit higher in carbs. It is not the same for everybody. I am OK with berries but cherries spike my sugar like you would not believe it. I also remember Dr. K saying that some people react to a protein shake, I don't. So I think it is useful to know what might be your "spiky food".

### jib — 2012-08-04T22:57:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blood-glucose-monitor-for-fun-and-profit.2857/post-42907>

Dr William Davis track your plaque site is where you'll find the blood glucose info I think. He posted alot on this right before he started the Wheat Belly site. He was testing ancient wheat with modern wheat, (still spikes), // It could be there too.

Jenny Ruhl's diabetes 101 has a lot of good info too about meter reading and such.

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-05T03:48:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blood-glucose-monitor-for-fun-and-profit.2857/post-42995>

I've got a one touch ultra left over from our diabetic lab you want to make sure you don't have more than a 20 pt spike w/any give food.

### ealachan — 2012-08-05T12:35:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blood-glucose-monitor-for-fun-and-profit.2857/post-43021>

> **Cavemam;43163 wrote:** I don't think I've got blood glucose probs, my fasting numbers are always fine. Have you bought one "just to test" and see whats going on and to see how things are effecting you? What's your experience? Reccomendations on what/how to buy one to just test for observation? Is blood glucose that useful?

That's exactly why I bought mine...then it turned out we needed it anyway because of DH being diabetic. I have a ReliOn Ultima - walmart brand cheapy, and it's super fast and easy to use. I found it really interesting to see how different foods affected my blood glucose, but I'm a nerd, so YMMV.

Also, haven't read the rest of the thread yet, but testing your BG for a week or so might show you trends you didn't realize were happening - spikes and such. It may answer your dreaded HWC question, even!

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-06T03:44:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blood-glucose-monitor-for-fun-and-profit.2857/post-43150>

ok, just felt like sharing. Fasted during day, and tested after eating meal of steak and an apple. 1 hr after dinner bg was 95. Sounds good to moi.

I'm going to take again when waking, and maybe every 15 mins after eating to see how long till BG goes up and what it goes down to? Hmm, perhaps I should test again 2 hrs after dinner? 4 hrs?

This might be a way to figure out how fast food goes through me?

### Melinda — 2012-08-06T04:50:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blood-glucose-monitor-for-fun-and-profit.2857/post-43158>

That is a super number. I recommend you take another reading at 2 hour and if it's going up then do another at 3hrs. But if the second hour is close or down, you are done testing for this meal.

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-06T05:22:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blood-glucose-monitor-for-fun-and-profit.2857/post-43159>

> **Melinda;43443 wrote:** That is a super number. I recommend you take another reading at 2 hour and if it's going up then do another at 3hrs. But if the second hour is close or down, you are done testing for this meal.

96 at two hrs

### Melinda — 2012-08-06T06:51:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blood-glucose-monitor-for-fun-and-profit.2857/post-43165>

You nailed for that meal. By the way, do you know to use the fatty pad closer to your fingernail instead of the tip of your finger for the prick? Jenny at Diabetes 101 teaches the best technique. I hardly feel it.

### Destiny — 2012-08-06T13:00:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blood-glucose-monitor-for-fun-and-profit.2857/post-43172>

> **Cavemam;43435 wrote:** ok, just felt like sharing. Fasted during day, and tested after eating meal of steak and an apple. 1 hr after dinner bg was 95. Sounds good to moi.
>
>
>
> I'm going to take again when waking, and maybe every 15 mins after eating to see how long till BG goes up and what it goes down to? Hmm, perhaps I should test again 2 hrs after dinner? 4 hrs?
>
>
>
> This might be a way to figure out how fast food goes through me?

I remember dr. K saying to test every 15 minutes after eating if you want to know how certain foods affect you. I confess I have never done it. I always tested at 1 hr and 2 hrs.

**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 4

### ALeigh — 2012-08-05T04:29:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/did-i-just-mess-up-my-reset.2861/post-43004>

i got woke up today to a phone call from my bff, upset and crying and such...well naturally i forgot all about BAB within 30 minutes..... i didn't get to it for an hour and a half after i got up :/ Is this going to disrupt my reset and am I going to need to reset my LR clock now? I was 2 weeks in..sigh....

### ealachan — 2012-08-05T12:25:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/did-i-just-mess-up-my-reset.2861/post-43020>

Depends on what you've got going on inside, but you probably didn't set yourself back that much, no. Just my non-professional opinion.

### Destiny — 2012-08-05T14:52:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/did-i-just-mess-up-my-reset.2861/post-43031>

> **ALeigh;43286 wrote:** i got woke up today to a phone call from my bff, upset and crying and such...well naturally i forgot all about BAB within 30 minutes..... i didn't get to it for an hour and a half after i got up :/ Is this going to disrupt my reset and am I going to need to reset my LR clock now? I was 2 weeks in..sigh....

Sometimes life just happens .... I would not stress about this since you cannot change it now. Just continue with your reset. You have to be on it until you get the LS signs.

### ALeigh — 2012-08-07T22:14:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/did-i-just-mess-up-my-reset.2861/post-43494>

Thanks  Not really stressing... just kinda thinking..... crap.... I don't want to start over since I am doing this in conjunction with hcg and am doing the LR in my 6 weeks between hcg rounds. And I didn't miss by much, so hopefully all is well! The first week my weight was rather erratic but this week I've evened out nicely and actually am losing a little...... I know I'm not supposed to but I feel like I'm stuffing myself at every meal so I dont want to eat more. I guess my body has its own plan lol.

# Thread: Awsome results achieved  in 5- 10 months!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/awsome-results-achieved-in-5-10-months.2862/>
**Subforum:** Success Stories
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Destiny — 2012-08-05T15:11:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/awsome-results-achieved-in-5-10-months.2862/post-43033>

I am posting this for my husband who was a participant in this new lifestyle with me. He was not active participant on the website but got a lot of its info through me.

Started LRx - the end of November 2011

Started CT - twice a day since the mid of February 2012

Starting weight - 229 lbs, 6'2"

Health issues - Crohn's disease, high BP, arthritis pain, elevated blood sugar, frequent sinusitis, allergies - was taking Claritin and Afrin all year round, acne

He also had a brain fog, tired - in his words - I felt like I was slowly dying ......

**Results:**

Lost 43 lbs - presently at 186 lbs

Crohn's - no problems - might be gone?

Normal BP

Normal blood sugar

Allergies gone - taking nothing

Arthritis - gone

Clear skin

Feeling sharp and healthy

Now, he is going to have all the recommended labs done to see if any additional tweaking is needed.

>
>
> Jack Kruse;44415 wrote:** you can gain with Stevia if the gut bacteria is way off.......it is possible. When I hear this I think there is a K2 issue in that gut.

I don't gain with it, but it makes me feel unbelievably miserable - maybe it's just a herx. I'm not sure, but I get extremely weak, fatigued and just flat out grumpy!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-10T21:07:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-you-want-to-now-why-progesterone-and-cortisol-are-huge-for-a-leaky-gut.2864/post-44175>

### Eve — 2012-08-10T22:09:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-you-want-to-now-why-progesterone-and-cortisol-are-huge-for-a-leaky-gut.2864/post-44193>

> **BJK77;44456 wrote:** I don't gain with it, but it makes me feel unbelievably miserable - maybe it's just a herx. I'm not sure, but I get extremely weak, fatigued and just flat out grumpy!

Just a thought...but it might just be a leaky gut reaction. I can't do any packaged foods, even organic ones. I reacted to one organic lemon juice in a glass bottle but was fine with an organic one from a different brand. I figure it must be something they prewash the glass with.

Re: fish

I started eating fish in May and was getting more and more bloated...I realized it's likely cause there is sodium phosphate in the fish. I tried organic salmon and the reaction was much much less, but still a problem...so I'm thinking the fish may have been packaged in ice with citric acid in it (citric acid is from corn and I am super sensitive).

### freshveggies — 2012-08-11T16:25:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-you-want-to-now-why-progesterone-and-cortisol-are-huge-for-a-leaky-gut.2864/post-44320>

> **Anna K.;44622 wrote:** how is Kevita different from Kombucha? Is one better for you then the other?

Kombucha has 2 billion probiotics and Kevita has 4 billion. I actually prefer the Kombucha if you were to buy them. I might start making my own Kevita drink as I really don't like the flavoring they add to them and stevia. I dont need anything sweet. The Kombucha is so good, I could drink it all day. Mine never tasted that good when I made it.

### Pamela — 2012-08-11T17:19:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-you-want-to-now-why-progesterone-and-cortisol-are-huge-for-a-leaky-gut.2864/post-44330>

I've been making Kombucha for a few months now and mine is way better than the store bought in my opinion. And the best part is it doesn't cost $3.50 a bottle!

### freshveggies — 2012-08-11T17:32:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-you-want-to-now-why-progesterone-and-cortisol-are-huge-for-a-leaky-gut.2864/post-44331>

> **Pamela;44638 wrote:** I've been making Kombucha for a few months now and mine is way better than the store bought in my opinion. And the best part is it doesn't cost $3.50 a bottle!

Hi Pamela, can you give me some tips? What do you start out with for tea? I really don't want much caffiene so is their low caffiene options. Do you add any flavorings or when it is done fermenting do you let it sit in a bottle longer to get more fizzy?

### kathylu — 2012-08-11T18:03:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-you-want-to-now-why-progesterone-and-cortisol-are-huge-for-a-leaky-gut.2864/post-44337>

I think kombucha is a lot more difficult to make than kevita is. Personnally, I like the flavor of kevita better too. There are tons of sites with DIY instructions for kombucha. Of course, you need to get a scoby (symbiotic colony of bacteria and yeast) from someone that's already brewing kombucha. You may be able to grow your own scoby from a bottle of raw kombucha, but it would take about a month for the first batch.

### BJK77 — 2012-08-13T20:39:59+0300

> **Souldanzer;44795 wrote:** Can't stand Kevita anymore. All I can taste is Stevia. Love my homemade kombucha. Ferment the heck out of it though. I like my homemade version of Kevita, though, but for some reason it resists becoming fizzy lately.

Hey Soul,

When you say "ferment the heck out of it," how long do you let your first ferment go? I started homemade kombucha about a month ago by growing my own scoby from a bottle of plain kombucha. After about 14 days I ended up with a nice, super thick scoby, but it seems to take forever to ferment a batch of tea. My current batch has been fermenting for 12 days and still tastes pretty sweet to me. Granted, I keep my house at a fairly cool 68 degrees, but I didn't expect it to take that long.

The kids and I devour this stuff like crazy and I can't afford store bought anymore, so I think I'm going to have to have 2-3 gallons going at a time!

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-18T00:50:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-you-want-to-now-why-progesterone-and-cortisol-are-huge-for-a-leaky-gut.2864/post-45449>

> **BJK77;45044 wrote:** Hey Soul,
>
>
>
> When you say "ferment the heck out of it," how long do you let your first ferment go? I started homemade kombucha about a month ago by growing my own scoby from a bottle of plain kombucha. After about 14 days I ended up with a nice, super thick scoby, but it seems to take forever to ferment a batch of tea. My current batch has been fermenting for 12 days and still tastes pretty sweet to me. Granted, I keep my house at a fairly cool 68 degrees, but I didn't expect it to take that long.
>
>
>
> The kids and I devour this stuff like crazy and I can't afford store bought anymore, so I think I'm going to have to have 2-3 gallons going at a time!

Sorry BJ I never saw this. I ferment my kombucha until it hits ph 2.8 - 3.5 with test sticks. Anything below 2.5 is vinegar if I recall correctly... but then I love vinegar  I try to get close to 3.5 or under but not over, and then I do a second ferment in the bottled state to allow it to become fizzy. The first fermentation can take forever based on the room temp, the lower the temp the longer it will take. 68 is pretty low for kombucha. Probably 3 weeks and counting at that temp.

I have 5 gallons going at a time..... my kitchen is a lab  Last scobies were taking up half the gallon jar, they were HUGE. Then the fruit flies got them.... oh well. I got more in the fridge.

I also go by taste. There is a degree of tart that is too tart.... undrinkable, vinegar. And I don't like the sweet stuff either. I think your problem might be the room temp. Try some plant starter mats or reptile warming mats (wrap the container or put it under) or find a warmer spot in your house (higher up will be warmer, my fermentation vessels are on top of my pantries in the kitchen right under the ceiling).

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-18T00:56:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-you-want-to-now-why-progesterone-and-cortisol-are-huge-for-a-leaky-gut.2864/post-45452>

**Anna K.;44853 wrote:** Ok, Kombucha does has some caffeine in it if you use normal caffeinated tea to make it, but the amount is so insignificant that MOST people don't notice that.

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

According to one site the amount of caffeine in kombucha is similar to decaf coffee:

[http://www.katalystkombucha.com/faq.html](http://http://www.katalystkombucha.com/faq.html)

Q: Is there any caffeine in Kombucha?

A: We have calculated that our Kombucha has approximately 5 mg of caffeine per serving. This as opposed to other caffeinated beverages is the lowest we have encountered , and those with sensitivity to caffeine have reported no adverse effects from our Kombucha.

Caffeine Content in 8-ounce Beverage: (in milligrams)

Coffee, Drip--115-175

Tea, instant--30

Tea, green--15

Hot cocoa--14

Coffee, Decaf, brewed--3-4

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

another place reports this:

"caffeine present in Kombucha is paired with a natural amino acid that slows itâ€™s absorption into the bloodstream, l-theanine, with great side benefits."

[http://www.kombuchakamp.com/2011/03/kombucha-and-caffeine-explained.html](http://http://www.kombuchakamp.com/2011/03/kombucha-and-caffeine-explained.html)

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

Looks like the culture eats some of the caffeine from the tea and the longer you culture it, the less caffeine is left and what is left in it is diluted in a much larger quantity of liquid then a cup of tea.

"the amount of caffeine in the finished product is about 1/2 of what it was when you started with the plain tea. We use a range of teas, Black being the highest, green next, white, and rooibos which has no caffeine and has to be combined with a black or green. The SCOBY "eats" the caffeine and converts it. Without caffeine it cannot live. So, if you took an 8oz cup of black tea which has 40mg of caffeine (less than a 1/3 cup of coffee) and used it to make kombucha you would yeild 20mg of caffeine. That's a couple sips of coffee."

[http://nessalla.com/node/11](http://http://nessalla.com/node/11)

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

Also, many people dont' recommend using caffeine-free tea for it because bacteria needs it. This is the best link about the use of different teas for it:

[http://www.happyherbalist.com/differentteas.aspx](http://http://www.happyherbalist.com/differentteas.aspx)

How to Decaffeinate Kombucha Tea

Kombucha Tea= about 1/3 of the typical caffeine of a cup of tea

Many kombucha authors and brewers debate whether Kombucha needs caffeine or not. In our research and in at least one journal the bacteria are energized by caffeine so some caffeine would seem wise. The typical amount of tea per batch of Kombucha Tea is 1/2 oz per 3 quarts (5-7 t-bags/ 15 grams). Strong tea (steeping a long time or using more tea ) provides more sterols and tannins and generally benefit the yeasts.

If you are sensitive to caffeine and prefer not to use herbal (non-caffeine\_ teas than we suggest to simply use less tea. (use 1/3 less) or Pre-Steep for 30 seconds in boiling water (removes 80% of the caffeine). Discard that water. Steep tea for less than 5 minutes.

Kombucha can survive on the above method. But this will make a very weak tasting Kombucha Tea. You may notice that you can also now ferment for a few days longer decreasing the sugar content as well as increasing the perceived health benefits form a longer brew cycle. A longer ferment will also reduce the caffeine as the caffeine gets cannibalized as the other food sources are depleted. Unfortunately longer ferments also produce a very sour kombucha tea. If that happens try mixing with sparkling mineral water and enjoy a pleasant Kombucha Tea without as much caffeine or sugar.

This is \*a\* site that says this there is other studies that show that the caffeine is almost unaffected by the fermentation. Most websites that sell you kombucha or supplies will try to keep this issue hidden. There is definitely caffeine in kombucha..... I live in the Republic of Kombucha drinkers and we all agree.... there is caffeine in it. I'm one of the best test individuals for caffeine... there is caffeine in it.

There is a reason why people are giving up their morning joe for kombucha... b/c it has caffeine. Why they want it as a pick me up.... b/c it has caffeine.

Anyways. Do with it what you want or run your own experiment. I still believe my own body first.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-18T01:03:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-you-want-to-now-why-progesterone-and-cortisol-are-huge-for-a-leaky-gut.2864/post-45454>

> **freshveggies;44832 wrote:** I don't like the caffeine either---what kind of tea do you use for your Kombucha?

I used to use black and green mix. That's the most traditional version. But b/c I'm so sensitive to the caffeine I've done a whole lot of experiments from decaf to roiboos to herbal. Thing is, you want to expose the mushroom to caffeine every few ferments to keep it nice and healthy. If you can't have the caffeine at all, make some friends happy  Or barter, that's what I do  Decaf and roiboos work both, herbal you have to be careful b/c some will damage the culture but it takes longer in general for the fermentation to happen. You could increase room temp to speed it up. In the summer it works no problem in the winter it can be a bit of a pain.

### Pamela — 2012-08-25T04:57:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-you-want-to-now-why-progesterone-and-cortisol-are-huge-for-a-leaky-gut.2864/post-46952>

I've been brewing Kombucha for a few months now and have gotten my procedure down pretty good. Right now I am only brewing one gallon at a time. The first scoby I bought was from someone on ebay and it was a very small scoby that didn't really produce how I wanted it to. Then I went to [kombuchakamp.com/](http://http://www.kombuchakamp.com/) and bought a very nice full size thick scoby, it was worth the price. I have also purchased my tea from them and the kombucha turns out very tasty every time. I've played around with the amount of sugar, 1C or 1.25C doesn't seem to matter much which I use and I brew for one full week in the summer, then do a 2nd ferment for 2 days. In the 2nd ferment I bottle into 3 bottles (old CO glass bottles) and add cut up ginger to them for the 2 days. At the end of the 2 days I then strain and pour the tea into old KT Kombucha glass bottles and then refrigerate. It is really great stuff and the cost is so much less.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-25T22:57:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-you-want-to-now-why-progesterone-and-cortisol-are-huge-for-a-leaky-gut.2864/post-47117>

> **diane;46343 wrote:** How do you ferment coconut water? I can't believe how much I'm learning here!

You can buy the frozen young coconut water (used to be called body ecology but now is branded diff but same company) at some specialty health food stores. You can also buy the coconuts and tap into them for the water. I've done both in the past. Now I am just using filtered water, some starter, and a little bit of fresh fruit in the jar and two days later I have fizzy, non-sweet, water. Really good. Plus I still make the Kevita water, which I guess this is like that too but it is not made with probiotics.

Then I went to [kombuchakamp.com/](http://http://www.kombuchakamp.com/) and bought a very nice full size thick scoby, it was worth the price. I have also purchased my tea from them and the kombucha turns out very tasty every time. I've played around with the amount of sugar, 1C or 1.25C doesn't seem to matter much which I use and I brew for one full week in the summer, then do a 2nd ferment for 2 days. In the 2nd ferment I bottle into 3 bottles (old CO glass bottles) and add cut up ginger to them for the 2 days. At the end of the 2 days I then strain and pour the tea into old KT Kombucha glass bottles and then refrigerate. It is really great stuff and the cost is so much less.

Thanks for this post. This is exactly the last few details I needed. Now I also know a reputable place to buy a scoby and I need one.

### melrito — 2012-08-26T17:07:41+0300

I got mine a couple years ago from kombuchakamp.com and it's still going strong...

# Thread: When to jump in when using ice bottles?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-to-jump-in-when-using-ice-bottles.2865/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Raiken3712 — 2012-08-05T21:51:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-to-jump-in-when-using-ice-bottles.2865/post-43105>

I recently stockpiled quite a few Fiji 1 liters and am now seeing if it will work for cold thermogenesis. It seems like it may work. At the moment the temp has gone down from 81 to 70 degrees and potentially still falling. At what point should I get in?

If I wait too long the water will start to rise in temperature. Will it continue to get colder as long as what I'm melting is colder than the water? Should I wait until the temperatures are completely equal? Right now the temp of the water bottles/ice packs and such are 42 for some freeze packs I got with eggs that were shipped....and 60 for the water bottles. They are both still below the water temperature of 70.

Will they continue to drop the temperature effectively till they reach equal temps? I'm guessing as the temperatures get closer there isn't as much room to go down.

Any recommendations?

### vkiernan — 2012-08-05T22:43:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-to-jump-in-when-using-ice-bottles.2865/post-43111>

Why wait at all? It is colder in the water rather than not being in the water. I would get in and stay as long as possible unless you only have so much time; then you will have to play around a bit to find your own sweet spot for water temp and length it will stay for you.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-08-05T22:58:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-to-jump-in-when-using-ice-bottles.2865/post-43114>

My tub isn't big enough the amount of water displaced by all the bottles and such takes up too much room for me to get in comfortably and not drop the water outside the tub lol.

If it were a bigger tub maybe but not with mine...most of my new house is an upgrade from the one I lived in at Midland, TX but ...the bath is a downgrade sadly. One of these days I'd love to use a bath that fits my 6'1" frame completely. Where I can lay down completely and relax completely submerged if I want to....probably won't be for awhile. I do have a pool though so its a good place to relax in...just not in the context of bathing.

### vkiernan — 2012-08-05T23:02:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-to-jump-in-when-using-ice-bottles.2865/post-43115>

Did I misunderstand? You aren't putting the bottles in the tub to cool your water? What are you doing?

### Raiken3712 — 2012-08-06T17:15:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-to-jump-in-when-using-ice-bottles.2865/post-43210>

I am putting the bottles in the water to cool it. After awhile they melt and the water is cold and than I take the water bottles out. The water is still cold after the water bottles are taken out. I stayed in for 20-30 minutes it it worked fine that way. I didn't have room in my bathtub to get in along with the bottles/ice packs it would have made the tub overflow.

### vkiernan — 2012-08-06T21:04:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-to-jump-in-when-using-ice-bottles.2865/post-43264>

> **Raiken3712;43497 wrote:** I am putting the bottles in the water to cool it. After awhile they melt and the water is cold and than I take the water bottles out. The water is still cold after the water bottles are taken out. I stayed in for 20-30 minutes it it worked fine that way. I didn't have room in my bathtub to get in along with the bottles/ice packs it would have made the tub overflow.

Ah, I see. Got it. Hmmm, you will certainly have to play around with that one. I would get in when the water was the temp I wanted just as if I filled my tub from the faucet. That will be one of those things you play around with. Keep checking the temp until it starts to go the other direction then you know your time.

### bigknitwit — 2012-08-07T01:00:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-to-jump-in-when-using-ice-bottles.2865/post-43312>

When I use a blanket of cryopacs to cool it, it cant ake upwards of 2 hours to fully drop the temp. I often don't wait that long... Just depends on my planning and timing.

### Greg rx — 2012-08-10T00:55:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-to-jump-in-when-using-ice-bottles.2865/post-43979>

why not use less water and jump in with the Bottles? less water will cool faster and you can place the Bottles on different parts of your body such as lower back, between your legs, under your chin,upper back. start with zero water and add till overflow or higher temps

### MartiD — 2012-08-10T01:20:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-to-jump-in-when-using-ice-bottles.2865/post-43984>

I fill up my tub about half way, add in frozen water bottles and some ice cubes and then fix my BAB. By the time I am done cooking and cleaning, the bottles have cooled the water nicely. I can only get the water down to about 65, stay in around 30 minutes. It isn't as cold as I would like but certainly better than nothing. I am hoping for an early fall as our tap water has gotten really warm with the blistering summer in Missouri.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-08-10T03:06:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-to-jump-in-when-using-ice-bottles.2865/post-44029>

Mine is as well 80-86 tap.....still I somehow got it down to 55 with ice bottles...that's quite a few 1 liter bottles..+ a few ice packs and such.

### PrimalPam — 2012-08-11T18:46:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-to-jump-in-when-using-ice-bottles.2865/post-44347>

I don't use water bottles, or any type of container with a small neck so the ice can't come out immediately.

I use old plastic containers (like to-go food comes in), and I bought Glad containers at the grocery store. I carry them all into the tub. As soon as the warm tap water hits them, the ice pops out, and the containers go into a large plastic bag so I can refill them. This way, the ice starts melting right away, rather than having to wait.

I get in the tub the minute I start filling it, with the ice blocks all around me. The water is only about 1" deep at first obviously, but the ice blocks start cooling me off immediately. This way, by the time the tub is filled (about 5 mins), I can turn it off, lay back, and the ice blocks have already cooled the water.

### freesia — 2012-08-12T02:29:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-to-jump-in-when-using-ice-bottles.2865/post-44427>

I've been doing this 4 months now and I have a system I'm happy with. I use 16 x 2 lb ice blocks to get my water to 45F. (It's winter here.) I spend 45 - 60 minutes in the water and it never gets above 48F.

I make the blocks in open topped containers and the blocks pop out after 3 - 10 minutes.

My full bath starts at 55F (tapwater temp). 20 minutes after the ice blocks are in it's at 51F, and 40 minutes after the water is at 45F with all iceblocks about 1/3 - 1/2 of their original size - enough ice to counteract my body heat pretty well for the next hour.

My skin is 52F - 54F after my bath.

### PrimalPam — 2012-08-12T16:49:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-to-jump-in-when-using-ice-bottles.2865/post-44525>

My tap comes out now in the low 80's (ugh). So it takes a lot of ice for me to get it to 50-52F, which I've learned is my "sweet spot". My rule for how much ice I need is that it needs to get to about 57F within the first 2-3 mins, then get to 50-52F, and stay there until I'm done (45 mins). I always want there to be a little bit of ice left, not melted, when I'm done, because that way, my temp stays in the low 50's the entire time.

I have a digital thermometer that sits on the side of my tub, and I check the temp every 10 mins or so. Then I just Pandora it, and enjoy.

**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-blood-sugar-levels.2866/>

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-blood-sugar-levels.2866/post-43113>

I was in 63 degree bath water for around 20-30 mins I believe I didn't time...is it supposed to be good at lowering blood sugar?

I went from 90 pre-meal to 1 hour level of 115 after a smoothy and 2 hour mark says 85....wonky blood sugar meter? It seems decently accurate...but maybe CT does this? I can't remember if I remember reading about lowering blood sugar acutely. I'm aware its good long term for blood sugar but can't remember if there was anything about short term like this.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-05T23:56:13+0300

it will lower it pretty fast.......ask Dali Dula.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-06T00:16:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-blood-sugar-levels.2866/post-43128>

my fasting BG went from 100-110 to 76 on my last labs..... not sure how long it took, a few months?

Might have been a fluke as I haven't been checking at home lately, I figured directionally accurate and putting my $$ somewhere else than expensive test strips.

CT does drop my BG post-prandial for me.... if it's cold enough I can get it down into the low 70.

# Thread: CT and post-op pain!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-post-op-pain.2868/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### RobH — 2012-08-05T23:31:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-post-op-pain.2868/post-43118>

After fighting an infected (and thankfully benign -- the infection occurred after a biopsy!) abdominal wall mass thought to be scar tissue from a prior surgery for nearly a month, I had surgery for it last Tuesday night.

The whole story is pretty bizarre, and I'm still recovering from a month of serious systemic inflammation (HS-CRP was 77 immediately pre-operatively 8^(.

But what I am here to say is wow! CT works for post-op pain -- went into the operation pretty well cold-adapted (have been doing full-body ice baths at least 3 times / week all summer, and sometimes 4-5 times / week. Even post-operatively I can't do immersion yet (drains in place) but have been doing a lot of spot icing of the surgical area.

Post-op pain has been nearly nonexistent -- NOTHING like I remember from the original operation (liposxn / abdominoplasty) that probably caused this scar tissue in the first place. I've taken nearly nothing for pain control since I left the hospital (1 pill -- my wife tricked me... 8^)) and feel great every time I ice the area. Drains are now out and I can't wait to be able to jump back into full-immersion (ok, I don't use a snorkel). It just doesn't hurt. And that is with about 24 inches of scar, a big T-shaped incision with a whole bunch of staples, and a whole lot of dissection and retracting necessary to get the mass out (according to the surgeons).

A long way to climb back towards optimal but I can literally feel the systemic difference now that the inflamed and infected tissue is out of me, and feeling so much better everyday.

I want to thank Jack for helping make the decision to have the operation (I knew it but he made sure I had no alternative... 8^)) and for all of the amazing work he has done and continues to do.... I'm trying to paleo-it-forward.

RobH

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-05T23:44:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-post-op-pain.2868/post-43121>

Rob you found out what I did when I did my bio hack.......it is amazing for surgical cases......

# Thread: Yawning during CT
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yawning-during-ct.2869/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### popcornsally — 2012-08-06T00:35:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yawning-during-ct.2869/post-43131>

I was just wondering if anyone else has experienced this: when in CT bath, as soon as I start to shiver a little, I start yawning a lot and my eyes start running (not crying). Any theories as to why this might happen? My eyes usually run a lot when it's cold outside but the yawning thing is new.

### freesia — 2012-08-06T23:54:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yawning-during-ct.2869/post-43293>

Search "yawning" and you'll find a couple of discussions touching on it. The suggestion is that thyroid hormones are out of balance if I remember correctly. My (immediately after) CT yawning was so intense it used to nearly knock me out!

Now I only occasionally yawn 20 - 30 minutes after CT and it's not out of control like it was. I didn't get the running eyes thing though.

### popcornsally — 2012-08-08T05:49:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yawning-during-ct.2869/post-43592>

awesome, thanks! Yes, my thyroid hormones ARE out of balance fo' sho'.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-07T03:32:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sunrise-photos.2875/post-43340>

ok I just signed up and I still can do it? How to you get it here from photobucket?

Attached files [](http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/43627=399-IMG_0829.JPG)

### ealachan — 2012-08-07T03:47:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sunrise-photos.2875/post-43342>

> **Jack Kruse;43627 wrote:** ok I just signed up and I still can do it? How to you get it here from photobucket?

Once you've uploaded the picture, if you look off to the right it should show you a little box that says "Links" and has 4 different types of code: email / IM, direct link, HTML and IMG. If you click on the code next to IMG, it should copy it for you automatically. Then you come back here and paste that code into the body of your message, and your picture should show up when you post.

You can also get the line of code directly from the album page once you've uploaded. Under each picture thumbnail, there will be the same little boxes of code. You want the IMG one.

Hope that helps!

# Thread: Help! Hemorrhoids
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-hemorrhoids.2877/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 28
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

### ealachan — 2012-08-06T16:48:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-hemorrhoids.2877/post-43201>

I dunno what to tell you about the hemorrhoids themselves, but re: the diahrrea, I found that taking a digestive enzyme before meals has helped MASSIVELY. I was basically running to the bathroom 20 minutes after every meal for a while there...turned out that because I don't have a gallbladder, my body was unable to break down all the fat I was eating and was just having a dump reaction every time. A couple days on the enzymes cleared it right up. I've been using the Rainbow Light brand in conjunction with an ox bile supplement and my toilet visits have since improved tremendously.

Do lots of probiotic foods, and the bone broths are great for helping the gut - once you start having it on hand then you realize all the things you can slip it in. Basically any cooking liquid can be a bone broth. Also, the non-probiotic veggies are great at feeding the good bacteria in your gut, so try and eat as much of the non-starchys and a variety of them. In case you didn't get the memo, more seafood and sea-veggies!

### chocolate — 2012-08-12T00:56:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-hemorrhoids.2877/post-44415>

[http://glutenfreeworks.com/blog/2010/07/28/understanding-copper-deficiency-in-celiac-disease/](http://http://glutenfreeworks.com/blog/2010/07/28/understanding-copper-deficiency-in-celiac-disease/)

I've seen on several websites that folks with bypass operations have trouble with copper deficiency.

> Copper deficiency is characterized by fatigue, anemia, neutropenia (low level of neutrophils, the most common type of white blood cell that protect against infection), leukopenia (abnormal decrease of leukocytes or white blood cells), bone and joint abnormalities, skin abnormalities, impairment of nerve and muscle function, impairment of adrenal and thyroid gland function, reproductive difficulties and loss of hair and skin color. In children, neutropenia and leukopenia are the best early indications of copper deficiency. **Changes that are due to defective connective tissue formation include bleeding into the covering of bones, bleeding from aneurysms and bleeding from hemorrhoids.** Death may result from brain degeneration, failure to make blood cells and hemorhage.

# Thread: Plaquenil
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/plaquenil.2878/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### TheKid — 2012-08-06T16:55:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/plaquenil.2878/post-43203>

Hey All,

I am wondering if people our there have any experience with Plaquenil to treat autoimmunity. I have Antiphospholipid Syndrome and Hashimoto's and because I tend to have periodic, severe autoimmune flares, my doctor wants me to be on Plaquenil to help prevent these episodes from happening.

The risk info on the insert is scary, what with mention of heart attacks and all. But the major concern seems to be macular degeneration, with the risk for this increasing the longer you take this stuff.

I was thinking that maybe i could use it as a bridge until I get to a point where my immune system is in better balance. I am just concerned that my autoimmune flares are doing damage to my body and I don't want to harm organs or other important parts of my body just because I don't like to take medicine.

Any advice would help. Thanks.

### chocolate — 2012-08-08T17:36:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/plaquenil.2878/post-43659>

Hey Kid, I don't know anything about the drug. I do know that lithium, a salt that my daughter was on flips a lot of switches the wrong way, and we seem to be fighting an uphill battle with the phospholipids. I'm kind of getting that you can't supplement with any success until you get them flipped back, which shouldn't take very long. Some genes switch in hours, some in two weeks, and some in one month. Then there is always the trying learn what is circadianly appropriate and the pitfalls or whatever. Why don't you try to see what Plaquenil does? and then google the description or pathways ? I have never taken any kind of prescription medication except for a rotten tooth with any success. I probably could've used vitamin c. I'll see what I can find. What happens with you auto immune flares, if its not too personal?

We are finding that with kidney trouble you are supposed to avoid phosphorus, which is the very thing we need the most... for phospholipids. It seems like maybe if you focused on your kidneys like doc mentioned in the webinar, you might gets some relief. The selenium, methionine, choline and inositol are supposed to be really good anti-oxidants. And the magnesium is like a great wonder drug. And honestly, the lithium really needs to be there, it has magical powers, its just trying to hit the right balance. Our new plan is to cocktail with betaine and lithium orotate and give the kidneys a break. I think they should be taken together. But, hey, that's just me.

If you google lithocholic acid, you might be impressed. It fixes everything.

Also, if you have a good day where you are soaking things in, the sesamin increases absorption a lot and helps with weight loss. Combined with quercetin, it protects the some part of the brain we need to not get excited and let the phosholipids do their thing in a petrie dish. Those two work together, not separately. I know the K2 is a huge player as well. I don't think avoiding phosporous is right.

Dude, I just looked. Its just an anti malarial. That's what my kid takes. The lithium and betaine and lots of magnesium might help you better. I've been searching for parasites for years. My grandmothers were both diagnosed with malaria and had goiters. Then I got to thinking about how hillbillies crack open boiled squirrel heads and mash their brains into a biscuit. My grandpa fed them to my kids. I thought she had mad cow or something. We need to partner up.

### TheKid — 2012-08-10T00:17:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/plaquenil.2878/post-43968>

Chocolate,

Thanks for the reply. I am wading in deep water on my autoimmune issues. I have both Antiphospolipid Syndrome and Hashimoto's. And with those two, who knows what else I have lurking.

ANyhow, you asked about my autoimmune flares. Well here's what happens:

Poor concentration & cognition (fuzzy thinking)

Poor memory (both short and long-term â€“ I feel like I am living only in this very moment)

Tendency to mentally â€œdrift offâ€￾

Stabbing abdominal pains (especially left side below rib cage)

Pain & irritation on my right side just under my ribs

Immense pressure and tension in chest

Sharp, painful pinch in the middle of my chest

Shortness of breath

AWFUL cramping all over body. Much worse at night.

GERD

Pain in my throat and under my chin

Mild, moderate and severe headaches that can come and go

General lack of energy

Episodic, extreme bouts of fatigue that leave me incapacitated

Extreme plantar fasciitis in my right foot

Extreme bursitis in my right wrist

Extreme neck pain, middle back pain and lower back pain

Pain throughout my rib cage

Eyelid twitch

Pain in my pelvis (dull pain similar to being kicked in the groin)

High blood pressure with spikes

Strong feelings of anxiety

Being awakened at night with pounding heart, chest pressure and discomfort and occasional sharp abdominal pains

Very little sex drive (when it does come, it shows up momentarily and then disappears)

Consistent minor constipation â€“ I defecate twice daily on average, but I donâ€™t pass the stool easily and often have to push some. This happens despite my drinking adequate water.

Anal and prostate pain and irritation

Bouts of hypoglycemia despite what appears to be good blood glucose labs & carb restriction

Very infrequent episodes of energy. Almost like a burst of energy and feeling of well-being that passes after no more than a day or so.

Persistent â€œfrog in the throat feelingâ€￾ + throat clearing with mucous production

Variability in my response to cold temperatures

Sharp pains that move around, starting in abdomen, moving to chest, ending in neck and under my chin

Any stress (no matter how small) seems to send me off the deep end (esp. chest discomfort)

When not in an accute flare, some of these symptoms ease, while others persist, but with less severity. So all in all, it's terribly uncomfortable and downright scary, especially with the chest pains. Makes me feel like I'm having a damn heart attack.

I've resisted Plaquenil because you risk macular degeneration. Additionally, I believe it will suppress immune function, which is not what I want. I want to reestablish proper balance.

### chocolate — 2012-08-10T01:56:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/plaquenil.2878/post-44001>

We feel much better with the just the potassium and magnesium. They help the bms from all I'm reading. We got some Russian kind of anxiety pill that nips things really quickly. Its called Phenibut. and then we are taking the 250 mg of betaine in the middle of each meal. My daughter slept 12 hours last night and we don't know why because we've added two things at once. Also every other meal we're taking 5mg of actual lithium (120 mg of lithium orotate) I kind of have the feeling that its all of them. Also, to sleep, she is taking Picamolar 50 mg and 200 mg glycine. The infuriating part of our situation is that a lot of it was for sure electrolytes. And we take the acetyl carnitine because its good for your heart. I keep reading about the heart and kidney troubles cause heart attacks. Also, another thing we added was the sesamin/vitamin c/ quercetin cocktail it helps with bones and inflammation. We finally got the good k2 for the phosphurus/phospholipid problems. The choline and inositol is supposed to be pretty good for panic pain. The choline goes with the lithium.

I was reading the part about hypoglycemia. My daughter doesn't sense hunger that well and fades. We don't know why either one of us are that way, because we eat lots. I bet in the end it all boils down to the k2. Just guessing. I think we're going to get better from it. The phosphorus has to go somewhere and you can't be so different that you are supposed to eat carbs forever. Its a bone thing just like we've got. We have a calcium problem, which defaults to every other kind of bone mineral problem there is. Her gene screen showed her as having a difficult time storing and processing fat, but the medicines she was on caused it. She was getting the bloat gone, with no carbs, but didn't get her strength until the acetyl carnitine. The potassium deficiency makes your guts act really weird too. They will puff up and hurt and do nothing. If you get really good at going bobo you'll sure feel better.

I just read an exercise article that it takes a month to be a fat burner in the muscles. I might have misunderstood something, but I think when everything gets going right, it will be a month from then.

I just hope you don't get desperate and eat carbs. I dreamed about cookies last night and that isn't me at all. I bet my gut flora sent crazy signals.

We are sure that we need that cycloset, but I think we are just going to call the drug company and have them find us a doctor.

### chocolate — 2012-08-10T02:33:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/plaquenil.2878/post-44017>

[http://www.umm.edu/altmed/articles/phosphorus-000319.htm](http://http://www.umm.edu/altmed/articles/phosphorus-000319.htm)

this is a good link

> **As the amount of phosphorus you eat rises, so does the need for calcium. The delicate balance between calcium and phosphorus is necessary for proper bone density and prevention of osteoporosis.**
>
>
>
> Read more: [http://www.umm.edu/altmed/articles/phosphorus-000319.htm#ixzz235uKdwQd](http://http://www.umm.edu/altmed/articles/phosphorus-000319.htm#ixzz235uKdwQd)

and then you have to have magnesium to absorb calcium and you have to have vitamin k2 to tell the calcium where to go and if it doesn't know where to go the phosphorus gets squirrelly and lands in plaques, its just one thing after another. And you need selenium and sulfur. All of that, just to get your crummy phospholipids. And taking a phospholipid pill won't work right unless you have your potassium. But you also need to take vitamin c for sure with your meat, to break up the plaques. We have ours with our Quercetin./sesamin. It helps you absorb more dha and makes your vitamin e last longer. It also uses a different pathway so Asian people like me can drink, so who knows what else it can hopscotch. And it helps turn fat into bone in a petrie dish. And it helps weight loss, with boron, which can be bad for the kidneys ( it think that was old news before vitamin k2 remember, that's what repairs teeth). Boron helps regulate growth hormone and can help you lose weight and heal.

### chocolate — 2012-08-11T02:19:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/plaquenil.2878/post-44223>

[http://www.kurzweilai.net/simple-mathematical-computations-underlie-brain-circuits](http://http://www.kurzweilai.net/simple-mathematical-computations-underlie-brain-circuits)

> The team combined this with calcium imaging inside the target pyramidal cells. Calcium levels reflect a cell’s electrical activity, allowing the researchers to determine how much activity was repressed by the inhibitory cells.

This whole article is important. we have the symptoms of too much calcium in the brain.... if I had to guess why your phosphorus isn't going there, I would guess you have too little. We found out that you need magnesium in order not to dump calcium. We need the k2 to direct the calcium. Everyone does. I think it means that if you have too little magnesium, by default you have too little calcium. But what we're up against is the glucose in the brain is different from the stuff we measure. I am going by the fix the body, the brain will follow. K2 will go to your brain and put it there if you need it, I don't know if it goes from your plaques to you brain or your plaques to your bones then to your brain. That's my thinking straight out of bag.

### chocolate — 2012-08-11T06:17:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/plaquenil.2878/post-44279>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/19018742](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/19018742)

\[Changes in mineral metabolism in stage 3, 4, and 5 chronic kidney disease (not on dialysis)\].

> Aluminum hydroxide may sometimes be required to control phosphoremia or the occurrence of hypercalcemia. Serum aluminum values should be maintained below 30 mcg/L.

This is kind of the opposite of you, or maybe not. I know there is lots of aluminum in Alzheimers and after reading and reading, the best practice for managing the aluminum seems to be to replace the magnesium, AGAIN. It makes the aluminum irrelevant. Every thing I've also read says that the aluminum has no purpose in the body, but it must. Maybe the aluminum is hoarding your phosphorus in your brain or somewhere, just waiting for the magnesium. It is my daughter's new drug of choice. I think your panic attacks mights stop so fast if you have more of it, with the potassium. Anyway, I'm not scared of aluminum any more. I just wonder why it is so heavy in CA.

And the betaine, so you can make your own nerve meds. Lithium orotate will shut an attack right down.

### chocolate — 2012-08-11T21:54:06+0300

Polymorphism of glutathione S-transferase P1 gene affects human vitamin C metabolism Biochemical and Biophysical Research

> Serine phosphorylation of glutathione S-transferase P1 (GSTP1) by PKCα enhances GSTP1-dependent cisplatin metabolism and resistance in human glioma cells.
>
> Citation:
>
> Biochemical pharmacology, 01 November 2010, vol./is. 80/9(1343-55), 1873-2968
>
> Author(s):
>
> Singh S,Okamura T,Ali-Osman F
>
> Abstract:
>
> Recently, we reported that the human GSTP1 is phosphorylated and functionally activated by the PKC class of serine**/threonine** kinases. In this study, we investigated the contribution of this post-translational modification of GSTP1 to tumor cisplatin resistance

This is about a tumor being resistance to a drug, but maybe some of this stuff will help. I take melatonin and hope it turns into glutathione. Its all brain gut stuff.

### chocolate — 2012-08-22T16:21:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/plaquenil.2878/post-46299>

[http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?2239-candida-and-arachidonic-acid&p=35493&highlight=histidine#post35493](http://http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?2239-candida-and-arachidonic-acid&p=35493&highlight=histidine#post35493)

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/10901420](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/10901420)

Dietary cod liver oil decreases arachidonic acid in rat gastric mucosa and increases stress-induced gastric erosions.

Science Institute, University of Iceland, Reykjavik.

Abstract

The purpose of this study was to examine the influence of long-term feeding of dietary fat rich in either n-3 or n-6 fatty acids on the availability of arachidonic acid (20:4n-6) in major phospholipids of gastric mucosa in rats. Three groups of male Wistar rats were fed either a standard diet, a cod liver oil-enriched diet (10% by weight), or a corn oil-enriched diet (10% by weight) for 8 mon. **Dietary cod liver oil significantly reduced the level of 20:4n-6 in phosphatidylcholine (PC) and in phosphatidylethanolamine (PE) of gastric mucosa. The loss of 20:4n-6 was compensated for by eicosapentaenoic acid (20:5n-3) in PC, whereas the decrease in 20:4n-6 in PE corresponded to the increase in three n-3 fatty acids: 20:5n-3, docosapentaenoic acid (22:5n-3), and docosahexaenoic acid (22:6n-3). The level of 20:5n-3 was higher than the level of 22:6n-3 both in PC and PE of mucosa in rats fed cod liver oil. Diets supplemented with corn oil increased the level of 18:2n-6 but decreased the monoene fatty acids 16:1 and 18:1n-7 in PC but not in PE of gastric mucosa. The 20:4n-6 levels of both PC and PE were markedly reduced by dietary cod liver oil, to about one-third of control levels. Similar changes were also observed in the stomach wall. Gastric erosions were observed in all rats exposed to restriction stress, but this form of stress induced twice the number of erosions in rats fed fish oil compared to control rats or rats fed corn oil. We conclude that a diet rich in fish oil altered the balance between n-6 and n-3 fatty acids in major gastric mucosal phospholipids, markedly reduced the availability of 20:4n-6, and increased the incidence of gastric erosions induced by restriction or emotional stress.

**This is box number 5... maybe if you did a teaspoon and half of olive oil, it would help... that's what we do on lettuce

### chocolate — 2012-08-26T01:43:30+0300

[http://www.mendeley.com/research/curcumin-modulates-dopaminergic-receptor-creb-phospholipase-c-gene-expression-cerebral-cortex-cerebellum-streptozotocin-induced-diabetic-rats-3/](http://http://www.mendeley.com/research/curcumin-modulates-dopaminergic-receptor-creb-phospholipase-c-gene-expression-cerebral-cortex-cerebellum-streptozotocin-induced-diabetic-rats-3/)

> Our results indicate that curcumin has a potential to regulate diabetes induced malfunctions of dopaminergic signalling, CREB and Phospholipase C expression in cerebral cortex and cerebellum and thereby improving the cognitive and emotional functions associated with these regions. Furthermore, in line with these studies an interaction between curcumin and dopaminergic receptors, CREB and **phospholipase C** is suggested, which attenuates the cortical and cerebellar dysfunction in diabetes. These results suggest that curcumin holds promise as an agent to prevent or treat CNS complications in diabetes.

this is just the tail end of the abstract. And it is rats.
